- **Active sign convention** is assumed universally in KTU; always draw current arrow entering the positive terminal.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Semiconductor Electronics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Semiconductor Electronics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Semiconductors

A **semiconductor** is a crystalline solid material whose electrical conductivity lies between that of a **conductor** (e.g., copper, $\sigma \approx 10^{7} \, \text{S/m}$) and an **insulator** (e.g., glass, $\sigma \approx 10^{-10} \, \text{S/m}$), typically in the range of $\mathbf{10^{-5} \text{ to } 10^{5} \, \text{S/m}}$. The defining feature of a semiconductor is that its electrical conductivity can be precisely controlled over many orders of magnitude by:

- **Doping** (intentional addition of impurity atoms)
- **Temperature** (conductivity increases with temperature, opposite to metals)
- **Electric field** (used in field-effect devices)
- **Light/optical excitation** (photoconductivity, basis of photodiodes and solar cells)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Mechatronic Systems, semiconductors form the *physical layer* of nearly every sensor, actuator driver, signal conditioner, and microcontroller. You are expected to know device *behavior* and *application*, not the quantum mechanics of band formation.

The two most important elemental semiconductors used in mechatronic hardware are:

| Element | Group | Lattice | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Silicon (Si)** | IV | Diamond cubic | Discrete devices, ICs, MEMS |
| **Germanium (Ge)** | IV | Diamond cubic | Early diodes, IR optics |
| **Gallium Arsenide (GaAs)** | III–V | Zinc blende | Optoelectronics, high-frequency |

## 1.2 Intrinsic vs. Extrinsic Semiconductors

**Intrinsic Semiconductor:** A *pure* semiconductor crystal with no impurities and a perfect lattice. At $T = 0 \, \text{K}$, the valence band is completely full and the conduction band is completely empty, making it behave as an insulator. As temperature rises, thermal energy excites electrons across the **forbidden energy gap ($E_g$)**, generating equal numbers of free electrons ($n$) in the conduction band and holes ($p$) in the valence band.

$$n_i = p_i \quad \text{(mass action law for intrinsic material)}$$

**Extrinsic Semiconductor:** A semiconductor *deliberately doped* with trace amounts of Group III or Group V elements to dominate either electron or hole conduction. The two types are:

- **N-type:** Doped with pentavalent impurities (P, As, Sb) → electrons are *majority carriers*, holes are *minority carriers*. Donors release electrons at a shallow donor energy level $E_D$ just below $E_C$.
- **P-type:** Doped with trivalent impurities (B, Ga, In) → holes are *majority carriers*, electrons are *minority carriers*. Acceptors capture electrons at a shallow acceptor level $E_A$ just above $E_V$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key fact:** Adding just **1 ppm** of donor atoms can increase conductivity by **6 orders of magnitude**. This sensitivity is exactly what enables transistors, sensors, and integrated circuits.

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy

Think of a semiconductor as a **multi-storey parking garage** (the crystal lattice):

- The **Valence Band** = the ground floor (cars parked, not moving).
- The **Conduction Band** = the top floor (cars driving = current flow).
- The **Forbidden Gap** ($E_g$) = the staircase between floors. For insulators the staircase is too steep to climb; for conductors there is no staircase; for semiconductors, a moderate push (heat, light, voltage) lets a few electrons "drive up."

**Doping analogy:** The "cars" in a pure garage are balanced. Injecting a few **trucks with trailers** (pentavalent atoms like P) leaves extra "spare cars" (free electrons) on the top floor → **N-type**. Removing a few parking spots by adding **compact cars with missing wheels** (trivalent atoms like B) leaves empty slots (holes) that other cars roll into → **P-type**.

## 1.4 Why Semiconductors Matter in Mechatronics

Every mechatronic subsystem ultimately depends on semiconductor physics:

- **Sensing:** Photodiodes, thermistors, Hall sensors, strain gauges (piezoresistive)
- **Signal Conditioning:** Op-amps, diodes for rectification, Zener for reference
- **Actuation:** MOSFET/IGBT drivers for motors, solenoid drivers
- **Processing:** Microcontrollers, FPGAs, ADCs/DACs
- **Power:** Diodes in H-bridges, freewheeling diodes, SMPS

> [!TIP]
> For the KTU board exam, always link the device back to a *mechatronic application*. Examiners award higher marks when you show you understand the *system-level relevance*, not just the device equation.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Energy Band Theory — The Foundation

In a single isolated atom, electrons occupy discrete energy levels. When $N$ atoms are brought together to form a solid, the atomic wavefunctions overlap, and each level splits into $N$ closely spaced levels called a **band**. Two bands are most relevant:

1. **Valence Band (VB)** — highest band fully occupied at $0 \, \text{K}$.
2. **Conduction Band (CB)** — lowest band partially filled or empty at $0 \, \text{K}$.

The separation between them is the **band gap** $E_g$.

### 2.1.1 Classification of Solids by Band Gap

| Material Type | $E_g$ (eV) | Behavior |
|---|---|---|
| Insulator | $> 4$ | VB and CB do not overlap; $E_g$ too large to bridge thermally |
| Semiconductor | $0.1$ to $3$ | $E_g$ bridgeable by thermal/optical energy |
| Conductor | $0$ (or overlap) | VB and CB overlap; free electrons always present |

**Important numerical values for KTU problems:**

- Silicon: $E_g = 1.12 \, \text{eV}$ at $300 \, \text{K}$
- Germanium: $E_g = 0.67 \, \text{eV}$ at $300 \, \text{K}$
- GaAs: $E_g = 1.42 \, \text{eV}$ at $300 \, \text{K}$

> [!NOTE]
> **$1 \, \text{eV}$** is the energy gained by one electron crossing a potential difference of $1 \, \text{V}$. Numerically, $1 \, \text{eV} = 1.602 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{J}$.

## 2.2 Intrinsic Carrier Concentration

The number of electrons thermally excited across the band gap per unit volume follows from Fermi–Dirac statistics. For an intrinsic semiconductor:

$$n_i = A T^{3/2} \exp\!\left(-\frac{E_g}{2 k_B T}\right)$$

where:
- $A$ is a material constant (depends on effective masses)
- $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23} \, \text{J/K} = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{eV/K}$ is **Boltzmann's constant**
- $T$ is absolute temperature in Kelvin

**Key result:** A *small* change in $T$ produces a *large* change in $n_i$ because of the exponential term. This is why semiconductor conductivity is highly temperature-sensitive — a property exploited in thermistors and temperature sensors.

## 2.3 Doping and Extrinsic Carrier Densities

When donors (concentration $N_D$) or acceptors (concentration $N_A$) are added:

**Charge Neutrality Condition:**

$$n_0 + N_A^{-} = p_0 + N_D^{+}$$

For an N-type semiconductor ($N_D \gg N_A$):

$$n_0 \approx N_D, \qquad p_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D}$$

For a P-type semiconductor ($N_A \gg N_D$):

$$p_0 \approx N_A, \qquad n_0 = \frac{n_i^2}{N_A}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The product $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ is **always true** (Law of Mass Action) at thermal equilibrium, regardless of doping.

## 2.4 Drift and Diffusion Currents

Two mechanisms transport charge in a semiconductor:

### 2.4.1 Drift Current
Charges move under an applied electric field $E$:

$$J_{\text{drift}} = q \left( n \mu_n + p \mu_p \right) E = \sigma E$$

where:
- $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, \text{C}$ (electron charge magnitude)
- $\mu_n, \mu_p$ = electron and hole mobilities (units: $\text{m}^2/\text{V·s}$)
- $\sigma$ = conductivity

For Si at 300 K: $\mu_n \approx 1350 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$, $\mu_p \approx 480 \, \text{cm}^2/\text{V·s}$.

### 2.4.2 Diffusion Current
Charges move down a concentration gradient (no field needed):

$$J_{\text{diff},n} = q D_n \frac{dn}{dx}, \qquad J_{\text{diff},p} = -q D_p \frac{dp}{dx}$$

The **Einstein Relation** links mobility and diffusivity:

$$\frac{D}{\mu} = \frac{k_B T}{q} = V_T \quad \text{(thermal voltage)}$$

At $T = 300 \, \text{K}$, $\, V_T \approx 25.85 \, \text{mV}$. **KTU problems routinely use $V_T = 26 \, \text{mV}$.**

## 2.5 The PN Junction Diode

When P-type and N-type regions are formed in the same crystal, a **PN junction** is created. At the metallurgical boundary:

- Electrons from the N-side diffuse into the P-side and recombine with holes.
- Holes from the P-side diffuse into the N-side and recombine with electrons.
- This leaves behind a region near the junction **depleted of mobile carriers**, called the **depletion region** or **space-charge region**.
- The exposed ionized dopants create a built-in **potential barrier** $V_0$.

$$V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

For Si with $N_A = N_D = 10^{16} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, $V_0 \approx 0.7 \, \text{V}$.
For Ge, $V_0 \approx 0.3 \, \text{V}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory trick:** "**0.7 for Si, 0.3 for Ge**" — these are the *cut-in voltages* you will use in every diode problem.

## 2.6 Depletion Width

The total depletion width $W$ is split into $W_p$ (P-side) and $W_n$ (N-side), inversely proportional to doping:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_0}{q} \left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} \right)}$$

where $\varepsilon_s = \varepsilon_r \varepsilon_0$ is the semiconductor permittivity ($\varepsilon_r \approx 11.7$ for Si, $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$).

**For a one-sided junction** (e.g., $N_D \gg N_A$, called $P^+N$):

$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_0 - V_{\text{applied}})}{q N_A}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> Under reverse bias, $V_{\text{applied}}$ is **negative**, so $(V_0 - V)$ grows and the depletion region widens. This is the operating principle of varactor diodes used for voltage-controlled tuning in RF mechatronic systems.

## 2.7 The Diode Equation (Shockley)

The current through a PN junction diode is:

$$I_D = I_S \left[ \exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{n V_T}\right) - 1 \right]$$

where:
- $I_S$ = reverse saturation current (typically $10^{-15}$ to $10^{-9} \, \text{A}$ for Si)
- $V_D$ = voltage across the diode (positive for forward bias)
- $n$ = ideality factor (1 to 2; usually $\approx 1$ for ideal, $\approx 2$ for real small-signal)
- $V_T = k_B T / q$

## 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> Memorize this table — these equations appear in nearly every KTU question on this module.

| # | Formula | Meaning | Typical Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $n_i = A T^{3/2} \exp(-E_g / 2k_B T)$ | Intrinsic carrier concentration | $\text{cm}^{-3}$ |
| 2 | $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$ | Mass action law | $\text{cm}^{-6}$ |
| 3 | $J_{\text{drift}} = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)E$ | Drift current density | $\text{A/m}^2$ |
| 4 | $J_{\text{diff}} = qD \dfrac{dp}{dx}$ | Diffusion current density | $\text{A/m}^2$ |
| 5 | $D/\mu = V_T = k_BT/q \approx 26 \, \text{mV}$ | Einstein relation | V |
| 6 | $V_0 = V_T \ln(N_A N_D / n_i^2)$ | Built-in potential | V |
| 7 | $W = \sqrt{ \dfrac{2\varepsilon_s (V_0 - V)}{q} \left(\dfrac{1}{N_A} + \dfrac{1}{N_D}\right) }$ | Depletion width | m |
| 8 | $I_D = I_S \left[ \exp(V_D / nV_T) - 1 \right]$ | Shockley diode equation | A |
| 9 | $I_{\text{Zener}} = V_Z / R_S$ (in regulator) | Zener regulator current | A |
| 10 | $r_d = nV_T / I_D$ | Small-signal diode resistance | $\Omega$ |
| 11 | $g_m = I_C / V_T$ | BJT transconductance | S |
| 12 | $I_C = \beta I_B = I_S \exp(V_{BE}/V_T)$ | BJT collector current | A |
| 13 | $I_D = \frac{k}{2} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ (sat.) | MOSFET drain current | A |

## 2.9 Special Semiconductor Devices for Mechatronics

### 2.9.1 Zener Diode
Heavily doped PN junction operated in **reverse breakdown**. The breakdown voltage $V_Z$ is *sharp* and *stable*, making it ideal as a voltage reference. In mechatronics: supply rails for sensor signal conditioning, over-voltage protection, ADC reference.

**Zener regulator design (KTU classic):**

$$R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_L + I_Z}$$

where $I_Z$ must lie between $I_{Z,\min}$ (knee) and $I_{Z,\max}$ (power rating).

### 2.9.2 Light Emitting Diode (LED)
Forward-biased PN junction in direct-bandgap material (GaAs, GaP, InGaN). Electron-hole recombination releases a photon of energy $hf \approx E_g$. Used in optocouplers, optical encoders, and panel indicators in mechatronic HMI.

### 2.9.3 Photodiode
Operated in reverse bias; incident photons with $hf > E_g$ create electron-hole pairs, increasing reverse current. Used in line-follower robots, optical encoders, light curtains, and laser range finders.

### 2.9.4 Schottky Diode
Metal–semiconductor junction with very low forward drop ($\approx 0.3 \, \text{V}$) and no minority-carrier storage → very fast switching. Used in SMPS, freewheeling in high-frequency motor drives.

### 2.9.5 BJT (Bipolar Junction Transistor)
Three-terminal device (E, B, C); current-controlled. NPN and PNP. Operating regions: **cut-off**, **active**, **saturation**. In mechatronics: low-side switches, level shifters, signal amplifiers for sensor signals.

**Regions and conditions (NPN):**
- **Cut-off:** $V_{BE} < 0.7 \, \text{V}$ → $I_C = 0$
- **Active:** $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$, $V_{CE} > V_{CE,\text{sat}}$ → $I_C = \beta I_B$
- **Saturation:** $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$, $V_{CE} = V_{CE,\text{sat}} \approx 0.2 \, \text{V}$ → $I_C < \beta I_B$

### 2.9.6 MOSFET (Metal–Oxide–Semiconductor FET)
Voltage-controlled device; gate insulated by a thin $\text{SiO}_2$ layer. Used as the *primary switch* in DC motor drivers, H-bridges, and PWM speed controllers in mechatronics.

**N-channel enhancement-mode MOSFET:**
- $V_{GS} < V_{th}$ → **OFF** (cutoff)
- $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ → **ON** (triode or saturation)

In saturation:

$$I_D = \frac{1}{2} \mu_n C_{ox} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2 = \frac{k}{2} \frac{W}{L} (V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$$

> [!TIP]
> For KTU problems, you are expected to know MOSFET switching behavior and its use as a *low-side driver* in motor control — you do *not* need to derive the $I_D$ equation from quantum mechanics.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Built-in Potential $V_0$ of a PN Junction

We want to show that the contact potential between the P-side and N-side at equilibrium is

$$V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

**Step 1: Set up the boundary condition.**
At thermal equilibrium, the **Fermi level** $E_F$ must be *constant* throughout the crystal (a single Fermi level is the definition of equilibrium). In the neutral P-region, $E_F$ lies near the valence band; in the neutral N-region, $E_F$ lies near the conduction band. The difference in band positions equals the built-in potential times $q$.

**Step 2: Write electron concentration on each side.**
In the neutral N-region, the electron concentration is $n_{n0} = N_D$. In the neutral P-region, $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$.

**Step 3: Apply the Boltzmann relation between two regions in equilibrium.**
For any carrier in equilibrium, the concentration in a region at potential $\phi$ relative to another is:

$$\frac{n_{n0}}{n_{p0}} = \exp\!\left(\frac{q V_0}{k_B T}\right) = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_0}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 4: Substitute the doping expressions.**

$$\frac{N_D}{n_i^2 / N_A} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_0}{V_T}\right)$$

$$\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_0}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 5: Take the natural logarithm of both sides.**

$$V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!NOTE]
> This equation tells us that *higher doping* (larger $N_A N_D$) → *larger barrier*. This makes physical sense: a heavily doped junction has many more charges to expose, so the resulting built-in field must be stronger to stop further diffusion.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Depletion Width $W$ in an Abrupt PN Junction

We model the depletion region as a parallel-plate capacitor with charge density $\rho = qN_D$ on the N-side and $\rho = -qN_A$ on the P-side. We solve Poisson's equation in one dimension:

$$\frac{d^2 V(x)}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho(x)}{\varepsilon_s}$$

**Step 1: Define coordinate system.**
Let $x = -x_p$ mark the edge of the depletion region on the P-side, and $x = +x_n$ mark the edge on the N-side. Total width is $W = x_p + x_n$.

**Step 2: Charge neutrality of the depletion region.**
The total positive charge on the N-side must equal the total negative charge on the P-side:

$$q N_D x_n = q N_A x_p$$

$$x_n = \frac{N_A}{N_D} x_p \quad \text{(N-side narrower when $N_D$ is larger)}$$

**Step 3: Solve Poisson's equation in the N-region** (where $\rho = qN_D$):

$$\frac{d^2 V}{dx^2} = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s}$$

Integrating twice with boundary conditions $E(x_n) = 0$ and $V(x_n) = 0$:

$$E(x) = -\frac{q N_D}{\varepsilon_s} (x - x_n)$$

$$V(x) = \frac{q N_D}{2 \varepsilon_s} (x - x_n)^2$$

**Step 4: Evaluate at $x = 0$** (the metallurgical junction):

$$V(0) = \frac{q N_D}{2\varepsilon_s} x_n^2$$

**Step 5: Repeat on the P-side** (where $\rho = -q N_A$):

$$V(0) = -\frac{q N_A}{2\varepsilon_s} x_p^2 + V_0$$

(We need a sign convention; the potential rises by $V_0$ from N to P.) Setting the two expressions equal and applying $x_n N_D = x_p N_A$:

$$V_0 = \frac{q N_D}{2\varepsilon_s} x_n^2 + \frac{q N_A}{2\varepsilon_s} x_p^2 = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s}(N_D x_n^2 + N_A x_p^2)$$

**Step 6: Eliminate $x_n$ and $x_p$** using $x_n = W N_A / (N_A + N_D)$ and $x_p = W N_D / (N_A + N_D)$:

$$V_0 = \frac{q}{2\varepsilon_s} \cdot \frac{N_A N_D}{(N_A + N_D)} \cdot W^2 \cdot \left(\frac{N_A + N_D}{N_A + N_D}\right)$$

After simplification:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_0}{q} \left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} \right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 7: For an applied reverse bias $V_R$**, replace $V_0$ with $(V_0 + V_R)$ because the external bias *adds* to the barrier:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s (V_0 + V_R)}{q} \left( \frac{1}{N_A} + \frac{1}{N_D} \right)} \quad \blacksquare$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Total Diode Current (Shockley Equation)

The diode current equals the *sum* of hole injection at the N-edge and electron injection at the P-edge. We model the minority-carrier concentrations as boundary values and solve the diffusion equation.

**Step 1: Minority carrier distribution in the N-region.**
At the edge of the depletion region on the N-side, the minority hole concentration is:

$$p_n(0) = p_{n0} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right) = \frac{n_i^2}{N_D} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right)$$

Far from the junction, $p_n \to p_{n0} = n_i^2 / N_D$.

**Step 2: Solve the steady-state diffusion equation** for excess holes $\delta p = p_n - p_{n0}$:

$$D_p \frac{d^2 (\delta p)}{dx^2} - \frac{\delta p}{\tau_p} = 0$$

The decaying exponential solution is $\delta p(x) = A \exp(-x/L_p)$ where $L_p = \sqrt{D_p \tau_p}$ is the **hole diffusion length**.

**Step 3: Apply boundary conditions:**

- At $x = 0$: $\delta p(0) = p_{n0}[\exp(V_D/V_T) - 1]$
- At $x \to \infty$: $\delta p \to 0$

This gives $A = p_{n0}[\exp(V_D/V_T) - 1]$, so:

$$p_n(x) = p_{n0} + p_{n0}\!\left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right) - 1\right] \exp\!\left(-\frac{x}{L_p}\right)$$

**Step 4: Hole diffusion current at $x = 0$:**

$$I_{p}(0) = -q A D_p \left.\frac{dp_n}{dx}\right|_{x=0} = \frac{q A D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right) - 1\right]$$

**Step 5: Similarly for electron diffusion from P-side**, then sum both contributions:

$$I_D = I_S \left[\exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right) - 1\right] \quad \text{with} \quad I_S = q A \left[\frac{D_p p_{n0}}{L_p} + \frac{D_n n_{p0}}{L_n}\right] \quad \blacksquare$$

> [!TIP]
> The factor of $-1$ in $[\exp(V_D/V_T) - 1]$ accounts for the small reverse saturation current that flows even under reverse bias.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: AC / Small-Signal Diode Resistance $r_d$

For small AC signals superimposed on a DC bias point, the diode behaves linearly. Differentiate Shockley's equation:

**Step 1:** Differentiate $I_D$ with respect to $V_D$:

$$\frac{d I_D}{d V_D} = \frac{I_S}{V_T} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_D}{V_T}\right) \approx \frac{I_D + I_S}{V_T}$$

**Step 2:** For forward bias, $I_D \gg I_S$, so:

$$\frac{d I_D}{d V_D} \approx \frac{I_D}{V_T}$$

**Step 3:** The reciprocal gives the small-signal resistance:

$$r_d = \frac{V_T}{I_D} = \frac{n V_T}{I_D} \quad \text{(with ideality factor $n$)} \quad \blacksquare$$

**Worked Example:** For $I_D = 10 \, \text{mA}$ and $n = 1$ at $T = 300 \, \text{K}$:

$$r_d = \frac{26 \, \text{mV}}{10 \, \text{mA}} = 2.6 \, \Omega$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: BJT Collector Current

For an NPN BJT in the active region, the emitter–base junction is forward biased and the collector–base junction is reverse biased.

**Step 1:** Electrons injected from the emitter into the base form the dominant current. The electron concentration in the base at the emitter edge is:

$$n_p(0) = n_{p0} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 2:** Assuming a thin base of width $W_B$ with $W_B \ll L_n$, the collector current is:

$$I_C = q A D_n \frac{n_{p0}}{W_B} \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right)$$

**Step 3:** Define $I_S = q A D_n n_{p0} / W_B$. Then:

$$I_C = I_S \exp\!\left(\frac{V_{BE}}{V_T}\right) \quad \blacksquare$$

**Step 4:** The base current is the recombination current plus hole injection from base to emitter, related by the common-emitter current gain:

$$I_C = \beta I_B$$

So $I_C$ is *exponentially* controlled by $V_{BE}$ and *linearly* related to $I_B$. This dual control is what makes the BJT a useful amplifier *and* switch.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Devices at a Glance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This comparison matrix is a favorite KTU question pattern. Memorize the bold entries.

| Property | **Diode** | **Zener Diode** | **BJT** | **MOSFET** |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Control variable | Voltage ($V_D$) | Reverse voltage ($V_Z$) | Base current ($I_B$) | Gate voltage ($V_{GS}$) |
| Terminals | 2 | 2 | 3 | 3 |
| Input impedance | Low (ohmic) | Low | Medium | **Very high** ($\text{G}\Omega$) |
| Switching speed | Medium (recovery time) | Medium | Medium | **Fast** (ns) |
| Forward drop / threshold | $0.7 \, \text{V}$ (Si) | $0.7 \, \text{V}$ fwd; $V_Z$ reverse | $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$ | $V_{th} = 1$–$3 \, \text{V}$ |
| Used as | Rectifier, switch | Voltage reference, regulator | Amplifier, switch | **Power switch, motor driver** |
| Key mechatronic role | H-bridge leg | Sensor supply reference | Signal amp for thermocouple | PWM motor speed control |

---

## 3.7 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A silicon PN junction at $300 \, \text{K}$ has $N_A = 10^{18} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$. Find (a) built-in potential, (b) depletion width, (c) peak electric field.

**Solution:**

**Part (a):** $V_T = k_B T / q = 8.617 \times 10^{-5} \times 300 = 0.02585 \, \text{V} \approx 26 \, \text{mV}$

$$V_0 = 0.026 \ln\!\left(\frac{10^{18} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.026 \ln(4.44 \times 10^{13})$$

$$\ln(4.44 \times 10^{13}) = \ln(4.44) + 13 \ln(10) = 1.491 + 29.934 = 31.425$$

$$V_0 = 0.026 \times 31.425 = 0.817 \, \text{V}$$

**Part (b):** $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{F/m}$

For a one-sided junction $N_A \gg N_D$:

$$W \approx \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_0}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.817}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{15} \times 10^{6}}}$$

Wait, $N_D = 10^{15} \, \text{cm}^{-3} = 10^{21} \, \text{m}^{-3}$. Converting:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.817}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{21}}}$$

$$= \sqrt{\frac{1.693 \times 10^{-10}}{1.6 \times 10^{2}}} = \sqrt{1.058 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.029 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m} = 1.03 \, \mu\text{m}$$

**Part (c):** Peak field (at the junction) for an asymmetric junction:

$$E_{\max} = \frac{q N_D x_n}{\varepsilon_s} = \frac{2 V_0}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.817}{1.03 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.59 \times 10^{6} \, \text{V/m}$$

> [!TIP]
> Notice that $E_{\max} = 2V_0 / W$ is a useful shortcut for triangular field profiles. You will save time in KTU exams by remembering it.

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> Mermaid `flowchart` is used to depict physical structure, current flow, and functional relationships, which is more reliable for a study-notes context than trying to draw a literal atomic diagram.

## 4.1 Energy Band Diagram: Intrinsic vs. Doped Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["VACUUM LEVEL E_VAC"] --> B["CONDUCTION BAND E_C"]
    B --> C["FORBIDDEN GAP E_g = 1.12 eV FOR Si"]
    C --> D["VALENCE BAND E_V"]
    D --> E["INTRINSIC: FERMI LEVEL E_F MID-GAP"]

    F["N-TYPE: E_F SHIFTS UP TOWARD E_C"] --> G["DONOR LEVEL E_D BELOW E_C"]
    H["P-TYPE: E_F SHIFTS DOWN TOWARD E_V"] --> I["ACCEPTOR LEVEL E_A ABOVE E_V"]
```

## 4.2 PN Junction Under Equilibrium and Bias

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph EQUILIBRIUM
        E1["E_C P-SIDE"] -->|BAND BENDING eV0| E2["E_C N-SIDE"]
        E3["E_F P-SIDE"] --> E4["E_F N-SIDE EQUAL AT EQUILIBRIUM"]
        E5["E_V P-SIDE"] --> E6["E_V N-SIDE"]
    end

    subgraph FORWARD_BIAS
        F1["APPLIED V REDUCES BARRIER"] --> F2["DEPLETION WIDTH NARROWS"]
        F2 --> F3["MAJORITY DIFFUSION CURRENT FLOWS"]
    end

    subgraph REVERSE_BIAS
        R1["APPLIED V INCREASES BARRIER"] --> R2["DEPLETION WIDTH EXPANDS"]
        R2 --> R3["MINORITY DRIFT SATURATION CURRENT"]
    end
```

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Mechatronic Subsystem Using Semiconductors

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["SENSOR: TEMPERATURE OR POSITION OR LIGHT"] --> B["SIGNAL CONDITIONING: OP-AMP AND DIODES"]
    B --> C["ADC AND MICROCONTROLLER"]
    C --> D["DRIVER STAGE: MOSFET OR BJT"]
    D --> E["ACTUATOR: DC MOTOR OR SOLENOID OR RELAY"]
    E --> F["MECHANICAL OUTPUT: POSITION OR SPEED"]

    G["POWER SUPPLY"] -->|"VOLTAGE REGULATION VIA ZENER"| B
    G --> D
```

## 4.4 Functional Flow: Zener Diode Voltage Regulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    VS["DC SOURCE V_S"] --> RS["SERIES RESISTOR R_S"]
    RS --> NODE["OUTPUT NODE V_OUT = V_Z"]
    NODE --> RL["LOAD RESISTOR R_L"]

    subgraph PARALLEL_BRANCH
        Z["ZENER DIODE REVERSE BIASED AT V_Z"] --> NODE
    end

    COND1["CONDITION: I_Z BETWEEN I_ZMIN AND I_ZMAX"] -.-> Z
    COND2["R_S = V_S MINUS V_Z DIVIDED BY I_L PLUS I_Z"] -.-> RS
```

## 4.5 MOSFET Switching for PWM Motor Speed Control

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A["MICROCONTROLLER PWM OUTPUT"] --> B["GATE DRIVER IC"]
    B --> C["MOSFET GATE TERMINAL"]
    C --> D{"V_GS GREATER THAN V_THRESHOLD?"}
    D -->|YES| E["MOSFET ON: D-S CHANNEL CONDUCTS"]
    D -->|NO| F["MOSFET OFF: NO CURRENT TO MOTOR"]

    E --> G["DC MOTOR ARMATURE CURRENT FLOWS"]
    F --> H["MOTOR FREEWHEELS VIA DIODE"]

    I["PWM DUTY CYCLE CONTROLS AVERAGE VOLTAGE"] -.-> A
    I -.-> G
```

## 4.6 BJT Operating Regions Summary

```mermaid
stateDiagram-v2
    [*] --> CUTOFF: V_BE LESS THAN 0.7 V
    CUTOFF --> ACTIVE: V_BE EQUAL 0.7 V AND V_CE GREATER THAN V_CESAT
    ACTIVE --> SATURATION: V_CE DROPS TO V_CESAT
    SATURATION --> CUTOFF: V_BE BELOW 0.7 V
    SATURATION --> ACTIVE: V_CE RISES ABOVE V_CESAT
```

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question A1** [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Differentiate between intrinsic and extrinsic semiconductors. State the mass action law.**

**Model Answer (≈ 100 words):**

An **intrinsic semiconductor** is a chemically pure crystal (e.g., pure Si) with no impurities. Its carrier concentration depends only on temperature, and $n_i = p_i$. An **extrinsic semiconductor** is doped with Group III (P-type) or Group V (N-type) impurities, making either holes or electrons the majority carriers. **Mass action law** states that at thermal equilibrium, the product of electron and hole concentrations is constant for a given temperature: $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$, independent of doping. *(3 marks: definition 1, distinction 1, law 1.)*

---

### **Question A2** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define built-in potential in a PN junction. Write the expression and state the typical value for silicon.**

**Model Answer (≈ 80 words):**

The **built-in potential** $V_0$ is the internal barrier voltage that forms across the depletion region of a PN junction at equilibrium, opposing further diffusion of majority carriers. It arises because of the charge separation between ionized donors and acceptors.

$$V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right)$$

For silicon at $300 \, \text{K}$ with typical doping, $V_0 \approx \mathbf{0.7 \, \text{V}}$. *(3 marks: definition 1, formula 1, value 1.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU Part B questions in ESE have an **internal choice**: the student answers *either* the main question *or* the alternative. Both options are equally demanding.

### **MAIN QUESTION — Semiconductor Electronics** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With neat energy band diagrams, explain the formation of a PN junction. Derive the expression for the built-in potential $V_0$ in terms of doping concentrations and intrinsic carrier density. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** A silicon PN junction has $N_A = 5 \times 10^{17} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, $N_D = 10^{15} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$, $n_i = 1.5 \times 10^{10} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$. Calculate (i) built-in potential, (ii) depletion width, (iii) peak electric field at zero bias. Take $\varepsilon_r = 11.7$. **— 7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

1. **Band diagrams:** Draw the energy bands of the P-region (where $E_F$ is close to $E_V$) and the N-region (where $E_F$ is close to $E_C$) before contact. After joining, align the Fermi levels; the bands bend in the depletion region. **(2 marks)**

2. **Diffusion process:** Electrons diffuse from N to P, holes from P to N, leaving behind ionized donors ($+$) and acceptors ($-$). This creates a depletion region with an electric field pointing from N to P, opposing further diffusion. **(2 marks)**

3. **Derivation:** At equilibrium, $E_F$ is constant. Applying the Boltzmann relation to the carrier concentrations on either side and using $n_{n0} = N_D$ and $n_{p0} = n_i^2 / N_A$:

$$\frac{N_D}{n_i^2 / N_A} = \exp\!\left(\frac{V_0}{V_T}\right) \implies V_0 = V_T \ln\!\left(\frac{N_A N_D}{n_i^2}\right) \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**(i) Built-in potential:** $V_T = 0.026 \, \text{V}$ (at 300 K)

$$V_0 = 0.026 \ln\!\left(\frac{5 \times 10^{17} \times 10^{15}}{(1.5 \times 10^{10})^2}\right) = 0.026 \ln(2.22 \times 10^{13})$$

$$\ln(2.22 \times 10^{13}) = \ln(2.22) + 13 \ln(10) = 0.798 + 29.934 = 30.73$$

$$\boxed{V_0 = 0.026 \times 30.73 = 0.799 \, \text{V} \approx 0.8 \, \text{V}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

**(ii) Depletion width:** Since $N_A \gg N_D$, it is a one-sided $P^+N$ junction, so depletion lies mostly in the N-side. $\varepsilon_s = 11.7 \times 8.854 \times 10^{-12} = 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \, \text{F/m}$. Converting $N_D = 10^{15} \, \text{cm}^{-3} = 10^{21} \, \text{m}^{-3}$:

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{2 \varepsilon_s V_0}{q N_D}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 1.036 \times 10^{-10} \times 0.8}{1.6 \times 10^{-19} \times 10^{21}}}$$

$$W = \sqrt{\frac{1.658 \times 10^{-10}}{1.6 \times 10^{2}}} = \sqrt{1.036 \times 10^{-12}} = 1.018 \times 10^{-6} \, \text{m}$$

$$\boxed{W \approx 1.02 \, \mu\text{m}} \quad \text{(3 marks)}$$

**(iii) Peak field:**

$$E_{\max} = \frac{2 V_0}{W} = \frac{2 \times 0.8}{1.02 \times 10^{-6}} = 1.57 \times 10^{6} \, \text{V/m} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

### **ALTERNATIVE QUESTION — Semiconductor Electronics** [KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** With the help of V-I characteristics, explain the operation of a Zener diode. Show how it is used as a voltage regulator. Derive the design equation for the series resistance. **— 7 marks**

**(b)** Explain the principle of operation of an N-channel enhancement MOSFET. Sketch its transfer characteristics and explain its use as a low-side switch in a mechatronic motor control circuit. **— 7 marks**

---

#### **Model Solution for (a):**

**Operation:** A Zener diode is a heavily doped PN junction designed to operate in the **reverse-breakdown region**. Beyond a sharp reverse voltage $V_Z$ (Zener voltage, typically 5.6 V to 200 V), the V-I curve becomes nearly vertical, meaning voltage is held almost constant over a wide current range. **Breakdown mechanisms:** Zener effect (tunneling) dominates for $V_Z < 5.6 \, \text{V}$; avalanche multiplication dominates for $V_Z > 5.6 \, \text{V}$. **(3 marks)**

**Regulator circuit:** A Zener diode is placed *in reverse bias* in parallel with the load $R_L$. A series resistor $R_S$ drops the excess voltage and limits current. Voltage across the load is clamped at $V_Z$ provided the Zener current $I_Z$ stays between $I_{Z,\min}$ (knee) and $I_{Z,\max}$ (power limit). **(2 marks)**

**Design equation:** Applying KCL at the output node, $I_S = I_Z + I_L$ and $V_S = I_S R_S + V_Z$:

$$\boxed{R_S = \frac{V_S - V_Z}{I_L + I_Z}} \quad \text{(2 marks)}$$

---

#### **Model Solution for (b):**

**Principle:** An N-channel enhancement MOSFET has a P-type substrate with two N+ regions (source and drain). A thin $\text{SiO}_2$ layer insulates the gate. With $V_{GS} = 0$, no channel exists and the device is OFF. When $V_{GS}$ exceeds the **threshold voltage $V_{th}$** (typically 1–3 V), an *inversion layer* of electrons forms at the oxide–semiconductor interface, creating a continuous N-channel that conducts current from drain to source. **(3 marks)**

**Transfer characteristic:** $I_D$ vs $V_{GS}$:

- For $V_{GS} < V_{th}$: $I_D = 0$ (cutoff).
- For $V_{GS} > V_{th}$: $I_D$ increases as $\frac{k}{2} \frac{W}{L}(V_{GS} - V_{th})^2$ in saturation.

**Low-side switch application:** In a mechatronic PWM motor driver, the MOSFET is placed between the motor's *negative* terminal and ground. A microcontroller output drives the gate through a gate resistor. When the gate is high, the MOSFET turns ON, current flows through the motor, and it spins. When low, the MOSFET is OFF and a **freewheeling diode** carries the inductive current. Varying the PWM duty cycle varies the *average* motor voltage, hence the speed. **(4 marks)**

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:**
> - When computing depletion width, **forget to convert** $\text{cm}^{-3}$ to $\text{m}^{-3}$ — this single mistake causes 3-mark deductions.
> - In diode problems, students often write $V_D = 0.7 \, \text{V}$ *and* apply the Shockley equation simultaneously. Pick **one** model: either the constant-voltage-drop model (ideal for switch analysis) or the Shockley model (for accurate current calculation).
> - For MOSFET questions, state **explicitly** which terminal is source vs. drain and whether it is N-channel or P-channel. Examiners deduct marks for ambiguous device identification.
> - In BJT questions, do not confuse **active region** (amplifier) with **saturation region** (switch closed). They are *not* synonyms.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> Use this as your last-page revision sheet before walking into the KTU exam hall.

- **Intrinsic carrier density** of Si at 300 K: $n_i \approx 1.5 \times 10^{10} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$. For Ge: $n_i \approx 2.4 \times 10^{13} \, \text{cm}^{-3}$.
- **Mass action law** is *always* true at equilibrium: $n_0 p_0 = n_i^2$.
- **Built-in potential** $V_0$ increases with doping logarithmically. For Si, $V_0 \approx 0.6$ to $0.9 \, \text{V}$ for typical doping.
- **Depletion width** $W$ scales as $1/\sqrt{N}$ for a one-sided junction. Higher doping → narrower depletion.
- **Cut-in voltage:** $0.7 \, \text{V}$ for Si diodes, $0.3 \, \text{V}$ for Ge. Use $V_T = 26 \, \text{mV}$ at $300 \, \text{K}$.
- **Shockley equation** is the master equation for a diode: $I_D = I_S[\exp(V_D/nV_T) - 1]$.
- **Small-signal resistance** $r_d = nV_T / I_D$: *decreases* with bias current.
- **Zener diode:** Operated in *reverse breakdown*; use as voltage reference; the design equation is $R_S = (V_S - V_Z) / (I_L + I_Z)$.
- **BJT regions:** Cut-off (off), Active (amplifier), Saturation (fully on). $V_{BE} = 0.7 \, \text{V}$ (Si) whenever the BE junction is forward biased.
- **MOSFET as switch:** $V_{GS} > V_{th}$ → ON, else OFF. Almost zero gate current → ideal for microcontroller-driven mechatronic loads.
- **Thermal voltage** $V_T$ appears in *every* semiconductor equation — memorize $V_T \approx 26 \, \text{mV}$ at room temperature.
- **Einstein relation** $D/\mu = V_T$ links diffusion and drift.
- **Mechatronic link:** For every device you study, *always* be ready to name *one specific mechatronic application* (sensor, driver, regulator, etc.). Examiners reward applied thinking.
- **Energy band gap $E_g$:** Si = 1.12 eV, Ge = 0.67 eV, GaAs = 1.42 eV (all at 300 K).
- **Drift current** is proportional to $E$; **diffusion current** is proportional to the concentration gradient $\partial n/\partial x$.
- **Reverse saturation current** $I_S$ doubles for every $\approx 10 \, \text{K}$ rise in temperature — a critical sensor effect.

---

> **END OF NOTES — SEMICONDUCTOR ELECTRONICS, MODULE 1**
> *Prepared in accordance with KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech (NEP 2020) ESE pattern.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Transistor Applications
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Transistor Applications ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition In the context of Mechatronic Systems (PEEET863), a **Transistor** is a three-terminal semiconductor active device that acts as a fundamental building block for signal conditioning, power interfacing, and intelligent control in electromechanical systems. **Transistor Applications** refer to the engineered deployment of Bipolar Junction Transistors (BJTs) and Metal-Oxide-Semiconductor Field-Effect Transistors (MOSFETs) in four principal roles within a mechatronic chain: (i) **Switching** of actuators and loads, (ii) **Amplification** of low-level sensor signals, (iii) **Level Shifting** between incompatible voltage domains, and (iv) **Driver/Buffer** stages for microcontrollers interfacing high-current devices. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** Transistor Applications form the *interface layer* between the low-power digital/analog domain (sensors, MCUs) and the high-power domain (motors, solenoids, relays). Mastery of cutoff and saturation regions is essential for KTU ESE questions. ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy Imagine a **water tap and a pressure booster** connected in series to a garden hose. - The **transistor as a SWITCH** behaves like the **water tap** — it is either fully **ON** (water gushes out, transistor in *saturation*) or fully **OFF** (no water, transistor in *cutoff*). It does not waste energy in between because the tap is either closed or fully open. - The **transistor as an AMPLIFIER** behaves like the **pressure booster** — a small finger press on the lever (a weak base/emitter current) controls a powerful jet of water (a large collector current). The transistor uses a small control signal to *govern* a much larger energy flow drawn from the supply rail. - The **transistor as a LEVEL SHIFTER** is like a **multilingual translator** — a 3.3 V signal from a microcontroller is "translated" into a 5 V or 12 V signal needed by an industrial sensor or driver. - The **transistor as a BUFFER/DRIVER** acts as a **bouncer at a club door** — a thin, weak signal (the customer) cannot push open a heavy steel door (a high-current load), so the transistor provides the muscle. > [!NOTE] > **Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bolded):** > - Silicon cut-in voltage: **V_BE(on) ≈ 0.7 V** for BJT, **V_GS(th) ≈ 1–4 V** for MOSFET. > - Thermal voltage at 300 K: **V_T = kT/q ≈ 25.85 mV** (≈ **26 mV** for KTU numericals). > - Saturation voltage: **V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V** (BJT), **R_DS(on) ≈ 0.01–1 Ω** (MOSFET). > - Logic-level threshold for digital switching: **V_IL ≤ 0.8 V**, **V_IH ≥ 2.0 V** (for standard 5 V CMOS). ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Three Operating Regions of a Transistor A BJT/MOSFET can operate in three distinct regions, each corresponding to a unique application class: #### 2.1.1 Cutoff Region (OFF Switch) - **Condition (BJT):** V_BE < 0.7 V, or base current I_B ≈ 0. - **Behavior:** Collector current I_C ≈ 0; transistor behaves as an **open switch**. - **Application:** Logic inverters, sleep-mode cutoff in battery-powered mechatronic nodes, emergency-stop interlocks. #### 2.1.2 Active Region (Linear Amplifier) - **Condition (BJT):** V_BE ≈ 0.7 V, V_CE > V_CE(sat), I_C = β·I_B. - **Behavior:** Output current is a faithful, scaled, and amplified replica of the input. - **Application:** Sensor signal pre-amplification (e.g., amplifying a thermocouple's microvolt-level output before feeding an ADC). #### 2.1.3 Saturation Region (ON Switch) - **Condition (BJT):** Both junctions forward-biased, I_B ≥ I_C(sat)/β, V_CE ≈ V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V. - **Behavior:** Transistor acts as a **closed switch** with negligible voltage drop. - **Application:** Driving relays, solenoids, DC motor windings, LED arrays, and stepper motor coils in a mechatronic actuator module. ### 2.2 Application 1 — Transistor as a Switch (DC Load Driver) A low-power digital signal (e.g., a GPIO pin of an Arduino/STM32) typically sources only **5–20 mA**, insufficient to energize a relay coil (≈ **70–100 mA**) or a DC motor (≈ **500 mA – 2 A**). A transistor switch solves this. **Operating Logic (Why it works):** - The MCU output drives the base through a base resistor R_B. - When V_IN = HIGH, I_B flows, I_C = β·I_B flows through the load, the load energizes. - When V_IN = LOW, I_B = 0, I_C = 0, the load de-energizes. - The transistor thus acts as a **current-controlled current valve**. **Critical Design Rule:** For **reliable saturation**, KTU examiners expect students to over-drive the base: $$I_B(\text{min}) = \frac{I_C(\text{sat})}{\beta_{\min}} \quad \Rightarrow \quad I_B(\text{design}) = \frac{I_C(\text{sat})}{\beta_{\min}} \cdot (\text{Overdrive Factor, typically } 2\text{–}5)$$ ### 2.3 Application 2 — Transistor as an Amplifier (Common-Emitter) A common-emitter (CE) configuration provides simultaneous **voltage** and **current** gain, making it the workhorse of low-frequency signal amplification in mechatronic front-ends (strain gauge bridges, piezoelectric sensors, thermistor bridges). - Small-signal AC voltage gain: A_v = -β·(R_C ∥ R_L) / r_π - The intrinsic emitter resistance: r_e = V_T / I_E - Input resistance: r_π = β·r_e ### 2.4 Application 3 — Transistor as a Level Shifter In mixed-voltage mechatronic boards (e.g., **3.3 V MCU ↔ 5 V sensor**), an NPN BJT in open-collector configuration with a pull-up resistor to the higher rail provides a simple, robust level translation. The output high level is set by the pull-up rail, not by the input voltage. ### 2.5 Application 4 — Transistor as a Driver (Darlington Pair) A **Darlington pair** (two BJTs cascaded) yields a composite current gain: $$\beta_{\text{Darlington}} \approx \beta_1 \cdot \beta_2$$ This is used in mechatronic systems to drive very high-current actuators (e.g., large solenoid valves, linear actuators) directly from low-power MCUs. The trade-off is a doubled V_BE drop (≈ **1.4 V**) and slower switching speed. ### 2.6 Application 5 — MOSFET Switch in H-Bridge / PWM In DC motor speed control, an **N-channel power MOSFET** driven by a PWM signal from the MCU (via a gate driver IC) provides: - Very low R_DS(on) → minimal conduction loss. - High switching speed (kHz range) → smooth PWM control. - Voltage-mode control at the gate → near-zero steady-state gate current. ### 2.7 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Application Context | Units / Boundary | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | BJT saturation I_C | I_C(sat) = (V_CC − V_CE(sat)) / R_C | Switch design | A | | 2 | Minimum base current | I_B(min) = I_C(sat) / β_min | Switch reliability | A | | 3 | Base resistor | R_B = (V_IN − V_BE) / I_B | Switch design | Ω | | 4 | DC load power | P_L = V_CC · I_C(sat) | Actuator drive | W | | 5 | Transistor dissipation | P_T = V_CE(sat) · I_C(sat) | Thermal design | W | | 6 | Small-signal gain (CE) | A_v = −β·(R_C ∥ R_L) / r_π | Amplifier | dimensionless | | 7 | Intrinsic emitter resistance | r_e = V_T / I_E ≈ 26 mV / I_E(mA) | Amplifier | Ω | | 8 | Hybrid-π input resistance | r_π = β · r_e | Amplifier | Ω | | 9 | Darlington composite gain | β_D ≈ β_1 · β_2 | High-current driver | dimensionless | | 10 | MOSFET conduction loss | P_D = I_D² · R_DS(on) | PWM driver | W | | 11 | Switching delay (BJT) | t_on, t_off ∝ τ_s = C_bc·(R_C ∥ R_L) | Speed limit | s | | 12 | Thermal derating | T_J = T_A + P_T · θ_JA | Reliability | °C | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The transistor switch is the *most common* interface between an MCU and a mechatronic actuator. KTU problems in Module 1 frequently test R_B calculation, saturation margin (overdrive factor), and dissipation P_T. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Base Resistor R_B for a Transistor Switch **Problem Statement (KTU style):** A BJT with β_min = 50 is to switch a 24 V relay coil (R_L = 480 Ω) using a 5 V MCU GPIO. Find the required R_B for guaranteed saturation with an overdrive factor of 5. **Step-by-Step Solution:** Step 1 — Identify the desired collector (load) current when ON. Applying KVL across the collector-emitter loop (relay coil R_L, V_CC supply, saturated transistor with V_CE(sat) ≈ 0.2 V): $$V_{CC} = I_C(\text{sat}) \cdot R_L + V_{CE(\text{sat})}$$ $$I_C(\text{sat}) = \frac{V_{CC} - V_{CE(\text{sat})}}{R_L}$$ $$I_C(\text{sat}) = \frac{24 - 0.2}{480} = \frac{23.8}{480}$$ $$I_C(\text{sat}) = 0.04958 \text{ A} \approx 49.6 \text{ mA}$$ Step 2 — Compute the *minimum* base current required to just barely saturate the transistor at the worst-case (lowest) β. $$I_{B(\text{min})} = \frac{I_C(\text{sat})}{\beta_{\min}}$$ $$I_{B(\text{min})} = \frac{49.6 \text{ mA}}{50} = 0.992 \text{ mA}$$ Step 3 — Apply the overdrive factor (ODF = 5) to ensure deep, noise-immune saturation. $$I_{B(\text{design})} = \text{ODF} \times I_{B(\text{min})}$$ $$I_{B(\text{design})} = 5 \times 0.992 \text{ mA} = 4.96 \text{ mA}$$ Step 4 — Apply KVL across the base-emitter loop (MCU voltage V_IN = 5 V, V_BE = 0.7 V, base resistor R_B): $$V_{IN} = I_B \cdot R_B + V_{BE}$$ $$R_B = \frac{V_{IN} - V_{BE}}{I_{B(\text{design})}}$$ $$R_B = \frac{5 - 0.7}{4.96 \text{ mA}}$$ $$R_B = \frac{4.3}{4.96 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$R_B \approx 866.9 \text{ \Omega}$$ Step 5 — Choose the nearest standard E12 resistor value: **R_B = 820 Ω** (preferred), or 1 kΩ for safety margin. > [!NOTE] > **Final Answer:** R_B ≈ 867 Ω; use standard 820 Ω or 1 kΩ. ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Small-Signal Voltage Gain of a Common-Emitter Amplifier **Step 1** — Start with the small-signal hybrid-π model. The collector current is i_c = g_m · v_be, where g_m = I_C / V_T is the transconductance. **Step 2** — Express g_m in terms of the DC emitter current I_E (since I_C ≈ I_E): $$g_m = \frac{I_C}{V_T} = \frac{I_E}{V_T}$$ **Step 3** — The intrinsic emitter resistance r_e is defined as r_e = 1/g_m: $$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{I_E(\text{mA})}$$ **Step 4** — Apply the voltage divider rule at the output. The output voltage v_out is dropped across the parallel combination (R_C ∥ R_L): $$v_{out} = -i_c \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = -g_m \cdot v_{in} \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L)$$ **Step 5** — Solve for the voltage gain A_v = v_out / v_in: $$A_v = -g_m \cdot (R_C \parallel R_L) = -\frac{(R_C \parallel R_L)}{r_e}$$ The negative sign indicates a **180° phase inversion** between input and output, a hallmark of CE amplifiers. **Step 6** — Boundary condition: gain magnitude |A_v| is maximized when R_L → ∞, giving the no-load gain: $$A_{v(\text{no load})} = -\frac{R_C}{r_e}$$ ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Power Dissipation and Thermal Limit **Step 1** — In a switching application, the transistor dissipates power only during transitions and in saturation: $$P_T = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \cdot I_C(\text{sat})$$ **Step 2** — The junction temperature must not exceed the maximum rating (typically 150 °C for silicon BJTs): $$T_J = T_A + P_T \cdot \theta_{JA}$$ **Step 3** — Solve for the maximum allowable ambient temperature: $$T_{A(\max)} = T_{J(\max)} - P_T \cdot \theta_{JA}$$ This is a critical KTU-style sanity check for any mechatronic driver PCB design. ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix — BJT vs. MOSFET as a Switch | Criterion | BJT (e.g., 2N2222) | MOSFET (e.g., IRF540) | |---|---|---| | Control variable | Current (I_B) | Voltage (V_GS) | | Steady-state gate/base current | I_B required (mA) | ≈ 0 (gate is insulated) | | Drive power | Continuous | Negligible | | Switching speed | Moderate (µs) | Fast (ns) | | Saturation voltage | ≈ 0.2 V (V_CE,sat) | I_D · R_DS(on) | | Typical current rating | 0.5–1 A | 10–100 A | | Best suited for | Low-current relay/LED | Motor/PWM/H-bridge | | KTU 2024 typical question | R_B calculation, β overdrive | R_DS(on), gate drive logic | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Transistor in a Mechatronic System ```mermaid flowchart LR MCU[McuGpio
3.3V or 5V Logic] --> Rgate[BaseResistor R_B
Current Limiter] Rgate --> BaseNode[Transistor Base
Control Terminal] BaseNode --> BJT[NPN BJT
2N2222 or MOSFET] BJT --> LoadBlock[Actuator Load
Relay Motor LED] LoadBlock --> VCC[Supply Rail
V CC 12V 24V] BJT --> GNDA[Ground Return
0V Reference] LoadBlock -.->|Flyback Diode| BJT BJT -.->|Sense V CE| Thermal[Thermal Check
T J less than 150C] Thermal --> HeatSink[Heat Sink
if P T greater than 0.5W] ``` ### 4.2 Transistor as a Switch — Application Map ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph ApplicationMatrix[Transistor Application Domains] SwitchApp[Switching Mode
Cutoff and Saturation] AmpApp[Amplification Mode
Active Region CE CC CB] LevelApp[Level Shifting
Open Collector with Pull Up] DriverApp[Driver Buffer
Darlington and MOSFET Bridge] end SwitchApp --> RelayDrv[Relay Coil Driver] SwitchApp --> MotorDrv[DC Motor ON OFF] SwitchApp --> SolenoidDrv[Solenoid Valve Control] AmpApp --> SensorPreamp[Sensor Signal Preamp] AmpApp --> AudioAmp[Audio Buzzer Amplifier] AmpApp --> BridgeAmp[Wheatstone Bridge Amplifier] LevelApp --> V33to5[3.3V to 5V Translation] LevelApp --> V5to24[5V to 24V Industrial Bus] DriverApp --> Darling[Darlington High Gain] DriverApp --> HBridge[H Bridge PWM Motor] DriverApp --> StepperDrv[Stepper Motor Coil Driver] ``` ### 4.3 Operating Region Decision Flowchart ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Input Signal V IN] --> Q1{Is V IN greater than V BE on?} Q1 -->|No| Cutoff[Cutoff Region
I C equals 0
Transistor OFF] Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Is I B enough to saturate?} Q2 -->|No| Active[Active Region
I C equals beta times I B
Linear Amplification] Q2 -->|Yes| Sat[Saturation Region
V CE equals 0.2V
Transistor ON as Switch] Cutoff --> App1[Application: Logic Inverter Sleep Mode] Active --> App2[Application: Sensor Amplifier Audio Stage] Sat --> App3[Application: Relay Motor LED Solenoid] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Set B]** Explain why a transistor in a switching application must be operated in either cutoff or saturation, but never in the active region. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) In the **active region**, the transistor dissipates substantial power because both V_CE and I_C are simultaneously large; this causes excessive heating and energy waste for a digital ON/OFF control task. - (1 mark) In **cutoff**, V_CE ≈ V_CC (max) but I_C ≈ 0, so P_T ≈ 0. In **saturation**, V_CE ≈ 0.2 V (min) but I_C is at its rated maximum, and P_T = V_CE(sat)·I_C is small because V_CE(sat) is tiny. - (1 mark) Hence, switching applications always bias the transistor at the two endpoints (cutoff/saturation) to minimize dissipation, maximize efficiency, and ensure a clean, noise-immune digital output. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]** Differentiate between BJT and MOSFET when used as a switch. Mention any two advantages of a MOSFET over a BJT in mechatronic actuator drives. **Model Answer (3 marks):** - (1 mark) A BJT is a **current-controlled** device requiring continuous base current I_B in the ON state, while a MOSFET is a **voltage-controlled** device with negligible steady-state gate current (the gate is insulated by an oxide layer). - (1 mark) **Advantage 1:** MOSFETs offer very low R_DS(on) (milliohms), enabling high-current switching (tens of amperes) with minimal conduction loss — ideal for DC motors. - (1 mark) **Advantage 2:** MOSFETs have faster switching speeds (ns vs. µs for BJT), making them superior for high-frequency PWM speed control of mechatronic actuators. ### 5.2 Part B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice) --- **Question A (14 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Main Paper, Q2(a)–(b)]** **(a)** With a neat circuit diagram, explain the operation of an NPN BJT used as a switch to drive a **24 V, 100 mA** relay coil from a **5 V microcontroller** GPIO. Given β_min = 50 and V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V, calculate the base resistor R_B using an overdrive factor of 5. Comment on why an overdrive factor is needed. (7 marks) **(b)** For the same circuit, compute (i) the power dissipated by the transistor in saturation, and (ii) the maximum ambient temperature the transistor can operate at, given θ_JA = 200 °C/W and T_J(max) = 150 °C. Suggest one practical method to reduce the operating junction temperature. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - **Circuit (2 marks):** Draw the standard low-side switch configuration: MCU GPIO → R_B → Base; Collector → Relay coil → +24 V; Emitter → GND; V_CC = 24 V; V_IN = 5 V. Show the flyback diode (1N4007) across the relay coil in reverse-bias. - **Step 1 — I_C(sat):** $$I_C(\text{sat}) = \frac{24 - 0.2}{R_L}; \quad R_L = \frac{24}{0.1} = 240 \text{ \Omega}$$ $$I_C(\text{sat}) = \frac{23.8}{240} = 99.17 \text{ mA} \approx 100 \text{ mA}$$ (0.5 mark) - **Step 2 — I_B(min):** $$I_{B(\text{min})} = \frac{100 \text{ mA}}{50} = 2 \text{ mA}$$ (0.5 mark) - **Step 3 — Overdrive:** $$I_{B(\text{design})} = 5 \times 2 = 10 \text{ mA}$$ (0.5 mark) - **Step 4 — R_B:** $$R_B = \frac{5 - 0.7}{10 \text{ mA}} = 430 \text{ \Omega}$$ Use standard **390 Ω** or **470 Ω**. (1 mark) - **Why overdrive is needed (2 marks):** To ensure the transistor is *deeply* saturated under worst-case conditions (lowest β, temperature variations, aging, β spread), so that V_CE stays near 0.2 V regardless of part-to-part variation. A higher I_B also speeds up turn-off by removing stored base charge faster. It guarantees noise immunity — accidental V_BE spikes will not falsely turn the transistor on. **Part (b) — 7 marks:** - **(i) Power dissipated (2 marks):** $$P_T = V_{CE(\text{sat})} \times I_C(\text{sat})$$ $$P_T = 0.2 \times 0.1 = 0.02 \text{ W} = 20 \text{ mW}$$ (Acceptable since very low; transistor is mostly OFF or fully ON.) - **(ii) Ambient temperature (3 marks):** $$T_{A(\max)} = T_{J(\max)} - P_T \cdot \theta_{JA}$$ $$T_{A(\max)} = 150 - (0.02)(200)$$ $$T_{A(\max)} = 150 - 4 = 146 \text{ °C}$$ (2 marks for substitution, 1 mark for final answer.) - **Practical method to reduce T_J (2 marks):** Attach a **heat sink** to the TO-220 package, which lowers the effective θ_JA (e.g., from 200 °C/W to 50 °C/W). Alternatively, reduce the switching frequency (less transition loss) or use a MOSFET with lower R_DS(on) to lower conduction loss. **(1 mark for identification, 1 mark for justification.)** --- **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Supplementary Paper, Q3(a)–(b)]** **(a)** Describe the use of a **MOSFET in a PWM-driven DC motor speed-control circuit** used in a mechatronic conveyor system. Explain why a gate driver IC is often used between the MCU PWM output and the MOSFET gate. (7 marks) **(b)** A mechatronic system uses an NPN BJT in common-emitter configuration to amplify a **strain-gauge bridge** output of 10 mV (peak). The collector resistor is 4.7 kΩ and the emitter current is biased at 1 mA. Calculate (i) r_e, (ii) the no-load voltage gain, and (iii) the peak output voltage. Comment on the suitability of this gain for driving an ADC of 5 V full-scale range. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **Part (a) — 7 marks:** - **PWM description (3 marks):** The MCU generates a fixed-frequency (e.g., 25 kHz) PWM with variable duty cycle. The MOSFET, when ON, connects the motor to V_CC; when OFF, the motor current freewheels through a flyback diode. The average motor voltage V_avg = D · V_CC (D = duty cycle), controlling speed. Higher D → higher average voltage → higher RPM. - **Need for gate driver (4 marks):** (i) MCUs cannot source/sink the high transient gate current needed to charge/discharge the MOSFET's gate capacitance C_iss quickly — a gate driver (e.g., TC4420, IR2104) provides ±1–2 A peak gate current. (ii) The MCU's 3.3 V/5 V logic may be insufficient to fully enhance a standard MOSFET (V_GS(th) = 4 V); a gate driver can be powered from 10–12 V to ensure full enhancement. (iii) Gate drivers also provide level shifting and isolation in H-bridge circuits (high-side and low-side drive). **Part (b) — 7 marks:** - **(i) r_e (2 marks):** $$r_e = \frac{V_T}{I_E} = \frac{26 \text{ mV}}{1 \text{ mA}} = 26 \text{ \Omega}$$ - **(ii) Voltage gain (3 marks):** $$A_v = -\frac{R_C}{r_e} = -\frac{4700}{26} \approx -180.8$$ Magnitude ≈ **181** (inverting). - **(iii) Output voltage (2 marks):** $$V_{out(\text{peak})} = \vert A_v \vert \times V_{in(\text{peak})}$$ $$V_{out(\text{peak})} = 180.8 \times 10 \text{ mV} = 1.808 \text{ V}$$ - **ADC suitability (2 marks):** An ADC with 5 V full-scale range can comfortably digitize a 1.8 V peak signal (uses ~36% of full scale, which is reasonable). However, the gain is *too high* if the input can surge beyond 27 mV (saturates the output). A gain of ~50–100 would be safer. Add an emitter-bypass capacitor to maintain high AC gain while improving DC bias stability. ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in Transistor-Application Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting V_CE(sat) and V_BE** in KVL equations — always subtract 0.2 V from V_CC and 0.7 V from V_IN. A common error is to write R_B = V_IN / I_B. > 2. **Using β (typical) instead of β_min** — KTU valuation strictly expects worst-case design. Always use the **minimum** β given in the problem. > 3. **Omitting the overdrive factor** — design I_B = 2–5 × I_B(min). Examiners deduct 1 mark for saturation margin. > 4. **Wrong sign in amplifier gain** — the negative sign in A_v = −R_C/r_e is significant; the inversion must be explicitly mentioned. > 5. **Ignoring the flyback diode** across inductive loads — relay and motor circuits *must* show a reverse-biased freewheeling diode; 1 mark lost if missing. > 6. **Confusing MOSFET as current-controlled** — it is voltage-controlled; gate current ≈ 0 in steady state. ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - A transistor in a mechatronic system is used as a **switch, amplifier, level shifter, or driver**. - **Switching applications** operate the device in **cutoff (OFF)** and **saturation (ON)** — never in the active region. - **I_C(sat) = (V_CC − V_CE(sat)) / R_L** is the fundamental load-current formula. - **Base resistor:** R_B = (V_IN − V_BE) / I_B, where I_B is the *overdriven* base current (ODF = 2 to 5). - **Power dissipation in saturation:** P_T = V_CE(sat) · I_C(sat) — small, but junction temperature T_J = T_A + P_T·θ_JA must be checked. - **BJT vs. MOSFET:** BJT is *current-controlled*, MOSFET is *voltage-controlled*; MOSFETs dominate high-current, high-speed mechatronic drivers. - **Darlington pair** provides composite β = β_1·β_2, useful for very high-current actuator drives; cost is doubled V_BE drop and slower switching. - **Flyback diode** across inductive loads (relay, motor, solenoid) is **mandatory** to protect against back-EMF. - **Common-emitter amplifier** inverts the signal (180° phase shift) and provides voltage gain |A_v| = R_C / r_e. - **r_e = 26 mV / I_E(mA)** — memorize this KTU-friendly approximation. - **MOSFET in PWM drive** — gate driver ICs are used to deliver fast, high-current gate charge/discharge and proper logic-level translation. - Always **state assumptions**: V_BE = 0.7 V, V_CE(sat) = 0.2 V, V_T = 26 mV, β = β_min. - **Thermal derating** is a safety check, not optional — KTU 14-mark questions often include a 2–3 mark sub-part on T_J. - For a **level shifter**, use an open-collector NPN with a pull-up to the higher rail; output high level is set by pull-up supply. - **PWM average output voltage** for a motor: V_avg = D · V_CC (D = duty cycle, 0 to 1). - **Switching speed of BJT** is limited by base-charge storage; speeding up turn-off requires reverse-biasing the base via a speed-up capacitor across R_B.

topics.title: Sensors and Transducers: Performance Terminology of Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Sensors and Transducers: Performance Terminology of Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition of Performance Terminology

In the context of Mechatronic Systems (KTU 2024 Scheme – PEEET863), the **Performance Terminology of Sensors** refers to the standardized set of static and dynamic characteristics used to quantitatively describe *how faithfully* a sensor or transducer converts a physical input (the **Measurand**) into a usable electrical or optical output (the **Response**). These terms form the bridge between the **Physical Domain** and the **Information Domain** in any measurement chain.

A **sensor** is a device that detects a physical stimulus (light, heat, motion, force, etc.) and converts it into a measurable electrical signal, whereas a **transducer** is the broader class of devices that convert energy from one form to another. Every sensor used in a mechatronic loop (e.g., robotic arm, CNC machine, ABS braking system) must be evaluated against these performance metrics before deployment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The Module 1 syllabus explicitly demands that students differentiate between *static* (time-invariant) and *dynamic* (time-variant) performance characteristics, and be able to compute the numerical values of sensitivity, error, and linearity from a given calibration graph.

### 1.2 The Two Universal Classes of Performance Characteristics

Performance terminology is universally grouped into two classes:

| Class | Domain | Typical Input |
|---|---|---|
| **Static Characteristics** | Time-independent (DC regime) | Slowly varying / constant input |
| **Dynamic Characteristics** | Time-dependent (AC regime) | Suddenly applied / sinusoidal input |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy: The Bathroom Weighing Scale

Imagine you step onto a digital **weighing scale** in your bathroom. Performance terminology gives you a vocabulary to describe *exactly* how good that scale is:

- **Accuracy** → Does the scale read **70.0 kg** when your reference weight is **70.0 kg**? *(closeness to the true value)*
- **Precision** → If you step on and off the scale 5 times at the same weight, do you get **70.0, 70.0, 70.0, 70.0, 70.0**? *(closeness of repeated readings to each other)*
- **Resolution** → Can the scale show a change of just **0.1 kg**, or does it jump from **69.9 kg** straight to **70.0 kg**? *(smallest detectable change)*
- **Sensitivity** → How many **volts** does the internal sensor output change per **kg** of weight added? *(output per unit input)*
- **Hysteresis** → Does the scale show **70.1 kg** when you step on from above, but **70.2 kg** when you slowly add weight up to the same mass? *(path-dependence of output)*
- **Drift** → After 6 months, does the same 70 kg weight read **70.5 kg** because the spring has weakened? *(slow change with time)*
- **Rise Time** → How long does the display take to jump from **0 kg** to the final stable **70 kg** reading after you step on? *(dynamic response)*

> [!NOTE]
> **Mnemonic for Static Characteristics — "A P R S R L H R D T S":**
> **A**ccuracy, **P**recision, **R**esolution, **S**ensitivity, **R**ange/**S**pan, **L**inearity, **H**ysteresis, **R**epeatability, **D**ead Band, **T**hreshold, **D**rift, **S**tability.

### 1.4 Block Diagram Context in a Mechatronic Loop

A sensor is never used in isolation. In a mechatronic system, it sits inside the **Measurement Chain** (also called the *Input Signal Conditioning* block of the overall Block Level Architecture):

$$
\text{Measurand} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{Sensor}} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{Signal Conditioner}} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{ADC}} \longrightarrow \boxed{\text{Controller}}
$$

The performance terminology applies **specifically to the Sensor block** — it characterizes how cleanly the Measurand is converted to the raw electrical signal that is then amplified, filtered, and digitized.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Static Characteristics — Time-Independent Behavior

When the input is constant or changes *very* slowly (so that inertial, thermal, and capacitive transients have settled), the sensor behaves according to its **static characteristics**.

#### 2.1.1 Accuracy
Accuracy is the degree of *closeness of a measurement to the true value* of the measurand. It is fundamentally a **qualitative judgment** and is best expressed numerically as an *error* or *inaccuracy* term.

$$
\text{Accuracy} = \frac{\text{True Value} - \text{Measured Value}}{\text{True Value}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> High accuracy implies *low* error. Accuracy is **not** a property of a single reading; it requires comparison with a **calibration standard** traceable to a national/international reference (e.g., NABL, NIST).

#### 2.1.2 Precision
Precision is the degree to which repeated measurements of the *same* quantity under *unchanged conditions* yield the *same result*. It refers to **reproducibility of the instrument itself**, independent of the true value.

- A precise instrument can still be **inaccurate** (consistently off by the same amount → **bias error**).
- An instrument can be accurate *on average* but imprecise (random scatter around the true value).

#### 2.1.3 Resolution
Resolution is the **smallest change in the measurand that produces a detectable change in the output**. It is governed by the *least count* of the readout, the *quantization step* in digital instruments, or the *dead-zone* in continuous instruments.

$$
\text{Resolution} = \frac{\text{Full-Scale Output (FSO)}}{2^{n}}
$$

where $n$ is the number of bits of the internal ADC.

#### 2.1.4 Sensitivity
Sensitivity is the **ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input** that causes it. For a linear sensor, it is a **constant** equal to the slope of the calibration curve.

$$
S = \frac{\Delta \text{Output}}{\Delta \text{Input}} = \frac{dy}{dx} \quad \text{(units: output units / input unit)}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A *high* sensitivity is not always *desirable*. Excessive sensitivity amplifies noise, reduces the usable **range**, and may push the sensor into a non-linear region. Sensor design always seeks an *optimum* sensitivity for the target application.

#### 2.1.5 Range and Span
- **Range** — The minimum and maximum values of the measurand for which the sensor is designed to operate. (e.g., 0 to 200 °C).
- **Span (or Dynamic Range)** — The algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum of the range. (e.g., span = 200 °C − 0 °C = 200 °C).

$$
\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}
$$

#### 2.1.6 Linearity
A sensor is *linear* if its output is a **straight-line function of the input**. Since no real sensor is perfectly linear, *linearity* is quantified as the **maximum deviation of the actual calibration curve from the best-fit straight line**, expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale Output (FSO).

$$
\text{Linearity} (\% \text{ FSO}) = \frac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The best-fit line is most commonly chosen using the **Least-Squares Method** (Independent Linearity) or alternatively the *end-point* line connecting the first and last calibration points (Terminal Linearity).

#### 2.1.7 Hysteresis
Hysteresis is the **maximum difference in output for the same input**, depending on whether the input is *increasing* (upscale) or *decreasing* (downscale). It arises from mechanical friction, magnetic remanence, elastic after-effects, or thermal lag.

$$
\varepsilon_H = \frac{\vert Y_{up}(x) - Y_{down}(x) \vert_{\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100\%
$$

#### 2.1.8 Repeatability
Repeatability is the **ability of a sensor to produce the same output for the same input, when approached from the same direction, under identical environmental conditions, over a short period of time**. It is the *random spread* in the calibration data of consecutive full-range traverses.

$$
\text{Repeatability} (\% \text{ FSO}) = \frac{\Delta Y_{r,\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100\%
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often confuse **Hysteresis** with **Repeatability**. The key differentiator: *Hysteresis compares upscale vs. downscale readings*; *Repeatability compares multiple upscale runs (or multiple downscale runs) to each other*.

#### 2.1.9 Dead Band and Threshold
- **Threshold** — The **smallest input change** that the sensor can detect, but below which it produces **zero** output. (Sub-resolution region.)
- **Dead Band** — The **range of input values** for which the output remains *unchanged* (typically zero). Dead band is a *range*; threshold is a *point*.

#### 2.1.10 Drift and Stability
- **Drift** — The *slow, time-dependent* change in the output of a sensor when the input is held constant. Common causes: aging of components, thermal effects, mechanical creep, contamination.
- **Stability** — The *inverse* of drift; the ability of a sensor to retain its calibration over a long period.

$$
\text{Drift} = \frac{Y(t_2) - Y(t_1)}{Y_{FSO} \cdot (t_2 - t_1)} \quad \text{(units: \% FSO / hour, day, month, year)}
$$

### 2.2 Dynamic Characteristics — Time-Dependent Behavior

When the input changes *rapidly*, the sensor's **inertia, mass, damping, and energy-storage elements** (capacitance, inductance, thermal mass) prevent the output from tracking the input perfectly. The dynamic characteristics describe this lag.

#### 2.2.1 Zero-Order Sensor
$$
y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$
A purely *proportional*, instantaneous device (e.g., a potentiometer, ideal op-amp). No dynamics — bandwidth is **infinite** in principle.

#### 2.2.2 First-Order Sensor
$$
K \cdot \tau \frac{dy(t)}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$
- $\tau$ = **Time Constant** = 63.2 % of final value is reached after $t = \tau$.
- Steady-state error to a step input is **zero** (in a first-order system, no overshoot).
- Bandwidth $f_{-3dB} = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$.

#### 2.2.3 Second-Order Sensor
$$
\frac{1}{\omega_n^2} \frac{d^2y}{dt^2} + \frac{2\zeta}{\omega_n}\frac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = K \cdot x(t)
$$
- $\omega_n$ = **Natural (undamped) frequency** — sets the upper usable frequency.
- $\zeta$ = **Damping Ratio**:
  - $\zeta = 0$: undamped, continuous oscillation.
  - $0 < \zeta < 1$: underdamped (overshoots, oscillates).
  - $\zeta = 1$: critically damped (fastest non-oscillatory response).
  - $\zeta > 1$: overdamped (sluggish).

#### 2.2.4 Step-Response Parameters
- **Rise Time ($t_r$)** — Time to go from 10 % to 90 % of final value (or 0 % to 100 %, depending on convention). For a second-order system: $t_r \approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$.
- **Settling Time ($t_s$)** — Time to reach and stay within $\pm 2\%$ (or $\pm 5\%$) of the final value. $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ (for 2 % criterion).
- **Overshoot ($M_p$)** — Maximum excursion above the final value, expressed as % of final value. $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- **Time Constant ($\tau$)** — Time to reach 63.2 % of final step value (first-order systems).

#### 2.2.5 Frequency Response Parameters
- **Bandwidth ($f_b$)** — The frequency range over which the sensor's amplitude response is within **−3 dB** of the DC value. For a first-order sensor: $f_b = \dfrac{1}{2\pi\tau}$.
- **Resonant Frequency ($f_r$)** — Frequency of maximum amplitude response in underdamped second-order systems: $f_r = f_n \sqrt{1 - 2\zeta^2}$.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Term | Definition (Concise) | Key Equation | Typical Unit |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Accuracy | Closeness to true value | $\varepsilon = \frac{X_{true}-X_{meas}}{X_{true}} \times 100$ | % of true value |
| 2 | Precision | Reproducibility of readings | Qualitative / Std. Dev. | Same as input |
| 3 | Resolution | Smallest detectable input change | $R = \frac{FSO}{2^n}$ | Same as input |
| 4 | Sensitivity | Output per unit input | $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ | Output / Input |
| 5 | Span | $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | $\text{Span} = X_{\max} - X_{\min}$ | Same as input |
| 6 | Linearity | Max deviation from best-fit line | $\%\text{FSO} = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100$ | % FSO |
| 7 | Hysteresis | Max up-vs-down output difference | $\%\text{FSO} = \dfrac{\vert Y_{up}-Y_{down} \vert_{\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100$ | % FSO |
| 8 | Repeatability | Max spread of repeated runs | $\%\text{FSO} = \dfrac{\Delta Y_{r,\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100$ | % FSO |
| 9 | Dead Band | Input range with no output | Qualitative | Same as input |
| 10 | Drift | Slow output change with time | $\%\text{FSO/hr}$ | % FSO / time |
| 11 | Time Constant | Time to 63.2 % of final value | $\tau$ | seconds |
| 12 | Rise Time | 10 % to 90 % of final | $t_r \approx 1.8/\omega_n$ | seconds |
| 13 | Settling Time | Time to stay within ±2 % of final | $t_s \approx 4/(\zeta\omega_n)$ | seconds |
| 14 | Overshoot | Max excursion above final | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$ | % |
| 15 | Bandwidth | −3 dB frequency range | $f_b = 1/(2\pi\tau)$ | Hz |
| 16 | Damping Ratio | Degree of oscillation | $\zeta$ | dimensionless |
| 17 | Natural Freq. | Undamped oscillation frequency | $\omega_n$ | rad/s |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Robotics** — Joint position sensors (encoders, resolvers) require high *resolution* and low *hysteresis* for accurate trajectory tracking.
- **Automotive ABS** — Wheel-speed sensors need wide *bandwidth* and fast *rise time* to detect wheel lock-up in milliseconds.
- **Aerospace (Inertial Navigation)** — MEMS accelerometers and gyroscopes demand ultra-low *drift* and *bias stability* over thousands of hours.
- **Process Control** — Industrial pressure/temperature transmitters prioritize *accuracy*, *linearity*, and *long-term stability* since they are rarely recalibrated.
- **Medical Instrumentation** — ECG, EEG, and pulse-oximeter sensors require *low noise* (high *signal-to-noise ratio*) and *flat frequency response* over the relevant physiological band.
