## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Sensitivity from a Calibration Curve

**Given:** A linear displacement sensor produces the following calibration data, where $x$ is the input displacement (mm) and $y$ is the output voltage (V):

| $x$ (mm) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (V) | 0.10 | 0.62 | 1.14 | 1.66 | 2.18 | 2.70 |

**Find:** Sensitivity $S$ and the zero-offset (intercept) $b$.

**Step 1 — Identify the model.**
Assume a linear model $y = Sx + b$ where $S$ is sensitivity and $b$ is the zero-load output (offset).

**Step 2 — Compute the slope using the endpoint method.**
The endpoint method uses only the first and last calibration points:

$$
S_{\text{end-point}} = \frac{y_5 - y_0}{x_5 - x_0}
$$

$$
S_{\text{end-point}} = \frac{2.70 \text{ V} - 0.10 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mm} - 0 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
S_{\text{end-point}} = \frac{2.60 \text{ V}}{5 \text{ mm}}
$$

$$
\boxed{S_{\text{end-point}} = 0.52 \text{ V/mm}}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the intercept.**

$$
b = y_0 - S \cdot x_0 = 0.10 - 0.52 \times 0 = 0.10 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify by checking the mid-point.**
Predicted output at $x = 2.5$ mm:

$$
y_{\text{pred}} = 0.52 \times 2.5 + 0.10 = 1.30 + 0.10 = 1.40 \text{ V}
$$

The actual interpolated value from data: $\dfrac{1.14 + 1.66}{2} = 1.40$ V. **Match — perfect linearity.**

### 3.2 Derivation: Linearity (% FSO) — Independent Linearity

**Given:** The same calibration data, but the *actual* sensor output (with non-linearity) at the mid-points is:

| $x$ (mm) | 0 | 1 | 2 | 3 | 4 | 5 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| $y$ (V) | 0.10 | 0.62 | 1.18 | 1.66 | 2.16 | 2.70 |
| Best-fit $y$ (V) | 0.10 | 0.62 | 1.14 | 1.66 | 2.18 | 2.70 |

**Find:** Independent linearity as % of FSO.

**Step 1 — Compute FSO.**

$$
Y_{FSO} = y_{\max} - y_{\min} = 2.70 - 0.10 = 2.60 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 2 — Compute deviation at every calibration point.**

$$
\Delta y_i = \vert y_{\text{actual},i} - y_{\text{fit},i} \vert
$$

| $x$ (mm) | $y_{actual}$ | $y_{fit}$ | $\Delta y$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.10 | 0.10 | 0.00 |
| 1 | 0.62 | 0.62 | 0.00 |
| 2 | 1.18 | 1.14 | 0.04 |
| 3 | 1.66 | 1.66 | 0.00 |
| 4 | 2.16 | 2.18 | 0.02 |
| 5 | 2.70 | 2.70 | 0.00 |

**Step 3 — Identify the maximum deviation.**

$$
\Delta y_{\max} = 0.04 \text{ V} \quad (\text{at } x = 2 \text{ mm})
$$

**Step 4 — Express as % of FSO.**

$$
\text{Linearity} = \frac{\Delta y_{\max}}{Y_{FSO}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\text{Linearity} = \frac{0.04}{2.60} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\text{Linearity} = 1.538\% \text{ FSO}}
$$

### 3.3 Derivation: Hysteresis Error

**Given:** A pressure sensor is calibrated in both upscale and downscale directions:

| True Pressure (kPa) | 0 | 50 | 100 | 150 | 200 | 250 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Output Upscale (V) | 0.00 | 0.98 | 2.00 | 3.05 | 4.02 | 5.00 |
| Output Downscale (V) | 0.00 | 1.02 | 2.06 | 3.10 | 4.05 | 5.00 |

**Find:** Hysteresis as % of FSO.

**Step 1 — Compute the hysteresis at each shared input value.**

$$
\varepsilon_{H,i} = \vert Y_{up,i} - Y_{down,i} \vert
$$

| Pressure (kPa) | $Y_{up}$ (V) | $Y_{down}$ (V) | $\varepsilon_H$ (V) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0.00 | 0.00 | 0.00 |
| 50 | 0.98 | 1.02 | 0.04 |
| 100 | 2.00 | 2.06 | 0.06 |
| 150 | 3.05 | 3.10 | 0.05 |
| 200 | 4.02 | 4.05 | 0.03 |
| 250 | 5.00 | 5.00 | 0.00 |

**Step 2 — Identify the maximum.**

$$
\varepsilon_{H,\max} = 0.06 \text{ V} \quad (\text{at } P = 100 \text{ kPa})
$$

**Step 3 — Compute FSO.**

$$
Y_{FSO} = 5.00 - 0.00 = 5.00 \text{ V}
$$

**Step 4 — Express as % of FSO.**

$$
\varepsilon_H = \frac{0.06}{5.00} \times 100\%
$$

$$
\boxed{\varepsilon_H = 1.20\% \text{ FSO}}
$$

### 3.4 Derivation: First-Order Sensor Step Response

**Given:** A temperature sensor with time constant $\tau = 4$ s is suddenly immersed from room temperature ($T_0 = 25\,°C$) into a bath at $T_\infty = 85\,°C$.

**Find:**
(a) The sensor reading at $t = 4$ s, $t = 8$ s, and $t = 12$ s.
(b) The time required to reach 90 % of the final value.
(c) The time required to reach 99 % of the final value.

**Step 1 — Write the standard first-order step response.**

$$
y(t) = y_{\infty} - (y_{\infty} - y_0) \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) \, e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
T(t) = 85 - (85 - 25) \, e^{-t/4} = 85 - 60 \, e^{-t/4} \quad [°C]
$$

**Step 2 — Evaluate at the requested instants.**

At $t = 4$ s (one time constant):

$$
T(4) = 85 - 60 \cdot e^{-1} = 85 - 60 \cdot 0.3679 = 85 - 22.07 = 62.93\,°C
$$

At $t = 8$ s (two time constants):

$$
T(8) = 85 - 60 \cdot e^{-2} = 85 - 60 \cdot 0.1353 = 85 - 8.12 = 76.88\,°C
$$

At $t = 12$ s (three time constants):

$$
T(12) = 85 - 60 \cdot e^{-3} = 85 - 60 \cdot 0.0498 = 85 - 2.99 = 82.01\,°C
$$

**Step 3 — Solve (b): Time to reach 90 % of final value.**

Target: $T(t) = 0.9 \times 85 + 0.1 \times 25 = 76.5 + 2.5 = 79.0\,°C$ (using the **steady-state** baseline of 85 °C and the *rise* from $T_0$).

Using the simpler definition (90 % of the **step change**): $T(t) - T_0 = 0.9 \cdot (T_\infty - T_0) = 54\,°C$, so $T(t) = 25 + 54 = 79\,°C$.

$$
79 = 85 - 60 \cdot e^{-t/4}
$$

$$
60 \cdot e^{-t/4} = 6
$$

$$
e^{-t/4} = 0.1
$$

$$
-\frac{t}{4} = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026
$$

$$
t = 4 \times 2.3026
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{90\%} = 9.21 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 4 — Solve (c): Time to reach 99 % of final value.**

$$
60 \cdot e^{-t/4} = 0.01 \times 60 = 0.6
$$

$$
e^{-t/4} = 0.01
$$

$$
t = 4 \times \ln(100) = 4 \times 4.6052
$$

$$
\boxed{t_{99\%} = 18.42 \text{ s}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Rule of Thumb for First-Order Systems:** After $1\tau$ → 63.2 %; $2\tau$ → 86.5 %; $3\tau$ → 95.0 %; $4\tau$ → 98.2 %; $5\tau$ → 99.3 %. Engineers use $5\tau$ as the practical "settling time" for a first-order system.

### 3.5 Derivation: Second-Order Damping Ratio from Overshoot

**Given:** A second-order position sensor is given a step input. It overshoots the final value by $M_p = 18.5\%$ and the steady-state value is reached after damped oscillations.

**Find:** Damping ratio $\zeta$.

**Step 1 — Express overshoot in the standard form.**

$$
M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}} \times 100\%
$$

$$
0.185 = e^{-\pi\zeta / \sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

**Step 2 — Take natural log of both sides.**

$$
\ln(0.185) = -\frac{\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

$$
-1.6864 = -\frac{\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

$$
1.6864 = \frac{\pi\zeta}{\sqrt{1 - \zeta^2}}
$$

**Step 3 — Square both sides.**

$$
2.8440 = \frac{\pi^2 \zeta^2}{1 - \zeta^2}
$$

$$
2.8440 \cdot (1 - \zeta^2) = \pi^2 \zeta^2
$$

$$
2.8440 - 2.8440 \, \zeta^2 = 9.8696 \, \zeta^2
$$

**Step 4 — Collect terms and solve for $\zeta^2$.**

$$
2.8440 = 9.8696 \, \zeta^2 + 2.8440 \, \zeta^2
$$

$$
2.8440 = 12.7136 \, \zeta^2
$$

$$
\zeta^2 = \frac{2.8440}{12.7136} = 0.2237
$$

$$
\boxed{\zeta = 0.473}
$$

This is an **underdamped** sensor ($0 < \zeta < 1$), as expected from the presence of overshoot.

### 3.6 KTU Analytical Matrix — Quick Decision Map

| If the Problem States… | The Correct Performance Term to Use Is… |
|---|---|
| "...the reading differs from the standard reference by 0.3 %…" | **Accuracy / Inaccuracy (Static Error)** |
| "...five readings are 70.1, 70.2, 70.0, 70.1, 70.1 V for the same input…" | **Precision / Repeatability** |
| "...output moves in steps of 0.05 V with input change of 0.1 mm…" | **Resolution (or Quantization step)** |
| "...output changes 2.3 mV per °C…" | **Sensitivity** |
| "...output for 50 kPa upscale is 1.0 V but for the same 50 kPa downscale is 1.06 V…" | **Hysteresis** |
| "...output slowly increases from 5.000 V to 5.012 V over 24 hours at constant input…" | **Drift** |
| "...output reaches 63.2 % of the final step in 2.1 s…" | **Time Constant ($\tau$)** |
| "...output first crosses the final value at 0.45 s, then peaks 22 % above…" | **Rise Time** and **Overshoot** |
| "...output stays within ±2 % of final after 1.2 s…" | **Settling Time** |
| "...amplitude response drops to 0.707 of DC at 1.2 kHz…" | **Bandwidth (−3 dB)** |

## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block-Level Architecture: Role of Performance Terminology in a Mechatronic System

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    M[Measurand Physical Input] --> S[Sensor Block]
    S --> SC[Signal Conditioner Amplify Filter Linearize]
    SC --> ADC[ADC Analog to Digital Converter]
    ADC --> C[Microcontroller or DSP]
    C --> A[Actuator]
    A --> P[Plant or Process]
    P -. feedback loop .-> M

    classDef sensorBlock fill:#FFD580,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef controlBlock fill:#A0D8FF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef plantBlock fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class S sensorBlock
    class SC,ADC,C controlBlock
    class A,P plantBlock
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The *Measurand* is the physical quantity being measured (force, temperature, position). The Sensor Block is the *only* location where the **physical domain** meets the **electrical domain** — and it is *this* block that the performance terminology characterizes.

### 4.2 Mermaid Schematic: Taxonomy of Sensor Performance Characteristics

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Performance Terminology of Sensors]
    P --> S[Static Characteristics Time Invariant DC Regime]
    P --> D[Dynamic Characteristics Time Dependent AC Regime]

    S --> S1[Accuracy]
    S --> S2[Precision]
    S --> S3[Resolution]
    S --> S4[Sensitivity]
    S --> S5[Range and Span]
    S --> S6[Linearity]
    S --> S7[Hysteresis]
    S --> S8[Repeatability]
    S --> S9[Dead Band]
    S --> S10[Threshold]
    S --> S11[Drift and Stability]

    D --> D1[Zero Order]
    D --> D2[First Order Time Constant Tau]
    D --> D3[Second Order Natural Frequency Wn Damping Ratio Zeta]
    D --> D4[Step Response Rise Time Settling Time Overshoot]
    D --> D5[Frequency Response Bandwidth Resonant Frequency]

    classDef rootStyle fill:#FFE082,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef staticStyle fill:#B3E5FC,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef dynStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef leafStyle fill:#FFFFFF,stroke:#333,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class P rootStyle
    class S staticStyle
    class D dynStyle
    class S1,S2,S3,S4,S5,S6,S7,S8,S9,S10,S11 leafStyle
    class D1,D2,D3,D4,D5 leafStyle
```

### 4.3 Calibration-Curve Visual (Mermaid Schematic Representation)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Ideal Linear Sensor] --> A1["Straight Line y = Sx + b Slope S = Sensitivity Intercept b = Zero Offset"]
    B[Real Sensor With Non Linearity] --> B1["S Curve or Slight Bow Slight Deviation From Best Fit Line"]
    C[Real Sensor With Hysteresis] --> C1["Two Separate Curves Upscale Path Above Downscale Path Below"]
    D[Real Sensor With Drift] --> D1["Curve Slowly Shifts Upward or Downward Over Time Same Input Different Output"]
    E[Real Sensor With Dead Band] --> E1["Flat Region Near Zero Small Input Produces No Output"]

    classDef idealStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#2E7D32,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef realStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000

    class A,A1 idealStyle
    class B,B1,C,C1,D,D1,E,E1 realStyle
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: Error-Budget Flow Inside a Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    I[True Input X true] --> G1[Gain Drift Error]
    G1 --> G2[Non Linearity Error]
    G2 --> G3[Hysteresis Error]
    G3 --> G4[Repeatability Random Error]
    G4 --> G5[Quantization or Resolution Error]
    G5 --> G6[Output Y meas]
    G6 --> O[Total Measurement Error epsilon total]

    G1 -. contributes .-> O
    G2 -. contributes .-> O
    G3 -. contributes .-> O
    G4 -. contributes .-> O
    G5 -. contributes .-> O

    classDef inputStyle fill:#E1F5FE,stroke:#01579B,stroke-width:2px,color:#000
    classDef errStyle fill:#FFCDD2,stroke:#B71C1C,stroke-width:1px,color:#000
    classDef outStyle fill:#C8E6C9,stroke:#1B5E20,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class I inputStyle
    class G1,G2,G3,G4,G5 errStyle
    class G6,O outStyle
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** Total measurement error is the **root-sum-square combination** of the individual error contributions. Each static characteristic contributes an *independent* source of uncertainty, and the *systematic* errors (hysteresis, linearity) are usually added algebraically while *random* errors (repeatability, noise) are added in quadrature (RSS).

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 (Adapted)]**

*Define the following terms with one example each: (i) Resolution, (ii) Sensitivity, (iii) Hysteresis of a sensor.*

**Model Answer:**

**(i) Resolution** is the smallest change in the measurand (input) that produces a detectable change in the output of the sensor. Mathematically, for a digital sensor with an $n$-bit ADC, the resolution is given by $R = \dfrac{Y_{FSO}}{2^n}$. **Example:** A 12-bit digital thermometer with a range of 0–100 °C has a resolution of $\dfrac{100\,°C}{2^{12}} = \dfrac{100}{4096} \approx 0.0244\,°C$.

**(ii) Sensitivity** is the ratio of the change in output to the corresponding change in input. For a linear sensor, $S = \dfrac{\Delta y}{\Delta x}$ (units: output / input). **Example:** A thermocouple producing 40 µV per °C change has a sensitivity of 40 µV/°C.

**(iii) Hysteresis** is the maximum difference in sensor output for the *same* input value, when approached from the *upscale* (increasing) and *downscale* (decreasing) directions. It is caused by friction, magnetic remanence, or elastic after-effects. **Example:** A Bourdon-tube pressure gauge that reads 99.8 kPa on increasing pressure and 100.4 kPa on decreasing pressure has a hysteresis of 0.6 kPa at that input.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 (Adapted)]**

*Distinguish between **Static** and **Dynamic** characteristics of a sensor. List any four static characteristics and any four dynamic characteristics.*

**Model Answer:**

| Aspect | Static Characteristics | Dynamic Characteristics |
|---|---|---|
| **Input type** | Constant or very slowly varying | Rapidly changing (step, ramp, sinusoidal) |
| **Domain** | Time-independent (DC regime) | Time-dependent (AC regime) |
| **Concerned with** | Accuracy, linearity, repeatability | Speed of response, fidelity of tracking |
| **Tested by** | Stepwise calibration in equilibrium | Step response, frequency response |

**Four Static Characteristics:** (1) Accuracy, (2) Sensitivity, (3) Linearity, (4) Hysteresis.

**Four Dynamic Characteristics:** (1) Time constant, (2) Rise time, (3) Settling time, (4) Damping ratio.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** *A thermistor is calibrated in a temperature bath. The following readings are taken:*

| True Temp (°C) | 20 | 30 | 40 | 50 | 60 | 70 | 80 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Sensor Output (mV) | 102 | 147 | 191 | 235 | 279 | 322 | 368 |

*Find:*
*(i) The sensitivity of the thermistor in mV/°C (use the end-point method). (3 marks)*
*(ii) The zero-load offset. (1 mark)*
*(iii) The maximum non-linearity in % FSO using the best-fit straight line. (3 marks)*

**(b)** *Explain with neat sketches the dynamic response of a **first-order** and a **second-order** sensor to a step input. Define: time constant, rise time, settling time, overshoot, and damping ratio. (7 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION for Question A

**Part (a) — (i) Sensitivity (end-point method):**

$$
S = \frac{y_{80} - y_{20}}{x_{80} - x_{20}} = \frac{368 - 102}{80 - 20} = \frac{266}{60}
$$

$$
\boxed{S = 4.433 \text{ mV/°C}}
$$

**Part (a) — (ii) Zero-load offset:**

Using $y = Sx + b$ at $x = 20, y = 102$:

$$
b = y - Sx = 102 - 4.433 \times 20 = 102 - 88.66 = 13.34 \text{ mV}
$$

$$
\boxed{b \approx 13.34 \text{ mV}}
$$

**Part (a) — (iii) Maximum non-linearity (% FSO):**

Full-Scale Output:

$$
Y_{FSO} = y_{80} - y_{20} = 368 - 102 = 266 \text{ mV}
$$

Best-fit line equation (same as endpoint line in this case): $y_{\text{fit}} = 4.433 \, x + 13.34$.

Compute the predicted values and deviations:

| $x$ (°C) | $y_{meas}$ (mV) | $y_{fit}$ (mV) | $\vert \Delta y \vert$ (mV) |
|---|---|---|---|
| 20 | 102 | 102.00 | 0.00 |
| 30 | 147 | 147.67 | 0.67 |
| 40 | 191 | 193.34 | 2.34 |
| 50 | 235 | 239.01 | 4.01 |
| 60 | 279 | 284.68 | 5.68 |
| 70 | 322 | 330.35 | 8.35 |
| 80 | 368 | 368.00 (approx) | small |

$\Delta y_{\max} \approx 8.35$ mV (occurring near $x = 70$ °C).

$$
\text{Linearity} = \frac{8.35}{266} \times 100\% = 3.14\% \text{ FSO}
$$

**Part (b) — Dynamic Response Discussion:**

**First-Order Sensor:** Governed by $K\tau \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = Kx(t)$. Step response:

$$
y(t) = y_{\infty} - (y_{\infty} - y_0) e^{-t/\tau}
$$

- **Time constant ($\tau$):** Time for output to reach 63.2 % of the final value.
- No overshoot, no oscillations.
- Settling time ≈ $4\tau$ to $5\tau$.

**Second-Order Sensor:** Governed by $\dfrac{1}{\omega_n^2} \dfrac{d^2y}{dt^2} + \dfrac{2\zeta}{\omega_n} \dfrac{dy}{dt} + y(t) = Kx(t)$. Step response characteristics:

- **Damping ratio ($\zeta$):** Determines the *shape*. $\zeta < 1$ → underdamped (overshoots), $\zeta = 1$ → critically damped, $\zeta > 1$ → overdamped.
- **Natural frequency ($\omega_n$):** Frequency at which the undamped system would oscillate.
- **Rise time ($t_r$):** $\approx \dfrac{1.8}{\omega_n}$ (10 % to 90 %).
- **Settling time ($t_s$):** $\approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta\omega_n}$ (for 2 % band).
- **Overshoot ($M_p$):** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$.

(Sketches: a smooth exponential rise for first-order; an underdamped oscillating rise for second-order that converges to the final value.)

---

#### Question B (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2023 (Adapted)]**

**(a)** *Define the following with proper units / typical values:*
*(i) Span, (ii) Threshold, (iii) Dead band, (iv) Drift, (v) Repeatability, (vi) Stability, (vii) Resolution. (7 marks — 1 mark each)*

**(b)** *A platinum RTD is used to measure the temperature of a chemical bath. The RTD has a nominal resistance of 100 Ω at 0 °C and a time constant of 6 s. The bath is suddenly switched from 25 °C to 90 °C.*
*Find:*
*(i) The RTD resistance after 6 s, 12 s, and 24 s. (3 marks)*
*(ii) The time required for the RTD to read within ±1 °C of the final bath temperature. (4 marks)*

---

#### MODEL SOLUTION for Question B

**Part (a) — Definitions:**

- **(i) Span** — Algebraic difference between the maximum and minimum values of the input range. *Unit: same as input.* E.g., 0–200 kPa pressure sensor has a span of 200 kPa.
- **(ii) Threshold** — Smallest input magnitude that produces a detectable output. Below this, the output is effectively zero. *Unit: same as input.*
- **(iii) Dead band** — The *range* of input values for which the output does *not* change at all. *Unit: same as input.* Different from threshold (a point) — dead band is a *range*.
- **(iv) Drift** — Slow, time-dependent change in sensor output at constant input. Typical units: **% FSO per hour, day, month, or year**. Caused by aging, thermal effects, contamination.
- **(v) Repeatability** — Ability to produce the *same* output for the *same* input, approached from the *same* direction, under identical conditions, over a *short* period. Typical unit: **% FSO**.
- **(vi) Stability** — Ability of the sensor to retain its performance characteristics over a *long* period of time. *Inverse* of drift. Quantified as drift rate or as time before re-calibration is required.
- **(vii) Resolution** — Smallest change in input that the sensor can detect. *Unit: same as input.* For a digital sensor: $R = \text{FSO} / 2^n$.

**Part (b) — (i) RTD resistance at $t = 6$ s, $12$ s, $24$ s:**

For a first-order thermal sensor, the *temperature* response is:

$$
T(t) = T_\infty - (T_\infty - T_0) e^{-t/\tau}
$$

$$
T(t) = 90 - (90 - 25) e^{-t/6} = 90 - 65 e^{-t/6} \quad [°C]
$$

At $t = 6$ s ($\tau$):

$$
T(6) = 90 - 65 \cdot e^{-1} = 90 - 65 \times 0.3679 = 90 - 23.91 = 66.09\,°C
$$

At $t = 12$ s ($2\tau$):

$$
T(12) = 90 - 65 \cdot e^{-2} = 90 - 65 \times 0.1353 = 90 - 8.79 = 81.21\,°C
$$

At $t = 24$ s ($4\tau$):

$$
T(24) = 90 - 65 \cdot e^{-4} = 90 - 65 \times 0.0183 = 90 - 1.19 = 88.81\,°C
$$

The RTD has a temperature coefficient of resistance for platinum of $\approx 0.385 \,\Omega/°C$ (IEC 60751 standard). Hence the resistance is:

$$
R(t) = R_0 \, [1 + \alpha T(t)] = 100 \, [1 + 0.00385 \cdot T(t)]
$$

| $t$ (s) | $T(t)$ (°C) | $R(t)$ (Ω) |
|---|---|---|
| 6 | 66.09 | $100 \times 1.2545 = 125.45$ |
| 12 | 81.21 | $100 \times 1.3127 = 131.27$ |
| 24 | 88.81 | $100 \times 1.3419 = 134.19$ |

**Part (b) — (ii) Time to reach within ±1 °C of final temperature:**

Target temperature band: $[89\,°C, 91\,°C]$. Within ±1 °C means $T(t) \geq 89\,°C$.

$$
89 = 90 - 65 \cdot e^{-t/6}
$$

$$
65 \cdot e^{-t/6} = 1
$$

$$
e^{-t/6} = \frac{1}{65} = 0.01538
$$

$$
-\frac{t}{6} = \ln(0.01538) = -4.176
$$

$$
t = 6 \times 4.176
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 25.06 \text{ s}}
$$

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls in PEEET863 (Mechatronic Systems) Module 1 Questions on Performance Terminology:**
>
> 1. **Confusing Precision with Accuracy:** A sensor can be precise but inaccurate. Examiners will deduct 1 mark if the student does not explicitly state this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting the FSO denominator:** All static errors (linearity, hysteresis, repeatability) must be expressed as **% of FSO** — using the wrong denominator (e.g., % of reading) is a **2-mark** deduction in 14-mark questions.
> 3. **Hysteresis vs. Repeatability:** Hysteresis compares *upscale vs. downscale paths*; repeatability compares *multiple upscale runs to each other*. Mixing these up is a 2-mark penalty.
> 4. **Time constant misinterpretation:** The time constant $\tau$ is the time to reach **63.2 %** of the *final value*, **not** the *time to reach 63.2 % of the change*. This is a frequent 1-mark deduction.
> 5. **Damping ratio sign:** For a **stable** second-order sensor, $\zeta > 0$. For an *underdamped* sensor, $0 < \zeta < 1$. Writing $\zeta = 0.473$ as $-0.473$ is a unit-mark loss.
> 6. **Units in the sensitivity answer:** Always write sensitivity with the correct *output / input* units (e.g., mV/°C, V/kPa). Numerical value alone loses 1 mark.

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Static characteristics** describe sensor behavior when the input is constant or changes very slowly; **dynamic characteristics** describe behavior under rapidly changing inputs.
- **Accuracy** = closeness to *true value*; **Precision** = closeness of *repeated readings* to each other.
- **Sensitivity** $S = \Delta y / \Delta x$ — slope of the calibration curve.
- **Resolution** is the smallest detectable input change (or the *least count* / *ADC quantization step*).
- **Span** = $X_{\max} - X_{\min}$; **Range** = [min, max] input interval.
- **Linearity** = maximum deviation of calibration curve from best-fit straight line, expressed as **% of FSO**.
- **Hysteresis** = max difference between upscale and downscale readings for the same input, **% of FSO**.
- **Repeatability** = max spread of repeated upscale (or downscale) runs, **% of FSO**; *short term*.
- **Drift** = slow, time-dependent output change at constant input, in **% FSO per unit time**; *long term*.
- **First-order sensor** → characterized by **time constant** $\tau$; no overshoot; bandwidth $f_b = 1/(2\pi\tau)$.
- **Second-order sensor** → characterized by **natural frequency** $\omega_n$ and **damping ratio** $\zeta$.
- For a step input: **Rise time** ($t_r$) = 10 % → 90 % of final; **Settling time** ($t_s$) = ±2 % band; **Overshoot** $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}}$.
- **Bandwidth** is the −3 dB frequency — i.e., the frequency at which the amplitude response falls to $1/\sqrt{2}$ (≈ 0.707) of the DC value.
- **KTU-ready engineering rule of thumb:** After $1\tau$ → 63.2 %; $2\tau$ → 86.5 %; $3\tau$ → 95 %; $4\tau$ → 98.2 %; $5\tau$ → 99.3 % of the final value.
- All static errors should be reported as **% of Full-Scale Output (FSO)** unless the question specifies otherwise.
- The **damping ratio** $\zeta$ determines the response *type*: underdamped ($0 < \zeta < 1$), critically damped ($\zeta = 1$), overdamped ($\zeta > 1$).
- **Dead band** is a *range*; **threshold** is a *point* — these are often confused in exams.
- When asked to *compute* sensitivity, use the **end-point method** (first and last calibration points) unless the *least-squares best-fit method* is explicitly demanded.
- **Total measurement error** is a root-sum-square combination of independent error sources; *systematic* errors add algebraically, *random* errors add in quadrature.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensors - I
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensors - I

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensors — The Foundation

In any **Mechatronic System**, the first and most critical step in the control loop is *sensing*. Before a controller can act, it must know **where** things are, **how far** they have moved, or **how close** they are to an object. This is the domain of **Displacement, Position, and Proximity Sensors**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A **displacement sensor** measures the change in distance between a reference point and the current position of a target, providing a continuous analog or digital output. A **position sensor** reports the absolute location of an object relative to a fixed reference frame, while a **proximity sensor** detects the presence or absence of an object within a defined non-contact sensing zone.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Blind Man's Stick"

Imagine a person walking in a dark room holding a stick (**sensor**).

- **Displacement Sensor** is like measuring *how far the stick has moved* from where it started (e.g., "the stick extended 30 cm").
- **Position Sensor** is like knowing the *absolute room coordinates* of the stick's tip (e.g., "the tip is at the 3 m mark on the wall").
- **Proximity Sensor** is like knowing *whether the stick is touching something or not* (e.g., "wall detected 2 cm ahead"), without needing physical contact.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction for KTU Exams:**
> - **Displacement** → Change in position (Δx), usually measured over a range.
> - **Position** → Absolute location (x) w.r.t. a reference.
> - **Proximity** → Boolean/digital detection (Yes/No) of an object within range.

### 1.3 Classification of Sensors (Module 1 Focus)

| Category | Operating Principle | Examples |
|---|---|---|
| **Resistive** | Resistance varies with wiper movement | Potentiometer (Linear/Rotary) |
| **Inductive** | Mutual/Self inductance variation | LVDT, RVDT, Eddy Current |
| **Capacitive** | Capacitance varies with gap/area | Variable-Dielectric Capacitor |
| **Magnetic** | Magnetic field change detection | Hall Effect, Magnetoresistive, Reed Switch |
| **Optical** | Light beam interruption/encoding | Photo-Interrupter, Optical Encoder |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module 1 specifically covers:** *Potentiometric sensors, LVDT, RVDT, Capacitive displacement sensors, Eddy current sensors, Hall effect sensors, and Inductive/Capacitive proximity sensors.* Optical encoders are typically covered in Module 2.

### 1.4 Why These Sensors Matter — Engineering Utility

| Field | Application Example |
|---|---|
| **Automotive** | Throttle position sensing, gearbox position, brake pedal displacement |
| **Robotics** | Robotic arm joint position feedback (degrees of freedom) |
| **CNC Machines** | Tool position feedback, linear slide displacement |
| **Aerospace** | Actuator position feedback in flight control surfaces |
| **Industrial Automation** | Counting parts on a conveyor (proximity), level measurement |

The **standard physical constant** involved is the permeability of free space: $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ **H/m** for inductive sensors, and the permittivity of free space: $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ **F/m** for capacitive sensors. Both appear repeatedly in KTU numerical problems.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Potentiometric Sensors (Resistive Displacement Sensors)

### Construction & Working
A **potentiometer** (or "pot") is a three-terminal variable resistor. It consists of a **resistive element** (carbon film, wire-wound, or conductive plastic) with a **wiper** that slides along it as the input displacement occurs.

- **Type 1: Linear Potentiometer** — Wiper moves in a straight line; resistance $R$ is proportional to displacement $x$.
- **Type 2: Rotary Potentiometer** — Wiper rotates on a circular track; resistance varies with angular displacement $\theta$.

### Output Equation Derivation Logic
Let the total resistance be $R_T$, the wiper position be $x$ along total length $L$, and the supply voltage be $V_s$. The output voltage $V_o$ is taken between the wiper and one end.

- **Step 1:** Resistance per unit length is uniform: $R/L$ (Ω/m).
- **Step 2:** Resistance from wiper to reference end is: $R_x = (R_T/L) \cdot x$.
- **Step 3:** Voltage divider rule: $V_o = V_s \cdot (R_x / R_T)$.
- **Step 4:** Substituting: $V_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ — a **linear transfer function**.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Highlight:** For a **rotary potentiometer** with angle range $0$ to $\theta_{max}$, the output becomes $V_o = V_s \cdot (\theta / \theta_{max})$. This linearity is what makes potentiometers popular for joystick and pedal sensing.

### Resolution Issue
Wire-wound pots have a **finite resolution** due to discrete wire turns (called **staircase effect**). The resolution $R_s$ is given by:

$$R_s = \frac{1}{N}$$

where $N$ is the number of wire turns. This is a *very common 3-mark KTU question*.

## 2.2 LVDT — Linear Variable Differential Transformer

### Construction
The **LVDT** is the most widely used inductive displacement sensor in industry. It has:
- One **primary coil** ($P$) at the center.
- Two identical **secondary coils** ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound on either side, connected in **series opposition**.
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** (armature) that slides through the coil assembly.

### Working Principle — The Core Idea
When AC excitation $V_p$ (typically $1$–$10$ **V RMS** at $50$ Hz–$20$ **kHz**) is applied to the primary, it induces EMFs $E_1$ and $E_2$ in the two secondaries. These are connected in **opposite polarity**, so the output is:

$$V_o = E_1 - E_2$$

- **Core at center (null):** $E_1 = E_2 \Rightarrow V_o = 0$.
- **Core moves toward $S_1$:** $E_1 > E_2 \Rightarrow V_o$ is positive (and in-phase with $V_p$).
- **Core moves toward $S_2$:** $E_2 > E_1 \Rightarrow V_o$ is negative (and 180° out-of-phase with $V_p$).

### Key Advantages (Frequent in KTU)
1. **Infinite resolution** (since output is analog, no steps).
2. **Frictionless operation** (no mechanical contact with core).
3. **Robust and reliable** — life exceeds 10⁶ cycles.
4. **Bidirectional** — phase of output indicates direction of motion.

## 2.3 RVDT — Rotary Variable Differential Transformer

The **RVDT** is the angular counterpart of the LVDT. Instead of a linear sliding core, it has a **rotating cam-shaped armature**. The output voltage is a linear function of angular displacement over a typical range of $\pm 40°$.

$$V_o = K \cdot \theta$$

where $K$ is the sensitivity in **V/rad**. The phase reversal (180°) at the null position indicates direction reversal of rotation.

## 2.4 Capacitive Displacement Sensors

A **capacitive sensor** uses the equation for a parallel-plate capacitor:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Three modes of operation exist:
1. **Variable gap ($d$ changes):** Most sensitive, used for small displacements (μm range).
2. **Variable area ($A$ changes):** Used for larger displacements.
3. **Variable dielectric ($\varepsilon_r$ changes):** Used for level sensing, material identification.

For a **variable gap** sensor, the sensitivity is:

$$S = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

The **nonlinear** inverse-square relationship is a *classic KTU derivation* — but linearization can be achieved by using a **differential configuration** with two capacitors.

## 2.5 Eddy Current Proximity Sensors

When a high-frequency AC current flows in a coil, it generates an alternating magnetic field. If a **conductive target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target's surface (per Faraday's law). These eddy currents create their own opposing magnetic field (per Lenz's law), which **reduces the coil's inductance**.

- The change in inductance is detected by an oscillator circuit, and the amplitude/frequency shift is converted to a voltage proportional to distance.
- **Range:** Typically $0.5$ to $25$ **mm**.
- **Only detects conductive (metallic) targets** — a frequently asked KTU point.

## 2.6 Hall Effect Sensors

When a current $I$ flows through a semiconductor strip in the presence of a magnetic flux density $B$ (perpendicular to the strip), a voltage $V_H$ is generated across the strip:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where:
- $R_H$ = **Hall coefficient** (m³/C)
- $t$ = thickness of the strip (m)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (Tesla)

A **linear Hall sensor** gives an analog output proportional to $B$ (used for position sensing). A **switching Hall sensor** (with Schmitt trigger) gives a digital output (used for proximity/blade counting).

## 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Reference)

| Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Output Type | Range | Key Parameter |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Linear Pot** | $V_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$ | Analog (Voltage) | $1$–$500$ mm | Resolution = $1/N$ |
| **Rotary Pot** | $V_o = V_s \cdot (\theta/\theta_{max})$ | Analog (Voltage) | $0$–$360°$ | Linearity ($\pm$%) |
| **LVDT** | $V_o = K \cdot x$ (in linear range) | Analog (AC Voltage) | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 250$ mm | Sensitivity $K$ (V/mm) |
| **RVDT** | $V_o = K \cdot \theta$ | Analog (AC Voltage) | $\pm 40°$ | Sensitivity $K$ (V/rad) |
| **Capacitive (Gap)** | $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ | Analog (Capacitance) | $0.01$–$10$ mm | Sensitivity $S = -C/d$ |
| **Eddy Current** | $L = f(\text{coil design}, d, \sigma)$ | Analog/Digital | $0.5$–$25$ mm | Only for metals |
| **Hall Effect** | $V_H = R_H I B / t$ | Analog/Digital | $0$–$100$ mm | $R_H$, $B$ in Tesla |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The LVDT equation $V_o = K \cdot x$ is valid **only in the linear operating range**. Outside this range, the output is **nonlinear** (saturates). This is a common 7-mark question.

### Engineering Utility Summary

| Sensor | Most Suitable For |
|---|---|
| **Potentiometer** | Low-cost joystick, pedal, low-frequency applications |
| **LVDT** | Precision industrial displacement in harsh environments |
| **RVDT** | Valve position feedback, aircraft control surfaces |
| **Capacitive** | Sub-micron precision, non-metallic targets |
| **Eddy Current** | Vibration monitoring, shaft displacement in turbo-machinery |
| **Hall Effect** | Brushless DC motor commutation, contactless switching |
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Output Voltage of Linear Potentiometer

**Given:** A linear potentiometer has total resistance $R_T = 10 \text{ k}\Omega$, total length $L = 100 \text{ mm}$, supply voltage $V_s = 10 \text{ V}$, and the wiper is at position $x = 35 \text{ mm}$. Find the output voltage.

**Step 1 — Identify the operating principle.**
The potentiometer is a voltage divider. The output voltage is taken between the wiper and one fixed end. The resistance of the active portion is proportional to the wiper displacement.

**Step 2 — Resistance per unit length (resistivity gradient).**
Since the resistance is uniformly distributed:

$$\frac{R_T}{L} = \frac{10 \times 10^3 \, \Omega}{100 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{m}} = 100 \times 10^3 \, \Omega/\text{m}$$

**Step 3 — Resistance from wiper to reference end.**
The wiper is at $x = 35$ mm $= 0.035$ m. The resistance $R_x$ of the portion between the wiper and the reference end is:

$$R_x = \left(\frac{R_T}{L}\right) \cdot x = (100 \times 10^3) \cdot (0.035)$$

**Step 4 — Numerical evaluation.**

$$R_x = 3500 \, \Omega = 3.5 \, \text{k}\Omega$$

**Step 5 — Apply the voltage divider rule.**

$$V_o = V_s \cdot \frac{R_x}{R_T}$$

**Step 6 — Substitute and compute.**

$$V_o = 10 \cdot \frac{3500}{10000} = 10 \cdot 0.35 = 3.5 \, \text{V}$$

**Final Answer:** $V_o = 3.5$ V.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Sensitivity and Output of an LVDT

**Given:** An LVDT is used to measure displacement. The sensitivity of the LVDT in its linear range is $K = 0.5$ V/mm. The supply voltage is $V_p = 5$ V (RMS) at 5 kHz. Find: (a) the output voltage for a core displacement of $x = 8$ mm, and (b) the output for a displacement of $x = -12$ mm (core on the other side).

**Step 1 — Write the LVDT output equation in the linear range.**
In the linear range, the differential output is:

$$V_o = K \cdot x$$

where $K$ is the sensitivity (V/mm) and $x$ is the displacement from null (mm).

**Step 2 — Compute output for +8 mm displacement.**

$$V_o = (0.5 \, \text{V/mm}) \cdot (8 \, \text{mm}) = 4.0 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

**Step 3 — Determine the phase.**
Since the core is displaced toward $S_1$, $V_o$ is **in-phase** with the primary excitation $V_p$. So:

$$V_o = 4.0 \, \angle 0° \, \text{V (w.r.t. } V_p)$$

**Step 4 — Compute output for -12 mm displacement.**
Note: $x = -12$ mm is *outside* the linear range (typical linear range is $\pm 10$ mm). The output will be **nonlinear** here. We model the saturation behavior as:

$$V_o(\text{nonlinear}) = V_{sat} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{x}{x_{linear}}\right)$$

where $V_{sat} \approx K \cdot x_{linear} = 0.5 \times 10 = 5$ V and $x_{linear} = 10$ mm.

$$V_o = 5 \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{-12}{10}\right) = 5 \cdot \tanh(-1.2)$$

**Step 5 — Evaluate numerically.**

$$\tanh(-1.2) = \frac{e^{-1.2} - e^{1.2}}{e^{-1.2} + e^{1.2}} \approx -0.8337$$

$$V_o = 5 \cdot (-0.8337) = -4.17 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

**Step 6 — Determine the phase for the negative direction.**
Since $V_o$ is negative and the core is on the $S_2$ side, the output is **180° out-of-phase** with $V_p$:

$$V_o = 4.17 \, \angle 180° \, \text{V (w.r.t. } V_p)$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Capacitive Sensor with Variable Gap

**Given:** A parallel-plate capacitive displacement sensor has plate area $A = 250 \text{ mm}^2 = 250 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, nominal gap $d_0 = 1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3}$ m, and the dielectric is air ($\varepsilon_r = 1$). The sensor is connected to a constant current source that converts capacitance change to voltage. (a) Find the nominal capacitance. (b) Find the sensitivity at the nominal gap. (c) Find the change in capacitance for $\Delta d = -0.1$ mm (gap decreased).

**Step 1 — Write the parallel-plate capacitor equation.**

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Compute nominal capacitance at $d_0$.**

$$C_0 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1) \cdot (250 \times 10^{-6})}{1 \times 10^{-3}}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{8.854 \times 250 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-3}} = \frac{2213.5 \times 10^{-18}}{10^{-3}} = 2.2135 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 \approx 2.21 \, \text{pF}}$$

**Step 3 — Derive the sensitivity function.**
Differentiate $C$ with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C_0}{d_0}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the sensitivity at $d_0 = 1$ mm.**

$$S = -\frac{C_0}{d_0} = -\frac{2.2135 \times 10^{-12}}{1 \times 10^{-3}} = -2.2135 \times 10^{-9} \, \text{F/m}$$

$$\boxed{S \approx -2.21 \, \text{nF/m}}$$

The negative sign indicates that capacitance **decreases** as the gap **increases**.

**Step 5 — Compute the capacitance change for $\Delta d = -0.1$ mm $= -1 \times 10^{-4}$ m.**
Using $\Delta C \approx S \cdot \Delta d$ (first-order linearization for small $\Delta d$):

$$\Delta C = (-2.2135 \times 10^{-9}) \cdot (-1 \times 10^{-4}) = 2.2135 \times 10^{-13} \, \text{F}$$

$$\Delta C = 0.221 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 6 — New capacitance value.**

$$C_{new} = C_0 + \Delta C = 2.2135 + 0.221 = 2.4345 \, \text{pF}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Hall Effect Sensor Output

**Given:** A Hall effect sensor is fabricated from a semiconductor strip of thickness $t = 0.5$ mm. The Hall coefficient of the material is $R_H = 1.2 \times 10^{-4}$ m³/C. A control current of $I = 5$ mA is passed through the strip. (a) Find the Hall voltage when $B = 0.4$ T. (b) Find the new Hall voltage if a flux concentrator doubles the effective $B$.

**Step 1 — State the Hall voltage equation.**

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the given values (a).**

$$V_H = \frac{(1.2 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (0.4)}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 3 — Numerator computation.**

$$(1.2 \times 10^{-4}) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-3}) = 6 \times 10^{-7}$$

$$(6 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot (0.4) = 2.4 \times 10^{-7}$$

**Step 4 — Denominator division.**

$$V_H = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-7}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = \frac{2.4 \times 10^{-7}}{5 \times 10^{-4}} = 0.48 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{V}$$

$$\boxed{V_H = 0.48 \, \text{mV}}$$

**Step 5 — Repeat for $B' = 2B = 0.8$ T (flux concentrator).**
Since $V_H \propto B$, doubling $B$ doubles $V_H$:

$$V_H' = 2 \cdot 0.48 = 0.96 \, \text{mV}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Insight:** This is why **flux concentrators** (ferromagnetic pieces placed near the sensor) are used in automotive and industrial applications — they amplify the Hall voltage without increasing current consumption.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Comparative Performance

| Parameter | Potentiometer | LVDT | Capacitive | Eddy Current | Hall Effect |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Range** | Short-Medium | Medium-Long | Very Short | Short | Short-Medium |
| **Resolution** | Limited (wire turns) | Infinite | Infinite | High | High |
| **Linearity** | Good | Excellent | Nonlinear | Good | Good |
| **Contact Type** | Wiping (wear) | Non-contact | Non-contact | Non-contact | Non-contact |
| **Target Material** | Any (direct) | Ferromagnetic | Any (conductive/dielectric) | Conductive only | Magnetic field source |
| **Cost** | Very Low | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low |
| **Harsh Environment** | Poor | Excellent | Good | Good | Good |
| **Frequency Response** | Limited by inertia | High (kHz) | Very High | High | Very High |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
> **Q1.** Differentiate between a *displacement sensor* and a *proximity sensor*. Give one industrial example for each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
A **displacement sensor** measures the *magnitude* of movement of an object from a reference position and provides a continuous analog or digital output proportional to the distance moved. *Example:* LVDT used to measure the displacement of a hydraulic cylinder piston in a CNC machine.

A **proximity sensor** detects the *presence or absence* of an object within a defined non-contact sensing zone and typically provides a binary (on/off) output. It does not measure the actual distance. *Example:* Inductive proximity sensor used to detect metal parts on a conveyor belt for counting.

**Key Difference:** Displacement sensor gives a *quantitative* (analog) output, while a proximity sensor gives a *qualitative* (binary) output. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for differentiation)

---

> **[KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
> **Q2.** State any **three** advantages of an LVDT over a potentiometric displacement sensor.

**Model Answer (3 marks — 1 mark each):**
1. **Infinite resolution:** LVDT output is a continuously varying AC voltage with no steps, whereas wire-wound potentiometers have discrete steps causing staircase error.
2. **Frictionless operation:** LVDT has a non-contact moving core, eliminating mechanical wear; potentiometers have a wiper that physically rubs against the resistive track and wears out.
3. **Higher accuracy and longer life:** LVDT has typical accuracy better than $\pm 0.5\%$ and life exceeding $10^6$ cycles, compared to $10^5$ cycles for potentiometers.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Module 1 Internal Choice:** *Answer ANY ONE full question from this group.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of an **LVDT**. Discuss its transfer characteristics. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Construction (3 marks):**
The LVDT consists of:
- A hollow cylindrical former (non-magnetic, e.g., stainless steel).
- One **primary coil** ($P$) wound centrally on the former.
- Two identical **secondary coils** ($S_1$ and $S_2$) wound on either side of the primary, with equal number of turns.
- $S_1$ and $S_2$ are connected in **series opposition** (i.e., their EMFs subtract).
- A movable **ferromagnetic core** (armature) attached to the object whose displacement is being measured.

**Working (3 marks):**
- An AC excitation voltage $V_p$ (typically $1$–$10$ V RMS at $1$–$20$ kHz) is applied to the primary.
- This produces a varying magnetic flux that links with both secondaries, inducing EMFs $E_1$ (in $S_1$) and $E_2$ (in $S_2$).
- **Null position (core at center):** $E_1 = E_2$ in magnitude, but in series opposition, so net output $V_o = E_1 - E_2 = 0$.
- **Core displaced toward $S_1$:** Flux linkage with $S_1$ increases, $E_1 > E_2$. Net $V_o = E_1 - E_2$ is **positive** and **in-phase** with $V_p$.
- **Core displaced toward $S_2$:** $E_2 > E_1$. Net $V_o$ is **negative** and **180° out-of-phase** with $V_p$.

**Transfer Characteristics (1 mark):**
The output is **linear** for small displacements from null. Beyond the linear range, the output **saturates** asymptotically. The phase of the output (0° or 180°) indicates the direction of displacement.

**(b)** An **LVDT** has a linear range of $\pm 10$ mm and a sensitivity of $0.4$ V/mm. The primary is excited by a $5$ V, $5$ kHz supply. Calculate: (i) the output voltage at a displacement of $+7$ mm, (ii) the output voltage and phase at a displacement of $-15$ mm (using the saturation model). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** Linear range $= \pm 10$ mm, Sensitivity $K = 0.4$ V/mm, $V_p = 5$ V at 5 kHz.

**Step 1 — Output at $+7$ mm (linear region).**

$$V_o = K \cdot x = (0.4 \, \text{V/mm}) \cdot (7 \, \text{mm}) = 2.8 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

Since the displacement is positive (toward $S_1$), the output is **in-phase** with $V_p$:

$$V_o = 2.8 \, \angle 0° \, \text{V}$$

**Step 2 — Identify the saturation voltage.**
The maximum output (at the edge of linear range) is:

$$V_{sat} = K \cdot x_{linear} = 0.4 \cdot 10 = 4.0 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the saturation model for $-15$ mm (outside linear range).**
Using the hyperbolic tangent saturation model:

$$V_o = V_{sat} \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{x}{x_{linear}}\right)$$

$$V_o = 4.0 \cdot \tanh\!\left(\frac{-15}{10}\right) = 4.0 \cdot \tanh(-1.5)$$

**Step 4 — Compute the hyperbolic tangent.**

$$\tanh(-1.5) = \frac{e^{-1.5} - e^{1.5}}{e^{-1.5} + e^{1.5}} = \frac{0.2231 - 4.4817}{0.2231 + 4.4817} = \frac{-4.2586}{4.7048} = -0.9051$$

**Step 5 — Compute the output magnitude.**

$$V_o = 4.0 \cdot (-0.9051) = -3.6204 \, \text{V (RMS)}$$

Magnitude: $\vert V_o \vert = 3.62$ V (RMS)

**Step 6 — Determine the phase.**
Since the core is displaced toward $S_2$ (negative direction), the output is **180° out-of-phase** with $V_p$:

$$\boxed{V_o = 3.62 \, \angle 180° \, \text{V (RMS) w.r.t. } V_p}$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the working principle of a **capacitive displacement sensor** with three modes of operation. Derive the sensitivity for the variable-gap mode. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Working Principle (2 marks):**
A capacitive sensor uses the principle of a parallel-plate capacitor, whose capacitance is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

where $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m, $\varepsilon_r$ is the relative permittivity, $A$ is the overlapping plate area, and $d$ is the gap. Any physical input (displacement, position, force) that changes one of these parameters will modulate $C$, which is then measured by a suitable bridge or oscillator circuit.

**Three Modes of Operation (3 marks):**

| Mode | Parameter Changed | Application | Range |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Variable gap** | $d$ changes | Small displacement, precision | $0.01$–$1$ mm |
| **Variable area** | $A$ changes | Angular position, large displacement | $1$–$100$ mm |
| **Variable dielectric** | $\varepsilon_r$ changes | Level sensing, material ID | $0$–$100$ mm |

**Derivation of Sensitivity — Variable Gap Mode (2 marks):**
Starting with:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

Differentiate with respect to $d$:

$$\frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2} = -\frac{C}{d}$$

The sensitivity $S$ is therefore:

$$S = \left\vert \frac{dC}{dd} \right\vert = \frac{C}{d} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

This shows an **inverse-square** relationship — the sensor is **nonlinear** in its raw form. To linearize, a **differential configuration** (two capacitors) is used.

**(b)** A capacitive displacement sensor has $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2$, nominal gap $d_0 = 0.5$ mm, and uses air as dielectric. (i) Calculate the nominal capacitance. (ii) Calculate the percentage change in capacitance for a displacement of $+0.05$ mm (gap increases to $0.55$ mm). **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $A = 400 \text{ mm}^2 = 400 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2$, $d_0 = 0.5 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\varepsilon_r = 1$, $\Delta d = +0.05 \text{ mm}$.

**Step 1 — Compute nominal capacitance $C_0$.**

$$C_0 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0}$$

$$C_0 = \frac{(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (1) \cdot (400 \times 10^{-6})}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}}$$

**Step 2 — Numerator.**

$$(8.854 \times 10^{-12}) \cdot (400 \times 10^{-6}) = 3541.6 \times 10^{-18} = 3.5416 \times 10^{-15}$$

**Step 3 — Division.**

$$C_0 = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{0.5 \times 10^{-3}} = 7.0832 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F}$$

$$\boxed{C_0 \approx 7.08 \, \text{pF}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the new capacitance at $d_{new} = d_0 + \Delta d = 0.55$ mm.**

$$C_{new} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_{new}} = \frac{3.5416 \times 10^{-15}}{0.55 \times 10^{-3}} = 6.4393 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F} \approx 6.44 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 5 — Calculate the change in capacitance.**

$$\Delta C = C_{new} - C_0 = 6.4393 - 7.0832 = -0.6439 \, \text{pF}$$

**Step 6 — Calculate the percentage change.**

$$\% \Delta C = \frac{\Delta C}{C_0} \times 100 = \frac{-0.6439}{7.0832} \times 100$$

$$\boxed{\% \Delta C \approx -9.09\%}$$

The negative sign indicates that capacitance **decreased** when the gap **increased**, as expected from the inverse relationship.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **LVDT Question:** Students often forget to specify the **phase** of the output. Always state whether $V_o$ is in-phase (0°) or 180° out-of-phase with $V_p$ when the displacement is negative. **Loss: up to 2 marks.**
> 2. **Capacitive Question:** Students frequently use $d$ in **mm** without converting to **meters** in the SI equation. The constants $\varepsilon_0$ are in F/m, so always convert. **Loss: 1 mark.**
> 3. **Potentiometer:** Do not confuse the **resolution** ($1/N$) with **linearity** ($\pm$% of full scale). These are distinct specifications.
> 4. **Hall Effect:** Always include units — $B$ in Tesla, $t$ in meters, $R_H$ in m³/C. Mixing units costs marks.
> 5. **LVDT Linear Range:** A common trap is to apply $V_o = Kx$ for $x$ outside the linear range. Examiners expect you to use the saturation model or state the limit.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Revision Points for KTU 2024 Module 1 Exam:**

- [x] **Potentiometer:** $V_o = V_s \cdot (x/L)$; resolution $= 1/N$ (wire-wound only); contact-type sensor.
- [x] **LVDT:** AC excited; non-contact; output $V_o = Kx$ in linear range; phase indicates direction; **infinite resolution**; series-opposed secondaries.
- [x] **RVDT:** Rotary version of LVDT; operates over $\pm 40°$; used for valve position and aerospace feedback.
- [x] **Capacitive Sensor:** $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$; three modes (gap, area, dielectric); inverse-square sensitivity in gap mode; non-contact; can detect non-metals.
- [x] **Eddy Current Sensor:** Detects only **conductive metals**; range $0.5$–$25$ mm; non-contact; used for vibration/shaft monitoring.
- [x] **Hall Effect Sensor:** $V_H = R_H I B / t$; requires magnetic field source; used for BLDC commutation and contactless switching.
- [x] **Key Constants:** $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12}$ F/m; $\mu_0 = 4\pi \times 10^{-7}$ H/m.
- [x] **Sensor Selection Rule of Thumb:** LVDT for industrial displacement, Capacitive for non-metal targets, Eddy Current for metals only, Hall for magnetic targets, Potentiometer for low-cost applications.
- [x] **Linear Range Trap:** LVDT output saturates outside $\pm x_{linear}$; do NOT apply $V_o = Kx$ blindly.
- [x] **Phase Identification:** LVDT output phase (0° or 180°) is what makes it **bidirectional** — a key KTU long-answer differentiator.
- [x] **Differential Advantage:** Capacitive and LVDT differential configurations **linearize** the output and double the sensitivity.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensors — II

## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> *Displacement, Position and Proximity Sensors (Part-II)* are non-contact and semi-contact transducer families that convert mechanical displacement, linear/angular position, or the presence/absence of a target into measurable electrical signals using **variations in electric field capacitance, magnetic field coupling, eddy-current losses, the Hall effect, optical beam interruption, and acoustic wave reflection*. They form the backbone of feedback control in modern mechatronic systems such as CNC machines, robotic arms, automotive ABS, and industrial automation lines.

In KTU 2024 parlance, this module (Part-II) builds upon the **resistive, potentiometric and LVDT-type transducers** introduced in Part-I, and introduces the **field-effect**, **electromagnetic**, and **wave-based** sensor classes that are widely used in **high-precision, harsh-environment, and non-contact applications**.

### 1.2 Sensor Families Covered in This Module

| # | Sensor Family | Output Quantity | Typical Range | Contact Type |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Capacitive Sensor | Capacitance → Voltage | 1 µm – 10 mm | Non-contact |
| 2 | Inductive Proximity Sensor | Inductance change → Switching | 0.5 – 20 mm | Non-contact |
| 3 | Eddy Current Sensor | Impedance change → Voltage | 0.1 – 50 mm | Non-contact |
| 4 | Hall Effect Position Sensor | Magnetic flux → Voltage | 1 – 50 mm | Non-contact |
| 5 | Optical Encoder (Incremental/Absolute) | Pulses / Gray code | Resolution 0.001° – 360° | Non-contact |
| 6 | Photoelectric Sensor | Beam interruption → Switching | 0.01 m – 50 m | Non-contact |
| 7 | Ultrasonic Proximity Sensor | Time-of-Flight → Distance | 0.1 m – 10 m | Non-contact |

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Six Senses" of a Mechatronic System

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Analogy (Real-World):**
> Think of a mechatronic system as a human body needing to "feel" its surroundings:
> - **Capacitive sensors** = *antennae of an insect* that sense the electric field perturbation when an object approaches.
> - **Inductive sensors** = *a metal detector at an airport*, sensing the buzz of a metallic object in your pocket.
> - **Eddy current sensors** = *a magician's levitation trick*, where invisible whirlpools of current form in a metal and resist its motion.
> - **Hall effect sensors** = *the compass needle* of a sailor, reacting to Earth's magnetic field.
> - **Optical encoders** = *the mileage counter* in a car's odometer, counting the slits on a rotating disc.
> - **Ultrasonic sensors** = *the echo-locating bat*, screaming a chirp and listening for its return.

### 1.4 Key Engineering Constants & Metrics

The following standard metrics are used across all sensors covered in this module:
- **Range** — the maximum distance the sensor can reliably measure.
- **Resolution** — the smallest detectable change in the measurand.
- **Linearity (% FS)** — the deviation of output from the best-fit straight line, expressed as a percentage of Full-Scale output. Typical target: **±0.1 % FS to ±1 % FS**.
- **Hysteresis** — the difference in output when approaching a point from opposite directions.
- **Repeatability** — the ability to reproduce the same output under identical conditions.
- **Response time / Bandwidth** — speed of response to a step change in input.
- **Operating temperature range** — typically **−40 °C to +85 °C** for industrial grade and **−55 °C to +150 °C** for military grade.

### 1.5 Why This Module is Critical in Mechatronics

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight (KTU 2024, CO1 – Mapped):**
> In every closed-loop mechatronic system — from a robotic gripper to a 6-DOF CNC tool changer — the **position/displacement feedback** is non-negotiable. Part-II of this module specifically equips the student with the **non-contact sensor classes** that are required in applications where:
> 1. The target is **moving at high speed** (no mechanical wear allowed).
> 2. The environment is **dusty, oily, hot, or wet**.
> 3. **Sub-micron resolution** is required (e.g., wafer stage positioning).
> 4. The target surface is **fragile or inaccessible** (e.g., biomedical or nuclear applications).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Capacitive Displacement & Position Sensors

### 2.1.1 Working Principle

A capacitive sensor operates on the principle of **variation in capacitance** $C$ of a capacitor in response to a mechanical displacement, position, or the approach of a target object. The three classical modes of operation are:

1. **Variable plate separation (d-mode):** One plate is fixed; the other moves. $C$ varies inversely with $d$.
2. **Variable overlapping area (A-mode):** Plates slide laterally; $C$ varies linearly with overlap.
3. **Variable dielectric (ε-mode):** A dielectric slab slides between fixed plates.

### 2.1.2 Fundamental Capacitance Formulas

The basic **parallel-plate capacitor** model is the foundation:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \cdot \varepsilon_r \cdot A}{d}$$

where:
- $C$ = capacitance in **Farads (F)**
- $\varepsilon_0$ = **permittivity of free space = $8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$**
- $\varepsilon_r$ = **relative permittivity (dielectric constant)** of the medium between plates
- $A$ = overlapping area of plates in **m²**
- $d$ = separation between plates in **m**

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Insight:** For an air-dielectric parallel-plate capacitor with $A = 100 \, \text{mm}^2$ and $d = 1 \, \text{mm}$, the nominal capacitance is approximately **$C \approx 8.85 \, \text{pF}$**. This extremely small value is why capacitive sensors require **carefully designed oscillator-amplifier circuits** and **shielded cables** to avoid stray capacitance pickup.

### 2.1.3 Sensitivity Analysis

The sensitivity $S$ of a capacitive sensor is the change in $C$ per unit change in the measurand.

**For d-mode (variable separation):**
$$S_d = \frac{dC}{dd} = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

This shows sensitivity is **non-linear** in $d$ (inverse square relationship). For linearization, **ratio-metric measurement** (using $C_1 / (C_1 + C_2)$ with a differential arrangement) is used.

**For A-mode (variable area):**
$$S_A = \frac{dC}{dA} = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r}{d}$$

This is **perfectly linear** in $A$, making it the preferred mode for **linear position** measurement.

**For ε-mode (variable dielectric):**
$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 A}{d}\left[\varepsilon_{r1}(1-x) + \varepsilon_{r2} x\right]$$

where $x$ is the fractional position of the dielectric slab.

### 2.1.4 Practical Capacitive Proximity Sensor

Modern capacitive proximity sensors are **two-electrode devices** with an **active guard electrode**. They detect the presence of **any material** (metals, plastics, liquids, wood, granulates, even human body) within a few millimetres.

---

## 2.2 Inductive Proximity Sensors

### 2.2.1 Working Principle

An inductive proximity sensor consists of a **ferrite-cored coil** driven by an **LC oscillator** at a typical frequency of **100 kHz to 1 MHz**. When a **ferrous or non-ferrous metal target** enters the magnetic field emanating from the coil's open face:

1. **Eddy currents** are induced in the target surface.
2. These eddy currents **oppose the original field** (Lenz's law).
3. The **effective inductance** $L$ of the coil changes.
4. The oscillator amplitude drops below a threshold.
5. The **output switching stage** triggers (open-collector NPN or PNP, or push-pull).

### 2.2.2 The Key Equation — Inductance of a Coil with Target

For a coil of $N$ turns, length $l$, and cross-sectional area $A_c$, with a target of permeability $\mu_r$ at distance $d$:

$$L = \frac{\mu_0 \mu_r N^2 A_c}{l} \cdot f(d)$$

where $f(d)$ is a non-linear function of the **gap** $d$ between coil face and target. The typical sensing range is **$0.5 \times d_{sn}$ to $d_{sn}$** (sn = nominal switching distance), with a **hysteresis of 1–15 % of $d_{sn}$** to prevent chattering.

### 2.2.3 Equation for Range of Detection (Inductive)

The nominal switching distance for a cylindrical inductive sensor of diameter $D$ is approximately:

$$d_{sn} \approx k \cdot D$$

where $k$ is a material constant:
- **Steel: $k = 1.0$** (reference)
- **Stainless steel (AISI 304): $k \approx 0.7$**
- **Brass: $k \approx 0.4$**
- **Aluminium: $k \approx 0.35$**
- **Copper: $k \approx 0.25$**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Point:** Inductive sensors **cannot detect non-metals** (wood, plastic, water). This is a standard viva question. The remedy is the **capacitive sensor**.

---

## 2.3 Eddy Current Position Sensors

### 2.3.1 Working Principle

An eddy current sensor uses **two coils** — an **active (driver) coil** and a **pickup (sense) coil** — wound on a common probe. The driver coil is excited with a high-frequency AC source (typically **1 MHz to 5 MHz**). The electromagnetic field from the driver induces **eddy currents in the target**, which create their own opposing field that **couples into the pickup coil**, changing its impedance $Z$.

### 2.3.2 Skin Depth — The Crucial Limitation

The eddy current does not penetrate the target uniformly. The **depth of penetration** (or **skin depth**) $\delta$ is given by:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho}{\omega \mu_0 \mu_r}} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

where:
- $\rho$ = **resistivity of the target material** in $\Omega \cdot m$
- $\omega = 2 \pi f$ = angular frequency in rad/s
- $f$ = excitation frequency in Hz
- $\mu_0$ = **$4\pi \times 10^{-7} \, \text{H/m}$**
- $\mu_r$ = relative permeability of the target

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Reality Check:** For aluminium ($\rho \approx 2.65 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$, $\mu_r \approx 1$) excited at $f = 1 \, \text{MHz}$, the skin depth is approximately $\delta \approx 65 \, \mu m$. The target must be at least $3\delta$ thick to behave as "infinite" for the eddy current — this is why eddy current sensors are essentially **surface-only devices**.

### 2.3.3 Output Impedance

The pickup coil's impedance magnitude varies as:

$$Z = R + j\omega L$$

and the change in $|Z|$ as a function of gap $d$ follows:

$$|Z|(d) = Z_\infty + (Z_0 - Z_\infty) \cdot e^{-k d}$$

where $Z_0$ is the impedance at zero gap, $Z_\infty$ is the far-field impedance, and $k$ is a constant determined by coil geometry.

### 2.3.4 Applications
- **Turbine blade tip clearance** measurement in jet engines (operating at 1000 °C+).
- **Shaft vibration monitoring** in turbo-machinery.
- **Thread/fastener inspection** in quality control.
- **Conductive target position** in harsh environments (oil, coolant, vacuum).

---

## 2.4 Hall Effect Position Sensors

### 2.4.1 The Hall Effect Phenomenon

When a **current-carrying conductor or semiconductor** is placed in a **transverse magnetic field** $\vec{B}$, the charge carriers experience a **Lorentz force** that deflects them to one side of the conductor. This accumulation of charge creates a measurable **Hall voltage** $V_H$ perpendicular to both $\vec{I}$ and $\vec{B}$.

### 2.4.2 The Hall Voltage Equation

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \cdot I \cdot B}{t}$$

where:
- $R_H$ = **Hall coefficient** (m³/C) — material property
- $I$ = current through the sensor (A)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density perpendicular to the sensor (T)
- $t$ = thickness of the Hall element in the direction of $B$ (m)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** For a typical silicon Hall sensor with $R_H \approx 10^{-4} \, \text{m}^3/\text{C}$, $I = 5 \, \text{mA}$, $B = 0.1 \, \text{T}$, and $t = 100 \, \mu m$, the Hall voltage is approximately $V_H = 5 \, \text{mV}$. This is small, so **on-chip amplification** is mandatory.

### 2.4.3 Hall Effect Sensor Categories

| Type | Output | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| **Hall switch (digital)** | ON/OFF (Schmitt trigger) | Limit sensing, BLDC commutation |
| **Latching Hall** | Toggle on pole change | Tachometers, brushless DC motors |
| **Linear Hall** | Analog ratiometric output (V/2 = zero field) | Position sensing of joystick, throttle |
| **3D Hall** | X, Y, Z field components | Joysticks, rotary encoders |

### 2.4.4 Derivation of Hall Coefficient (Carrier Type Identification)

For an n-type semiconductor with carrier concentration $n$ and charge $q = e$:

$$R_H = -\frac{1}{n e}$$

For a p-type semiconductor:

$$R_H = +\frac{1}{p e}$$

The **sign of $V_H$** thus directly tells us whether the material is **n-type (negative $V_H$)** or **p-type (positive $V_H$)**.

---

## 2.5 Optical Encoders

### 2.5.1 Working Principle

An **optical encoder** uses a **light source (LED)**, a **code disc/linear scale with alternating transparent and opaque patterns**, and a **photodetector array**. As the disc/scale moves:
- **Transparent slot** → light passes → photodetector outputs HIGH.
- **Opaque mark** → light blocked → photodetector outputs LOW.
This produces a **pulse train** whose frequency is proportional to velocity and whose count is proportional to displacement.

### 2.5.2 Incremental vs Absolute Encoders

| Feature | Incremental | Absolute |
|---|---|---|
| Output | A, B (quadrature) + Z index | Unique binary / Gray code per position |
| Position on power-up | Lost — must home | Retained |
| Resolution | Determined by CPR (counts per rev) | Determined by $n$-bit word ($2^n$ positions) |
| Cost | Lower | Higher |
| Typical use | Velocity feedback, simple position | Multi-turn robotic joints, CNC tool changers |

### 2.5.3 Resolution Formula

For a **rotary incremental encoder** with $N$ slots on the disc and quadrature decoding (4×):

$$\theta_{res} = \frac{360°}{4N}$$

For a **linear encoder** with line pitch $p$ (µm):

$$x_{res} = \frac{p}{4} \quad \text{(with 4× interpolation)}$$

### 2.5.4 Why Gray Code for Absolute Encoders?

In Gray code, **only one bit changes at a time** between adjacent positions. This eliminates the risk of multiple-bit transition errors at the decoder — critical for high-speed absolute position reading. Conversion from Gray to binary uses:

$$B_n = G_n \oplus B_{n+1}$$

where $\oplus$ is the XOR operator.

---

## 2.6 Photoelectric Proximity Sensors (Through-Beam, Retro-Reflective, Diffuse)

### 2.6.1 Three Standard Modes

1. **Through-beam:** Emitter and receiver in separate housings; target breaks the beam. **Longest range (up to 50 m).**
2. **Retro-reflective:** Emitter and receiver in one housing; a **retroreflector** returns the beam; target breaks it. Range up to **15 m**.
3. **Diffuse reflective:** Emitter and receiver in one housing; target *itself* is the reflector. Range up to **3 m**; depends on target colour and surface.

### 2.6.2 Excess Gain Concept

**Excess gain (E.G.)** is the ratio of light energy reaching the receiver to the minimum energy needed to trigger the output.

$$E.G. = \frac{I_{received}}{I_{threshold}}$$

A **safety margin** of E.G. ≥ 2 is required for reliable operation in the presence of dust, dirt, or misalignment.

---

## 2.7 Ultrasonic Proximity / Distance Sensors

### 2.7.1 Working Principle

Ultrasonic sensors use a **piezoelectric transducer** to emit a **burst of acoustic waves** (typically at **40 kHz**, 200 kHz, or 400 kHz) and listen for the echo reflected from the target. The **time-of-flight (TOF)** is measured.

### 2.7.2 Distance from Time-of-Flight

For a sound wave travelling through air at speed $v_{sound}$:

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$$

where the division by 2 accounts for the **round-trip path** (outward + return).

### 2.7.3 Speed of Sound in Air (Temperature-Dependent)

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \cdot T \, \text{(m/s)}$$

where $T$ is the air temperature in °C. At **20 °C**, $v_{sound} \approx 343 \, \text{m/s}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested:** At $T = 20°\text{C}$, a 1 m distance produces a round-trip time of approximately $t = 2 / 343 \approx 5.83 \, \text{ms}$. The minimum measurable distance is limited by the **transducer ring-down time** (typically 1–3 ms), giving a **blind zone of ~10–30 cm**.

---

## 2.9 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Industry | Preferred Sensor | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| CNC machine tools | Optical linear encoder, eddy current | Sub-µm resolution, oil-resistant |
| Automotive ABS | Hall effect, AMR | Reliable, contactless, low cost |
| Robotic arm joints | Absolute optical encoder, resolver | Multi-turn, no power-on homing |
| Food packaging | Capacitive, photoelectric | Detect any material (plastic, glass, liquid) |
| Hydraulic cylinder position | LVDT (Part-I), magnetostrictive | Long stroke in oil-filled environment |
| Autonomous vehicles | Lidar (ultrasonic principle, optical version) | Long-range obstacle detection |
| Mobile phones (focus) | Hall effect | Detects flip cover closure |
| Washing machine drum | Hall switch (latching) | Speed feedback for BLDC motor |
