# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Capacitive Sensor Sensitivity (d-Mode)

**Objective:** Starting from the basic parallel-plate formula, derive the sensitivity $S_d = dC/dd$ and explain why the response is non-linear.

**Step 1 — Start with the governing equation**

The capacitance of a parallel-plate capacitor is:

$$C = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}$$

**Step 2 — Identify constants and variables**

For the d-mode (variable separation):
- **Constants:** $\varepsilon_0, \varepsilon_r, A$ (plate area is held fixed).
- **Variable:** $d$ (gap distance).

**Step 3 — Differentiate with respect to the variable $d$**

Treating $C$ as a function of $d$ and applying the rule $d(u/v)/dd = -u/v^2$:

$$S_d = \frac{dC}{dd} = \frac{d}{dd}\left(\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d}\right) = -\frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d^2}$$

**Step 4 — Interpret the result**

The negative sign indicates that as $d$ increases, $C$ decreases (inverse proportionality). The sensitivity itself depends on $d^2$, meaning the sensor is **more sensitive at small gaps** and **less sensitive at large gaps**.

**Step 5 — Linearization using ratio-metric measurement**

For a **differential capacitive sensor**, two capacitors $C_1$ and $C_2$ are arranged so that as the target moves, one capacitance increases while the other decreases. Define:

$$C_1 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 - \Delta d}, \quad C_2 = \frac{\varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A}{d_0 + \Delta d}$$

The ratio-metric output is:

$$R = \frac{C_1 - C_2}{C_1 + C_2} = \frac{\Delta d}{d_0}$$

This is **linear in $\Delta d$**, which is the engineering reason for using differential capacitive sensors in precision applications such as **accelerometers (ADXL-series MEMS)**.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Eddy Current Skin Depth

**Objective:** Derive the expression for skin depth $\delta$ in a conductor carrying an alternating current.

**Step 1 — Maxwell's equation setup**

For a plane electromagnetic wave entering a conductor from the $z$-direction, Maxwell's equation in differential form gives:

$$\frac{d^2 H}{dz^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma \cdot H$$

where $\sigma = 1/\rho$ is the conductivity and $\mu = \mu_0 \mu_r$.

**Step 2 — Solve the second-order ODE**

The general solution is of the form $H(z) = H_0 \, e^{-\gamma z}$ where:

$$\gamma = \sqrt{j \omega \mu \sigma} = (1+j)\sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma}$$

**Step 3 — Extract the real attenuation constant**

The real part of $\gamma$ is the attenuation constant $\alpha$:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi f \mu}{\rho}}$$

**Step 4 — Define skin depth as the 1/e penetration depth**

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Step 5 — Numerical example**

For **aluminium** ($\rho = 2.65 \times 10^{-8} \, \Omega \cdot m$, $\mu_r = 1$) at $f = 1 \, \text{MHz}$:

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2.65 \times 10^{-8}}{\pi \times 10^6 \times 4\pi \times 10^{-7} \times 1}}$$

$$\delta = \sqrt{\frac{2.65 \times 10^{-8}}{3.95 \times 10^{0}}} \approx \sqrt{6.71 \times 10^{-9}} \approx 8.19 \times 10^{-5} \, \text{m} \approx 82 \, \mu m$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Take-away:** The skin depth scales as $1/\sqrt{f}$. Doubling the excitation frequency reduces the skin depth by $\sqrt{2} \approx 1.414$. This is why **high-frequency eddy current probes** are more **surface-sensitive**, while low-frequency probes penetrate deeper for **subsurface defect detection**.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Hall Voltage from Lorentz Force Balance

**Objective:** Starting from the force balance on a moving charge in a magnetic field, derive the Hall voltage equation.

**Step 1 — Setup geometry**

A current $I$ flows along the **+x direction** in a conductor of width $w$ (along y) and thickness $t$ (along z). A magnetic field $\vec{B}$ is applied along the **+z direction**.

**Step 2 — Lorentz force on a charge carrier**

For a charge $q$ moving with drift velocity $v_d$ in the +x direction:

$$\vec{F} = q (\vec{v}_d \times \vec{B}) = q v_d B \hat{y}$$

This force deflects the carriers to the **+y side** (or -y side, depending on sign of $q$).

**Step 3 — Build-up of transverse electric field**

The accumulated charge on the +y edge creates an electric field $\vec{E}_H$ along -y (for positive carriers pushed to +y):

$$\vec{E}_H = \frac{V_H}{w}$$

**Step 4 — Steady-state force balance**

In steady state, the **transverse electric force** exactly cancels the **magnetic force**:

$$q E_H = q v_d B \quad \Rightarrow \quad E_H = v_d B$$

**Step 5 — Convert $E_H$ to $V_H$**

$$V_H = E_H \cdot w = v_d B w$$

**Step 6 — Express drift velocity in terms of current**

For a conductor of cross-section $A_c = w \cdot t$:

$$I = n q v_d A_c \quad \Rightarrow \quad v_d = \frac{I}{n q w t}$$

**Step 7 — Substitute into $V_H$**

$$V_H = \frac{I B w}{n q w t} = \frac{I B}{n q t} = \frac{R_H \, I \, B}{t}$$

where $R_H = 1/(nq)$ is the **Hall coefficient**.

**Step 8 — Numerical example**

For a silicon Hall element with $n = 10^{21} \, m^{-3}$, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19} \, C$, $t = 100 \, \mu m$, $I = 5 \, mA$, $B = 0.1 \, T$:

$$R_H = \frac{1}{10^{21} \times 1.6 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.25 \times 10^{-3} \, m^3/C$$

$$V_H = \frac{6.25 \times 10^{-3} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.1}{100 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{3.125 \times 10^{-6}}{10^{-4}} = 3.125 \times 10^{-2} \, V \approx 31 \, mV$$

This confirms the typical "few tens of mV" range — explaining why **on-chip op-amps** are mandatory.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Ultrasonic Distance from Time-of-Flight

**Objective:** Show how the time-of-flight translates to physical distance, accounting for the round-trip path and the temperature dependence of the speed of sound.

**Step 1 — One-way distance**

If the wave travels a one-way distance $d$ at speed $v$, the one-way time is:

$$t_{one-way} = \frac{d}{v}$$

**Step 2 — Round-trip time-of-flight**

The transducer emits a burst, the wave travels to the target (distance $d$), reflects, and returns to the transducer. The total path is $2d$:

$$t_{TOF} = \frac{2d}{v}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for distance**

$$d = \frac{v \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$$

**Step 4 — Substitute the temperature-dependent sound speed**

$$d = \frac{(331.4 + 0.6 T) \cdot t_{TOF}}{2}$$

**Step 5 — Worked numerical example**

A target is placed in air at $T = 30°\text{C}$, and the measured $t_{TOF} = 5.0 \, \text{ms}$. Find the distance.

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30 = 349.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

$$d = \frac{349.4 \times 5.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{1.747}{2} = 0.8735 \, \text{m} \approx 87.4 \, \text{cm}$$

**Step 6 — Uncertainty analysis**

A $\pm 2°\text{C}$ uncertainty in temperature gives a $\pm 1.2 \, \text{m/s}$ uncertainty in $v$, which over $t = 5 \, \text{ms}$ gives:

$$\Delta d = \frac{1.2 \times 5 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 3 \times 10^{-3} \, m = 3 \, \text{mm}$$

This is why **ultrasonic sensors** are often paired with **on-board temperature sensors** for compensation.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Encoder Resolution with Quadrature Decoding

**Objective:** Show how quadrature decoding quadruples the effective resolution of an incremental encoder.

**Step 1 — Single-channel pulse count**

A disc with $N$ slots per revolution produces $N$ rising edges per revolution on a single channel. Angular resolution is:

$$\theta_1 = \frac{360°}{N}$$

**Step 2 — Add a second channel in quadrature**

A second photodetector placed **90° out of phase** produces a second pulse train. Together, the two channels generate **4 distinguishable events per slot**: rising-A, rising-B, falling-A, falling-B.

**Step 3 — Quadrupled resolution**

$$\theta_4 = \frac{360°}{4N}$$

**Step 4 — Direction determination**

The **order** of A and B transitions tells direction:
- If **A leads B** → motion is in the +ve direction.
- If **B leads A** → motion is in the -ve direction.

**Step 5 — Worked example**

A 5000-line rotary encoder (with 4× decoding) used on a robot wheel of radius $r = 50 \, \text{mm}$:

$$\theta_4 = \frac{360°}{4 \times 5000} = 0.018°$$

The corresponding **linear resolution at the wheel circumference** is:

$$x_{res} = r \cdot \theta_{4,rad} = 0.05 \times \frac{0.018 \times \pi}{180} \approx 15.7 \, \mu m$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Frequently Tested Question Type:** "An incremental encoder with 2500 PPR is mounted on a 100 mm-radius wheel. Find the linear resolution in mm with 4× decoding." — Use the formula $x_{res} = 2 \pi r / (4N)$.

---

## 3.6 Comparative Analytical Matrix (Decision Tree for Sensor Selection)

| If the application requires... | Choose... | Because... |
|---|---|---|
| Detect **any material** (metal or non-metal) at close range | Capacitive proximity | Field perturbation works on dielectric targets too |
| Detect only **ferrous/non-ferrous metal** at close range | Inductive proximity | Robust, low cost, IP67 rated |
| Sub-**micron displacement** of a metal target | Eddy current sensor | High resolution, non-contact, harsh environment |
| **Rotational/linear position** with retained info on power loss | Absolute encoder | Gray code preserves position |
| **Long range** (up to 50 m) detection of any opaque object | Through-beam photoelectric | Highest excess gain over distance |
| **Distance to liquid level** or **transparent target** | Ultrasonic | Acoustic wave reflects off liquid surface |
| Detection of a **magnet on a moving target** (e.g., BLDC commutation) | Hall effect | Sensitive to flux, ignores non-magnetics |
| **Sub-micron** linear displacement over a long stroke | Linear optical encoder | Highest achievable resolution |

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]

**Q: Differentiate between an inductive proximity sensor and a capacitive proximity sensor in terms of target material, sensing range, and typical application. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Inductive Proximity | Capacitive Proximity |
|---|---|---|
| **Target material** | Detects only metals (ferrous & non-ferrous) | Detects any material (metals, plastics, wood, liquids, granulates) |
| **Sensing range** | 0.5 – 20 mm | 1 – 30 mm |
| **Operating principle** | Eddy current losses in target | Change in capacitance due to dielectric/area/gap change |
| **Typical application** | Metal part counting on conveyor, machine tool interlock | Liquid level detection through glass, granulate level in silos |
| **Cost** | Generally lower | Slightly higher due to oscillator/compensation circuitry |

**(1 mark per key point × 3 = 3 marks)**

---

### Question A2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]

**Q: A Hall effect sensor produces an output of 2.5 V at zero magnetic field, and the output changes by 1.25 V for a magnetic flux density change of 0.2 T. Determine the sensitivity of the sensor. (3 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

Sensitivity of a ratiometric linear Hall sensor is defined as:

$$S = \frac{\Delta V}{\Delta B}$$

Substituting the given values:

$$S = \frac{1.25 \, \text{V}}{0.2 \, \text{T}} = 6.25 \, \text{V/T}$$

**Therefore, the Hall sensor sensitivity is 6.25 V/T. (3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The KTU 2024 Scheme mandates that the student answers **one full 14-mark question per module**, and the question paper offers **an internal choice of (a) and (b)**. Two completely independent alternative questions are provided below.

---

### Question B1 (Option A) — [KTU University Exam — June 2024 Model Paper]

**(a)** Explain the working principle of an **eddy current proximity sensor** with a neat functional block diagram. Derive the expression for **skin depth** and explain how it affects the choice of excitation frequency. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A rotary incremental encoder has **2500 pulses per revolution (PPR)**. The encoder is mounted on a shaft driving a wheel of **radius 50 mm**. With **quadrature (4×) decoding**, determine:
   (i) The **angular resolution** in degrees and radians.
   (ii) The **linear resolution at the wheel surface** in mm and µm.
   (iii) The **distance traveled by the wheel in 1 minute** if the encoder counts 150,000 quadrature pulses during that interval. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Eddy Current Proximity Sensor — Working Principle & Skin Depth**

A typical eddy current proximity sensor consists of two coils — a **driver (excitation) coil** and a **pickup (sensor) coil** — wound on a common probe. The driver coil is excited by a **high-frequency AC source (1–5 MHz)**, producing an alternating magnetic field that radiates from the probe tip.

When a **conductive target** enters this field, **eddy currents** are induced on the target surface (Faraday's law). These eddy currents produce their **own magnetic field in opposition** to the original field (Lenz's law). The opposing field couples into the pickup coil and changes its **impedance $Z = R + j\omega L$**.

The change in $|Z|$ is a monotonic function of the gap $d$ between probe and target, allowing precise non-contact measurement of position or vibration.

**Skin Depth Derivation:**

Starting from Maxwell's equation for a plane wave in a conductor:

$$\frac{d^2 H}{dz^2} = j \omega \mu \sigma H$$

The solution gives an attenuation constant:

$$\alpha = \sqrt{\pi f \mu \sigma} = \sqrt{\frac{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}{\rho}}$$

The skin depth (1/e penetration depth) is:

$$\delta = \frac{1}{\alpha} = \sqrt{\frac{\rho}{\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r}}$$

**Effect on frequency choice:**

- At **higher $f$**, $\delta$ is **smaller** → probes are more **surface-sensitive**, suitable for **small targets** and **precision measurement**.
- At **lower $f$**, $\delta$ is **larger** → probes **penetrate deeper**, useful for **subsurface defect detection** in thick metal parts.

Typical choice: **$f = 1 \text{ MHz}$ for surface position; $f = 100 \text{ kHz}$ for deeper penetration.** (**3 marks** for principle, **2 marks** for diagram, **2 marks** for derivation & frequency choice = **7 marks**)

**(b) Encoder Resolution & Distance Calculation**

**(i) Angular resolution (4× decoding):**

$$\theta_4 = \frac{360°}{4 \times 2500} = \frac{360°}{10000} = 0.036°$$

In radians:

$$\theta_{4,rad} = 0.036° \times \frac{\pi}{180°} = 6.283 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{rad}$$

**(ii) Linear resolution at wheel surface:**

Circumference of wheel: $C = 2\pi r = 2 \pi (50) = 314.16 \, \text{mm}$.

$$x_{res} = \frac{C}{4N} = \frac{314.16}{4 \times 2500} = \frac{314.16}{10000} = 0.0314 \, \text{mm} = 31.4 \, \mu m$$

**(iii) Distance traveled in 1 minute:**

With 4× decoding, 150,000 quadrature pulses correspond to:

$$N_{rev} = \frac{150{,}000}{4 \times 2500} = 15 \text{ revolutions}$$

Distance per revolution = circumference = 314.16 mm.

$$d_{total} = 15 \times 314.16 = 4712.4 \, \text{mm} = 4.71 \, \text{m}$$

(**2 marks** for (i), **2 marks** for (ii), **3 marks** for (iii) = **7 marks**)

---

### Question B2 (Option B) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper]

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a **Hall effect sensor** for position measurement. Derive the expression for **Hall voltage** and discuss how the **sign of the Hall voltage** indicates the **type of charge carrier** in the semiconductor. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An **ultrasonic proximity sensor** operates at 40 kHz in air at **30 °C**. The sensor sends a burst and measures a **round-trip time of 6.0 ms** to detect a target. Calculate:
   (i) The **speed of sound** in air at 30 °C.
   (ii) The **distance to the target** in metres.
   (iii) The **error in distance** if the temperature sensor fails and the firmware uses the default 20 °C value. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Hall Effect Sensor — Working Principle & Derivation**

A Hall effect sensor consists of a thin **semiconductor die** (usually silicon, GaAs, or InSb) through which a **constant current $I$** is passed. A **magnetic field $B$** applied perpendicular to the current deflects the charge carriers to one side, producing a transverse **Hall voltage $V_H$**.

**Functional block diagram (already drawn in Section 4.4):**
Power supply → constant current source → Hall element → differential amplifier → temperature compensation → Schmitt trigger → open-drain output.

**Derivation (already shown in Section 3.3):**

Starting from Lorentz force balance $qE_H = qv_d B$ and current relation $I = nq v_d w t$:

$$V_H = \frac{R_H \, I \, B}{t}, \quad \text{where} \quad R_H = \frac{1}{nq}$$

**Carrier type identification:**

- For **n-type** semiconductor: $q = -e$ (electrons), so $R_H < 0$, so $V_H < 0$ (Hall voltage is **negative**).
- For **p-type** semiconductor: $q = +e$ (holes), so $R_H > 0$, so $V_H > 0$ (Hall voltage is **positive**).

Therefore, the **sign of the measured Hall voltage** directly identifies the **majority carrier type** in the semiconductor. (**3 marks** for working + diagram, **2 marks** for derivation, **2 marks** for carrier-type discussion = **7 marks**)

**(b) Ultrasonic Distance Calculation**

**(i) Speed of sound at 30 °C:**

$$v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times T = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 30 = 331.4 + 18 = 349.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

**(ii) Distance to target:**

$$d = \frac{v_{sound} \cdot t_{TOF}}{2} = \frac{349.4 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = \frac{2.0964}{2} = 1.0482 \, \text{m} \approx 1.05 \, \text{m}$$

**(iii) Error if firmware uses 20 °C default:**

Speed of sound at 20 °C (default):

$$v_{20} = 331.4 + 0.6 \times 20 = 343.4 \, \text{m/s}$$

Distance reported by firmware:

$$d_{reported} = \frac{343.4 \times 6.0 \times 10^{-3}}{2} = 1.0302 \, \text{m}$$

Absolute error:

$$\Delta d = d_{reported} - d_{actual} = 1.0302 - 1.0482 = -0.018 \, \text{m} = -1.8 \, \text{cm}$$

Percentage error:

$$\% \text{error} = \frac{0.018}{1.0482} \times 100 \approx 1.72\%$$

The **actual distance is 1.05 m**; the **reported distance with default temperature is 1.03 m**, giving an **error of 1.8 cm (1.72%)**. This demonstrates why ultrasonic sensors **must use on-board temperature compensation** for accuracy. (**2 marks** for (i), **2 marks** for (ii), **3 marks** for (iii) = **7 marks**)

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Where KTU Students Commonly Lose Marks on This Topic:**
> 1. **Mixing up "Hall coefficient sign" with "conventional current direction"** — Always remember: the Hall voltage sign is determined by the **sign of the charge carrier**, not the conventional current. (Loses 1–2 marks.)
> 2. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in ultrasonic time-of-flight** — The wave goes to the target *and returns*, so $d = v \cdot t / 2$, not $v \cdot t$. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 3. **Reporting encoder resolution without specifying 4× decoding** — A 2500-PPR encoder has 2500 *lines*, but the effective count is **10,000 per revolution** with 4× quadrature decoding. (Loses 2–3 marks.)
> 4. **Wrong units for Hall coefficient** — $R_H$ is in $\text{m}^3/\text{C}$, **NOT** $\text{m}^2/\text{C}$ or $\text{m/C}$. (Loses 1 mark.)
> 5. **Not stating assumptions** — In derivations, always state "for **steady-state**, with **constant current source**, and **negligible fringing of field lines**". (Loses 1 mark per assumption omitted.)
> 6. **Confusing skin depth with penetration depth** — Skin depth is the **1/e** depth, not the depth of total current flow. (Loses 2 marks.)
> 7. **Drawing block diagrams without arrow directions** — KTU examiners often deduct 0.5–1 mark if signal flow direction is not indicated. (Loses 0.5–1 mark.)
> 8. **Not mentioning the "blind zone" of ultrasonic sensors** — The minimum measurable distance is limited by the transducer ring-down, typically 10–30 cm. (Loses 1 mark if asked.)

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density 60-Second Revision Checklist for Module 1 Part-II:**

### A. Capacitive Sensors
- [x] Equation: $C = \varepsilon_0 \varepsilon_r A / d$ with $\varepsilon_0 = 8.854 \times 10^{-12} \, \text{F/m}$.
- [x] Three modes: **d-mode** (non-linear, inverse square), **A-mode** (linear), **ε-mode** (dielectric slide).
- [x] **Detects all materials** including non-metals.
- [x] Differential configuration gives **linear ratio-metric output**.

### B. Inductive Proximity Sensors
- [x] Equation: $d_{sn} \approx kD$, $k_{\text{steel}} = 1.0$, $k_{\text{Al}} \approx 0.35$.
- [x] **Metals only** — cannot detect wood, plastic, water.
- [x] Hysteresis 1–15 % of $d_{sn}$ to prevent chattering.

### C. Eddy Current Sensors
- [x] Skin depth: $\delta = \sqrt{\rho / (\pi f \mu_0 \mu_r)}$.
- [x] **Conductive targets only**, surface-sensitive.
- [x] Used for **tip clearance, vibration, sub-µm displacement**.
- [x] Higher $f$ → smaller $\delta$ → more surface-focused.

### D. Hall Effect Sensors
- [x] Hall voltage: $V_H = R_H I B / t$, with $R_H = 1/(nq)$.
- [x] Sign of $V_H$ reveals **carrier type** (n- or p-type).
- [x] Types: **switch**, **latching**, **linear**, **3D**.
- [x] On-chip amplifier mandatory (output is only mV).

### E. Optical Encoders
- [x] Incremental → A, B, Z (index). Resolution = $360° / (4N)$ with 4× decoding.
- [x] Absolute → Gray code prevents multi-bit transition errors.
- [x] Linear resolution $x_{res} = p/4$ with interpolation.

### F. Photoelectric Sensors
- [x] Three modes: **through-beam** (longest range), **retro-reflective** (mid range), **diffuse** (short range, target is reflector).
- [x] **Excess gain ≥ 2** for reliable operation in dusty environments.

### G. Ultrasonic Sensors
- [x] Distance: $d = v_{sound} \cdot t_{TOF} / 2$.
- [x] $v_{sound} = 331.4 + 0.6T$ m/s, with $T$ in °C.
- [x] **Blind zone** of 10–30 cm due to transducer ring-down.
- [x] **Temperature compensation** is essential for sub-cm accuracy.

### H. Sensor Selection Golden Rule
- [x] **Any material, close range** → Capacitive.
- [x] **Metals only, robust industrial** → Inductive.
- [x] **Sub-µm on metal, harsh environment** → Eddy current.
- [x] **Magnetic target, low cost** → Hall effect.
- [x] **Long range, opaque target** → Photoelectric through-beam.
- [x] **Liquid level, transparent target** → Ultrasonic.
- [x] **High resolution, retained position** → Absolute optical encoder.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Force
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Force ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition of Force In the context of **Mechatronic Systems**, *force* is defined as any **interaction that, when unopposed, changes the motion of an object**. It is a **vector quantity** — possessing both **magnitude** and **direction** — and is the fundamental cause of acceleration, deformation, or stress within a mechanical subsystem. Mathematically, force is described by **Newton's Second Law of Motion**: $$\vec{F} = m \cdot \vec{a}$$ where: - $\vec{F}$ is the net force vector (in **Newtons (N)**) - $m$ is the **mass** of the body (in **kilograms (kg)**) - $\vec{a}$ is the **acceleration** vector (in **m/s²**) > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** *"Force is the agent which tends to change the state of rest or uniform motion of a body, or tends to deform it. In mechatronics, force is the primary input to the mechanical domain that links actuation (electrical/hydraulic/pneumatic) to motion output."* ### 1.2 SI Unit and Standard Metric The **SI unit of force is the Newton (N)**, defined as the force required to accelerate a mass of **1 kg** at **1 m/s²**. $$1 \text{ N} = 1 \text{ kg} \cdot \text{m/s}^2$$ Other commonly encountered units in engineering practice: - **Kilogram-force (kgf)** ≈ **9.80665 N** - **Pound-force (lbf)** ≈ **4.44822 N** - **Dyne (CGS unit)** = **10⁻⁵ N** > [!NOTE] > **Fundamental vs. Derived Force Concepts in Mechatronics:** > - **Contact forces**: Normal force, frictional force, tension, applied force > - **Body forces (field forces)**: Gravitational force, electromagnetic force > - **Inertial forces**: Centrifugal, Coriolis, Euler forces (critical in rotating mechatronic assemblies) ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Imagine you are pushing a **broken-down car** on a flat road. The harder you push (greater force), the faster it accelerates. If you push it sideways, it doesn't move forward — it just skids. This shows two things: 1. **Force has magnitude** — how hard you push. 2. **Force has direction** — where you push. Now imagine the car is on an **icy slope**. A much smaller push can cause it to slide because **gravity is also pulling it down the slope**. The total (net) force is the **vector sum** of your push and gravity. In a **mechatronic system** (say, a robotic arm lifting a payload), the motor produces an **electromagnetic force** at the joint. This force, combined with **gravity**, **friction**, and **inertial reaction**, determines whether the arm moves, stays still, or vibrates. The art of mechatronic design is computing and controlling this net force. > [!TIP] > **One-liner for exams:** *"Force is a vector push or pull that causes or tries to cause a change in motion or shape of a body."* ### 1.4 Why Force is Central to Mechatronics A mechatronic system integrates mechanical, electronic, and control domains. The **mechanical domain** is essentially about how forces and motions interact. Whether the actuator is a **DC motor** (producing electromagnetic torque/force), a **solenoid** (producing linear electromagnetic force), or a **hydraulic cylinder** (producing pressure-driven force), the result is always a **force** applied to a mechanical structure. | Mechatronic Component | Force Generated By | Typical Magnitude Range | |---|---|---| | DC Servo Motor | Electromagnetic torque → tangential force | 0.1 N to 1000 N | | Solenoid Actuator | Magnetic field on ferrous plunger | 0.05 N to 500 N | | Pneumatic Cylinder | Compressed air pressure × piston area | 10 N to 50,000 N | | Piezoelectric Stack | Applied voltage → crystal deformation | 0.1 N to 10,000 N | | Voice Coil Actuator | Lorentz force on current-carrying coil | 0.01 N to 100 N | ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Newton's Laws of Motion — The Foundation **Law 1 (Inertia):** *A body remains at rest or in uniform motion unless acted upon by an external force.* **Law 2 (Momentum):** *The rate of change of momentum of a body is proportional to the applied force and takes place in the direction of the force.* $$\vec{F} = \frac{d\vec{p}}{dt} = \frac{d(m\vec{v})}{dt}$$ For constant mass: $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ **Law 3 (Action-Reaction):** *For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction.* $$\vec{F}_{AB} = -\vec{F}_{BA}$$ ### 2.2 Resolution of a Force into Components Any 2D force can be split into **Cartesian components** $F_x$ and $F_y$: $$F_x = F \cos\theta$$ $$F_y = F \sin\theta$$ The **resultant magnitude** is: $$F_R = \sqrt{F_x^2 + F_y^2}$$ And its **direction** with respect to the x-axis: $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{F_y}{F_x}\right)$$ For a 3D force, a third component $F_z = F \cos\phi$ is added, often using **direction cosines**: $$F_x = F \cdot \ell, \quad F_y = F \cdot m, \quad F_z = F \cdot n$$ where $\ell^2 + m^2 + n^2 = 1$. ### 2.3 Composition of Forces (Resultant of Concurrent Forces) If multiple forces act on a point, the resultant is the **vector sum**: $$\vec{F}_R = \sum_{i=1}^{n} \vec{F}_i$$ For **two coplanar concurrent forces** of magnitudes $F_1$ and $F_2$ at angle $\alpha$ between them, the **Parallelogram Law** gives: $$F_R = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha}$$ The direction is given by: $$\tan\beta = \frac{F_2 \sin\alpha}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\alpha}$$ ### 2.4 Moment of a Force (Torque) A force produces a **turning effect** about a point, called the **moment** or **torque**: $$M = F \times d$$ where $d$ is the **perpendicular distance** from the line of action of the force to the pivot point. In vector form: $$\vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}$$ ### 2.5 Conditions of Static Equilibrium (Crucial for Linkage Design) For a body to remain in static equilibrium, **two conditions** must be satisfied: 1. **Force equilibrium (translation):** $\sum \vec{F} = 0$ 2. **Moment equilibrium (rotation):** $\sum \vec{M} = 0$ In 2D scalar form: $$\sum F_x = 0, \quad \sum F_y = 0, \quad \sum M_z = 0$$ ### 2.6 Friction — The Ever-Present Opponent Friction is a **contact force parallel to the surfaces** in contact, opposing relative motion. **Static friction** (no motion yet): $$f_s \leq \mu_s N$$ **Kinetic friction** (motion occurring): $$f_k = \mu_k N$$ **Rolling friction** (wheel/roller): $$f_r = \mu_r N$$ where $\mu$ is the **coefficient of friction** and $N$ is the normal reaction. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Angle of Friction ($\phi$):** The angle between the normal reaction and the resultant reaction. > $$\tan\phi = \mu$$ > When a body is on the verge of sliding, the resultant reaction is inclined at $\phi$ to the normal. ### 2.7 Inertial and Centrifugal Forces in Rotating Mechatronic Systems In a rotating reference frame (e.g., a robotic arm, a CNC spindle), an observer experiences fictitious forces: **Centrifugal force** (radial, outward): $$F_c = m \omega^2 r$$ **Coriolis force** (perpendicular to velocity and rotation axis): $$F_{Cor} = 2m (\vec{\omega} \times \vec{v})$$ **Euler force** (due to angular acceleration): $$F_{Euler} = -m \dot{\omega} r$$ ### 2.8 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet) | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | Key Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Newton's Second Law | $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ | F in N, m in kg, a in m/s² | Fundamental of dynamics | | 2 | Weight (Gravitational Force) | $W = mg$ | g ≈ **9.81 m/s²** | Acts vertically downward | | 3 | Resolution of Force | $F_x = F\cos\theta, \; F_y = F\sin\theta$ | θ from reference axis | Use for inclined plane problems | | 4 | Resultant of Two Forces | $F_R^2 = F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1F_2\cos\alpha$ | α is angle between them | Parallelogram law | | 5 | Lami's Theorem | $\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha_1} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\alpha_2} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\alpha_3}$ | Three concurrent coplanar forces | Each α is the angle *opposite* to F | | 6 | Moment of Force | $M = F \times d$ | d = perpendicular distance (m) | Torque about a point | | 7 | Couple Moment | $M = F \times s$ | s = perpendicular distance between forces | Two equal, opposite, parallel forces | | 8 | Equilibrium | $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum M = 0$ | — | Three equations in 2D | | 9 | Static Friction Max | $f_{s,max} = \mu_s N$ | $\mu_s$ dimensionless | At impending motion | | 10 | Kinetic Friction | $f_k = \mu_k N$ | $\mu_k < \mu_s$ | During sliding | | 11 | Angle of Friction | $\tan\phi = \mu$ | φ in degrees/radians | Used in self-locking analysis | | 12 | Centripetal Force | $F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r} = m\omega^2 r$ | v in m/s, ω in rad/s | Inward, toward center | | 13 | Centrifugal Force | $F_{cf} = m\omega^2 r$ | Outward in rotating frame | Pseudo force | | 14 | Coriolis Force | $F_{Cor} = 2m\omega v$ | Acts ⊥ to velocity | Significant in high-speed robotics | | 15 | Work Done | $W = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{d} = Fd\cos\theta$ | W in Joules (J) | Energy transferred | | 16 | Power | $P = \vec{F} \cdot \vec{v}$ | P in Watts (W) | Force × velocity | | 17 | Kinetic Energy | $KE = \frac{1}{2}mv^2$ | J | Energy of motion | | 18 | Impulse | $J = F \cdot t = \Delta p$ | N·s | For impact problems | | 19 | Momentum | $p = mv$ | kg·m/s | Vector quantity | | 20 | Spring Force (Hooke's Law) | $F = kx$ | k = stiffness (N/m), x = displacement (m) | Linear elastic deformation | ### 2.9 Real-World Utility in Engineering - **Robot manipulator design**: Resolving actuator forces at each joint using **free body diagrams** and writing **Newton-Euler equations** for dynamics. - **Vehicle suspension**: Spring, damper, and gravitational forces must balance for ride comfort. - **CNC machine tools**: Cutting forces must be counteracted by the structural stiffness to maintain tolerance. - **MEMS sensors**: Piezoelectric force generation and detection. - **Aerial drones**: Centrifugal and Coriolis effects on rotating propellers dictate balancing requirements. - **Conveyor belts and grippers**: Friction force is the only thing preventing slipping of payloads. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Resultant of Two Coplanar Concurrent Forces (Parallelogram Law) **Given:** Two forces $\vec{F_1}$ and $\vec{F_2}$ acting at a point O, with angle $\alpha$ between them. **To Find:** Magnitude and direction of the resultant $\vec{F_R}$. **Step 1 — Vector representation:** Express both forces as vectors from the common point of application O. **Step 2 — Apply the parallelogram rule:** Complete the parallelogram OACB, where OA represents $\vec{F_1}$ and OB represents $\vec{F_2}$. The diagonal OC represents the resultant $\vec{F_R}$. **Step 3 — Use the law of cosines in triangle ODC** (where D is the foot of the perpendicular from C to OA extended): $$OC^2 = OD^2 + DC^2$$ **Step 4 — Express OD and DC:** $OD = OA + AD = F_1 + F_2 \cos\alpha$ $DC = F_2 \sin\alpha$ **Step 5 — Substitute and square:** $$\begin{aligned} F_R^2 &= (F_1 + F_2 \cos\alpha)^2 + (F_2 \sin\alpha)^2 \\ F_R^2 &= F_1^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha + F_2^2 \cos^2\alpha + F_2^2 \sin^2\alpha \\ F_R^2 &= F_1^2 + F_2^2(\cos^2\alpha + \sin^2\alpha) + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha \\ F_R^2 &= F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha \end{aligned}$$ **Step 6 — Final magnitude:** $$F_R = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha}$$ **Step 7 — Direction:** Let $\beta$ be the angle between $\vec{F_R}$ and $\vec{F_1}$. From triangle ODC: $$\tan\beta = \frac{DC}{OD} = \frac{F_2 \sin\alpha}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\alpha}$$ > [!TIP] > **Quick check:** When $\alpha = 0$ (forces parallel in same direction), $F_R = F_1 + F_2$ ✓. When $\alpha = 180°$ (opposite direction), $F_R = \vert F_1 - F_2 \vert$ ✓. --- ### 3.2 Derivation: Centripetal Force from Kinematics **Given:** A particle of mass $m$ moving in a circle of radius $r$ with uniform angular velocity $\omega$. **Step 1 — Write the position vector** in polar form: $$\vec{r}(t) = r\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + r\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j}$$ **Step 2 — Differentiate to get velocity:** $$\begin{aligned} \vec{v}(t) &= \frac{d\vec{r}}{dt} \\ \vec{v}(t) &= -r\omega\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{i} + r\omega\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{j} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Differentiate again to get acceleration:** $$\begin{aligned} \vec{a}(t) &= \frac{d\vec{v}}{dt} \\ \vec{a}(t) &= -r\omega^2\cos(\omega t)\,\hat{i} - r\omega^2\sin(\omega t)\,\hat{j} \\ \vec{a}(t) &= -\omega^2 \vec{r}(t) \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Interpret:** The acceleration is **inversely directed** to the position vector (i.e., pointing toward the center) with magnitude $a = \omega^2 r$. **Step 5 — Apply Newton's Second Law:** $$\vec{F} = m\vec{a} = -m\omega^2 \vec{r}$$ The magnitude is: $$F_c = m\omega^2 r = \frac{mv^2}{r}$$ > [!NOTE] > The negative sign indicates that the centripetal force always points **inward** (toward the center of rotation), as it must to keep the particle on the curved path. --- ### 3.3 Derivation: Friction Angle and Self-Locking Condition **Given:** A block of weight $W$ resting on an inclined plane at angle $\theta$ to the horizontal. **Step 1 — Free body diagram:** Three forces act on the block: - Weight $W$ (vertically down) - Normal reaction $N$ (perpendicular to plane) - Friction force $f$ (along plane, up the slope, opposing impending motion) **Step 2 — Resolve weight** along and perpendicular to the plane: $$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\theta \quad \text{(along plane, down the slope)}$$ $$W_{\perp} = W \cos\theta \quad \text{(into plane)}$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrium perpendicular to plane:** $$N = W \cos\theta$$ **Step 4 — Equilibrium along plane (just before sliding):** $$f = W \sin\theta$$ **Step 5 — Apply friction law at impending motion:** $$f = \mu_s N \implies W\sin\theta = \mu_s W\cos\theta$$ **Step 6 — Solve for the critical angle:** $$\tan\theta_{crit} = \mu_s = \tan\phi$$ Therefore: $$\theta_{crit} = \phi$$ **Conclusion:** A body will remain stationary on an inclined plane (self-locking) as long as: $$\theta \leq \phi \quad \text{or equivalently} \quad \tan\theta \leq \mu_s$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering application:** This is the basis for **screw jack self-locking** and **wedge brake design** in mechatronic clamping systems. If $\theta > \phi$, the body slides down — the system is *non-self-locking* and requires an external holding force. --- ### 3.4 Worked Numerical Example — Typical KTU Question Style **Problem:** A robotic arm joint experiences three concurrent coplanar forces: - $F_1 = 100$ N acting along the positive x-axis - $F_2 = 150$ N acting at 60° from positive x-axis - $F_3 = 200$ N acting at 135° from positive x-axis Find the **magnitude and direction of the resultant force**. **Solution:** **Step 1 — Resolve each force into x and y components:** $$\begin{aligned} F_{1x} &= 100 \cos 0° = 100 \text{ N} \\ F_{1y} &= 100 \sin 0° = 0 \text{ N} \\ F_{2x} &= 150 \cos 60° = 150 \times 0.5 = 75 \text{ N} \\ F_{2y} &= 150 \sin 60° = 150 \times 0.866 = 129.9 \text{ N} \\ F_{3x} &= 200 \cos 135° = 200 \times (-0.707) = -141.4 \text{ N} \\ F_{3y} &= 200 \sin 135° = 200 \times (0.707) = 141.4 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Sum components:** $$\begin{aligned} F_{Rx} &= 100 + 75 - 141.4 = 33.6 \text{ N} \\ F_{Ry} &= 0 + 129.9 + 141.4 = 271.3 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Magnitude of resultant:** $$F_R = \sqrt{(33.6)^2 + (271.3)^2} = \sqrt{1129 + 73603} = \sqrt{74732}$$ $$F_R \approx 273.4 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Direction:** $$\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\frac{271.3}{33.6}\right) = \tan^{-1}(8.075) \approx 82.9°$$ **Answer:** The resultant force has magnitude **≈ 273.4 N** acting at an angle of **≈ 82.9°** from the positive x-axis. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Matrix — Common Force Problem Types in KTU | Problem Type | Given | Find | Method | |---|---|---|---| | Resolution | Force magnitude + direction | Components $F_x, F_y$ | $F_x = F\cos\theta, \; F_y = F\sin\theta$ | | Resultant | Multiple forces | Single equivalent force | Vector addition / Parallelogram law | | Equilibrium | Forces on a body | Unknown reaction forces | $\sum F_x = 0, \sum F_y = 0, \sum M = 0$ | | Friction Analysis | Inclination, μ | Holding force / Friction force | $f = \mu N$ and force balance | | Rotating mass | m, ω, r | Centripetal force | $F_c = m\omega^2 r$ | | Lami's Theorem | 3 forces in equilibrium | One unknown force | Sine rule on force triangle | | Impact / Impulse | Velocity change, time | Average force | $F = \frac{m\Delta v}{\Delta t}$ | ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Free Body Diagram — Block on Inclined Plane with Friction The following Mermaid block diagram illustrates the conceptual architecture of a **Free Body Diagram (FBD)** for a classic inclined-plane problem with friction, which is a recurring topic in KTU mechanics modules. ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Block of Mass m] --> B[Weight W = mg
Acts Vertically Downward] A --> C[Normal Reaction N
Perpendicular to Plane] A --> D[Friction Force f
Up the Plane, Opposing Impending Slide] A --> E[Applied Force P
Optional, e.g., from Actuator] B --> F[Resolve W into Components] F --> F1[W sin theta : Parallel to Plane] F --> F2[W cos theta : Perpendicular to Plane] F1 --> G[Force Balance Along Plane] F2 --> H[Force Balance Perpendicular to Plane] C --> H D --> G E --> G G --> I[Net force along plane = 0 for equilibrium] H --> J[Net force perpendicular to plane = 0 for equilibrium] I --> K[Solve for unknowns] J --> K K --> L[Friction Condition Check: f less than or equal to mu N] ``` ### 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Force Analysis in a Mechatronic Actuator This diagram shows how **force** flows through a typical **mechatronic actuator chain**, from electrical input to mechanical output. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph INPUT[Electrical Input Domain] V[Voltage V applied to Motor] end subgraph ENERGY_CONV[Energy Conversion Domain] EMF[Electromagnetic Force Generation
F em = B I L in voice coil] T[Torque T = k t times I in DC motor] end subgraph MECH[Mechanical Transmission Domain] G[Gear Train with Ratio n] L[Linkage / Cam / Screw Mechanism] F_OUT[Mechatronic Output Force F out] end subgraph LOADS[External Load Domain] LOAD[Inertial Load m times a] FRIC[Friction Force mu N] GRAV[Gravitational Force mg] end subgraph SENSING[Sensor Feedback Domain] F_S[Force Sensor / Strain Gauge] ENC[Encoder for Position] CTRL[Controller / PID] end V --> EMF V --> T EMF --> F_OUT T --> G G --> L L --> F_OUT F_OUT --> LOAD F_OUT --> FRIC F_OUT --> GRAV LOAD --> F_S FRIC --> F_S F_S --> CTRL ENC --> CTRL CTRL --> V ``` ### 4.3 Decision Flow — Equilibrium Analysis Procedure ```mermaid flowchart TD START[Start: Force Problem Given] --> FBD[Step 1: Draw Free Body Diagram] FBD --> FORCES[Step 2: Identify all forces including reactions, weight, friction, applied] FORCES --> AXES[Step 3: Choose convenient x and y axes] AXES --> RESOLVE[Step 4: Resolve inclined forces into components] RESOLVE --> EQX[Step 5: Apply Sigma Fx equals 0] EQX --> EQY[Step 6: Apply Sigma Fy equals 0] EQY --> EQM[Step 7: Apply Sigma M equals 0 about a chosen point] EQM --> SOLVE[Step 8: Solve the simultaneous equations] SOLVE --> CHECK[Step 9: Check physical reasonableness and units] CHECK --> END[Output: Reaction forces or unknown quantity] ``` ### 4.4 Force Triangle — Lami's Theorem Visualization ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Three Concurrent Coplanar Forces in Equilibrium] --> B[Form a Closed Force Triangle] B --> C[Force F1 with opposite angle alpha 1] B --> D[Force F2 with opposite angle alpha 2] B --> E[Force F3 with opposite angle alpha 3] C --> F[Lami Equation: F1 over sin alpha 1 equals F2 over sin alpha 2 equals F3 over sin alpha 3] D --> F E --> F F --> G[Use to solve for any one unknown force given the other two and geometry] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Exam Tip:** When the Mermaid diagram is complex, students should also practice drawing a **hand-sketched free body diagram** with arrow heads, angle markings, and clearly labeled force vectors. KTU examiners award **2 to 3 marks** specifically for a correct, well-labeled FBD in any mechanics question. ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]** State and explain Newton's three laws of motion. Why are these laws fundamental to mechatronic system analysis? **Model Answer:** **Newton's First Law (Law of Inertia):** Every body continues in its state of rest or of uniform motion in a straight line unless compelled by an external force to change that state. This is the basis of **free body diagrams** — isolating a body and identifying the forces that act on it. **Newton's Second Law:** The rate of change of momentum of a body is directly proportional to the net external force and takes place in the direction of that force. $$\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$$ This is the **workhorse equation** of all mechatronic dynamics — from motor torque calculations to robot arm trajectory tracking. **Newton's Third Law:** For every action, there is an equal and opposite reaction. This explains **actuator reaction forces** transmitted to the supporting structure — a critical design constraint in precision mechatronics. *Significance:* These laws allow the engineer to predict motion, design actuators, calculate structural loads, and write the equations of motion needed for **control system design**. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2023]** Define the **angle of friction** and **angle of repose**. Show that they are equal in magnitude. **Model Answer:** **Angle of Friction (φ):** It is the angle between the normal reaction $N$ and the resultant reaction $R$ when a body is on the verge of sliding. The tangent of this angle equals the coefficient of static friction: $$\tan\phi = \mu_s = \frac{f_s}{N}$$ **Angle of Repose (α):** It is the steepest angle of an inclined plane (with respect to the horizontal) at which a body placed on it remains in equilibrium without sliding. **Proof of equality:** Consider a block on an inclined plane at angle $\alpha$ to the horizontal, on the verge of sliding. The forces are: - Weight $W$ vertically down - Normal $N$ perpendicular to plane - Friction $f$ along the plane Resolving and applying equilibrium: - $N = W\cos\alpha$ - $f = W\sin\alpha$ At impending motion: $f = \mu_s N$, so: $$W\sin\alpha = \mu_s W\cos\alpha \implies \tan\alpha = \mu_s$$ Comparing: $\tan\alpha = \mu_s = \tan\phi$, hence $\alpha = \phi$. > [!WARNING] > **Common Mistake:** Students often write $\alpha = \phi$ without showing the derivation. The examiner expects the **balanced-force argument** explicitly drawn out for full 3 marks. --- ### 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) --- #### QUESTION A (14 Marks) **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2021 / Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Two forces of magnitudes **80 N** and **120 N** act on a point, with an angle of **60°** between them. Find: 1. The magnitude of the resultant force. 2. The direction of the resultant force relative to the 80 N force. 3. The equilibrant force required to maintain equilibrium. **(b) [7 Marks]** A cylindrical roller of mass **500 kg** and radius **0.4 m** is being pulled up an inclined plane (inclination **30°**) by a force **P** acting parallel to the plane. The coefficient of rolling friction is **0.02**. Determine: 1. The force **P** required to just start the roller moving up. 2. The minimum force **P** (and its direction) needed to keep the roller moving at constant speed if μ_rolling is the same. --- **SOLUTION (a):** **Step 1 — Use parallelogram law:** $$F_R = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1 F_2 \cos\alpha}$$ $$\begin{aligned} F_R &= \sqrt{80^2 + 120^2 + 2(80)(120)\cos 60°} \\ F_R &= \sqrt{6400 + 14400 + 19200 \times 0.5} \\ F_R &= \sqrt{6400 + 14400 + 9600} \\ F_R &= \sqrt{30400} \\ F_R &\approx 174.36 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 2 — Direction β (angle with 80 N force):** $$\begin{aligned} \tan\beta &= \frac{F_2 \sin\alpha}{F_1 + F_2 \cos\alpha} \\ \tan\beta &= \frac{120 \times \sin 60°}{80 + 120 \times \cos 60°} \\ \tan\beta &= \frac{120 \times 0.866}{80 + 120 \times 0.5} \\ \tan\beta &= \frac{103.92}{140} \\ \tan\beta &= 0.7423 \\ \beta &\approx 36.58° \end{aligned}$$ **Step 3 — Equilibrant force:** The equilibrant is **equal in magnitude** and **opposite in direction** to the resultant. $$F_E = F_R \approx 174.36 \text{ N}$$ Direction: **180° + 36.58° = 216.58°** from the 80 N force (or equivalently, 180° − 36.58° = 143.42° from the 120 N force). --- **SOLUTION (b):** **Step 1 — Identify forces on the roller:** - Weight $W = mg = 500 \times 9.81 = 4905$ N (downward) - Normal $N$ (perpendicular to plane) - Applied force $P$ (parallel to plane, up the slope) - Rolling friction $f_r = \mu_r N$ (opposing motion, down the slope) **Step 2 — Normal reaction:** $$N = W\cos 30° = 4905 \times 0.866 = 4247.7 \text{ N}$$ **Step 3 — Force P to just start moving up:** For impending motion up the plane: $$P = W\sin 30° + \mu_r N$$ $$\begin{aligned} P &= 4905 \times 0.5 + 0.02 \times 4247.7 \\ P &= 2452.5 + 84.95 \\ P &= 2537.45 \text{ N} \approx 2.54 \text{ kN} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 4 — Minimum force at constant speed:** Constant speed means equilibrium. If P is parallel to the plane: $$P_{min} = W\sin 30° + \mu_r N = 2537.45 \text{ N}$$ But the truly **minimum force** is obtained when P is applied at an angle to the plane such that the component of weight *plus* the friction is minimized. For a horizontal force $P$ applied at angle $\theta$ to the plane, equilibrium gives: Along plane: $P\cos\theta = W\sin 30° + \mu_r (W\cos 30° - P\sin\theta)$ $$P(\cos\theta + \mu_r \sin\theta) = W(\sin 30° + \mu_r \cos 30°)$$ The minimum P occurs when $\frac{d}{d\theta}(\cos\theta + \mu_r \sin\theta) = 0$, giving: $$\tan\theta = \mu_r = 0.02 \implies \theta \approx 1.15°$$ For small μ, this is approximately parallel to the plane. Therefore: $$P_{min} \approx 2537.45 \text{ N} \text{ (along the plane)}$$ --- #### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — INTERNAL CHOICE **[KTU University Exam — June 2022 / Model Paper Pattern]** **(a) [7 Marks]** Define **moment of a force** and **couple**. A spanner of length **25 cm** is used to tighten a nut. If the minimum force required at the end of the spanner to just turn the nut is **50 N**, determine: 1. The moment applied to the nut. 2. The force required at a point **10 cm** from the nut to produce the same moment. 3. Why is a long-handled spanner preferred in mechanical assembly? **(b) [7 Marks]** A mechatronic pick-and-place robot uses a **gripper** to lift a metal block of mass **2 kg**. The coefficient of friction between the gripper pads and the block is **0.35**. Determine: 1. The minimum gripping force $F$ (applied by each finger) needed to prevent slipping under gravity. 2. If the robot accelerates the block upward at **2 m/s²**, what is the new minimum gripping force? 3. Comment on how the choice of gripper pad material affects the design. --- **SOLUTION (a):** **Step 1 — Definitions:** **Moment of a force** about a point is the product of the force magnitude and the perpendicular distance from the point to the line of action of the force. It measures the turning tendency. $$M = F \times d \quad \text{(vector form: } \vec{M} = \vec{r} \times \vec{F}\text{)}$$ **Couple** is a pair of equal, opposite, and parallel forces whose lines of action are different. The net force is zero, but it produces a pure turning effect (pure moment). $$M_{couple} = F \times s$$ where $s$ is the perpendicular distance between the two forces. **Step 2 — Moment applied:** $$M = 50 \text{ N} \times 0.25 \text{ m} = 12.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 3 — Force at 10 cm:** For the same moment: $F_{new} \times 0.10 = 12.5$ $$F_{new} = 125 \text{ N}$$ **Step 4 — Why long-handled spanner?** A longer handle increases the **moment arm** $d$. For the same applied force, the moment (and thus the turning effect) is greater. Equivalently, less force is needed at the end for the same torque. This reduces operator fatigue and risk of injury. It also allows precise tightening without slipping. --- **SOLUTION (b):** **Step 1 — Free body of the gripped block:** - Weight $W = mg = 2 \times 9.81 = 19.62$ N (down) - Two normal forces from gripper fingers: $2N$ (one from each side) - Two friction forces: $2f = 2\mu N$ (acting upward, opposing gravity) **Step 2 — Equilibrium for static gripping:** $$\begin{aligned} 2\mu N &= W \\ 2 \times 0.35 \times N &= 19.62 \\ N &= \frac{19.62}{0.7} = 28.03 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ So the minimum gripping force per finger is **≈ 28.03 N**. **Step 3 — With upward acceleration $a = 2$ m/s²:** Newton's second law: $2\mu N_{new} - W = ma$ $$\begin{aligned} 2 \times 0.35 \times N_{new} &= 19.62 + 2 \times 2 = 23.62 \\ N_{new} &= \frac{23.62}{0.7} = 33.74 \text{ N} \end{aligned}$$ So the minimum gripping force per finger becomes **≈ 33.74 N** — about 20% higher. **Step 4 — Material selection:** Higher $\mu$ (rubber, silicone, textured polymer) allows **lower gripping force** for the same payload, reducing actuator size and energy consumption. However, soft materials wear out faster. Hard materials (metal pads) are durable but require higher force. The choice involves a **trade-off** between grip reliability, actuator force capacity, payload weight, and cycle life — a classic mechatronic design optimization. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting units** in final answers → 1 mark deduction. Always write N, N·m, m/s² etc. > 2. **Confusing static and kinetic friction coefficients** → Read the problem carefully: "just start" means static μ; "in motion" means kinetic μ. > 3. **Sign errors in moment equations** → Choose a consistent sign convention (e.g., counterclockwise positive) and stick to it. > 4. **Not drawing an FBD** → KTU awards **2 marks** specifically for a correct labeled FBD. Skipping it = direct loss. > 5. **Treating forces as scalars** → Always remember to **resolve** forces into components before summing. > 6. **Misapplication of Lami's Theorem** → It is valid only for **three concurrent coplanar forces** in equilibrium, with the angles being the ones *between* the forces (opposite to each in the force triangle). --- ### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER > [!NOTE] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Force** - **Force is a vector** — has both magnitude and direction. Always resolve when summing. - **SI unit of force is the Newton (N)**: $1\,\text{N} = 1\,\text{kg·m/s}^2$. - **Newton's Second Law** $\vec{F} = m\vec{a}$ is the central equation of mechatronic dynamics. - **Resultant of two forces** is given by the **Parallelogram Law**: $F_R = \sqrt{F_1^2 + F_2^2 + 2F_1F_2\cos\alpha}$. - **Equilibrium requires** $\sum F_x = 0$, $\sum F_y = 0$, AND $\sum M = 0$ — all three must hold in 2D. - **Moment of force**: $M = F \times d$ where $d$ is the **perpendicular** distance (not the slant distance). - **Lami's Theorem** applies only to **three concurrent coplanar forces** in equilibrium: $$\frac{F_1}{\sin\alpha_1} = \frac{F_2}{\sin\alpha_2} = \frac{F_3}{\sin\alpha_3}$$ - **Static friction** $f_s \leq \mu_s N$; **kinetic friction** $f_k = \mu_k N$ (constant during sliding); **rolling friction** $f_r = \mu_r N$ (much smaller than sliding friction). - **Angle of friction φ** satisfies $\tan\phi = \mu_s$. **Angle of repose α** equals φ. Self-locking requires $\theta \leq \phi$. - **Centripetal force** is the **real** inward force on a body in circular motion: $F_c = \frac{mv^2}{r} = m\omega^2 r$. - **Centrifugal force** is the **fictitious** outward force observed in a rotating reference frame. - **Coriolis force** $F_{Cor} = 2m\omega v$ acts perpendicular to velocity — important in high-speed robotic arms and rotating machinery. - **Impulse–momentum theorem**: $F \cdot \Delta t = m\Delta v$ — useful for impact and collision problems. - **Work done by a force**: $W = Fd\cos\theta$. **Power** delivered: $P = Fv$. - **Free Body Diagrams (FBDs) are mandatory** in every KTU force problem — they earn easy marks. - **Weight** $W = mg$ with $g = 9.81$ m/s² near Earth's surface — always acts vertically downward through the center of mass. - **Hooke's Law** for springs: $F = kx$, where $k$ is the stiffness (N/m). - In **mechatronic actuators**, the electrical input (V or I) is converted to electromagnetic force, which is then transmitted through mechanical linkages to produce a controlled output force or motion. - **The dot product and cross product** are mathematical essentials: $\vec{F}\cdot\vec{d}$ for work, $\vec{r}\times\vec{F}$ for moment. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final KTU Exam Strategy for Force Questions:** > 1. **Always draw the FBD first** — 2 marks free. > 2. **Resolve all inclined forces** into x and y components before applying equilibrium. > 3. **State the principle you are using** (Newton's law, parallelogram law, Lami's theorem) — examiners award method marks. > 4. **Plug in numerical values with units** at every step. > 5. **End with a boxed answer** stating the magnitude and direction clearly. > 6. **Sanity check**: Does the direction of your answer make physical sense? Is the magnitude reasonable?

topics.title: Fluid Pressure
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Fluid Pressure

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Fluid Pressure** is defined as the normal force exerted by a fluid per unit area on any surface (real or imaginary) with which it is in contact. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$P = \lim_{\Delta A \to 0} \frac{\Delta F}{\Delta A}$$

In the context of mechatronic systems, fluid pressure is the fundamental physical variable that enables the conversion, transmission, and control of mechanical energy in **hydraulic** and **pneumatic** actuation systems. It serves as the primary domain variable in fluid power circuits, analogous to voltage in electrical circuits.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** In Mechatronic Systems, fluid pressure is not merely a fluid statics concept. It is the *coupling variable* between the mechanical input (force/displacement) and the electrical control domain (sensors, microcontrollers, valves). Mastering pressure–force–area relationships is mandatory.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition)

Imagine pushing a thumbtack into a wooden board. Your finger applies a small force over a large contact area, but the **sharp tip** of the thumbtack concentrates that same force over a tiny area. The result? A huge pressure at the tip pierces the wood easily. **Fluid pressure behaves identically** — the same force spread over a smaller piston area creates a much higher pressure, and vice versa.

In a mechatronic hydraulic press, a small electric motor drives a small piston with a large mechanical advantage. The fluid transmits this pressure undiminished to a larger piston, multiplying the output force — this is the heart of every hydraulic robot actuator, injection molding machine, and aircraft flight control system.

## 1.3 Key Classifications of Pressure

| Type | Definition | Reference Point |
|------|------------|-----------------|
| **Absolute Pressure** ($P_{abs}$) | Total pressure measured from a perfect vacuum | 0 Pa (vacuum) |
| **Gauge Pressure** ($P_g$) | Pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure | 1.013 × 10⁵ Pa (atm) |
| **Vacuum Pressure** ($P_v$) | Pressure below atmospheric | Used for negative gauge values |
| **Differential Pressure** ($\Delta P$) | Difference between two arbitrary points | Reference dependent |

The governing relationship is:

$$P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$$

> [!NOTE]
> All KTU numerical problems will state explicitly whether the answer is required in **gauge** or **absolute** terms. By default, unless otherwise specified, the working fluid (oil/air) pressure in mechatronic circuits is treated as **gauge pressure**.

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants and Units

- **Standard Atmospheric Pressure:** $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Density of water (at 4 °C):** $\rho_{water} = 1000 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of standard hydraulic oil:** $\rho_{oil} \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Standard gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$
- **SI Unit of Pressure:** **Pascal (Pa)** = 1 N/m²
- **Common Engineering Units:** **bar**, **psi**, **atm**, **mm Hg (Torr)**, **m H₂O**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Conversion Snapshot (must memorize):**
> - 1 bar = 100 kPa = 10⁵ Pa
> - 1 atm = 101.325 kPa
> - 1 psi = 6.895 kPa
> - 1 mm Hg = 133.32 Pa
> - 1 m H₂O = 9.81 kPa

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Fluid Pressure

### 2.1.1 The Hydrostatic Pressure Law (Stevin's Law)

When a fluid is in static equilibrium, the pressure at any point depends only on the depth of that point below the free surface (and not on the shape of the container — this is the famous **hydrostatic paradox**). The underlying principle is a simple force balance on a vertical column of fluid.

**Physical Logic (Why it works):**
1. A static fluid has no shear stress; only normal stresses (pressure) act.
2. Consider a small vertical fluid column of cross-section $A$ and height $h$ submerged in a liquid of density $\rho$.
3. Three forces act vertically: weight of the column ($W = \rho g h A$) acting downward, pressure at the top ($P_1 \cdot A$) acting downward, pressure at the bottom ($P_2 \cdot A$) acting upward.
4. For equilibrium, $P_2 \cdot A = P_1 \cdot A + \rho g h A$, which simplifies to $P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$.

### 2.1.2 Pascal's Principle (The Heart of Mechatronic Hydraulics)

**Statement:** A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the containing vessel.

**Why it matters in mechatronics:** Pascal's law is the enabling principle behind:
- Hydraulic cylinders and motors
- Hydraulic brakes in vehicles and robotic platforms
- Hydraulic presses and clamping systems
- Hydraulic accumulators (energy storage)
- Pneumatic grippers in pick-and-place robots

The **mechanical advantage** of a hydraulic system comes directly from applying Pascal's law to two pistons of different cross-sectional areas.

### 2.1.3 Bulk Modulus and Compressibility

Although Pascal's law assumes incompressibility, real hydraulic fluids compress slightly under high pressure. The **bulk modulus** $K$ (or its inverse, the **compressibility** $\beta = 1/K$) quantifies this:

$$\beta = -\frac{1}{V}\frac{\Delta V}{\Delta P} \quad \Rightarrow \quad \Delta V = -\beta V \Delta P$$

For hydraulic oils, $K \approx 1.5 - 2.0 \text{ GPa}$, meaning a pressure rise of 100 bar causes a volume reduction of only about 0.5–0.7% — usually negligible in introductory analysis but critical in high-frequency servo-hydraulic systems (where it can excite resonances).

### 2.1.4 Surface Pressure, Hydrostatic Pressure, and Dynamic Pressure

- **Static pressure** ($P_s$): The thermodynamic pressure of the fluid at rest.
- **Hydrostatic pressure** ($\rho g h$): Pressure due to gravity-induced weight of overlying fluid.
- **Dynamic pressure** ($\frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$): Pressure due to fluid motion (Bernoulli's domain — not part of this module but referenced in Module 1 of fluid power systems).
- **Total pressure** (along a streamline, Bernoulli): $P_{total} = P_{static} + \rho g h + \frac{1}{2}\rho v^2$.

## 2.2 Pressure Measurement Principles

In mechatronic systems, pressure is sensed electrically and fed back to the controller. The major sensing principles are:

1. **Strain-Gauge Pressure Sensors:** A diaphragm deflects under pressure; embedded strain gauges change resistance. Output is a millivolt signal proportional to pressure — the **workhorse of industrial mechatronics**.
2. **Piezoelectric Pressure Sensors:** Used for dynamic, high-frequency pressure measurements (e.g., engine cylinder pressure).
3. **Capacitive Pressure Sensors:** Diaphragm displacement changes capacitance.
4. **Bourdon Tube Gauges:** Mechanical-only indication (C-shaped tube straightens under internal pressure).
5. **Manometers (U-tube and inclined):** Liquid column height indicates pressure difference.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical:** All pipes in LaTeX are written as `\vert` to prevent breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Description | SI Units |
|---|---------|-------------|----------|
| 1 | $P = \dfrac{F}{A}$ | Basic pressure definition | Pa |
| 2 | $P_2 = P_1 + \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure (Stevin's law) | Pa |
| 3 | $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$ | Absolute vs gauge relationship | Pa |
| 4 | $\dfrac{F_1}{A_1} = \dfrac{F_2}{A_2}$ | Pascal's law applied to hydraulic press | — |
| 5 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Force amplification in hydraulic system | N |
| 6 | $V_1 = V_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_1 x_1 = A_2 x_2$ | Volume continuity (incompressible fluid) | m³ |
| 7 | $P - P_0 = (\rho_f - \rho_{air}) g h$ | Pressure at fluid column base (above $P_0$) | Pa |
| 8 | $P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$ | U-tube differential manometer | Pa |
| 9 | $\Delta P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure difference between two depths | Pa |
| 10 | $W = \rho g V$ | Weight of fluid column | N |
| 11 | $\beta = \dfrac{1}{K} = -\dfrac{\Delta V/V}{\Delta P}$ | Compressibility of fluid | Pa⁻¹ |
| 12 | $P_{gage} = h \cdot SG$ | Pressure in metres of fluid head, SG = specific gravity | kPa (if h in m, SG dimensionless) |
| 13 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Force from pressure (cylinder thrust) | N |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Engineering Domain | Application of Fluid Pressure |
|--------------------|------------------------------|
| **Robotics** | Hydraulic actuators in heavy-payload industrial robots, pneumatic grippers |
| **Automotive** | Hydraulic brake systems, power steering, suspension (active dampers) |
| **Aerospace** | Aircraft hydraulic flight control systems (typically 210 bar), landing gear actuation |
| **Manufacturing** | Hydraulic presses (forging, stamping), injection moulding clamping |
| **Medical** | Pneumatic ventilators, hydraulic surgical tables |

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure (Stevin's Law)

**Problem Setup:** Derive the pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface of a static liquid of density $\rho$, with the free surface exposed to atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$.

**Geometric Setup:**
- Take a control volume: a vertical cylindrical fluid column of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$.
- Top of column: at free surface, pressure $= P_{atm}$.
- Bottom of column: at depth $h$, pressure $= P$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Diagram of the Fluid Column:**

Three vertical forces act on the cylinder:

1. Pressure force on the **top face** (pushing down): $F_{top} = P_{atm} \cdot A$
2. Pressure force on the **bottom face** (pushing up): $F_{bottom} = P \cdot A$
3. Weight of the fluid in the cylinder (acting down): $W = \rho \cdot V \cdot g = \rho \cdot (A \cdot h) \cdot g$

**Step 2 — Apply Static Equilibrium ($\sum F_y = 0$):**

Taking upward as positive:

$$\sum F_y = F_{bottom} - F_{top} - W = 0$$

$$P \cdot A - P_{atm} \cdot A - \rho g h A = 0$$

**Step 3 — Divide Through by $A$:**

$$P - P_{atm} - \rho g h = 0$$

$$\boxed{P = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

**Step 4 — Gauge Form:**

If the question asks for **gauge pressure** (above atmospheric), we subtract $P_{atm}$:

$$P_{gauge} = P - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Examiner's Insight:** The hydrostatic pressure depends **only on the vertical depth $h$**, not on the shape, size, or slant of the container. This counter-intuitive result is called the **hydrostatic paradox** and is a frequent KTU conceptual question.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hydraulic Press / Force Multiplier

**Problem Setup:** A hydraulic system has two pistons — a small "master" piston of area $A_1$ and a large "slave" piston of area $A_2$. An input force $F_1$ is applied at the small piston. Derive the output force $F_2$ produced at the large piston.

**Step 1 — Apply Pascal's Law:**

Pressure is transmitted undiminished throughout the enclosed fluid, so the pressure at the small piston equals the pressure at the large piston:

$$P_1 = P_2 \;\Rightarrow\; \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2}$$

**Step 2 — Solve for $F_2$:**

$$\boxed{F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}}$$

**Step 3 — Mechanical Advantage (MA):**

$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_2}{F_1} = \frac{A_2}{A_1} = \frac{(\pi/4) D_2^2}{(\pi/4) D_1^2} = \left(\frac{D_2}{D_1}\right)^2$$

**Step 4 — Displacement Trade-off (Energy Conservation):**

For an incompressible fluid, the volume displaced by the small piston equals the volume entering the large piston:

$$V_1 = V_2 \;\Rightarrow\; A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$\boxed{x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}}$$

**Step 5 — Verify Energy Conservation:**

Work input = Work output (assuming ideal, lossless system):

$$F_1 \cdot x_1 = F_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$F_1 \cdot x_1 = \left(F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}\right) \cdot \left(x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2}\right) = F_1 \cdot x_1 \quad \checkmark$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Energy Insight:** The hydraulic system trades **force for distance** (and vice versa). Force is multiplied by the area ratio, but the displacement is divided by the same ratio. The work done is conserved — a direct application of the **work-energy principle**, which examiners love to test.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — U-Tube Differential Manometer

**Problem Setup:** Two pressure points in a pipe flow carry pressures $P_1$ and $P_2$ (with $P_1 > P_2$). They are connected by a U-tube manometer filled with a manometer fluid of density $\rho_m$ (heavier than the working fluid, e.g., mercury for water, or carbon tetrachloride for oil). The height difference of the manometer fluid columns is $h$. Derive the relationship between $P_1 - P_2$ and $h$.

**Step 1 — Set Up the Manometer Geometry:**

Let point $A$ be at the interface between working fluid and manometer fluid on the high-pressure side, and point $B$ be the equivalent interface on the low-pressure side. The manometer fluid is at the bottom of the U-tube.

**Step 2 — Pressure at the Bottom of the U-Tube (from side 1):**

Starting at $P_1$ and going down through the working fluid of density $\rho_f$ by height $h_1$, then across the manometer fluid by height $h_2$ to the lowest point:

$$P_{bottom} = P_1 + \rho_f g h_1 + \rho_m g h_2$$

**Step 3 — Pressure at the Bottom (from side 2):**

Going from $P_2$ down through the working fluid by height $h_1'$ to the manometer surface, then through manometer fluid by $h_2'$ to the bottom:

$$P_{bottom} = P_2 + \rho_f g h_1' + \rho_m g h_2'$$

**Step 4 — Equate the Two Expressions:**

$$P_1 + \rho_f g h_1 + \rho_m g h_2 = P_2 + \rho_f g h_1' + \rho_m g h_2'$$

**Step 5 — Simplify Using the Geometry:**

Note that $h_1 - h_1' = h$ (the differential height of the manometer fluid) and that the manometer fluid paths $h_2, h_2'$ are arranged so that their difference yields the same $h$ but with opposite signs. After cancellation of working-fluid terms, the result is:

$$P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Special Case (gas–mercury manometer):** When the working fluid is a gas ($\rho_f \ll \rho_m$), the term $\rho_f g h$ becomes negligible, and the equation simplifies to the familiar $P_1 - P_2 \approx \rho_m g h$.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A hydraulic cylinder of piston diameter $D = 50 \text{ mm}$ is connected to a pump delivering a gauge pressure of $P = 80 \text{ bar}$. Calculate:
(a) The thrust force produced by the cylinder.
(b) The work done per stroke if the piston travels $L = 0.2 \text{ m}$.
(c) The force required at the pump-side master piston of diameter $d = 20 \text{ mm}$.

**Given Data:**
- $P = 80 \text{ bar} = 80 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 8 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- $D = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $d = 20 \text{ mm} = 0.02 \text{ m}$
- $L = 0.2 \text{ m}$

**Part (a) — Thrust Force:**

$$A_{slave} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.05)^2 = 1.9635 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{slave} = P \cdot A_{slave} = (8 \times 10^6) \times (1.9635 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_{slave} = 15{,}708 \text{ N} \approx 15.71 \text{ kN}}$$

**Part (b) — Work Done per Stroke:**

$$W = F_{slave} \cdot L = 15{,}708 \times 0.2$$

$$\boxed{W = 3{,}141.6 \text{ J} \approx 3.14 \text{ kJ}}$$

**Part (c) — Force at Master Piston:**

$$A_{master} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.02)^2 = 3.1416 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$F_{master} = P \cdot A_{master} = (8 \times 10^6) \times (3.1416 \times 10^{-4})$$

$$\boxed{F_{master} = 2{,}513.3 \text{ N} \approx 2.51 \text{ kN}}$$

**Verification of Force Multiplication:**

$$\text{MA} = \frac{F_{slave}}{F_{master}} = \frac{15{,}708}{2{,}513.3} = 6.25 = \left(\frac{50}{20}\right)^2 = (2.5)^2 = 6.25 \quad \checkmark$$

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — Fluid Pressure in a Mechatronic Hydraulic Loop

The following block diagram illustrates the functional architecture of a mechatronic hydraulic actuation system, showing the role of fluid pressure as the central coupling variable between the mechanical, fluid, and electrical domains.

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[Electrical Input Signal] --> B[Microcontroller ECU]
    B --> C[PWM Driver Stage]
    C --> D[Proportional Solenoid Valve]
    E[Hydraulic Reservoir] --> F[Fixed Displacement Pump]
    F --> G[High Pressure Fluid Rail]
    G --> D
    D --> H[Pressure Transducer PT]
    H --> I[Signal Conditioning Amplifier]
    I --> J[ADC Input of Microcontroller]
    D --> K[Double Acting Hydraulic Cylinder]
    K --> L[Mechanical Load Piston Rod]
    G --> M[Pressure Relief Valve Set 200 bar]
    M --> E
    H --> N[Display HMI Pressure Reading]
    B --> N
    B --> O[Actuation Command to Valve]
```

**Reading the diagram:**
- The **pump** raises fluid to high pressure.
- The **pressure transducer (PT)** measures pressure and converts it to a voltage.
- The **signal conditioner** amplifies and filters the millivolt signal from the strain-gauge bridge.
- The **microcontroller** reads the conditioned pressure via its ADC, displays it on the **HMI**, and uses the value in a closed-loop control law to drive the **proportional valve** — completing the mechatronic feedback loop.

## 4.2 Process Flow — Deriving Hydraulic Press Force

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[Input Force F1 Applied] --> B[Pressure Created P equals F1 divided by A1]
    B --> C[Pascal Transmission Through Fluid]
    C --> D[Pressure Acts on Larger Piston A2]
    D --> E[Output Force F2 equals P times A2]
    E --> F[Mechanical Advantage MA equals A2 over A1]
    F --> G[Displacement Reduced by Same Ratio]
```

## 4.3 Manometer Schematic (Conceptual Block Architecture)

```mermaid
graph TB
    A[High Pressure Point P1 in Pipe] --> B[Left Limb Working Fluid Column]
    B --> C[Left Interface with Manometer Fluid]
    C --> D[U Tube Bottom Mercury Column]
    D --> E[Right Interface with Manometer Fluid]
    E --> F[Right Limb Working Fluid Column]
    F --> G[Low Pressure Point P2 in Pipe]
    H[Differential Height h between Two Interfaces] --> I[Equation Delta P equals rho m minus rho f times g times h]
    I --> J[Solved Pressure Difference]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a block diagram rather than a physical drawing?** Fluid pressure in mechatronic systems is best understood at the system level — as the **coupling variable** between domains. A physical drawing of a U-tube would not capture this mechatronic essence. The block diagram explicitly shows the electrical-mechanical-fluid interaction that is the core of the course.
