# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Unit mix-up trap:** Students frequently compute force in kgf (kilogram-force) instead of Newtons. Always convert mass-related quantities: $1 \text{ kgf} = 9.81 \text{ N}$. Marks are deducted instantly for wrong units.
> 2. **Gauge vs Absolute confusion:** If a problem says "a pressure of 5 bar" without specifying, it is **gauge** by convention. If you add atmospheric pressure and call it gauge, you lose 1 mark.
> 3. **Area calculation errors:** Forgetting the $\frac{\pi}{4}$ factor when going from diameter to area. This is the single most common arithmetic error in KTU papers.
> 4. **Direction of piston force:** Single-acting cylinders have one fluid port; double-acting have two. KTU questions usually specify, but be alert.
> 5. **Not stating assumptions:** Always state *incompressible fluid*, *no friction*, *no leakage* in your solution. Examiners reward explicitly stated assumptions.
> 6. **Forgetting energy conservation check:** In hydraulic press problems, examiners expect a verification that $F_1 x_1 = F_2 x_2$ to demonstrate understanding of the work principle.

---

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model Paper]`
**Define fluid pressure. Differentiate between absolute pressure and gauge pressure with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Definition (1 mark):** Fluid pressure is the normal force exerted per unit area by a fluid on a surface, given by $P = F/A$, measured in Pascals (Pa).
- **Absolute pressure (1 mark):** Pressure measured with respect to a perfect vacuum (zero reference). It is always positive and equals gauge pressure + atmospheric pressure: $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$.
- **Gauge pressure (1 mark):** Pressure measured relative to local atmospheric pressure. It can be positive (above atm) or negative (vacuum). A typical tire pressure gauge reads gauge pressure.

---

### Question A2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**State Pascal's law. Give any one application of Pascal's law in a mechatronic system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Statement (2 marks):** Pascal's law states that a pressure change applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every part of the fluid and to the walls of the container. Mathematically, $P_1 = P_2$ at any two points in the connected fluid.
- **Application (1 mark):** In a mechatronic **hydraulic robotic arm**, a small force applied on a master piston creates a pressure that is transmitted through hydraulic oil to act on a larger slave piston, producing a much larger output force for lifting heavy payloads — the core principle of force multiplication in hydraulic actuation.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Two alternatives are provided below. You must answer EITHER **Question B1 (a) & (b)** OR **Question B2 (a) & (b)**.

---

### Question B1 — Choice (a) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**(a) [7 marks]** Derive an expression for the pressure at a depth $h$ below the free surface of a liquid of density $\rho$. State two assumptions made in the derivation.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic press has a master piston of diameter 30 mm and a slave piston of diameter 150 mm. A force of 200 N is applied on the master piston. Calculate:
(i) The pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid.
(ii) The force exerted by the slave piston.
(iii) The distance moved by the slave piston when the master piston moves down by 40 mm.

---

#### Model Solution for B1(a) — Pressure Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (2 marks):**
1. The fluid is **incompressible** (constant density $\rho$).
2. The fluid is in **static equilibrium** (no acceleration, no shear stress).
3. The free surface is exposed to a uniform atmospheric pressure $P_{atm}$.
4. Temperature is uniform (no density variation with depth).

**Derivation (5 marks):**

Consider a vertical fluid column of cross-section $A$ and height $h$ extending from the free surface (point 1) to a point at depth $h$ (point 2).

**Forces acting on the column:**
- Pressure force on top face (downward): $F_1 = P_1 \cdot A$
- Pressure force on bottom face (upward): $F_2 = P_2 \cdot A$
- Weight of the column (downward): $W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$

**Equilibrium equation ($\sum F_{vertical} = 0$):**

$$P_2 \cdot A - P_1 \cdot A - \rho g h A = 0$$

Dividing by $A$:

$$P_2 - P_1 = \rho g h$$

If $P_1 = P_{atm}$ (gauge-free surface), then:

$$\boxed{P_2 = P_{atm} + \rho g h}$$

The **gauge pressure** at depth $h$ is therefore $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$.

---

#### Model Solution for B1(b) — Hydraulic Press (7 marks)

**Given:**
- $D_1 = 30 \text{ mm} = 0.03 \text{ m}$
- $D_2 = 150 \text{ mm} = 0.15 \text{ m}$
- $F_1 = 200 \text{ N}$
- $x_1 = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$

**Step 1 — Calculate the piston areas (1 mark):**

$$A_1 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_1^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.03)^2 = 7.0686 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_2 = \frac{\pi}{4} D_2^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.15)^2 = 1.7671 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pressure developed (2 marks):**

By Pascal's law, the pressure in the fluid is uniform:

$$P = \frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{200}{7.0686 \times 10^{-4}} = 2.829 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$

$$\boxed{P \approx 282.9 \text{ kPa} \approx 2.83 \text{ bar}}$$

**Step 3 — Output force at slave piston (2 marks):**

$$F_2 = P \cdot A_2 = (2.829 \times 10^5) \times (1.7671 \times 10^{-2})$$

$$\boxed{F_2 \approx 5000 \text{ N} = 5 \text{ kN}}$$

**Cross-check using force ratio:**

$$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1} = 200 \times \left(\frac{0.15}{0.03}\right)^2 = 200 \times 25 = 5000 \text{ N} \quad \checkmark$$

**Step 4 — Displacement of slave piston (2 marks):**

By volume conservation of incompressible fluid:

$$A_1 \cdot x_1 = A_2 \cdot x_2$$

$$x_2 = x_1 \cdot \frac{A_1}{A_2} = 40 \times \frac{(0.03)^2}{(0.15)^2} = 40 \times \frac{1}{25}$$

$$\boxed{x_2 = 1.6 \text{ mm}}$$

**Energy check:** $W_{in} = F_1 x_1 = 200 \times 0.04 = 8 \text{ J}$; $W_{out} = F_2 x_2 = 5000 \times 0.0016 = 8 \text{ J}$ ✓

---

### Question B2 — Choice (b) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model Paper]`
**(a) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the working of a U-tube manometer. Derive the expression for the pressure difference between two points in terms of the manometer fluid heights and densities.

**(b) [7 marks]** A hydraulic cylinder of bore diameter 100 mm is supplied with oil at a gauge pressure of 60 bar. The piston rod has a diameter of 40 mm. Calculate:
(i) The pushing (extension) force.
(ii) The pulling (retraction) force.
(iii) The volumetric displacement of oil in litres when the piston extends by 0.3 m.

(Take density of oil as 870 kg/m³; neglect rod-side area pressure drop.)

---

#### Model Solution for B2(a) — U-tube Manometer (7 marks)

**Working Principle (2 marks):**

A U-tube manometer is a device used to measure the pressure difference between two points in a fluid system. It consists of a U-shaped glass tube containing a *manometer fluid* (heavier than the working fluid, e.g., mercury for water pipes). The two ends of the U-tube are connected to the two pressure points $P_1$ and $P_2$ in the system. The manometer fluid settles at a differential height $h$ that depends on the pressure difference.

**Sketch Description (1 mark):**
- A U-tube with two vertical limbs.
- Left limb connected to high-pressure point $P_1$.
- Right limb connected to low-pressure point $P_2$.
- Mercury (or manometer fluid) at the bottom.
- Working fluid fills the space above the mercury in each limb.

**Derivation (4 marks):**

Let the working fluid have density $\rho_f$ and the manometer fluid have density $\rho_m$, with $\rho_m > \rho_f$. Let $h$ be the difference in heights of the manometer fluid in the two limbs.

Applying hydrostatic pressure balance along the path from point 1 to point 2:

Going from $P_1$ down to the lower mercury surface (depth $h + y$ in the left limb), then up to the higher mercury surface in the right limb (height $y$ in the right limb), then up to $P_2$:

$$P_1 + \rho_f g (h + y) - \rho_m g h - \rho_f g y = P_2$$

Simplifying:

$$P_1 + \rho_f g h + \rho_f g y - \rho_m g h - \rho_f g y = P_2$$

$$P_1 - P_2 = \rho_m g h - \rho_f g h = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h$$

$$\boxed{\Delta P = P_1 - P_2 = (\rho_m - \rho_f) g h}$$

**Special case (gas as working fluid, $\rho_f \ll \rho_m$):** $\Delta P \approx \rho_m g h$.

---

#### Model Solution for B2(b) — Double-Acting Cylinder (7 marks)

**Given:**
- Bore diameter $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.1 \text{ m}$
- Rod diameter $d = 40 \text{ mm} = 0.04 \text{ m}$
- Pressure $P = 60 \text{ bar} = 6 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa}$
- Stroke $L = 0.3 \text{ m}$
- $\rho_{oil} = 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$ (not actually needed for force calculations)

**Step 1 — Calculate the areas (1.5 marks):**

$$A_{bore} = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.1)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$A_{rod} = \frac{\pi}{4} d^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.04)^2 = 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Effective area on the rod side (annulus):**

$$A_{annulus} = A_{bore} - A_{rod} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} - 1.2566 \times 10^{-3} = 6.597 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Pushing (extension) force (2 marks):**

When extending, full bore area receives pressure:

$$F_{push} = P \cdot A_{bore} = (6 \times 10^6) \times (7.854 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_{push} = 47{,}124 \text{ N} \approx 47.12 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 3 — Pulling (retraction) force (2 marks):**

When retracting, oil acts on the annular (rod) side area. With the problem stating to neglect rod-side pressure drop, we assume the same pressure:

$$F_{pull} = P \cdot A_{annulus} = (6 \times 10^6) \times (6.597 \times 10^{-3})$$

$$\boxed{F_{pull} = 39{,}584 \text{ N} \approx 39.58 \text{ kN}}$$

**Step 4 — Volumetric displacement (1.5 marks):**

The volume of oil entering the bore side:

$$V = A_{bore} \cdot L = (7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \times 0.3 = 2.356 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$

Converting to litres (1 m³ = 1000 L):

$$\boxed{V = 2.356 \text{ litres}}$$

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!TIP]
> **Read this list 5 minutes before entering the exam hall.**

- [x] **Pressure = Force per unit Area**, $P = F/A$, SI unit = **Pascal (Pa) = N/m²**.
- [x] **Hydrostatic pressure** at depth $h$ in a static liquid: $P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$ (absolute) or $P_{gauge} = \rho g h$.
- [x] **Pascal's Law:** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every part. Core of every hydraulic system.
- [x] **Hydraulic Press Force Formula:** $F_2 = F_1 \cdot (A_2 / A_1) = F_1 \cdot (D_2 / D_1)^2$.
- [x] **Displacement Trade-off:** $x_2 = x_1 \cdot (A_1 / A_2)$ (inversely proportional to area ratio).
- [x] **Energy is conserved** in an ideal hydraulic system: $F_1 x_1 = F_2 x_2$.
- [x] **Absolute vs Gauge:** $P_{abs} = P_{gauge} + P_{atm}$, where $P_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1 \text{ atm}$.
- [x] **U-tube manometer equation:** $\Delta P = (\rho_m - \rho_f) \cdot g \cdot h$. For gas working fluids, $\Delta P \approx \rho_m g h$.
- [x] **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $1.01325 \text{ bar} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 14.696 \text{ psi} = 760 \text{ mm Hg}$.
- [x] **Conversions to memorize:** 1 bar = 100 kPa; 1 psi = 6.895 kPa; 1 mm Hg = 133.32 Pa.
- [x] **Hydraulic cylinder force formulas:**
  - **Pushing (extension):** $F = P \cdot A_{bore}$
  - **Pulling (retraction):** $F = P \cdot (A_{bore} - A_{rod})$
- [x] **Bulk modulus** of hydraulic oil ≈ 1.5–2.0 GPa → very low compressibility → high stiffness in servo systems.
- [x] **Mechatronic context:** Pressure → Sensor (strain gauge / piezo) → Signal Conditioner → ADC → Microcontroller → Control Law → Actuator Valve.
- [x] **Always state assumptions:** Incompressible fluid, no leakage, no friction, static equilibrium.
- [x] **Always carry units** through every calculation — KTU examiners *will* check.
- [x] **Area calculation trap:** $A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2$, never $A = \pi D^2$ — that single $\frac{1}{4}$ factor can mean the difference between full and zero marks.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquid Flow Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Liquid Flow Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Liquid Flow Sensor:** A mechatronic transducer element that converts the kinematic state of a flowing liquid (volumetric flow rate $Q$ or mass flow rate $\dot{m}$) into a measurable electrical, mechanical, or optical signal proportional to the flow magnitude. In KTU mechatronic nomenclature, it functions as the **primary sensing element** in a closed-loop fluid control subsystem, providing real-time feedback to actuators such as solenoid valves, variable frequency pumps, and PID controllers.

In the context of **Mechatronic Systems**, a liquid flow sensor is not just a stand-alone instrument — it is an integrated sub-system comprising:
- A **primary element** (the physical interaction mechanism, e.g., rotor, orifice, heated wire).
- A **transducer element** (converts the physical response into electrical signal).
- A **signal conditioning unit** (amplification, filtering, linearization).
- An **output interface** (analog 4–20 mA, digital Modbus, pulse output).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Focus:** The course PEEET863 emphasizes the *integration* of the sensor with the mechatronic loop. Examiners expect you to discuss the sensor's role in **sensing → decision → actuation** architecture, not just its standalone operating principle.

## 1.2 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

Think of a liquid flow sensor like a **water-metre installed in your apartment**. When you open a tap, water passes through the metre. Inside, a small **turbine wheel spins** like a pinwheel, and every rotation is counted electronically. The faster the water flows, the faster the wheel spins, and the metre calculates the total volume used.

Now scale this concept to **industrial mechatronics**:
- The **tap** = actuator (pump or valve controlled by a microcontroller).
- The **water metre** = flow sensor.
- The **digital display** = signal conditioning + data acquisition.
- The **billing mechanism** = controller (PLC/MCU) that decides to open/close valves.

In an **automated bottling plant**, a flow sensor ensures exactly 1 litre of milk enters each bottle. The sensor reports flow to a PLC; the PLC stops the conveyor when 1 L is reached. This is mechatronics — a fusion of **mechanics (fluid), electronics (sensor signal), and computing (control logic)**.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Engineering Metrics (must be memorized):**
> - **Volumetric Flow Rate ($Q$):** measured in $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$, $\text{L/min}$, or **GPM (Gallons Per Minute)**. SI unit: $\text{m}^3/\text{s}$.
> - **Mass Flow Rate ($\dot{m}$):** measured in $\text{kg}/\text{s}$.
> - **Fluid Velocity ($v$):** measured in **m/s**.
> - **Reynolds Number ($Re$):** dimensionless — indicates laminar ($Re < 2300$) vs turbulent ($Re > 4000$) flow.
> - **Density of water ($\rho$):** **1000 kg/m³** at 4°C.
> - **Dynamic viscosity of water ($\mu$):** **$1.002 \times 10^{-3}$ Pa·s** at 20°C.
> - **Standard Reference:** **1 atm = 101.325 kPa**, **1 bar = 100 kPa**.

## 1.4 Classification Overview

Liquid flow sensors are broadly classified into two families:

| Category | Energy Extraction Principle | Intrusiveness |
|----------|------------------------------|----------------|
| **Obstruction-type** | Creates a pressure drop (energy loss) | Intrusive (in flow path) |
| **Non-obstruction type** | Measures natural properties (velocity, Doppler shift) | Non-intrusive (clamp-on) |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** Examiners frequently ask students to distinguish between **invasive** (orifice, turbine) and **non-invasive** (ultrasonic, electromagnetic) sensors. Memorize at least three examples of each.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle Classification

Liquid flow sensors can be grouped by their underlying physical transduction principle. Each is analyzed below.

### A. Differential Pressure (Obstruction-Type) Flow Meters

**Operating Principle:** A constriction in the pipe forces the liquid to accelerate, converting **pressure energy → kinetic energy**. The resulting pressure difference ($\Delta P$) is measured and correlated to flow rate using the **Bernoulli equation** and **continuity equation**.

**Types of primary elements:**
1. **Orifice Plate** — a thin flat plate with a concentric bore.
2. **Venturi Tube** — a smoothly converging–diverging nozzle.
3. **Pitot Tube** — measures stagnation + static pressure to get point velocity.
4. **Flow Nozzle** — between orifice and Venturi in terms of pressure loss.

### B. Turbine Flow Meter

**Operating Principle:** A multi-bladed rotor is mounted coaxially in the flow stream. Fluid momentum spins the rotor at a speed proportional to flow velocity. A **magnetic pickup** (variable reluctance) or **Hall-effect sensor** detects blade passage, producing a **frequency output** proportional to $Q$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight:** Turbine flow meters produce a **pulsed frequency output**, making them inherently digital and easy to interface with microcontrollers (pulse-counting input).

### C. Positive Displacement (PD) Flow Meter

**Operating Principle:** Fluid is mechanically trapped in fixed-volume compartments (gears, pistons, lobes, or diaphragms) and carried to the outlet. Each cycle displaces a known volume. **Total flow = (cycles counted) × (volume per cycle).**

Used for high-viscosity fluids, lubrication oils, and custody-transfer applications (e.g., fuel dispensers).

### D. Electromagnetic Flow Meter (Magmeter)

**Operating Principle:** Based on **Faraday's Law of Electromagnetic Induction**. A conductive liquid flowing through a magnetic field (of flux density $B$) generates an EMF perpendicular to both flow and field:

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v$$

where $L$ is the electrode spacing (= pipe diameter), and $v$ is fluid velocity. **Requires electrically conductive fluid** ($\sigma > 5\,\mu\text{S/cm}$); cannot measure hydrocarbons or pure water.

### E. Ultrasonic Flow Meter

**Operating Principle:** Two piezoelectric transducers alternately transmit and receive ultrasonic pulses through the liquid. The transit-time difference (between upstream and downstream paths) is proportional to flow velocity.

**Transit time equation:**

$$\Delta t = t_2 - t_1 = \frac{2 \cdot L \cdot v \cdot \cos\theta}{c^2 - v^2 \cos^2\theta}$$

where $c$ = speed of sound in liquid, $\theta$ = angle of beam to flow direction.

Two subtypes:
- **Transit-time (time-of-flight)** — for clean liquids.
- **Doppler-shift** — for dirty/slurry liquids (reflects off particles/bubbles).

### F. Coriolis Mass Flow Meter

**Operating Principle:** Fluid flows through a U-shaped or curved tube that is vibrated at its natural frequency. The flowing mass induces a **Coriolis force** that twists the tube. The **phase shift** between the inlet and outlet sides of the tube is **directly proportional to mass flow rate**. **Density** is also derivable from the tube's natural frequency.

> [!NOTE]
> **Industry Insight:** Coriolis metres are the gold standard for high-accuracy mass flow measurement (±0.1%) and are widely used in the **Kerala dairy industry** (Milma), pharmaceutical batch processing, and LPG measurement.

### G. Vortex Shedding Flow Meter

**Operating Principle:** A bluff body placed in the flow creates alternating low-pressure vortices (Kármán vortex street). The shedding frequency $f$ is linearly proportional to flow velocity:

$$f = \text{St} \cdot \frac{v}{d}$$

where $\text{St}$ = Strouhal number ($\approx 0.2$ for a circular cylinder), $d$ = bluff body width.

### H. Variable Area Flow Meter (Rotameter)

**Operating Principle:** A float rises inside a tapered vertical tube until the upward drag force of the fluid equals the float's net weight. The **float height** is a direct visual indicator of flow rate. (Primarily a local indicator, but electronic versions exist with magnetic float tracking.)

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The table below is your exam-day revision kit. Memorize all entries with their units and validity conditions.**

| # | Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Variables & Units | Validity / Notes |
|---|-------------|---------------------|--------------------|-----------------|
| 1 | Orifice / Venturi | $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$ | $C_d$: discharge coefficient (–), $A_2$: throat area ($\text{m}^2$), $\Delta P$: pressure drop (Pa), $\rho$: density ($\text{kg/m}^3$), $\beta = D_2/D_1$ | $\beta \le 0.75$ for orifice |
| 2 | Pitot Tube | $v = \sqrt{\dfrac{2(P_{stag} - P_{static})}{\rho}}$ | $P_{stag}$: stagnation pressure (Pa), $P_{static}$: static pressure (Pa) | Point velocity measurement |
| 3 | Turbine | $f = k \cdot Q$ | $f$: pulse frequency (Hz), $k$: K-factor (pulses/m³) | Linear in turbulent regime |
| 4 | Positive Displacement | $V_{total} = N \cdot V_{cycle}$ | $N$: number of cycles (–), $V_{cycle}$: displacement per cycle ($\text{m}^3$) | High accuracy for viscous fluids |
| 5 | Electromagnetic | $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$ | $E$: induced EMF (V), $B$: flux density (T), $L$: electrode spacing (m), $v$: velocity (m/s) | Requires conductive liquid $\sigma \ge 5\,\mu\text{S/cm}$ |
| 6 | Ultrasonic (Transit-time) | $\Delta t \approx \dfrac{2 L v \cos\theta}{c^2}$ | $\Delta t$: time difference (s), $L$: path length (m), $\theta$: beam angle, $c$: sonic velocity (m/s) | Valid when $v \ll c$ |
| 7 | Coriolis | $\dot{m} = \dfrac{K_c \cdot \Delta \phi}{f_v}$ | $\dot{m}$: mass flow (kg/s), $\Delta \phi$: phase shift (rad), $f_v$: tube freq (Hz), $K_c$: constant | Direct mass flow; density from $f_v$ |
| 8 | Vortex | $f = \text{St} \cdot v/d$ | $f$: vortex freq (Hz), $v$: velocity (m/s), $d$: bluff body width (m), $\text{St} \approx 0.2$ | Requires $Re > 10^4$ |
| 9 | Rotameter | $Q = C_d A_a \sqrt{\dfrac{2g V_f (\rho_f - \rho)}{\rho A_f}}$ | $A_a$: annulus area ($\text{m}^2$), $V_f$: float volume, $\rho_f$: float density, $A_f$: float drag area | Equilibrium of forces |
| 10 | Reynolds Number | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | $D$: pipe diameter (m), $\mu$: dynamic viscosity (Pa·s) | Laminar: $Re < 2300$; Turbulent: $Re > 4000$ |

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Differential Pressure** — natural gas, oil & gas custody transfer, HVAC chilled water.
- **Turbine** — aerospace fuel flow, water treatment, chemical dosing.
- **PD** — fuel dispensing (HP, BPCL pumps), lubrication oil in CNC machines.
- **Magmeter** — wastewater, slurries, food & beverage (Kerala coir industry effluents).
- **Ultrasonic** — district cooling, large-pipeline water networks, dam spillways.
- **Coriolis** — pharmaceutical, dairy (Milma), LNG, injection moulding.
- **Vortex** — steam flow in power plants, compressed air in factories.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Full Derivation: Differential Pressure (Orifice) Flow Equation

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU exams. We derive it from **first principles** using the **steady-state incompressible Bernoulli equation** and the **continuity equation**.

### Setup

Consider a horizontal pipe of diameter $D_1$ carrying an incompressible liquid of density $\rho$. A concentric orifice plate creates a throat of diameter $D_2$ where the fluid contracts to a **vena contracta** (minimum cross-section). Let:
- Section 1: upstream of orifice, area $A_1 = \pi D_1^2/4$, pressure $P_1$, velocity $v_1$.
- Section 2: at the vena contracta, area $A_2 = \pi D_2^2/4$, pressure $P_2$, velocity $v_2$.

### Step 1: Continuity Equation (Conservation of Mass)

For an incompressible fluid:

$$A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2$$

Solving for the upstream velocity $v_1$:

$$v_1 = \frac{A_2}{A_1} v_2 = \beta^2 v_2 \quad \text{where} \quad \beta = \frac{D_2}{D_1}$$

### Step 2: Bernoulli's Equation (Mechanical Energy Conservation)

For a horizontal, frictionless, steady flow (ideal):

$$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$$

Rearranging to get the pressure drop:

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho (v_2^2 - v_1^2)$$

### Step 3: Substitute $v_1$ from Step 1

$$P_1 - P_2 = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 \left(1 - \beta^4\right)$$

Let $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$:

$$\Delta P = \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 (1 - \beta^4)$$

### Step 4: Solve for Theoretical Velocity $v_{2,\text{ideal}}$

$$v_{2,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

### Step 5: Apply Discharge Coefficient $C_d$

In reality, viscous effects, turbulence, and vena contracta reduce the actual flow. We introduce the dimensionless **discharge coefficient** $C_d \in [0.6, 0.65]$ for a standard orifice:

$$v_2 = C_d \cdot v_{2,\text{ideal}} = C_d \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

### Step 6: Convert Velocity to Volumetric Flow Rate

$$Q = A_2 v_2 = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Result — must be reproduced exactly in the exam:**
> $$\boxed{Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}}$$

### Step 7: Mass Flow Rate (for completeness)

$$\dot{m} = \rho Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \rho \Delta P}{(1-\beta^4)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Full Derivation: Electromagnetic Flow Meter (Faraday's Law Applied)

A conductive liquid of velocity $v$ moves perpendicular to a uniform magnetic field $\vec{B}$ of flux density $B$ (Tesla). The effective length of the conductor is $L = D$ (the pipe inner diameter between electrodes).

**Faraday's Law in motional form:**

$$\text{EMF} = \oint (\vec{v} \times \vec{B}) \cdot d\vec{l}$$

For a uniform field, all points of the conductor experience the same $\vec{v} \times \vec{B}$:

$$E = B \cdot L \cdot v = B \cdot D \cdot v$$

Solving for velocity:

$$v = \frac{E}{B \cdot D}$$

Volumetric flow rate:

$$Q = A \cdot v = \frac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot \frac{E}{B D} = \frac{\pi D \cdot E}{4 B}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Result:** $E$ is **linearly proportional** to $v$ (and hence to $Q$). This linearity is the chief advantage of the magmeter over orifice metres. The output is **independent of fluid density, viscosity, and turbulence**.

---

## 3.3 Full Derivation: Vortex Shedding Frequency

A bluff body of width $d$ immersed in a uniform flow of velocity $v$ sheds Kármán vortices at a frequency $f$ that depends on the **Strouhal number** $\text{St}$ (a dimensionless constant $\approx 0.2$ for a wide range of $Re$).

By definition:

$$\text{St} = \frac{f \cdot d}{v}$$

Solving for shedding frequency:

$$\boxed{f = \text{St} \cdot \frac{v}{d}}$$

Substituting $v = Q/A$ where $A = \pi D^2/4$:

$$f = \frac{4 \cdot \text{St} \cdot Q}{\pi D^2 d}$$

This linear relation between $f$ and $Q$ is why a **frequency-to-voltage converter** is sufficient to obtain a calibrated analog output.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (For Exam Comparison Questions)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU examiners often pose "Compare and contrast" questions. Use this matrix as your template answer.**

| Property | Orifice | Turbine | Magmeter | Ultrasonic | Coriolis | Vortex |
|----------|---------|---------|----------|------------|----------|--------|
| **Output type** | $\Delta P$ (analog) | Frequency pulses | EMF (mV) | Time / Frequency | Phase shift / Frequency | Frequency pulses |
| **Accuracy** | ±1–2% | ±0.5–1% | ±0.5% | ±1–2% | **±0.1% (best)** | ±1% |
| **Pressure loss** | High | Moderate | **Zero** | **Zero** | Moderate | Low |
| **Intrusive?** | Yes | Yes | No (wetted electrodes) | **No (clamp-on)** | Yes | Yes |
| **Conductive fluid required?** | No | No | **Yes** | No | No | No |
| **Mass flow direct?** | No (volumetric) | No | No | No | **Yes** | No |
| **Cost** | Low | Medium | High | High | **Very high** | Medium |
| **Suitable for dirty fluids?** | Limited | No | Yes | Doppler only | No | No |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mechatronic Block Diagram: Flow Sensor in a Closed-Loop Control System

This diagram illustrates how a liquid flow sensor functions inside an integrated mechatronic control loop — a KTU-favourite question.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Controller MCU or PLC] -->|PWM or analog command| B[Actuator: Pump or Solenoid Valve]
    B -->|Forces liquid| C[Process: Pipe Network with Liquid]
    C -->|Flow induced| D[Liquid Flow Sensor]
    D -->|Analog mV or pulse train| E[Signal Conditioning Amplifier and Filter]
    E -->|Calibrated 4 to 20 mA or 0 to 10 V| F[ADC Input of MCU]
    F -->|Digital flow value| A
    G[Setpoint Reference e.g. 5 L per min] -->|Desired value| A
    A -->|Error driven control signal| B
    H[HMI or Display] <-->|User interface| A
    style A fill:#ffcc66,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style D fill:#66ccff,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style E fill:#99ff99,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
    style C fill:#ff9999,stroke:#333,stroke-width:2px
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- The **Controller (A)** is the brain — typically a microcontroller (Arduino, STM32) or PLC.
- The **Actuator (B)** acts on the physical plant.
- The **Process (C)** is the physical fluid system.
- The **Flow Sensor (D)** is the *primary element* in the feedback path.
- **Signal Conditioning (E)** is critical because most flow sensors output weak or non-standard signals (mV, pulse, differential pressure).
- **ADC (F)** digitizes for the MCU.
- The loop is **closed by feedback**, achieving precise flow regulation via **PID control**.

---

## 4.2 Functional Architecture: Internal Subsystems of a Smart Flow Transmitter

A modern "smart" flow transmitter (e.g., Endress+Hauser, Rosemount) integrates multiple subsystems:

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph SENSING_SUBSYSTEM
        P1[Primary Element: Orifice or Magnetic Coil] --> P2[Transducer: Differential Pressure Sensor or EMF Electrodes]
    end
    subgraph SIGNAL_PATH
        P2 --> P3[Preamplifier: Instrumentation Amplifier]
        P3 --> P4[Analog Filter: Low pass anti aliasing]
        P4 --> P5[ADC: 16 to 24 bit sigma delta]
    end
    subgraph DIGITAL_SUBSYSTEM
        P5 --> P6[Microcontroller: Linearization and Compensation]
        P6 --> P7[Memory: Calibration Constants and Look up Tables]
        P6 --> P8[PID Controller]
        P6 --> P9[Digital Communication: HART or Modbus]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT_INTERFACE
        P8 --> O1[Analog Output: 4 to 20 mA current loop]
        P9 --> O2[Digital Output: RS485 or Ethernet]
        P6 --> O3[Display: LCD showing L per min]
    end
    style P1 fill:#ff9966
    style P6 fill:#ffcc66
    style O1 fill:#66ff99
```

**Subsystem Functions:**
- **Sensing Sub-system:** Converts fluid motion into a raw electrical signal.
- **Signal Path:** Boosts, filters, and digitizes the raw signal.
- **Digital Sub-system:** Performs **linearization** (e.g., square-root extraction for orifice), **temperature compensation** (using a built-in RTD), and **PID control**.
- **Output Interface:** Standard industrial interfaces (4–20 mA, HART, Modbus) for SCADA/PLC integration.

---

## 4.3 Decision Tree: Selecting the Right Liquid Flow Sensor

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0{Is the liquid electrically conductive?}
    S0 -- Yes --> S1{Is non-intrusive installation required?}
    S0 -- No --> S2{Is direct mass flow measurement required?}
    S1 -- Yes --> S3[Use Ultrasonic clamp on type]
    S1 -- No --> S4[Use Electromagnetic flow meter]
    S2 -- Yes --> S5[Use Coriolis mass flow meter]
    S2 -- No --> S6{Is the fluid clean and low viscosity?}
    S6 -- Yes --> S7[Use Turbine flow meter]
    S6 -- No --> S8[Use Positive Displacement meter]
    style S0 fill:#ffe066
    style S4 fill:#66ccff
    style S5 fill:#99ff99
    style S3 fill:#66ff99
    style S7 fill:#ff9966
    style S8 fill:#ff9966
```

**How to use this tree in an exam:** When asked *"Which sensor is best for a given application?"*, follow the decision branches and justify the choice with two physical reasons.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**`[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`**
*With a neat diagram, explain the working of an orifice plate flow meter. State the governing equation for the volumetric flow rate.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Working (2 marks):** An orifice plate is a thin flat disc with a precisely machined concentric circular hole, installed concentrically inside a pipe carrying the liquid. As liquid approaches the plate, the cross-section available for flow suddenly reduces. To conserve mass (continuity equation), the velocity must increase at the throat, which by Bernoulli's principle causes a corresponding **drop in static pressure**. Two pressure tappings (upstream and downstream) measure this pressure difference $\Delta P = P_1 - P_2$. The measured $\Delta P$ is fed to a **differential pressure transducer**, which converts it into a 4–20 mA signal proportional to the square root of flow rate (i.e., proportional to $Q$).

**Governing Equation (1 mark):**
$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2 \Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$
where $C_d$ = discharge coefficient, $A_2$ = throat area, $\rho$ = density, $\beta = D_2/D_1$.

---

### Question 2
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`**
*State Faraday's law of electromagnetic induction and explain how it is applied in electromagnetic flow meters.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Faraday's Law (1 mark):** When a conductor moves through a magnetic field, an EMF is induced across it, given by $E = B \cdot L \cdot v$, where $B$ = magnetic flux density, $L$ = effective conductor length, $v$ = velocity perpendicular to the field.

**Application in Magmeter (2 marks):** The flowing conductive liquid itself acts as the moving conductor. Two diametrically opposed electrodes embedded in the insulated pipe wall measure the induced EMF. The magnetic field is generated by external coils (pulsed DC to prevent polarization). Since $E$ is linearly proportional to velocity $v$, the volumetric flow rate $Q = (\pi D/4B) \cdot E$ is obtained directly. The output is **independent of fluid density, viscosity, temperature, and pressure** — making magmeters ideal for slurries, acids, and wastewater.

---

## PART B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)
> *Internal choice: Answer either Question A or Question B in full.*

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Derive the volumetric flow rate equation for an **orifice meter** starting from Bernoulli's equation and the continuity equation. Clearly state the assumptions made. (7 marks)

**(b)** Compare the performance of **orifice, turbine, and electromagnetic** flow meters under the heads: accuracy, pressure drop, intrusive nature, and fluid-conductivity requirement. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Q.A(a) — Full Derivation (7 marks)

**Assumptions (1 mark):**
1. Steady-state, incompressible flow.
2. Horizontal pipe (no elevation change).
3. Inviscid flow (frictionless) at the ideal level; viscous effects lumped into $C_d$.
4. Uniform velocity profile at sections 1 and 2.
5. No heat transfer.

**Step 1 — Continuity equation (1 mark):** $A_1 v_1 = A_2 v_2 \Rightarrow v_1 = \beta^2 v_2$ with $\beta = D_2/D_1$.

**Step 2 — Bernoulli equation (1 mark):** $P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2$.

**Step 3 — Substituting and solving for $v_{2,\text{ideal}}$ (2 marks):**
$$\Delta P = \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2(1-\beta^4) \Rightarrow v_{2,\text{ideal}} = \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$

**Step 4 — Discharge coefficient correction (1 mark):** $v_{\text{actual}} = C_d \cdot v_{2,\text{ideal}}$.

**Step 5 — Final flow rate (1 mark):** $Q = A_2 v_{\text{actual}} = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\dfrac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$.

### Model Solution to Q.A(b) — Comparison Table (7 marks)

| Head | Orifice | Turbine | Electromagnetic |
|------|---------|---------|------------------|
| **Accuracy** | ±1 to 2% | ±0.5 to 1% | ±0.5% |
| **Pressure drop** | High (significant head loss) | Moderate | **Zero (no obstruction)** |
| **Intrusive?** | Yes (plate inside pipe) | Yes (rotor in flow) | No (only wetted electrodes) |
| **Conductivity requirement** | None | None | **Required ($\sigma \ge 5\,\mu\text{S/cm}$)** |
| **Suitable fluids** | Clean liquids/gases | Clean, low-viscosity | Conductive liquids/slurries |
| **Cost** | Lowest | Medium | High |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning:** Many students write "turbine metre has zero pressure drop." This is **wrong** — the rotor is in the flow path and creates friction. Only magmeters and clamp-on ultrasonics have truly zero pressure drop. Marks are deducted for absolute statements. Always qualify with "negligible" or "approximately zero."

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks)
**`[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024]`**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **Coriolis mass flow meter** with a suitable sketch. Derive the relationship between mass flow rate and the phase shift between the two tube ends. (7 marks)

**(b)** An orifice meter with $D_1 = 0.20\,\text{m}$ and $D_2 = 0.10\,\text{m}$ is installed in a water pipeline ($\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$). The differential pressure recorded is $\Delta P = 10\,\text{kPa}$ and $C_d = 0.62$. Calculate: (i) volumetric flow rate, (ii) flow velocity at the throat, (iii) Reynolds number at the throat, and (iv) comment on the flow regime. (7 marks)

### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — Coriolis Metre (7 marks)

**Construction (2 marks):** A Coriolis metre consists of a **U-shaped (or S-shaped or straight) flow tube** mounted on an electromagnetic driver coil. The driver excites the tube at its natural frequency (typically 80–100 Hz). Two **pickoff sensors** (electromagnetic or optical) are placed symmetrically on either side of the U-bend to measure the **angular displacement** of each leg.

**Working (3 marks):** When fluid flows through the vibrating tube, the **Coriolis force** acts on the fluid mass in opposition to its flow direction on one leg and along the flow direction on the other. Since the tube is rigidly constrained at the supports, this differential force creates a **twisting moment** on the tube, causing the two legs to deflect slightly out of phase. The **phase difference** $\Delta\phi$ between the two pickoff signals is directly proportional to the **mass flow rate** $\dot{m}$.

**Derivation (2 marks):** For a tube of length $L$, mass $M$ vibrating at frequency $\omega$ with mass flow $\dot{m}$ through it, the time taken for the fluid to traverse the U-bend is $\tau = 2L/v$ (where $v$ is fluid velocity). The Coriolis-induced twist produces a phase shift:

$$\Delta \phi = \omega \tau = \omega \cdot \frac{2L}{v} = \frac{2 L \omega A}{\dot{m}} \cdot \rho$$

Solving for mass flow:

$$\dot{m} = \frac{K_c \cdot \rho}{\Delta\phi} \quad \text{where} \quad K_c = 2 L \omega A$$

Equivalently, **mass flow is directly proportional to the phase shift** (calibrated by the manufacturer).

### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — Numerical (7 marks)

**Given:** $D_1 = 0.20\,\text{m}$, $D_2 = 0.10\,\text{m}$, $\Delta P = 10\,\text{kPa} = 10^4\,\text{Pa}$, $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$, $\mu = 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$, $C_d = 0.62$.

**Step 1 — Compute $\beta$ and $A_2$ (1 mark):**
$$\beta = \frac{D_2}{D_1} = \frac{0.10}{0.20} = 0.5$$
$$A_2 = \frac{\pi D_2^2}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.10)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Compute $1 - \beta^4$ (1 mark):**
$$1 - \beta^4 = 1 - (0.5)^4 = 1 - 0.0625 = 0.9375$$

**Step 3 — Volumetric flow rate $Q$ (2 marks):**
$$Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{\frac{2\Delta P}{\rho(1-\beta^4)}}$$
$$Q = (0.62)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{1000 \times 0.9375}}$$
$$Q = (4.869 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{\frac{2 \times 10^4}{937.5}}$$
$$Q = (4.869 \times 10^{-3}) \sqrt{21.333}$$
$$Q = (4.869 \times 10^{-3}) (4.619) = 0.02249\,\text{m}^3/\text{s}$$
$$\boxed{Q \approx 0.0225\,\text{m}^3/\text{s} = 22.5\,\text{L/s}}$$

**Step 4 — Throat velocity $v_2$ (1 mark):**
$$v_2 = \frac{Q}{A_2} = \frac{0.02249}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 2.864\,\text{m/s}$$

**Step 5 — Reynolds number at the throat (1 mark):**
$$Re = \frac{\rho v_2 D_2}{\mu} = \frac{1000 \times 2.864 \times 0.10}{10^{-3}} = 2.864 \times 10^5$$

**Step 6 — Flow regime comment (1 mark):** Since $Re = 2.864 \times 10^5 \gg 4000$, the flow is **fully turbulent**. The orifice meter operates most accurately in this regime. $C_d = 0.62$ is consistent with standard orifice design in turbulent flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Warning (Numerical):**
> 1. **Forgetting $\beta^4$** instead of $\beta^2$ — common mistake. The factor is $(1-\beta^4)$, not $(1-\beta^2)$. Both come from squaring the velocity ratio $v_1/v_2 = \beta^2$, then substituting into Bernoulli.
> 2. **Unit conversion error:** $\Delta P$ must be in Pa, not kPa. Convert $10\,\text{kPa} \to 10^4\,\text{Pa}$ before substituting.
> 3. **Forgetting $C_d$** entirely — students sometimes derive the ideal equation and stop. The examiner awards 1 mark specifically for introducing and applying $C_d$.

---

## TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Bullet Checklist — Read this 30 minutes before the exam.**

- **Orifice plate** — $Q = C_d A_2 \sqrt{2\Delta P/[\rho(1-\beta^4)]}$ — most derivable, most asked.
- **Bernoulli + Continuity** is the universal starting point for *all* obstruction-type metres.
- **Discharge coefficient $C_d \in [0.6, 0.65]$** for a standard orifice; 0.97 for a Venturi.
- **$\beta = D_2/D_1 \le 0.75$** for orifices — beyond this, the flow separates excessively.
- **Magmeter** — Faraday's law $E = B L v$ — requires **conductive** fluid, **non-intrusive** (only wetted electrodes), **zero pressure drop**.
- **Ultrasonic** — uses **transit-time difference** or **Doppler shift**; truly clamp-on, but accuracy depends on sound speed in fluid.
- **Coriolis** — **direct mass flow** measurement via **phase shift**; also gives **density** from natural frequency; **most accurate (±0.1%)** but **most expensive**.
- **Vortex** — $f = \text{St} \cdot v/d$, $\text{St} \approx 0.2$ for circular cylinder; needs $Re > 10^4$ for stable shedding.
- **Turbine** — output is a **pulse train**; K-factor relates frequency to volume.
- **Positive Displacement** — fixed-volume counting; ideal for **viscous fluids** and **custody transfer** (fuel pumps).
- **Rotameter** — variable area, vertical, local indication; float position = flow rate.
- **Reynolds number $Re = \rho v D / \mu$** — always check flow regime; $Re < 2300$ laminar, $Re > 4000$ turbulent.
- **Standard water properties:** $\rho = 1000\,\text{kg/m}^3$ (at 4°C), $\mu = 1.002 \times 10^{-3}\,\text{Pa·s}$ (at 20°C).
- **Mechatronic integration:** Sensor → Signal Conditioner → ADC → MCU/PLC → Actuator (Pump/Valve) → Process. Always draw this loop in descriptive answers.
- **Choose the sensor using the decision tree:** conductive? → magmeter; non-conductive clean? → turbine or vortex; mass flow? → Coriolis; non-intrusive? → ultrasonic clamp-on; cheap? → orifice.
- **High-accuracy industries:** dairy (Milma) → Coriolis; wastewater → magmeter; oil & gas → orifice + turbine; steam → vortex.

> [!NOTE]
> **Final KTU Board Tip:** The 14-mark questions in Module 1 always combine a **derivation** (7 marks) with a **comparison or numerical** (7 marks). Practice this two-part structure. Always write the assumptions for derivations, always state units in numericals, and always finish with a brief physical interpretation of the final number.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Temperature Sensors
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Temperature Sensors

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Temperature Sensor** is a transduction device that converts a thermal input quantity (temperature) into a measurable electrical output signal (voltage, current, or resistance change). In the KTU 2024 Mechatronic Systems framework, temperature sensors are classified as **primary sensing elements** in the measurement subsystem of a mechatronic product, forming the first stage of the signal conditioning and control chain.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Definition Highlight:** A temperature sensor is defined as a *physically-based transducer* that exploits a well-defined, repeatable relationship between a material's physical/electrical property and its thermodynamic temperature, governed by the laws of blackbody radiation, Seebeck effect, and temperature-dependent resistivity.

The fundamental operational principle is governed by the **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics**, which establishes that two bodies in thermal equilibrium share a single scalar quantity — temperature $T$, typically expressed in **Kelvin (K)** or derived scales such as **Celsius (°C)** and **Fahrenheit (°F)**.

The standard reference conversion relationships are:

$$
T(\text{K}) = T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 273.15
$$

$$
T(^{\circ}\text{F}) = \frac{9}{5}T(^{\circ}\text{C}) + 32
$$

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of your **human skin** as nature's most elegant temperature sensor. When you touch a hot cup of coffee, nerve endings in your skin convert the heat energy into electrical nerve impulses that your brain interprets as "hot." A mechatronic temperature sensor operates on the **exact same principle** — it converts thermal energy into an electrical signal that a microcontroller (the "brain" of the mechatronic system) can process.

Consider this everyday analogy:

| Human Body Part | Mechatronic Equivalent | Underlying Physics |
|-----------------|------------------------|---------------------|
| Skin nerve endings | RTD / Thermistor sensing element | Resistance vs. Temperature |
| Pain from hot/cold | Output voltage/current signal | Electrical transduction |
| Brain processing | Microcontroller / ADC | Signal interpretation |
| Reflex withdrawal | Actuator / Control valve | Feedback response |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight for KTU Students:** In a mechatronic system, temperature sensors are rarely used in isolation. They form the "senses" that feed data to the controller, which then commands actuators to maintain, regulate, or react to thermal conditions. This is the classic **Sense → Think → Act** paradigm of mechatronics.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

The following fundamental physical constants and standard metrics are critical for temperature sensor analysis:

- **Stefan–Boltzmann Constant:** $\sigma = 5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$
- **Absolute Zero:** $0 \, \text{K} = -273.15^{\circ}\text{C} = -459.67^{\circ}\text{F}$
- **Triple Point of Water:** $273.16 \, \text{K} = 0.01^{\circ}\text{C}$
- **ITS-90 (International Temperature Scale of 1990):** The current international standard defining temperature measurement procedures.
- **Normal Body Temperature (Reference):** $36.8^{\circ}\text{C} \approx 310 \, \text{K}$

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** KTU examiners often use the **ITS-90 scale** as the reference for fixed-point calibration questions. Always cite the standard scale when answering definition-type questions.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Temperature Sensors

Temperature sensors are broadly classified into two categories based on their contact with the measurement medium:

### A. Contact-Type Sensors (Direct Measurement)
These sensors physically touch the object and reach thermal equilibrium.

1. **Thermocouples (TC)** — Based on the **Seebeck Effect**
2. **Resistance Temperature Detectors (RTD)** — Based on **Temperature-Dependent Resistivity**
3. **Thermistors (NTC/PTC)** — Highly sensitive semiconductor-based resistors
4. **Integrated Circuit (IC) Sensors** — Solid-state analog/digital output
5. **Bimetallic Strips** — Mechanical deflection-based indicators

### B. Non-Contact Type Sensors (Radiation-Based)
These sensors measure emitted thermal radiation without physical contact.

1. **Pyrometers** — Optical detection of emitted radiation
2. **Infrared (IR) Thermometers / Thermal Cameras** — Bolometer or photon detector arrays
3. **Fiber-Optic Temperature Sensors** — Used in harsh EMI environments

## 2.2 Working Principles and Governing Physics

### 2.2.1 Thermocouple (Seebeck Effect)

When two dissimilar metals are joined at a junction and subjected to a temperature difference, an electromotive force (EMF) is generated. This is the **Seebeck Effect**, discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in **1821**.

The generated EMF is approximately given by:

$$
E = S(T) \cdot \Delta T
$$

Where:
- $E$ = Generated thermoelectric EMF in **Volts (V)**
- $S(T)$ = Seebeck coefficient in **μV/°C** (material-dependent, typically $5$ to $60 \, \mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $\Delta T = T_{\text{hot}} - T_{\text{cold}}$ = Temperature difference between hot and cold (reference) junctions

> [!NOTE]
> **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal into a thermocouple circuit does not alter the net EMF, provided both junctions formed with the third metal are at the **same temperature**. This is critical for understanding cold-junction compensation (CJC) in practical circuits.

### 2.2.2 Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD)

An RTD exploits the linear, predictable increase in electrical resistance of a pure metal (typically **Platinum**) with temperature. The governing relation is the **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation**:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 + C \cdot (T - 100) \cdot T^3 \right]
$$

For $T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$, the cubic term vanishes ($C = 0$):

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right]
$$

Where:
- $R(T)$ = Resistance at temperature $T$
- $R_0$ = Resistance at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ (commonly $100 \, \Omega$ for PT100)
- $A$, $B$, $C$ = Callendar-Van Dusen coefficients
  - For Platinum: $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$
  - $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-2}$
  - $C = -4.183 \times 10^{-12} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-4}$

The temperature coefficient of resistance (TCR) is defined as:

$$
\alpha = \frac{R_{100} - R_0}{100 \cdot R_0}
$$

For the standard PT100, $\alpha = 0.00385 \, \Omega / \Omega / ^{\circ}\text{C}$ (European standard).

### 2.2.3 Thermistor (NTC Type)

A thermistor is a semiconductor device whose resistance varies **non-linearly** with temperature. The resistance-temperature relationship follows the **B-H Equation** (Steinhart-Hart equation is the most accurate form):

$$
\frac{1}{T} = a + b \cdot \ln(R) + c \cdot (\ln(R))^3
$$

A simplified exponential form often used is:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}
$$

Where:
- $R(T)$ = Resistance at absolute temperature $T$ (Kelvin)
- $R_0$ = Resistance at reference temperature $T_0$ (typically $298.15 \, \text{K} = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$)
- $\beta$ = Material constant (typically $3000$ to $5000 \, \text{K}$)
- $T$, $T_0$ in Kelvin

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Pitfall:** Thermistors are **highly non-linear**. For KTU numerical problems, always convert Celsius to Kelvin before applying the exponential equation, or the result will be incorrect.

### 2.2.4 Non-Contact Sensors — Stefan-Boltzmann Law

For radiation-based sensors, the total power radiated per unit area of a blackbody is:

$$
P = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4
$$

Where:
- $P$ = Radiated power in **Watts (W)**
- $\varepsilon$ = Emissivity ($0 \leq \varepsilon \leq 1$, $\varepsilon = 1$ for ideal blackbody)
- $\sigma$ = Stefan-Boltzmann constant ($5.670 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$)
- $A$ = Surface area in $\text{m}^2$
- $T$ = Absolute temperature in **K**

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Sensor Type | Governing Equation | Key Variables | Typical Range | Sensitivity |
|---|------------|-------------------|---------------|---------------|-------------|
| 1 | Thermocouple | $E = S \cdot \Delta T$ | $S$ (Seebeck coeff.) | $-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $2300^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $5$–$60 \, \mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 2 | RTD (PT100) | $R(T) = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ | $R_0$, $A$, $B$ | $-200^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $850^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $0.385 \, \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 3 | Thermistor (NTC) | $R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | $R_0$, $\beta$ | $-55^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $-2\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $-6\%/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 4 | IC Sensor (LM35) | $V_{\text{out}} = 10 \, \text{mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $V_{\text{out}}$ | $-55^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $150^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $10 \, \text{mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| 5 | Pyrometer (Radiation) | $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ | $\varepsilon$, $A$ | $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $3000^{\circ}\text{C}$ | Non-linear $T^4$ |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

Temperature sensors are ubiquitous across engineering domains:

- **Automotive:** Engine coolant monitoring, exhaust gas temperature (EGT), cabin climate control.
- **Industrial Process Control:** Furnace temperature regulation, chemical reactor monitoring, semiconductor fabrication ovens.
- **Medical Devices:** Patient monitoring (core body temp), MRI-safe fiber-optic probes, infant incubators.
- **Aerospace:** Cryogenic fuel tank monitoring, turbine inlet temperature (TIT) measurement.
- **Consumer Electronics:** CPU/GPU thermal management, smartphone battery protection, smart thermostats.
- **Food & Pharmaceutical:** Cold chain logistics, autoclave sterilization validation, HACCP compliance.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Practical Application:** When designing a mechatronic temperature monitoring system, the choice of sensor depends on four key parameters: **(1) Temperature range, (2) Required accuracy, (3) Response time, and (4) Environmental conditions (EMI, corrosion, vibration).**
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Linear Approximation of RTD Resistance

We start from the first-principle physics that resistance of a metal conductor varies linearly with temperature over a limited range. For an ideal conductor, the resistance is given by:

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

Where $\rho$ is the resistivity. As temperature changes, $\rho$ changes according to:

$$
\rho(T) = \rho_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]
$$

**Step 1:** Substitute this temperature-dependent resistivity into the resistance equation:

$$
R(T) = \rho(T) \cdot \frac{L}{A} = \rho_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right] \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

**Step 2:** Recognize that $R_0 = \rho_0 \cdot \frac{L}{A}$ is the resistance at reference temperature $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha (T - T_0) \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Set $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ (the ice point), so $T - T_0 = T$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + \alpha \cdot T \right]
$$

**Step 4:** To account for non-linearity at higher temperatures, introduce a second-order correction term $B \cdot T^2$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right]
$$

This is the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation** for $T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$, used in PT100 and PT1000 RTDs.

## 3.2 Analytical Worked Example: PT100 RTD Temperature Calculation

**Given:** A PT100 RTD has a measured resistance of $R(T) = 138.50 \, \Omega$ at an unknown temperature $T$ (in °C). Standard PT100 parameters: $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$, $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$, $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-2}$.

**Find:** Temperature $T$ in °C.

**Step 1:** Set up the Callendar-Van Dusen equation for $T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \left[ 1 + A \cdot T + B \cdot T^2 \right]
$$

**Step 2:** Substitute the known values:

$$
138.50 = 100 \left[ 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot T + (-5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot T^2 \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Divide both sides by 100:

$$
1.385 = 1 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot T - (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot T^2
$$

**Step 4:** Subtract 1 from both sides:

$$
0.385 = (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot T - (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot T^2
$$

**Step 5:** Rearrange into standard quadratic form $aT^2 + bT + c = 0$:

$$
-(5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot T^2 + (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot T - 0.385 = 0
$$

Multiply by $-1$:

$$
(5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \cdot T^2 - (3.9083 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot T + 0.385 = 0
$$

**Step 6:** Identify coefficients:
- $a = 5.775 \times 10^{-7}$
- $b = -3.9083 \times 10^{-3}$
- $c = 0.385$

**Step 7:** Apply the quadratic formula:

$$
T = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}
$$

**Step 8:** Compute the discriminant:

$$
b^2 = (-3.9083 \times 10^{-3})^2 = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5}
$$

$$
4ac = 4 \times (5.775 \times 10^{-7}) \times 0.385 = 8.8935 \times 10^{-7}
$$

$$
\Delta = b^2 - 4ac = 1.5275 \times 10^{-5} - 8.8935 \times 10^{-7} = 1.4386 \times 10^{-5}
$$

**Step 9:** Take the positive square root (physical temperature is positive):

$$
\sqrt{\Delta} = \sqrt{1.4386 \times 10^{-5}} = 3.793 \times 10^{-3}
$$

**Step 10:** Solve for $T$:

$$
T = \frac{3.9083 \times 10^{-3} + 3.793 \times 10^{-3}}{2 \times 5.775 \times 10^{-7}} = \frac{7.7013 \times 10^{-3}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 99.84^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

**Step 11:** Verify by taking the negative root to check validity:

$$
T = \frac{3.9083 \times 10^{-3} - 3.793 \times 10^{-3}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} = \frac{1.153 \times 10^{-4}}{1.155 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 99.83^{\circ}\text{C}
$$

Both roots converge, confirming **$T \approx 100^{\circ}\text{C}$**, which is physically consistent for a PT100 RTD.

> [!NOTE]
> **Verification Insight:** The PT100 RTD is named "PT100" because it reads exactly $100 \, \Omega$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$. At $100^{\circ}\text{C}$, the ideal linear approximation gives $R = 100(1 + 0.00385 \times 100) = 138.5 \, \Omega$, which matches our input — confirming the solution's validity.

## 3.3 Analytical Worked Example: Thermistor Resistance Calculation

**Given:** An NTC thermistor has $R_0 = 10 \, \text{k}\Omega$ at $T_0 = 25^{\circ}\text{C}$ and material constant $\beta = 3892 \, \text{K}$. Find the resistance at $T = 100^{\circ}\text{C}$.

**Step 1:** Convert all temperatures to Kelvin:

$$
T_0 = 25 + 273.15 = 298.15 \, \text{K}
$$

$$
T = 100 + 273.15 = 373.15 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the exponential thermistor equation:

$$
R(T) = R_0 \cdot e^{\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right)}
$$

**Step 3:** Compute the inverse temperatures:

$$
\frac{1}{T} = \frac{1}{373.15} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

$$
\frac{1}{T_0} = \frac{1}{298.15} = 3.354 \times 10^{-3} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 4:** Compute the difference:

$$
\frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} = 2.680 \times 10^{-3} - 3.354 \times 10^{-3} = -6.740 \times 10^{-4} \, \text{K}^{-1}
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply by $\beta$:

$$
\beta \left( \frac{1}{T} - \frac{1}{T_0} \right) = 3892 \times (-6.740 \times 10^{-4}) = -2.623
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the exponential:

$$
e^{-2.623} = 0.0726
$$

**Step 7:** Calculate final resistance:

$$
R(100^{\circ}\text{C}) = 10{,}000 \times 0.0726 = 726 \, \Omega
$$

This demonstrates the typical **NTC behavior**: resistance drops by ~93% as temperature increases from 25°C to 100°C.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Sensor Selection Trade-offs

| Parameter | Thermocouple | RTD (PT100) | Thermistor (NTC) | IC Sensor (LM35) |
|-----------|--------------|-------------|------------------|------------------|
| **Range (°C)** | $-200$ to $2300$ | $-200$ to $850$ | $-55$ to $150$ | $-55$ to $150$ |
| **Accuracy** | $\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 0.5^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.1$ to $\pm 1^{\circ}\text{C}$ | $\pm 0.5$ to $\pm 2^{\circ}\text{C}$ |
| **Sensitivity** | Low ($10$–$60 \, \mu\text{V}/^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Medium ($0.385 \, \Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$) | Very High ($-3$ to $-6 \%/^{\circ}\text{C}$) | High ($10 \, \text{mV}/^{\circ}\text{C}$) |
| **Linearity** | Moderate (over wide range) | Excellent (with calibration) | Poor (highly non-linear) | Excellent |
| **Response Time** | Fast ($0.1$ to $10 \, \text{s}$) | Moderate ($1$ to $50 \, \text{s}$) | Fast ($0.1$ to $5 \, \text{s}$) | Fast ($1$ to $10 \, \text{s}$) |
| **Cost** | Low | High | Low | Very Low |
| **Self-Heating** | None | Yes (use low excitation current) | Yes (minimize current) | Minimal |
| **Best For** | Extreme temps, ovens | Precision lab work | Narrow range, high sensitivity | Embedded systems |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Temperature Sensor Classification Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[Temperature Sensors]:::root
    A --> B[Contact Type]:::branch
    A --> C[Non Contact Type]:::branch

    B --> D[Thermocouple]:::sensor
    B --> E[RTD PT100 PT1000]:::sensor
    B --> F[Thermistor NTC PTC]:::sensor
    B --> G[IC Sensor LM35 LM335]:::sensor
```

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: Define the Seebeck Effect. How is it utilized in a thermocouple for temperature measurement?**

**Model Answer:**

The **Seebeck Effect** is the phenomenon whereby a voltage (thermoelectric EMF) is generated at the junction of two dissimilar conductors when the junction is maintained at a temperature different from the reference (cold) end. It was discovered by Thomas Johann Seebeck in 1821.

In a **thermocouple**, two dissimilar metal wires (e.g., Chromel and Alumel for a Type K TC) are joined at one end, forming the **hot junction** (measuring junction) exposed to the unknown temperature $T_{\text{hot}}$. The other ends are connected to the measuring instrument at the **cold junction** reference temperature $T_{\text{cold}}$. The generated EMF is:

$$
E = \int_{T_{\text{cold}}}^{T_{\text{hot}}} [S_B(T) - S_A(T)] \, dT
$$

For a small temperature range, this simplifies to $E = S \cdot \Delta T$, where $S$ is the relative Seebeck coefficient. The measured EMF is then converted to temperature using standardized **thermocouple reference tables** (ITS-90).

### Question 2: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

**Q: Differentiate between NTC and PTC thermistors. State one application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | NTC Thermistor | PTC Thermistor |
|---------|----------------|----------------|
| Full Form | Negative Temperature Coefficient | Positive Temperature Coefficient |
| Resistance vs. Temperature | Resistance **decreases** as temperature rises | Resistance **increases** as temperature rises |
| Material | Metal oxides (Mn, Ni, Co oxides sintered) | Doped Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃) |
| Behavior | Exponential decrease | Sharp increase at Curie temperature |
| Governing Equation | $R(T) = R_0 e^{\beta(1/T - 1/T_0)}$ | Non-linear with switching behavior |
| Application | Inrush current limiting in power supplies, precision temperature measurement in medical equipment | Overcurrent protection, motor starting, self-regulating heating elements |

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

**Q: (a)** Explain the construction and working principle of a Resistance Temperature Detector (RTD) with a neat diagram. Derive the Callendar-Van Dusen equation for RTD. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A PT100 RTD is used in a Wheatstone bridge circuit for temperature measurement. The bridge is excited by a 5V DC source. All four arms have $100 \, \Omega$ resistance at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$. One arm contains the PT100 sensor. Calculate the bridge output voltage when the temperature is $50^{\circ}\text{C}$. Assume the temperature coefficient $\alpha = 0.00385 \, \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ and neglect the $T^2$ term. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Construction of RTD:**
An RTD consists of a pure metal wire (typically Platinum, sometimes Nickel or Copper) wound in a helical coil around a ceramic or glass mandrel. The coil is protected by a stainless steel sheath filled with ceramic powder for vibration damping and thermal conductivity. Lead wires (3-wire or 4-wire configuration) connect the RTD to the measurement circuit.

**Working Principle:**
The RTD operates on the principle that the electrical resistance of a pure metal increases linearly with temperature due to increased lattice vibration (phonon scattering) impeding electron flow.

**Derivation:**

Starting from the definition of resistance:

$$
R = \rho \cdot \frac{L}{A}
$$

The resistivity varies with temperature as:

$$
\rho(T) = \rho_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]
$$

Therefore:

$$
R(T) = R_0[1 + \alpha(T - T_0)]
$$

For $T_0 = 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ and including a quadratic non-linearity correction:

$$
R(T) = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]
$$

This is the **Callendar-Van Dusen equation**, where $A$ and $B$ are empirically determined constants. For Platinum:
- $A = 3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$
- $B = -5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-2}$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Calculate RTD resistance at $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ using the linear approximation:

$$
R_{50} = R_0(1 + \alpha \cdot T) = 100(1 + 0.00385 \times 50)
$$

$$
R_{50} = 100(1 + 0.1925) = 100 \times 1.1925 = 119.25 \, \Omega
$$

**Step 2:** Apply Wheatstone bridge analysis.

A Wheatstone bridge with excitation $V_{\text{ex}} = 5V$ has four arms: $R_1$, $R_2$, $R_3$, $R_4$. The output voltage is:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = V_{\text{ex}} \left[ \frac{R_1}{R_1 + R_2} - \frac{R_4}{R_3 + R_4} \right]
$$

**Step 3:** Configure the bridge. Let:
- $R_1 = R_2 = R_3 = 100 \, \Omega$ (fixed arms)
- $R_4 = R_{\text{RTD}} = 119.25 \, \Omega$ (sensor arm)

**Step 4:** Substitute into the bridge equation:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 5 \left[ \frac{100}{100 + 100} - \frac{119.25}{100 + 119.25} \right]
$$

**Step 5:** Compute each term:

$$
\frac{100}{200} = 0.500
$$

$$
\frac{119.25}{219.25} = 0.5439
$$

**Step 6:** Compute the difference and multiply by $V_{\text{ex}}$:

$$
V_{\text{out}} = 5 \times (0.500 - 0.5439) = 5 \times (-0.0439) = -0.2195 \, \text{V}
$$

**Result:** $V_{\text{out}} \approx -219.5 \, \text{mV}$

The negative sign indicates the polarity of the differential output, which is then amplified by an instrumentation amplifier (e.g., INA128 with gain of 10) to produce a usable $0$–$5V$ signal for the ADC.

---

### Question B (14 Marks): [KTU University Exam - May 2024] — Internal Choice Alternative

**Q: (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of a non-contact infrared (IR) temperature sensor. State **Stefan's Law** and derive the relationship between the radiated power and the object's temperature. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A blackbody furnace has a surface area of $0.05 \, \text{m}^2$ and emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.85$. If the temperature of the surface is $1200^{\circ}\text{C}$, calculate the total radiated power. Given: Stefan-Boltzmann constant $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$. **(7 Marks)**

---

**Model Solution for (a):**

**Block Diagram Description:**
An IR temperature sensor consists of:
1. **Optical Lens** (Germanium or Silicon) — focuses IR radiation from the target onto the detector
2. **IR Detector** (Thermopile / Bolometer / Pyroelectric) — converts IR radiation to electrical signal
3. **Ambient Temperature Sensor** — compensates for sensor body temperature
4. **Signal Processing Electronics** — amplifies, linearizes, and outputs the signal
5. **Output Display / Interface** — 4-20 mA, 0-10V, or digital output

**Stefan's Law Statement:**
The total radiant energy emitted per unit area per unit time by a blackbody is directly proportional to the **fourth power of its absolute temperature**.

**Derivation:**
Consider a small element $dA$ of a blackbody at temperature $T$. By electromagnetic theory and integrating Planck's spectral distribution over all wavelengths, the energy emitted per unit area is:

$$
E = \int_0^{\infty} E_\lambda \, d\lambda = \sigma T^4
$$

For a **graybody** (emissivity $\varepsilon < 1$) with area $A$:

$$
P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4
$$

**Model Solution for (b):**

**Step 1:** Convert temperature to Kelvin:

$$
T = 1200 + 273.15 = 1473.15 \, \text{K}
$$

**Step 2:** Apply the Stefan-Boltzmann equation:

$$
P = \varepsilon \cdot \sigma \cdot A \cdot T^4
$$

**Step 3:** Substitute values:

$$
P = 0.85 \times (5.67 \times 10^{-8}) \times 0.05 \times (1473.15)^4
$$

**Step 4:** Compute $T^4$:

$$
(1473.15)^2 = 2{,}170{,}051
$$

$$
(1473.15)^4 = (2{,}170{,}051)^2 = 4.709 \times 10^{12} \, \text{K}^4
$$

**Step 5:** Multiply all terms:

$$
P = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 0.05 \times 4.709 \times 10^{12}
$$

$$
P = 0.85 \times 5.67 \times 0.05 \times 4.709 \times 10^{4}
$$

$$
P = 0.85 \times 1.335 \times 10^{4} = 11{,}347.5 \, \text{W}
$$

**Step 6:** Express final result:

$$
P \approx 11.35 \, \text{kW}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - **Common Mistake 1:** Forgetting to convert Celsius to Kelvin — this error alone causes **2–3 marks deduction** in KTU exams.
> - **Common Mistake 2:** Using $\varepsilon = 1$ (blackbody assumption) when the problem explicitly states a graybody — another **1–2 marks loss**.
> - **Common Mistake 3:** Not stating the **Law of Intermediate Metals** when explaining thermocouples — KTU examiners allocate **1 mark** specifically for this.
> - **Common Mistake 4:** Writing the Callendar-Van Dusen equation without specifying the **temperature range validity** ($T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$ vs. $T < 0^{\circ}\text{C}$) — this costs **1 mark**.
> - **Common Mistake 5:** For thermistor problems, **NOT** converting to Kelvin before applying the exponential — this is a **fatal error** worth **3–4 marks**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Quick-Fire Revision Checklist

- **Temperature Scales:** Always remember $T(K) = T(^{\circ}C) + 273.15$ for any thermodynamic or radiation-based sensor calculation.
- **Zeroth Law of Thermodynamics:** The foundational principle that makes temperature a measurable, transitive quantity.
- **ITS-90:** The current international standard for temperature measurement — cite this in definitions.
- **Seebeck Effect:** Two dissimilar metals + temperature gradient = EMF. Governs thermocouples.
- **Law of Intermediate Metals:** Inserting a third metal does not change the net EMF if its two new junctions are at the same temperature.
- **Cold-Junction Compensation (CJC):** Essential for accurate thermocouple readings — uses an IC sensor at the reference junction.
- **PT100 RTD:** $R_0 = 100 \, \Omega$ at $0^{\circ}\text{C}$, $\alpha = 0.00385 \, \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **Callendar-Van Dusen Equation:** $R(T) = R_0[1 + AT + BT^2]$ for $T \geq 0^{\circ}\text{C}$.
- **NTC Thermistor:** Resistance **decreases** exponentially with temperature; governed by the $\beta$-equation with temperatures in **Kelvin**.
- **Stefan-Boltzmann Law:** $P = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$ — for non-contact radiation sensors.
- **Wheatstone Bridge:** Standard signal conditioning circuit for RTDs, producing a differential mV output.
- **Self-Heating Error:** Minimize excitation current in RTDs and thermistors to reduce measurement error.
- **Sensor Selection Criteria:** Range, accuracy, sensitivity, linearity, response time, cost, and environmental compatibility.

### Key Material Constants (Must Memorize)

| Constant | Value | Application |
|----------|-------|-------------|
| Stefan-Boltzmann $\sigma$ | $5.67 \times 10^{-8} \, \text{W} \cdot \text{m}^{-2} \cdot \text{K}^{-4}$ | Radiation sensors |
| PT100 $\alpha$ | $0.00385 \, \Omega/\Omega/^{\circ}\text{C}$ | RTD calculations |
| PT100 $A$ coefficient | $3.9083 \times 10^{-3} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-1}$ | Callendar-Van Dusen |
| PT100 $B$ coefficient | $-5.775 \times 10^{-7} \, ^{\circ}\text{C}^{-2}$ | Callendar-Van Dusen |
| Triple Point of Water | $273.16 \, \text{K}$ | ITS-90 reference |

> [!TIP]
> **Final Exam Strategy:** For KTU 2024 Module 1 questions on Temperature Sensors, always: (1) Draw a labeled diagram (2 marks), (2) State the governing principle with a one-line definition (2 marks), (3) Provide the mathematical equation with units (3 marks), and (4) Solve any numerical with all conversion steps explicitly shown (3 marks). This 2+2+3+3 = 10 mark structure aligns with typical 14-mark KTU questions where 4 additional marks go to derivations or special case analyses.

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
