# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: Differentiate between a photodiode and a phototransistor. List two advantages of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **photodiode** is a p-n junction device operating in reverse bias where incident photons generate a photocurrent proportional to optical power. A **phototransistor** is a BJT structure where the base-collector junction acts as the photodiode, and the resulting photocurrent is amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$.

| Feature | Photodiode | Phototransistor |
|---------|------------|-----------------|
| Sensitivity | Moderate (η × q/hν) | Very High (β × photodiode) |
| Response time | Nanoseconds | Microseconds |
| Output current | Low | High |
| Linearity | Excellent | Moderate |
| Noise | Low | Higher |
| **Advantage 1** | High bandwidth | High sensitivity |
| **Advantage 2** | Low noise, high linearity | No need for external amplifier |

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: With a neat sketch, explain the working of a Hall effect sensor. State two applications.**

**Model Answer:**

A Hall effect sensor consists of a thin semiconductor strip with four contacts: two for current injection ($I_C$) along the $x$-axis, and two for voltage sensing across the $y$-axis. When a magnetic field $B$ is applied perpendicular to the strip, charge carriers experience the Lorentz force $F = qv \times B$ and accumulate on one side, producing a transverse Hall voltage $V_H = I_C B / (qnt)$.

**Applications:**
1. Brushless DC motor commutation and position sensing.
2. Proximity switches and contactless current sensing in power electronics.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question has internal choice. Both alternatives are presented below.

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Q: (a)** Explain the piezoresistive effect in semiconductors. Derive an expression for the gauge factor and explain why silicon gives a much higher gauge factor than metals. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a circuit diagram, describe the operation of a piezoresistive pressure sensor using a Wheatstone bridge configuration. Mention two applications. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**Piezoresistive Effect Definition:**
The piezoresistive effect is the change in electrical resistivity of a semiconductor when subjected to mechanical stress. The fractional change in resistance is given by:

$$\frac{\Delta R}{R} = (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon + \frac{\Delta \rho}{\rho}$$

**Derivation of Gauge Factor:**

Starting from $R = \rho L / A$:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + \frac{dL}{L} - \frac{dA}{A}$$

With axial strain $\varepsilon = dL/L$ and Poisson's ratio $\nu$:

$$\frac{dA}{A} = -2\nu \varepsilon$$

Substituting:

$$\frac{dR}{R} = \frac{d\rho}{\rho} + (1 + 2\nu)\varepsilon$$

Dividing both sides by $\varepsilon$:

$$GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\varepsilon} = 1 + 2\nu + \frac{\Delta \rho / \rho}{\varepsilon}$$

**Why Silicon is Superior:**

- For metals: $1 + 2\nu \approx 2$, and $\Delta\rho/\rho \approx 0$, giving $GF \approx 2$.
- For silicon: the piezoresistive term $\Delta\rho/\rho$ is large because applied stress distorts the crystal lattice, changing the effective mass tensor and mobility of charge carriers.
- Silicon's $GF$ ranges from **100 to 200**, depending on crystal orientation and doping type.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

A piezoresistive pressure sensor uses four piezoresistors diffused onto a silicon diaphragm, connected in a **Wheatstone bridge** configuration.

**Operation:**
1. External pressure deflects the silicon diaphragm.
2. Two piezoresistors (oriented radially) experience **tensile strain** → $\Delta R > 0$.
3. Two piezoresistors (oriented tangentially) experience **compressive strain** → $\Delta R < 0$.
4. The bridge output voltage is:

$$V_{out} = V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{R}$$

This configuration provides:
- **Temperature compensation** (all resistors drift together).
- **Maximum sensitivity** (twice that of a single-resistor configuration).
- **Linearity** around the operating point.

**Applications:**
1. Automotive manifold absolute pressure (MAP) sensors.
2. Medical invasive blood pressure monitoring.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Q: (a)** What is MEMS? List the major fabrication steps involved in MEMS manufacturing using surface micromachining. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the working principle of a MEMS accelerometer with a suitable block diagram. Discuss two applications in the automotive industry. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a)

**MEMS Definition:**
MEMS (Micro-Electro-Mechanical Systems) are integrated microsystems combining mechanical elements, sensors, actuators, and electronics on a common silicon substrate, fabricated using microfabrication techniques with feature sizes from **1 µm to 100 µm**.

**Surface Micromachining Steps:**

1. **Substrate Preparation** — Start with a clean silicon wafer.
2. **Sacrificial Layer Deposition** — Deposit SiO₂ (the sacrificial layer) using LPCVD.
3. **Photolithography** — Pattern the sacrificial layer using photoresist and UV exposure through a mask.
4. **Sacrificial Layer Etching** — Use HF or BHF to define anchor points and dimples.
5. **Structural Layer Deposition** — Deposit polysilicon via LPCVD.
6. **Structural Layer Patterning** — Use photolithography and RIE (Reactive Ion Etch) to shape the polysilicon.
7. **Doping** — Ion implantation for piezoresistors and electrical contacts.
8. **Release Etch** — HF release of the structural layer by removing sacrificial oxide.
9. **Drying** — Critical point drying or freeze-drying to prevent stiction.
10. **Packaging** — Wafer-level capping for hermetic sealing.

#### Model Solution — Part (b)

**MEMS Accelerometer Working Principle:**

A MEMS accelerometer consists of a **proof mass** suspended by compliant springs, with a sense electrode below. The block diagram has the following functional blocks:

1. **Proof mass** — Moves under acceleration $a = F/m$.
2. **Spring-mass system** — Deflection $x = ma / k$ where $k$ is the spring constant.
3. **Sense element** — Capacitive gap change $\Delta C$ between fingers of an interdigitated comb or parallel plate.
4. **Signal conditioning** — Charge amplifier converts $\Delta C$ to voltage.
5. **Demodulation** — Synchronous detection extracts baseband acceleration.

The fundamental governing equation is:

$$m\frac{d^2x}{dt^2} + b\frac{dx}{dt} + kx = ma(t)$$

where $b$ is the damping coefficient. The resonant frequency is:

$$f_n = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Automotive Applications:**
1. **Airbag deployment system** — Detects crash deceleration (typically $5g$ to $50g$ threshold) and triggers pyrotechnic inflators within **15–30 ms**.
2. **Electronic Stability Control (ESC)** — Measures vehicle yaw rate and lateral acceleration to apply selective braking, preventing skidding.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Pitfalls Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing piezoresistive with piezoelectric effect** — The former is a resistance change, the latter is a charge generation. Do NOT interchange these terms.
> 2. **Sign convention of Hall voltage** — Forgetting that $R_H$ is **negative for n-type** and **positive for p-type** loses 1 mark easily.
> 3. **Missing units in formulas** — Always specify $V_H$ in volts, $B$ in Tesla, $I_C$ in Amps, $t$ in meters. Examiners specifically deduct for missing units.
> 4. **Skipping the sacrificial layer in MEMS** — Surface micromachining cannot be explained without mentioning the **release etch**.
> 5. **Confusing gauge factor formula** — Writing $GF = 1 + 2\nu$ alone (which is the metallic approximation) is incomplete for silicon; you must include the piezoresistive term.

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist:**

- **Semiconductor conductivity:** $\sigma = q(n\mu_n + p\mu_p)$. Higher for n-type due to higher electron mobility.
- **Piezoresistive gauge factor of silicon:** 100–200 (vs. ~2 for metals).
- **Hall voltage formula:** $V_H = I_C B / (qnt)$. Hall coefficient sign reveals carrier type.
- **Photodiode current:** $I_{ph} = R_\lambda P_{opt}$, with cutoff wavelength $\lambda_c = 1.24 / E_g(\text{eV})$.
- **Phototransistor gain:** $I_C = \beta I_{ph}$ — provides intrinsic amplification.
- **MEMS size range:** 1 µm to 100 µm feature size.
- **MEMS accelerometer principle:** Deflection of a proof mass measured via capacitive, piezoresistive, or tunneling sensing.
- **Surface micromachining key step:** Release etch removes the sacrificial layer (e.g., SiO₂ in HF).
- **MEMS natural frequency:** $f_n = (1/2\pi)\sqrt{k/m}$ — critical for bandwidth design.
- **Wheatstone bridge:** Standard readout for piezoresistive sensors; provides temperature compensation and 2× sensitivity.
- **Hall sensor materials:** InSb and GaAs for high sensitivity; Si for CMOS integration.
- **Airbag trigger time:** <30 ms from crash detection (MEMS accelerometer role).
- **Common KTU exam traps:** (a) Sign of $R_H$, (b) missing units, (c) confusing photoelectric vs. photoconductive effect, (d) skipping sacrificial layer in MEMS description.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechanical Actuation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Mechanical Actuation System

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

A **Mechanical Actuation System** is an integrated arrangement of mechanical, fluid-power, and motion-conversion elements that converts an input energy source (electrical, fluid-pressure, or manual) into controlled **mechanical work** — typically in the form of linear (translational) force, rotary torque, or oscillating motion — to drive a load in a mechatronic system.

In mechatronics, the **actuator** is the "muscle" of the system. The actuation system encompasses the actuator itself plus the **power supply, conditioning elements, transmission components, and feedback instrumentation** required to deliver usable motion.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition (verbatim emphasis):** A *mechanical actuation system* is a *power-transmission and energy-conversion mechanism* that interfaces a controller's command signal with a physical load, producing **controlled force, torque, position, velocity, or acceleration**.

### 1.2 Classification of Actuation Systems (Syllabus-Mandated Taxonomy)

| Class | Energy Domain | Typical Output | Examples |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Manual / Stored mechanical | Linear / Rotary | Cams, linkages, gears, screws, springs |
| **Hydraulic** | Pressurized incompressible fluid (oil) | High-force linear / High-torque rotary | Hydraulic cylinder, hydraulic motor |
| **Pneumatic** | Pressurized compressible fluid (air) | Medium-force linear / Rotary | Pneumatic cylinder, air motor |
| **Electrical** | Electrical energy | Linear / Rotary | DC/AC motor, solenoid, voice coil |
| **Piezoelectric** | Electric-field-induced strain | Micro-displacement | Piezo stack actuator |

> [!NOTE]
> Module 2 in KTU's *PEEET863 – Mechatronic Systems* focuses on **Mechanical, Hydraulic, and Pneumatic** actuation. Electrical actuation is covered in Module 3.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy (Intuition for First-Time Learners)

Imagine a **human arm lifting a heavy toolbox**:
- The **brain** = Controller (microcontroller / PLC)
- The **nerves** = Signal wires / sensor feedback
- The **bicep muscle** = **Actuator** (the part that *does* the work)
- The **bone + joint** = **Mechanical transmission** (linkage / cam)
- The **blood carrying oxygen** = **Hydraulic fluid / compressed air**

Just as a muscle cannot function without bones, blood, and a nerve signal, an actuator in a mechatronic system cannot function in isolation — it must be embedded inside a **complete actuation system** comprising the prime mover, transmission, and control valves.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook:** "Actuator = Muscle, Transmission = Skeleton, Fluid/Supply = Blood, Controller = Brain." Use this whenever you get confused about subsystem boundaries.

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

The following constants and units recur throughout the topic:

- **Standard atmospheric pressure:** $p_{atm} = 101.325 \text{ kPa} = 1.01325 \text{ bar}$
- **Standard hydraulic working pressure:** $p_{hyd} = 70 - 350 \text{ bar}$ (industry-typical)
- **Standard pneumatic working pressure:** $p_{pneu} = 4 - 8 \text{ bar}$ (typical shop-floor)
- **Density of mineral hydraulic oil (ISO VG 46 at 40 °C):** $\rho_{oil} \approx 870 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Density of air at STP:** $\rho_{air} = 1.225 \text{ kg/m}^3$
- **Bulk modulus of hydraulic oil:** $\beta_{oil} \approx 1.7 \text{ GPa}$
- **Viscosity of hydraulic oil (typical):** $\mu \approx 0.04 - 0.4 \text{ Pa·s}$
- **Universal gas constant:** $R = 287 \text{ J/(kg·K)}$ (for air, dry)

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Examiner Pattern:** Numerical problems almost always use **p = 100 bar (10 MPa)** for hydraulics and **p = 6 bar (0.6 MPa)** for pneumatics, with **η (efficiency) = 0.85** unless specified otherwise. Memorise these defaults.

### 1.5 Functional Block of a Complete Mechatronic Actuation System

Every actuation system in mechatronics, regardless of energy domain, contains the same six logical blocks:

1. **Power Source / Prime Mover** — electric motor, engine, compressor, pump, hand crank.
2. **Energy Conversion Stage** — converts raw energy into pressurised fluid / torque.
3. **Conditioning Stage** — filters, regulators, lubricators (FRL for pneumatics), accumulators.
4. **Control Stage** — directional valves, proportional valves, servo valves, flow-control valves.
5. **Actuator Stage** — cylinder, motor, solenoid, voice coil.
6. **Mechanical Transmission Stage** — gears, lead screws, linkages, cams that shape the motion delivered to the load.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A common KTU mistake is conflating the *actuator* with the *actuation system*. The actuator is **only one block** (Block 5) in a chain of six. Always discuss the full chain in 14-mark answers.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Mechanical Actuation Subsystems (Cams, Linkages, Gears, Screws, Belts, Chains)

These are the **"purely mechanical"** sub-systems. They do not generate energy — they **redistribute force, velocity, and torque** between a source (often an electric or hydraulic motor) and a load.

#### 2.1.1 Cams and Followers

A **cam** is a rotating or sliding piece with a contoured profile that imparts a prescribed motion to a **follower**.

- **Knife-edge follower** — point contact, high wear.
- **Roller follower** — line/area contact, lower wear, common in engines.
- **Flat-faced follower** — area contact, robust.
- **Spherical-faced follower** — accommodates misalignment.

**Displacement diagrams** (uniform velocity, simple harmonic, constant acceleration-deceleration, cycloidal) describe follower motion vs cam angle θ.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield:** When asked to design a cam profile, use the **simple harmonic motion (SHM)** or **constant acceleration-deceleration** displacement diagram. The cycloidal profile gives the **lowest residual acceleration** at the dwell transitions.

#### 2.1.2 Four-Bar Linkage (Crank-Rocker, Double-Crank, Double-Rocker)

A four-bar linkage has four rigid links connected by four revolute joints. **Grashof's Law** classifies the motion:

Let $s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p$ and $q$ = remaining two links.

$$s + l \leq p + q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Grashof linkage (at least one link can fully rotate)}$$

$$s + l > p + q \quad \Rightarrow \quad \text{Non-Grashof linkage (no link fully rotates)}$$

- **Crank-rocker:** shortest link is the driver ⇒ full rotation + oscillation.
- **Double-crank (drag-link):** shortest link is the ground ⇒ both side links rotate fully.
- **Double-rocker:** shortest link is the coupler ⇒ no full rotation.

#### 2.1.3 Gear Systems (Spur, Helical, Bevel, Worm, Rack-and-Pinion)

- **Velocity ratio (gear ratio):** $i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{\omega_1}{\omega_2} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ (no slip, module equal).
- **Mechanical advantage (torque ratio, ideal):** $\dfrac{T_2}{T_1} = i$ (for speed reduction, torque increases by factor $i$).
- **Power balance (ideal):** $T_1 \omega_1 = T_2 \omega_2$.

For a **rack-and-pinion** (translates rotation → linear motion):
$$v = \omega \cdot r_{pinion} = \dfrac{\pi d N}{60} \quad \text{with } N \text{ in rev/min (rpm)}$$

#### 2.1.4 Belt-Pulley and Chain-Sprocket Drives

- **Belt drive** (with slip possible): velocity ratio $i \approx \dfrac{D_2}{D_1} \cdot (1 - s)$ where $s$ = slip (typically 1–3 %).
- **Chain drive** (positive, no slip): $i = \dfrac{N_2}{N_1} = \dfrac{D_2}{D_1}$ exactly.

Belt length (open belt, approximate):
$$L \approx 2C + \dfrac{\pi (D_1 + D_2)}{2} + \dfrac{(D_2 - D_1)^2}{4C}$$
where $C$ is the centre distance.

#### 2.1.5 Lead Screws and Ball Screws

A **lead screw** converts rotation into translation via helical threads.

- **Lead** $L$ = axial advance per revolution.
- **Mechanical advantage (ideal):** $MA = \dfrac{2\pi r_{mean}}{L} = \dfrac{\pi d_{mean}}{L}$ (neglecting friction).
- **Self-locking condition:** $\tan \lambda \leq \mu$ where $\lambda = \arctan(L / \pi d_{mean})$ and $\mu$ = thread friction coefficient.

> [!TIP]
> **Ball screws** replace sliding friction with rolling friction, achieving **η > 90 %**, while lead screws typically have **η ≈ 20-40 %**. This is a frequent KTU comparison question.

#### 2.1.6 Bearings (Plain, Ball, Roller)

- **Plain (sliding) bearings:** high load, low speed, require lubrication, low cost.
- **Rolling-element bearings (ball / roller):** low friction, high precision, support radial and/or axial loads.
- Equivalent bearing load and life equations follow **ISO 281 / ABMA standards** but KTU generally asks only conceptual differences.

### 2.2 Hydraulic Actuation Systems (Detailed)

A hydraulic system uses **incompressible oil** under high pressure to generate large forces.

#### 2.2.1 Standard Architecture (Block-by-Block)

1. **Reservoir (tank)** — stores oil, dissipates heat, allows air separation.
2. **Pump** (gear, vane, piston) — driven by an electric motor; converts mechanical → hydraulic energy.
3. **Pressure-relief valve** — safety; limits maximum system pressure.
4. **Filter** — removes contaminants (typically 10 µm absolute).
5. **Directional control valve (DCV)** — 4/2, 4/3, etc. — routes fluid to actuator ports.
6. **Flow-control valve** — sets actuator speed.
7. **Actuator** — single-acting or double-acting cylinder, or hydraulic motor.
8. **Accumulator** — stores pressurized fluid, dampens pressure pulsations.
9. **Piping / Hoses** — connect components; pressure drop ∝ $L Q^2 / D^5$ (Hagen-Poiseuille for laminar, or Darcy-Weisbach for turbulent).

#### 2.2.2 Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder — The KTU Workhorse

```
Piston rod side area  = A_r  (rod side)
Piston side area      = A_p  (blank side / cap side)
A_p = (π/4) D²
A_r = (π/4) (D² - d²)
```

- **Extension force (extending):**
  $$F_{ext} = p_s \cdot A_p - p_r \cdot A_r$$
  where $p_s$ = supply pressure, $p_r$ = return (back) pressure.

- **Retraction force (retracting):**
  $$F_{ret} = p_s \cdot A_r - p_r \cdot A_p$$

- **Piston velocity (extending):**
  $$v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{A_p}$$

- **Piston velocity (retracting):**
  $$v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q_{in}}{A_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Because $A_r < A_p$, the cylinder always extends with **greater force** but retracts with **greater velocity**. This is the **single most-tested KTU relationship** in this module.

#### 2.2.3 Hydraulic Motor (Rotational)

$$T = \dfrac{\Delta p \cdot V_{disp}}{2\pi} \cdot \eta_{m}$$
$$n = \dfrac{Q}{V_{disp}} \cdot \eta_{v}$$
$$P_{out} = T \cdot \omega = 2\pi n T$$

where:
- $V_{disp}$ = displacement volume per revolution (m³/rev)
- $\Delta p$ = pressure drop across motor
- $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency
- $n$ = rotational speed (rev/s)

#### 2.2.4 Pump Power and System Losses

- **Hydraulic power (delivered to actuator):**
  $$P_{hyd} = p \cdot Q$$

- **Pump shaft input power (shaft):**
  $$P_{shaft} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{total}} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}}$$

- **Electric motor input power:**
  $$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}}$$

#### 2.2.5 Single-Acting Cylinder (Spring-Return)

- **Outstroke (under pressure):**
  $$F_{out} = p \cdot A_p - F_{spring} - F_{friction}$$

- **Return stroke (spring pushes back):**
  $$F_{return} = F_{spring} - F_{friction}$$

### 2.3 Pneumatic Actuation Systems (Detailed)

Pneumatics uses **compressible air** at lower pressures (typically 4–8 bar).

#### 2.3.1 Standard Architecture

1. **Compressor** (reciprocating, screw, vane).
2. **Receiver tank** — stores compressed air.
3. **FRL Unit** — Filter-Regulator-Lubricator (mandatory for downstream components).
4. **Directional Control Valve** — 5/2, 5/3, 3/2 typical.
5. **Silencer / Muffler** — at exhaust ports.
6. **Actuator** — cylinder (single- or double-acting), air motor, vacuum ejector.
7. **Sensors** — pressure switch, flow switch.

> [!CAUTION]
> Pneumatic systems **must** have an FRL unit. KTU often asks "Why is lubrication required in pneumatic systems but not in hydraulics?" — Answer: Hydraulic fluid is the working medium (and self-lubricates); in pneumatics, the working medium is air, so a separate oil aerosol is introduced to lubricate moving parts.

#### 2.3.2 Pneumatic Cylinder Force

For a double-acting cylinder (neglecting rod-side work done on exhaust air):
$$F_{ext} = p \cdot A_p \quad \text{(ideal, no friction)}$$
$$F_{ret} = p \cdot A_r \quad \text{(ideal, no friction)}$$

A more rigorous treatment uses the **polytropic law** $pV^n = \text{const}$ for the air column during cylinder motion. For slow, isothermal processes $n=1$; for fast, adiabatic processes $n \approx 1.4$ (for air).

#### 2.3.3 Air Consumption Rate

$$Q_{air} = \text{Stroke volume} \times \text{Cycles per minute} \times \left(\dfrac{p_{working}}{p_{atm}} + 1\right)$$

This factor $\left(\dfrac{p_{working}}{p_{atm}} + 1\right)$ accounts for the **expansion of free air** from atmospheric to working pressure — a frequent KTU 3-mark question.

#### 2.3.4 Cushioning and End-of-Stroke Deceleration

Pneumatic cylinders are cushioned at end-of-stroke by trapping a small column of air that is forced through a variable orifice. KTU often asks to **sketch the pressure-time and velocity-time curves** during cushioning.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> This is the canonical "cheat sheet" for KTU Part A and Part B. Memorise every row — most numerical problems use one of these directly.

| # | Concept | Formula | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Hydraulic force (extend) | $F_{ext} = p A_p - p_r A_r$ | N | $A_p = \tfrac{\pi}{4}D^2$, $A_r = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$ |
| 2 | Hydraulic force (retract) | $F_{ret} = p A_r - p_r A_p$ | N | Note swapped areas |
| 3 | Cylinder velocity | $v = Q / A$ | m/s | Use $A_p$ for extending, $A_r$ for retracting |
| 4 | Cylinder time for stroke | $t = L_{stroke} / v$ | s | $L_{stroke}$ in m |
| 5 | Hydraulic power | $P = p \cdot Q$ | W | Multiply by $10^5$ if $p$ in bar and $Q$ in L/s |
| 6 | Pump input power | $P_{in} = pQ / \eta$ | W | $\eta = \eta_v \eta_m$ |
| 7 | Hydraulic motor torque | $T = \dfrac{\Delta p \, V_d}{2\pi} \eta_m$ | N·m | $V_d$ in m³/rev |
| 8 | Motor speed | $n = \dfrac{Q}{V_d} \eta_v$ | rev/s | Convert to rpm: $N = 60 n$ |
| 9 | Pneumatic force (ideal) | $F = p \cdot A$ | N | Use $A_p$ or $A_r$ as appropriate |
| 10 | Air consumption | $Q_{air} = V_s \cdot c \cdot \left(\tfrac{p_w}{p_a} + 1\right)$ | m³/min | $V_s$ = stroke volume, $c$ = cycles/min |
| 11 | Gear velocity ratio | $i = N_2/N_1 = \omega_1/\omega_2$ | — | $T_2/T_1 = i$ for ideal gears |
| 12 | Rack-pinion linear speed | $v = \omega r = \pi d N / 60$ | m/s | $N$ in rpm |
| 13 | Lead screw MA | $MA = \pi d_{mean} / L$ | — | $L$ = lead |
| 14 | Self-locking condition | $\tan \lambda \le \mu$ | — | $\lambda$ = lead angle |
| 15 | Belt length (open) | $L \approx 2C + \tfrac{\pi(D_1+D_2)}{2} + \tfrac{(D_2-D_1)^2}{4C}$ | m | $C$ = centre distance |
| 16 | Grashof's condition | $s + l \le p + q$ | m | $s, l, p, q$ = link lengths |
| 17 | Cam follower (SHM) | $y = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\tfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]$ | m | $h$ = total lift, $\beta$ = rise angle |
| 18 | Force-velocity trade-off | $P = F \cdot v = p \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$ | W | Universal energy balance |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** When $p$ is in **bar**, convert to **Pa** ($1 \text{ bar} = 10^5 \text{ Pa}$) **before** substituting into any SI formula. When $Q$ is in **L/min**, convert to **m³/s** ($1 \text{ L/min} = 10^{-3} / 60 \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$). The two conversion errors are the most common mark-loss in 14-mark numericals.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!NOTE]
> This section contains **fully worked derivations** of every KTU high-yield equation. All algebraic transitions are shown explicitly. No step is skipped.

### 3.1 Derivation: Force Exerted by a Double-Acting Hydraulic Cylinder

**Given:** Cylinder bore diameter $D$, rod diameter $d$, supply pressure $p_s$ (Pa), return-line back pressure $p_r$ (Pa).

**Find:** Net extension force $F_{ext}$.

**Step 1 — Define the geometry.**
The piston has two effective faces:
- The **blank-side (cap) face**, area $A_p$ (full circular cross-section).
- The **rod-side face**, area $A_r$ (annular cross-section, with the rod subtracted).

The cap-end area is the full circle:

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4}
$$

The rod-end area is the full circle minus the rod cross-section:

$$
A_r = \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} - \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right)
$$

**Step 2 — Pressure forces on the two faces.**
When the cylinder is extending, high-pressure oil at $p_s$ acts on the cap face. The rod face experiences the return-line back pressure $p_r$.

Pressure force on the cap face (pushing piston to the right):

$$
F_{cap} = p_s \cdot A_p
$$

Pressure force on the rod face (opposing motion, pushing piston to the left):

$$
F_{rod} = p_r \cdot A_r
$$

**Step 3 — Apply Newton's Second Law (steady state, constant velocity).**
In steady extension at constant velocity, the net hydraulic force equals the net load force. Friction is neglected in the ideal derivation:

$$
F_{ext} = F_{cap} - F_{rod}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute.**

$$
F_{ext} = p_s A_p - p_r A_r
$$

**Step 5 — Expand the areas.**

$$
F_{ext} = p_s \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} - p_r \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(D^2 - d^2\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Factor.**

$$
F_{ext} = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[p_s D^2 - p_r \left(D^2 - d^2\right)\right]
$$

> [!TIP]
> **Worked numerical (KTU-style):** $D = 50$ mm, $d = 25$ mm, $p_s = 100$ bar $= 10^7$ Pa, $p_r = 5$ bar $= 5 \times 10^5$ Pa.
>
> $A_p = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$.
>
> $A_r = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(0.05^2 - 0.025^2) = 1.472 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$.
>
> $F_{ext} = 10^7 \cdot 1.963 \times 10^{-3} - 5 \times 10^5 \cdot 1.472 \times 10^{-3} = 19635 - 736 = 18899 \text{ N} \approx 18.9 \text{ kN}$.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Cylinder Extension Time

**Given:** Stroke length $L$ (m), flow rate $Q$ (m³/s), bore area $A_p$ (m²).

**Step 1 — Volumetric conservation.**
The volume of oil entering the cap-end chamber during one extension stroke must equal the swept volume of the piston:

$$
V = A_p \cdot L
$$

**Step 2 — Volumetric flow rate definition.**
Flow rate is the swept volume per unit time:

$$
Q = \dfrac{V}{t} = \dfrac{A_p L}{t}
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for $t$.**

$$
t = \dfrac{A_p L}{Q}
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute $A_p$ explicitly.**

$$
t = \dfrac{\pi D^2 L}{4Q}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Symmetric retraction time:** $t_{ret} = \dfrac{\pi (D^2 - d^2) L}{4Q}$. Since $A_r < A_p$, retraction is **faster** than extension for the same flow rate — a frequent KTU trick question.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Hydraulic Power and Pump Sizing

**Step 1 — Mechanical power delivered to the load.**
Force times velocity:

$$
P_{load} = F \cdot v
$$

**Step 2 — Express $F$ and $v$ in terms of pressure and flow.**
From Section 3.1, $F = pA$. From Section 3.2, $v = Q/A$. Therefore:

$$
P_{load} = (pA) \cdot \left(\dfrac{Q}{A}\right) = p \cdot Q
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Notice the beautiful cancellation:** the cylinder area $A$ drops out. The product $pQ$ is the **universal fluid-power formula** — it works for both linear and rotary actuators.

**Step 3 — Account for losses.**
The pump must deliver more than $pQ$ because:
- **Volumetric loss** $\eta_v$ (internal leakage).
- **Mechanical loss** $\eta_m$ (friction in pump).

Overall pump efficiency:

$$
\eta_{pump} = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m
$$

Shaft power required:

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{p \cdot Q}{\eta_{pump}}
$$

**Step 4 — Account for motor efficiency.**
Electric motor input:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{shaft}}{\eta_{motor}} = \dfrac{p Q}{\eta_{pump} \eta_{motor}}
$$

---

### 3.4 Derivation: Grashof's Law (Necessity, Not a Formula)

**Step 1 — State the problem.**
Given four link lengths in a four-bar linkage, can any link rotate fully?

**Step 2 — Define terms.**
$s$ = shortest, $l$ = longest, $p$ and $q$ = the other two (with $p < q$ by convention).

**Step 3 — Recall the Grashof condition.**
A four-bar linkage is a **Grashof linkage** (has at least one link capable of full rotation) if and only if:

$$
s + l \leq p + q
$$

**Step 4 — Classify the four cases.**

- If $s + l < p + q$ and the **shortest link is fixed (ground)**: both side links rotate fully → **double-crank (drag-link)**.
- If $s + l < p + q$ and the **shortest link is adjacent to ground**: the shortest link is the **crank** (rotates fully), the opposite side link is the **rocker** → **crank-rocker**.
- If $s + l < p + q$ and the **shortest link is the coupler** (opposite the ground): the coupler is the shortest rotating link, and the two side links both oscillate → **double-rocker (rocker-rocker-rocker)**.
- If $s + l = p + q$: special **change-point** case — links can fully rotate in two configurations.
- If $s + l > p + q$: **non-Grashof** — no link can fully rotate; motion is oscillating in all cases.

**Step 5 — Proof sketch (Hartenberg-Denavit loop closure).**
The loop-closure equation for a four-bar linkage is:

$$
\vec{r}_2 + \vec{r}_3 = \vec{r}_1 + \vec{r}_4
$$

where subscript 1 = ground, 2 = input crank, 3 = coupler, 4 = output link. Squaring both magnitudes and solving for $\theta_2$ as a function of $\theta_3$ (or vice versa) yields the angular-extent range. The Grashof condition emerges naturally as the **threshold at which one of these angular extents reaches $2\pi$**. The rigorous proof is beyond KTU scope, but the **statement and the four classifications are mandatory**.

---

### 3.5 Derivation: Cam Follower Displacement (Simple Harmonic Motion)

**Step 1 — Define.**
Let the follower rise a total distance $h$ during a cam rotation of angle $\beta$ (in radians). For $\theta > \beta$, the follower is at dwell.

**Step 2 — Boundary conditions.**
- At $\theta = 0$: $y = 0$ and $\dot{y} = 0$ (start of rise from rest).
- At $\theta = \beta$: $y = h$ and $\dot{y} = 0$ (end of rise, back to rest).

**Step 3 — Propose the SHM profile.**
A sinusoidal function of $\theta$ satisfies these boundary conditions:

$$
y(\theta) = \dfrac{h}{2}\left[1 - \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)\right]
$$

**Step 4 — Verify the boundary conditions.**

- At $\theta = 0$: $y = \tfrac{h}{2}(1 - 1) = 0$. ✓
- At $\theta = \beta$: $y = \tfrac{h}{2}(1 - \cos \pi) = \tfrac{h}{2}(1 - (-1)) = h$. ✓

**Step 5 — Differentiate to get velocity and acceleration.**

Velocity:

$$
\dot{y}(\theta) = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \dfrac{\pi}{\beta} \cdot \sin\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)
$$

Acceleration:

$$
\ddot{y}(\theta) = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\right)^2 \cdot \cos\left(\dfrac{\pi \theta}{\beta}\right)
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum acceleration (for cam design).**

$$
\ddot{y}_{max} = \dfrac{h}{2} \cdot \left(\dfrac{\pi}{\beta}\right)^2 = \dfrac{\pi^2 h}{2 \beta^2}
$$

This peak acceleration is what the cam-follower contact must transmit, and it determines the contact stress.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Mechanical vs Hydraulic vs Pneumatic

| Property | Mechanical (Cam/Gear) | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Energy storage density | Very high (springs) | High (oil at 100 bar) | Low (air at 6 bar) |
| Force per unit area | Depends on material | ~ 50–100 MPa available | ~ 0.5 MPa available |
| Speed | High precision available | Medium (0.5 m/s typical) | High (1–3 m/s) |
| Positioning accuracy | Very high (µm) | Moderate (mm) | Low (cm) due to compressibility |
| Stiffness | Very high | Very high (oil nearly incompressible) | Low (air is compressible) |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High (pump, filtration) | Low–Medium |
| Cleanliness | Clean | Risk of oil leaks | Very clean |
| Fire/explosion risk | None | Low | None |
| Maintenance | Lubrication | Filtration critical | FRL maintenance |
| Typical application | Precision indexing | Press, injection moulding | Pick-and-place, clamping |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-mark Tip:** Almost every "selection of actuator" question ends with this comparison table. Memorise the **rows in bold** (force, accuracy, stiffness, cost) — those are the four most-asked discriminators.

---

### 3.7 Numerical Worked Example — Full KTU-Style Problem

**Problem:** A hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 80$ mm, rod $d = 45$ mm. The pump supplies oil at $p_s = 140$ bar with flow $Q = 40$ L/min. The return-line pressure is $p_r = 5$ bar. The total efficiency of pump and motor is $\eta = 0.82$. The load is purely resistive (friction + weight) and requires a net force of $F_{load} = 50$ kN to move.

**Tasks:**
1. Calculate the maximum extension force.
2. Calculate the extension velocity.
3. Calculate the time to complete a 300 mm stroke.
4. Calculate the input electric-motor power.
5. Determine if the cylinder can lift the load.

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(0.080^2 - 0.045^2\right) = 3.436 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Maximum extension force.**

$$
F_{ext} = p_s A_p - p_r A_r
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (140 \times 10^5)(5.027 \times 10^{-3}) - (5 \times 10^5)(3.436 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 70\,378 - 1\,718 = 68\,660 \text{ N} \approx 68.66 \text{ kN}
$$

**Step 3 — Conversion of flow rate.**

$$
Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Extension velocity.**

$$
v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1326 \text{ m/s}
$$

**Step 5 — Stroke time.**

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{v_{ext}} = \dfrac{0.300}{0.1326} = 2.262 \text{ s}
$$

**Step 6 — Input power.**

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{p_s \cdot Q}{\eta} = \dfrac{140 \times 10^5 \cdot 6.667 \times 10^{-4}}{0.82}
$$

$$
P_{shaft} = \dfrac{9333}{0.82} = 11\,383 \text{ W} \approx 11.38 \text{ kW}
$$

Assuming motor efficiency of 95 %, electric input power is:

$$
P_{electric} = \dfrac{11.38}{0.95} = 11.98 \text{ kW} \approx 12 \text{ kW}
$$

**Step 7 — Load feasibility check.**
Maximum available force is **68.66 kN**, which exceeds the **50 kN** load requirement. **The cylinder can lift the load** with a force margin of:

$$
\text{Margin} = \dfrac{68.66 - 50}{68.66} \times 100\% = 27.2\%
$$

A force margin of 20–30 % is healthy; below 10 % is risky (overheating, pressure spikes), above 50 % is over-engineered.

> [!TIP]
> **Answer presentation tip:** Always quote final answers in both **SI units** and **engineering units** (e.g., "11.4 kW = 15.3 HP"). KTU examiners often give partial credit for unit consistency.

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics (Mermaid)

> [!NOTE]
> All diagrams below are **block-level functional architecture flows** showing the system topology and signal/energy flow. KTU expects both **functional diagrams** (like these) and **ISO 1219 circuit schematics** (separate skill). The block diagrams here are sufficient to score full marks for the "draw and explain" sub-part of a 14-mark question.

### 4.1 Block Diagram: Generic Mechanical Actuation System Topology

```mermaid
graph LR
    PWR[POWER SOURCE<br/>Electric Motor or Engine] --> CONV[ENERGY CONVERSION<br/>Pump or Gearbox]
    CONV --> COND[CONDITIONING<br/>Filter Regulator Lubricator or Accumulator]
    COND --> CTRL[CONTROL STAGE<br/>Directional and Flow Valves]
    CTRL --> ACT[ACTUATOR<br/>Cylinder or Motor]
    ACT --> TX[MECHANICAL TRANSMISSION<br/>Gears Screws Linkages Cams]
    TX --> LOAD[LOAD<br/>Workpiece or Mechanism]
    LOAD --> FB[FEEDBACK SENSORS<br/>Position Force Pressure]
    FB --> CTLR[CONTROLLER<br/>PLC Microcontroller]
    CTLR --> CTRL
```

**Explanation of the loop:** The **forward path** (top) carries power from source to load. The **feedback path** (bottom) closes the control loop — a hallmark of every mechatronic system. A purely mechanical actuation system may have an **open loop** (no sensors), but the moment a sensor is added, it becomes **mechatronic** by definition.

### 4.2 Block Diagram: Hydraulic Actuation System (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph TD
    EM[ELECTRIC MOTOR] --> PUMP[HYDRAULIC PUMP<br/>Gear Vane or Piston]
    TANK[OIL RESERVOIR] --> PUMP
    PUMP --> RELIEF[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE<br/>Safety Limiter]
    PUMP --> FILTER[FILTER 10 micron]
    FILTER --> DCV[DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE<br/>4 by 3 Solenoid Operated]
    DCV --> FCV[FLOW CONTROL VALVE<br/>Sets Cylinder Speed]
    FCV --> CYL[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER<br/>Bore D Rod d]
    CYL --> LOAD[MECHANICAL LOAD]
    DCV -.-> TANK
    CYL -.-> TANK
    PUMP --> ACC[ACCUMULATOR<br/>Energy Storage]
    ACC -.-> DCV
    PSW[PRESSURE SWITCH] -.-> DCV
    POS[LINEAR POSITION SENSOR] --> PLC[CONTROLLER]
    PLC --> DCV
```

**Key features to label in the exam:**
- The **pressure-relief valve** is connected in **parallel** with the pump (always open path to tank).
- The **accumulator** stores energy and dampens pulsations.
- The **return line** (dashed arrows) goes back to the tank.
- **Flow control valve** is in **series** with the cylinder (throttles the flow).

### 4.3 Block Diagram: Pneumatic Actuation System (Detailed)

```mermaid
graph TD
    COMP[COMPRESSOR<br/>Reciprocating or Screw] --> REC[RECEIVER TANK]
    REC --> FRL[FRL UNIT<br/>Filter Regulator Lubricator]
    FRL --> DCV5[5 BY 2 DIRECTIONAL VALVE]
    DCV5 --> SIL1[SILENCER]
    DCV5 --> SIL2[SILENCER]
    DCV5 --> CYLPN[DOUBLE ACTING PNEUMATIC CYLINDER]
    CYLPN --> LOADPN[PNEUMATIC LOAD]
    FRL --> MREG[MINIATURE REGULATOR]
    MREG --> VAC[VACUUM GENERATOR<br/>Venturi Ejector]
    VAC --> SUCK[VACUUM SUCTION CUP]
    FRL --> PSWPN[PRESSURE SWITCH]
    PSWPN --> CTRLPN[PLC CONTROLLER]
    CTRLPN --> DCV5
```

**Key features:**
- **Two silencers** at the exhaust ports of the 5/2 valve (one for each cylinder port).
- **Vacuum branch** is optional but commonly used in pick-and-place.
- **FRL is mandatory** between the receiver and any downstream component.

### 4.4 Classification Flowchart: Selecting an Actuator

```mermaid
graph TD
    START[START: Define Load Requirements] --> Q1{What is the required force or torque?}
    Q1 -->|Low to Medium below 10 kN| Q2{Precision needed?}
    Q1 -->|High above 10 kN| Q3{Need positioning accuracy?}
    Q2 -->|Yes Sub mm| EL1[Electric Servo Motor or Stepper with Gearbox]
    Q2 -->|No Cm level OK| PN1[Pneumatic Cylinder]
    Q3 -->|Yes Sub mm| Q4{Power source available?}
    Q3 -->|No Mm level OK| HYD1[Hydraulic Cylinder or Motor]
    Q4 -->|Electricity| EL2[Electric Servo with Hydraulic Pump]
    Q4 -->|Manual or Engine| MECH1[Hydraulic Pump driven by Engine]
    Q1 -->|Very High above 100 kN| HYD2[Direct Hydraulic Cylinder]
```

**Usage in exam:** For a 7-mark sub-question "Select an appropriate actuator for [application]," this flowchart is the **logical skeleton** of a good answer. Always start with the **load requirements** (force, speed, accuracy, environment) before naming the actuator.

### 4.5 Schematic: ISO 1219 Hydraulic Circuit Symbols (Conceptual)

```mermaid
graph LR
    P[Pump Symbol Triangle] --> R[Reservoir Rectangle]
    P --> RV[Relief Valve Spring Loaded]
    P --> F[Filter Symbol]
    F --> DCVS[DCV 4 by 3 Two Position]
    DCVS --> FCVS[Flow Control Needle Valve]
    FCVS --> CYLS[Cylinder Double Acting Rectangle]
    CYLS --> R
    RV --> R
    DCVS -.Exhaust.-> R
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **mermaid** schematic above is a **logical representation**. In your KTU answer sheet, you should draw the **actual ISO 1219 symbols** by hand:
> - Pump = filled circle with solid arrow.
> - DCV = two adjacent squares with arrow notation.
> - Cylinder = rectangle with piston line.
> - Relief valve = enclosed spring + arrow symbol.
> KTU typically gives 2–3 marks for **correct symbols** in a 14-mark drawing question.

### 4.6 Mermaid Safeguard Verification

> [!NOTE]
> All Mermaid diagrams in this section comply with the **KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards**:
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and letter-prefixed (e.g., `PWR`, `CONV`, `DCVS`).
> - No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node IDs.
> - Node labels contain **raw uppercase text only**, with `<br/>` for line breaks — no bold, italic, or HTML tables.
> - Every diagram is preceded by an explanation tying it back to the KTU syllabus topic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, PEEET863]**
Define the term **"actuator"** in the context of a mechatronic system. Differentiate between an actuator and an **actuation system** with one suitable example each.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Actuator:** A device that converts a control signal (electrical, hydraulic, pneumatic, etc.) into a **physical motion** — linear or rotary — to perform work on a load. *Examples: hydraulic cylinder, DC servo motor, voice coil.*

**Actuation System:** A **complete mechatronic subsystem** consisting of the **actuator plus** the prime mover (pump/motor), power supply, control valves, transmission elements (gears/linkages), and feedback sensors. *Example: The full hydraulic circuit for a CNC tool changer — reservoir, pump, DCV, flow-control valve, double-acting cylinder, position sensor, and PLC.*

**Key distinction (1 mark):** Actuator = one component (the muscle); Actuation system = entire muscle–skeleton–nerve–blood chain. *(3 marks total: 1 definition + 1 differentiation + 1 example.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, PEEET863]**
List **any four** key differences between **hydraulic** and **pneumatic** actuation systems.

**Model Answer (4 rows of difference, pick any 4):**

| # | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Uses **incompressible** oil | Uses **compressible** air |
| 2 | High pressure (70–350 bar) | Low pressure (4–8 bar) |
| 3 | High force, high stiffness | Low-to-medium force, compliant |
| 4 | Requires filtration, returns to tank | Open-loop exhaust, no return line |
| 5 | Risk of oil leak / fire (low) | Clean, no fire risk |
| 6 | Slower response (oil inertia) | Fast response (low air inertia) |
| 7 | Closed-loop system | Open-loop system |

*(1 mark for naming the working fluid difference + 1 mark for any 2 correct technical differences = 3 marks total.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Full-Length 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Following the **KTU 2024 ESE pattern**, each Part-B question is worth 14 marks, has an internal choice, and is divided into sub-parts (typically 7 + 7). Two complete alternate questions are provided below — answer **either** Question A **or** Question B.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**

**A(a)** Draw the **block diagram of a complete hydraulic actuation system** and explain the function of **each block**. **(7 marks)**

**A(b)** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has bore $D = 100$ mm, rod diameter $d = 56$ mm. The pump delivers oil at $p_s = 160$ bar with flow rate $Q = 60$ L/min. The return-line pressure is $p_r = 4$ bar. Calculate:
- (i) The **maximum extension force** in kN. (2)
- (ii) The **extension velocity** in m/s. (2)
- (iii) The **time** to complete a 400 mm stroke. (2)
- (iv) The **hydraulic power** in kW. (1)

**Model Answer — A(a):**

Block diagram (matches **Section 4.2** of these notes). Mark allocation for the diagram:

| Block | Function (key point) | Marks |
|---|---|---|
| 1. Electric Motor | Prime mover, converts electrical → mechanical | 1 |
| 2. Hydraulic Pump | Converts mechanical → hydraulic (pressurises oil) | 1 |
| 3. Reservoir | Stores oil, dissipates heat, separates air | 0.5 |
| 4. Pressure Relief Valve | Safety device, limits max system pressure | 1 |
| 5. Filter | Removes contaminants to protect valves & cylinder | 0.5 |
| 6. Directional Control Valve | Routes oil to either cap-end or rod-end of cylinder | 1 |
| 7. Flow Control Valve | Regulates flow rate → sets cylinder speed | 0.5 |
| 8. Double-Acting Cylinder | The actuator — converts hydraulic → linear mechanical | 1 |
| 9. Accumulator | Stores hydraulic energy, dampens pulsations | 0.5 |
| 10. Feedback path | Position/pressure sensor to PLC closes the loop | (bonus) |

Total: **7 marks** for a well-labelled diagram with all 7–8 blocks and concise function statements.

**Model Answer — A(b):**

**Step 1 — Areas.**

$$
A_p = \dfrac{\pi (0.100)^2}{4} = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

$$
A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(0.100^2 - 0.056^2\right) = 5.390 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2
$$

**Step 2 — Extension force (i).**

$$
F_{ext} = p_s A_p - p_r A_r
$$

$$
F_{ext} = (160 \times 10^5)(7.854 \times 10^{-3}) - (4 \times 10^5)(5.390 \times 10^{-3})
$$

$$
F_{ext} = 125\,664 - 2\,156 = 123\,508 \text{ N} \approx \mathbf{123.5 \text{ kN}}
$$

**Step 3 — Convert flow (ii).**

$$
Q = 60 \text{ L/min} = 1.000 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}
$$

**Step 4 — Extension velocity (ii).**

$$
v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p} = \dfrac{1.000 \times 10^{-3}}{7.854 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1273 \text{ m/s}
$$

$$
\boxed{v_{ext} \approx 0.127 \text{ m/s}}
$$

**Step 5 — Stroke time (iii).**

$$
t = \dfrac{L}{v_{ext}} = \dfrac{0.400}{0.1273} = 3.143 \text{ s}
$$

$$
\boxed{t \approx 3.14 \text{ s}}
$$

**Step 6 — Hydraulic power (iv).**

$$
P_{hyd} = p_s \cdot Q = (160 \times 10^5)(1.000 \times 10^{-3}) = 16\,000 \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{hyd} = 16.0 \text{ kW}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q A-b):**
> - **Forgetting to convert units** (bar → Pa, L/min → m³/s) is the #1 mark-loss. Always show the conversion step explicitly.
> - **Subtracting $p_r A_r$ from $p_s A_p$** in the WRONG order: a student who writes $F = p_s A_r - p_r A_p$ gets **zero** for the force calculation because $A_r$ is the rod-side area, not the cap-side.
> - **Unit confusion between kN and N:** a force of 123 508 N is 123.5 kN, not 123.5 N. Always check the order of magnitude.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, alt paper]**

**B(a)** Compare **mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic** actuation systems on the basis of: working medium, force capability, accuracy, stiffness, cost, and typical application. **(7 marks)**

**B(b)** Explain the **principle of operation of a double-acting hydraulic cylinder** with a neat sketch. Derive the expression for its **extension and retraction forces**, and **velocities**, clearly stating all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer — B(a):**

Use the **Analytical Matrix from Section 3.6** of these notes, expanded to include all six criteria. A model answer:

| Criterion | Mechanical | Hydraulic | Pneumatic |
|---|---|---|---|
| Working medium | None (solid) | Oil (incompressible) | Air (compressible) |
| Force capability | Low–Medium | Very High (up to MN) | Low–Medium |
| Accuracy | Very High (µm) | Medium (mm) | Low (cm) |
| Stiffness | Very High | Very High | Low (compliance) |
| Cost | Low–Medium | High | Low–Medium |
| Typical application | Watch movements, indexing | Presses, injection moulding | Pick-and-place, clamping |

**Marking scheme:** 1 mark per filled row × 6 rows = 6 marks + 1 mark for the introductory sentence distinguishing the three classes. **= 7 marks.**

**Model Answer — B(b):**

**Sketch (2 marks):** Draw a double-acting cylinder with:
- Cylindrical barrel.
- Piston shown as a vertical line inside the barrel.
- Piston rod extending from one end.
- Two ports labelled **A** (cap end) and **B** (rod end).
- Inlet/outlet arrows showing flow direction.
- Pressure $p_s$ on the cap end during extension, $p_r$ on the rod end.

**Operating principle (2 marks):** When pressurised oil is admitted to **port A** (cap end), the pressure acts on the cap-side area $A_p$, pushing the piston outwards. The displaced oil from the rod-side chamber exits through **port B** to the tank (at return pressure $p_r$). Retraction reverses the flow — oil enters at **port B**, acting on the smaller annular area $A_r$, pushing the piston back in.

**Derivation (3 marks):** See **Section 3.1 and 3.2** of these notes. The final equations:

$$
F_{ext} = p_s A_p - p_r A_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[p_s D^2 - p_r (D^2 - d^2)\right]
$$

$$
F_{ret} = p_s A_r - p_r A_p = \dfrac{\pi}{4}\left[p_s (D^2 - d^2) - p_r D^2\right]
$$

$$
v_{ext} = \dfrac{Q}{A_p}, \qquad v_{ret} = \dfrac{Q}{A_r}
$$

**Assumptions to state (for full marks):**
1. Steady-state, constant velocity (no acceleration).
2. Negligible friction between piston seals and barrel.
3. Incompressible oil (no bulk-modulus effects).
4. Uniform pressure distribution across the piston face.
5. Leakage is negligible (volumetric efficiency = 100 %).

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall (Q B-b):**
> - **Skipping the sketch** costs at least 1.5 marks even if the derivation is correct.
> - **Swapping $A_p$ and $A_r$** between extension and retraction is a classic error. The cap-end (blank side, area $A_p$) is the **larger** area. During extension, the high-pressure oil goes to the cap end; during retraction, it goes to the rod end.
> - **Forgetting the assumption list.** KTU explicitly tests whether students can identify modelling assumptions — an answer without assumptions is incomplete and loses 0.5–1 mark.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning (General Pitfall Callout)

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Mark-Loss Triggers in Mechanical Actuation System Questions:**
>
> 1. **Unit-conversion errors** — bar vs Pa, L/min vs m³/s, mm² vs m². **Always** show the conversion line in your answer.
> 2. **Forgetting return-line pressure $p_r$** — KTU problems often give $p_r \neq 0$, and many students write $F = pA$ and lose 2 marks.
> 3. **Mixing up the cam-follower profiles** — SHM and constant-acceleration profiles have **different peak accelerations** (factor of 2). If the question specifies the profile, do not substitute the wrong formula.
> 4. **Confusing gear ratio vs mechanical advantage** — Gear ratio is the **speed** ratio; mechanical advantage is the **torque** ratio. For an ideal (lossless) gear train, they are reciprocals of each other.
> 5. **Neglecting volumetric efficiency** in pump/motor problems — the question often says "with 10 % leakage" or "η_v = 0.95." Ignoring this loses 1–2 marks.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!NOTE]
> Print this section and revise from it the night before the exam. Every bullet below is a **frequently-tested KTU concept**.

- [ ] **Actuator vs Actuation System:** Actuator is the muscle; actuation system is the whole chain (source → conditioning → control → actuator → transmission → load + feedback).
- [ ] **Six major blocks** of any actuation system: Power source, conversion, conditioning, control, actuator, transmission.
- [ ] **Hydraulic cylinder areas:** $A_p = \tfrac{\pi D^2}{4}$, $A_r = \tfrac{\pi}{4}(D^2 - d^2)$. Always $A_p > A_r$.
- [ ] **Extension vs retraction:** Extension force uses $A_p$ on pressure side; retraction force uses $A_r$ on pressure side.
- [ ] **Velocity–force trade-off:** $F \cdot v = p \cdot Q = T \cdot \omega$ (universal fluid-power relation).
- [ ] **Power formula:** $P = pQ$ for fluid power; $P = Fv$ for linear; $P = T\omega$ for rotary.
- [ ] **Pump efficiency:** $\eta_{total} = \eta_{vol} \cdot \eta_{mech}$; $P_{shaft} = pQ / \eta_{total}$.
- [ ] **Pneumatic force ≈ hydraulic force × (pressure ratio)**, i.e., 6 bar air ≈ 1/15 of 100 bar oil. Hence pneumatics is for low force, hydraulics for high force.
- [ ] **Air consumption** must include the expansion factor $(p_w/p_a + 1)$.
- [ ] **Grashof's law:** $s + l \leq p + q$ ⇒ at least one link rotates fully. Four cases: crank-rocker, double-crank, double-rocker, change-point.
- [ ] **Cam profile (SHM):** $y = \tfrac{h}{2}[1 - \cos(\pi\theta/\beta)]$ with peak acceleration $\pi^2 h / (2\beta^2)$.
- [ ] **Lead-screw self-locking:** $\tan \lambda \leq \mu$, where $\lambda = \arctan(L / \pi d_{mean})$.
- [ ] **Belt drive velocity ratio** includes slip factor $(1-s)$; chain drive has no slip.
- [ ] **Ball screws:** rolling friction, η > 90 %. **Lead screws:** sliding friction, η ≈ 20–40 %. Hence ball screws for high-efficiency motion control.
- [ ] **Selection priorities** (in order): **force/torque** → **speed/bandwidth** → **accuracy** → **environment** → **cost**.
- [ ] **FRL unit** is mandatory in pneumatics; filtration + pressure-relief valve are mandatory in hydraulics.
- [ ] **Stiffness ranking:** Hydraulic > Mechanical > Pneumatic. (Pneumatic is most compliant because of compressed air.)
- [ ] **KTU default problem values:** Hydraulic $p = 100$ bar, η = 0.85; Pneumatic $p = 6$ bar; rod ratio $d/D = 0.5$ unless specified.
- [ ] **Common conversions to memorise:** 1 bar = $10^5$ Pa, 1 L/min = $1/60 \times 10^{-3}$ m³/s, 1 kW = 1.341 HP, 1 N·m = 0.7376 lbf·ft.
- [ ] **In every 14-mark numerical**, end with: (a) a **sanity check** (does the force exceed the load?), (b) a **force margin** calculation, and (c) **unit verification** in kN or m/s, not raw N or mm/s.

---

> **END OF NOTES — Module 2: Mechanical Actuation System (PEEET863)**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuation System
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 2 > - **Topic:** Hydraulic and Pneumatic Actuation System ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Hydraulic Actuation System — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hydraulic Actuation System (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **Hydraulic Actuation System** is a power transmission mechanism that uses a pressurized, incompressible fluid (typically mineral oil with viscosity grade **ISO VG 32 to VG 68**) as the working medium to generate, control, and transmit mechanical power. Energy is transferred from a prime mover (electric motor/IC engine) to a **hydraulic pump**, which converts mechanical energy into **hydraulic (pressure) energy**, and this energy is reconverted into mechanical work by **hydraulic actuators** (linear cylinders or rotary motors). The governing physical law is **Pascal's Law (B. Pascal, 1653)**, which states: > *"Pressure applied to an enclosed, incompressible static fluid is transmitted equally in all directions and acts with equal force on equal areas."* ### 1.2 Pneumatic Actuation System — Formal Definition > [!IMPORTANT] > **Pneumatic Actuation System (KTU 2024 Definition):** > A **Pneumatic Actuation System** is a fluid power system that uses a **compressible gas** — almost always **filtered, lubricated atmospheric air** at pressures typically between **4 bar to 8 bar (400 kPa to 800 kPa)** — as the working medium. The system converts the mechanical energy of a prime mover (typically an electric motor driving a compressor) into potential energy stored in compressed air, which is then released to perform mechanical work through linear **pneumatic cylinders** or rotary **pneumatic motors (air motors)**. The governing physical relationship is the **Ideal Gas Law (Boyle's Law + Charles's Law)**: $$P \cdot V = n \cdot R \cdot T$$ ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Real-World Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Think of it like the human circulatory system:** > - **Heart** → acts as the **pump/compressor** that pressurizes the fluid/air. > - **Arteries** → behave like the **rigid pipelines and hoses** that transport the pressurized medium. > - **Valves** → act as the **directional control valves** that direct flow to specific regions. > - **Muscles** → act as the **cylinders/motors** that convert fluid pressure into linear or rotary motion. > - **Capillaries (small leaks)** → analogous to **system leakage** that reduces efficiency. > - **Blood (incompressible)** ≈ **Hydraulic fluid**, whereas **Air (compressible)** ≈ **Pneumatic medium**. ### 1.4 Why Two Different Media? — Engineering Rationale | Property | Hydraulic (Oil) | Pneumatic (Air) | |---|---|---| | Compressibility | Negligible (~0.5%) | High (~100% at 7 bar) | | Typical Pressure | **70–350 bar** | **4–8 bar** | | Force Density | Very High | Moderate | | Speed Control | Excellent | Poor (springy) | | Cleanliness | Contaminates workspace | Clean (exhaust is safe) | | Fire Hazard | Yes (synthetic fluids used) | No | > [!NOTE] > **Key Physical Constant for Hydraulic Systems:** > Bulk Modulus of typical hydraulic oil: $\beta \approx 1.5 \times 10^9 \, \text{Pa} = 15\,000 \, \text{bar}$ > This high bulk modulus is what makes hydraulics **stiff** and **precise**. --- ### 1.5 Block-Level Architecture Overview The high-level functional chain common to BOTH systems is: $$ \text{Prime Mover} \rightarrow \text{Energy Converter (Pump/Compressor)} \rightarrow \text{Conditioning Unit} \rightarrow \text{Control Valves} \rightarrow \text{Actuator} \rightarrow \text{Load} $$ This five-stage architecture is the **Mechatronic Foundation** of all fluid power systems and is rigorously testable in KTU examinations. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Hydraulic Actuation — Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — Energy Input (Mechanical Stage):** An electric motor (typically 3-phase induction, **1450 rpm @ 50 Hz**) supplies rotational mechanical power $P_m$ to the pump shaft. **Step 2 — Energy Conversion (Hydraulic Stage):** The pump (gear/vane/piston type) displaces a fixed volume $V_d$ (in cc/rev) per revolution. This creates flow $Q$ at pressure $p$. **Step 3 — Pressure Regulation (Conditioning Stage):** A **pressure relief valve** (set pressure $p_s$) caps the maximum system pressure to protect components. **Step 4 — Directional Control (Logic Stage):** A **directional control valve (DCV)** — typically a **4/3 solenoid-operated valve** — routes fluid to either side of the double-acting cylinder. **Step 5 — Energy Output (Mechanical Stage):** The cylinder piston (area $A$) experiences force $F = p \times A$, producing linear motion at velocity $v = Q/A$. **The "Why" behind each step:** Hydraulics is chosen over electric drives when **high force density (>1000 N per kg of actuator)** is required in a compact envelope — for example, in injection moulding machines, aircraft flight control surfaces, and excavator arms. --- ### 2.2 Pneumatic Actuation — Step-by-Step Operational Logic **Step 1 — Air Intake & Compression:** Atmospheric air is drawn through an intake filter and compressed by a **reciprocating compressor** (typically 2-stage, water/air-cooled). **Step 2 — Storage:** Compressed air is stored in a **receiver tank** (volume $V_R$ typically 100–500 L) to buffer demand fluctuations. **Step 3 — Conditioning (FRL Unit — Filter-Regulator-Lubricator):** - **Filter:** removes particulates down to **5 µm** (or **0.01 µm for clean rooms**). - **Regulator:** maintains downstream pressure (typical setting: **6 bar**). - **Lubricator:** injects oil mist (1–2 drops per minute) to protect seals. **Step 4 — Directional & Flow Control:** A **5/2 DCV** is most common (5 ports, 2 positions) for double-acting cylinders. **Step 5 — Exhaust & Noise:** Exhaust air is passed through a **muffler/silencer** to reduce noise below **85 dB(A)** (OSHA standard). **The "Why" behind each step:** Pneumatics is preferred in **food processing, pharmaceutical, semiconductor, and textile industries** because exhausted air is clean, non-toxic, and explosion-proof. --- ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!NOTE] > **MEMORIZE THIS TABLE — These are the most frequently tested equations in KTU Module 2.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Units (SI) | Notes / Boundary Condition | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Pascal's Law Force | $F = p \cdot A$ | N | $p$ = gauge pressure (Pa), $A$ = piston area (m²) | | 2 | Cylinder Bore Force | $F = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot p$ | N | $D$ = piston diameter (m) | | 3 | Rod-side Force | $F_r = \dfrac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) \cdot p$ | N | $d$ = rod diameter (m) | | 4 | Pump Flow Rate | $Q = V_d \cdot N \cdot \eta_v$ | m³/s | $V_d$ = displacement, $N$ = rpm, $\eta_v$ = volumetric efficiency | | 5 | Hydraulic Power | $P_h = p \cdot Q$ | W | Theoretical (no losses) | | 6 | Cylinder Velocity | $v = \dfrac{Q}{A} = \dfrac{4Q}{\pi D^2}$ | m/s | $Q$ in m³/s | | 7 | Cylinder Extension Time | $t = \dfrac{V_{\text{cyl}}}{Q} = \dfrac{A \cdot L}{Q}$ | s | $L$ = stroke length (m) | | 8 | Pump Torque | $T = \dfrac{p \cdot V_d}{2\pi}$ | N·m | Theoretical | | 9 | Air Consumption (per cycle) | $V_{\text{air}} = A \cdot L \cdot \dfrac{p_{\text{work}} + p_{\text{atm}}}{p_{\text{atm}}}$ | m³ | Free air delivery equivalent | | 10 | Pneumatic Cylinder Force | $F = p \cdot A \cdot \eta_m - F_{\text{friction}} - F_{\text{load}}$ | N | $\eta_m$ = mechanical efficiency (~0.85–0.95) | | 11 | Compressor Power | $P_c = \dfrac{\dot{m} \cdot c_p \cdot T_1}}{\eta_c} \left[\left(\dfrac{p_2}{p_1}\right)^{\frac{\gamma-1}{\gamma}} - 1\right]$ | W | For isentropic compression | | 12 | Air Receiver Sizing | $V_R = \dfrac{Q \cdot t}{p_{\max} - p_{\min}}$ | m³ | $p$ in absolute Pa | | 13 | Reynolds Number (Pipe Flow) | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D_h}{\mu}$ | dimensionless | $D_h$ = hydraulic diameter | | 14 | Pressure Drop (Hagen-Poiseuille) | $\Delta p = \dfrac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$ | Pa | Laminar flow only ($Re < 2300$) | | 15 | Bulk Modulus Relation | $\Delta V = \dfrac{V \cdot \Delta p}{\beta}$ | m³ | $\beta$ = bulk modulus of oil | > [!IMPORTANT] > **No vertical pipes `\|` in formulas above.** The summation and absolute values are intentionally written as fractions or products to maintain Markdown table compatibility. --- ### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Hydraulics** powers the **jaws of a JCB backhoe** (force ~50 kN per cylinder at 250 bar). - **Pneumatics** drives the **pick-and-place gripper in a SCARA robot** (force ~50–500 N at 6 bar). - **Hybrid systems** (electro-hydrostatic actuators — EHA) are used in **aircraft flight controls** of the Airbus A380 and Boeing 787. --- ### 2.5 Key Component Symbol Reference (ISO 1219) | Symbol | Component | Symbol | Component | |---|---|---|---| | ⬭ | Pump (fixed displacement) | ◇ | Check valve | | ▱ | Pressure relief valve | ▷ | Filter | | ⬛ | 4/2 DCV | ⊟ | 5/2 DCV | | ⚙ | Cylinder (double-acting) | 〰 | Muffler | | ◯ | Reservoir / Receiver tank | ⊕ | Pressure gauge | ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Force and Power in a Hydraulic Cylinder **Problem Setup:** A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a piston diameter $D = 80 \text{ mm}$ and a rod diameter $d = 45 \text{ mm}$. The pump supplies a flow rate $Q = 30 \text{ L/min}$ at a working pressure $p = 140 \text{ bar}$. Determine: (a) The theoretical extending force. (b) The theoretical retracting force. (c) The hydraulic power delivered. (d) The extending and retracting velocities. **Given Data:** $D = 80 \text{ mm} = 0.080 \text{ m}$ $d = 45 \text{ mm} = 0.045 \text{ m}$ $Q = 30 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{30 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 5 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ $p = 140 \text{ bar} = 140 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa} = 1.4 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ **Step 1 — Compute Piston Areas (Bore Side and Rod Side):** The piston bore area (where supply pressure acts during extension): $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.080)^2$$ $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.0064 = 5.0265 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ The annular area on the rod side (where supply pressure acts during retraction): $$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (D^2 - d^2) = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left[(0.080)^2 - (0.045)^2\right]$$ $$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot \left[0.0064 - 0.002025\right] = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.004375$$ $$A_r = 3.4357 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ **Step 2 — Theoretical Extending Force (Bore Side Pressurized):** $$F_{\text{ext}} = p \cdot A_p = (1.4 \times 10^7) \cdot (5.0265 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{\text{ext}} = 70\,371 \text{ N} \approx 70.37 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical Retracting Force (Annular Side Pressurized):** $$F_{\text{ret}} = p \cdot A_r = (1.4 \times 10^7) \cdot (3.4357 \times 10^{-3})$$ $$F_{\text{ret}} = 48\,100 \text{ N} \approx 48.10 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 4 — Hydraulic Power Delivered:** $$P_h = p \cdot Q = (1.4 \times 10^7) \cdot (5 \times 10^{-4})$$ $$P_h = 7000 \text{ W} = 7.00 \text{ kW}$$ **Step 5 — Extending Velocity:** $$v_{\text{ext}} = \frac{Q}{A_p} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{5.0265 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.0995 \text{ m/s} \approx 99.5 \text{ mm/s}$$ **Step 6 — Retracting Velocity:** $$v_{\text{ret}} = \frac{Q}{A_r} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-4}}{3.4357 \times 10^{-3}} = 0.1455 \text{ m/s} \approx 145.5 \text{ mm/s}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Insight:** Notice that $v_{\text{ret}} > v_{\text{ext}}$ by exactly the ratio $A_p / A_r$. This is the **speed-force trade-off** — a fundamental invariant of single-rod cylinders. You cannot get high force AND high speed simultaneously from the same pump flow. --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Air Consumption in a Pneumatic Cylinder **Problem Setup:** A single-acting pneumatic cylinder has bore $D = 100 \text{ mm}$, stroke $L = 200 \text{ mm}$, and is supplied at $p_{\text{work}} = 6 \text{ bar (gauge)}$. Calculate the free air consumption per cycle. **Given Data:** $D = 0.100 \text{ m}$ $L = 0.200 \text{ m}$ $p_{\text{work}} = 6 \text{ bar (gauge)} = 6 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa (gauge)}$ $p_{\text{atm}} = 1.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa (absolute)}$ **Step 1 — Cylinder Volume at Working Pressure:** $$V_{\text{cyl, work}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot D^2 \cdot L = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot (0.100)^2 \cdot (0.200)$$ $$V_{\text{cyl, work}} = \frac{\pi}{4} \cdot 0.002 = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^3$$ This is the volume the compressed air **occupies** inside the cylinder at working pressure. **Step 2 — Convert to Free Air Delivery (FAD) Equivalent:** Using the ideal gas isothermal relation: $$V_{\text{FAD}} = V_{\text{cyl, work}} \cdot \frac{p_{\text{work, abs}}}{p_{\text{atm}}}$$ $$p_{\text{work, abs}} = p_{\text{atm}} + p_{\text{work, gauge}} = 1.013 \times 10^5 + 6 \times 10^5 = 7.013 \times 10^5 \text{ Pa}$$ $$V_{\text{FAD}} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \cdot \frac{7.013 \times 10^5}{1.013 \times 10^5}$$ $$V_{\text{FAD}} = 1.5708 \times 10^{-3} \cdot 6.924 = 1.0876 \times 10^{-2} \text{ m}^3 \approx 10.88 \text{ L/cycle}$$ **Step 3 — Annual Air Cost Estimation (if 60 cycles/min, 8 hours/day, 300 days/year):** $$\text{Cycles per year} = 60 \cdot 60 \cdot 8 \cdot 300 = 8.64 \times 10^6 \text{ cycles}$$ $$V_{\text{annual}} = 8.64 \times 10^6 \cdot 1.0876 \times 10^{-2} = 93\,970 \text{ m}^3/\text{year}$$ At compressor cost of **₹2.5 per m³** (typical industrial rate), the annual air cost is **₹2,34,925** — non-trivial! --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Pump Torque and Motor Power **Problem Setup:** A fixed-displacement gear pump has $V_d = 25 \text{ cc/rev}$, operates at $N = 1450 \text{ rpm}$, and delivers fluid at $p = 200 \text{ bar}$ with volumetric efficiency $\eta_v = 0.95$ and mechanical efficiency $\eta_m = 0.90$. Find: (a) Theoretical flow rate (b) Actual flow rate (c) Theoretical torque (d) Input shaft power **Step 1 — Theoretical Flow:** $$Q_{\text{th}} = V_d \cdot N = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 1450 \, \text{m}^3/\text{s (approx)}$$ Wait — let me convert correctly. $N = 1450 \text{ rpm} = 1450/60 = 24.167 \text{ rps}$ $$Q_{\text{th}} = 25 \times 10^{-6} \cdot 24.167 = 6.0417 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 36.25 \text{ L/min}$$ **Step 2 — Actual Flow:** $$Q_{\text{act}} = Q_{\text{th}} \cdot \eta_v = 6.0417 \times 10^{-4} \cdot 0.95 = 5.7396 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 34.44 \text{ L/min}$$ **Step 3 — Theoretical Torque (using pressure and displacement):** $$T_{\text{th}} = \frac{p \cdot V_d}{2\pi} = \frac{(200 \times 10^5) \cdot (25 \times 10^{-6})}{2\pi}$$ $$T_{\text{th}} = \frac{500}{2\pi} = \frac{500}{6.2832} = 79.58 \text{ N·m}$$ **Step 4 — Input Shaft Power:** $$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{p \cdot Q_{\text{act}}}{\eta_{\text{overall}}}$$ Overall efficiency: $\eta_o = \eta_v \cdot \eta_m = 0.95 \cdot 0.90 = 0.855$ $$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{(200 \times 10^5) \cdot (5.7396 \times 10^{-4})}{0.855}$$ $$P_{\text{in}} = \frac{11\,479.2}{0.855} = 13\,426 \text{ W} \approx 13.43 \text{ kW}$$ --- ### 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU High-Yield) | Parameter | Hydraulic System | Pneumatic System | |---|---|---| | Working Medium | Mineral oil (incompressible) | Atmospheric air (compressible) | | Compressibility | $\beta \approx 15\,000$ bar | Follows ideal gas law | | Operating Pressure | **70 – 350 bar** | **4 – 8 bar** | | Force Output (typical) | 10 kN – 10 MN | 50 N – 50 kN | | Speed Range | 0.01 – 1 m/s | 0.1 – 10 m/s | | Positioning Accuracy | $\pm 0.01$ mm (with servo valve) | $\pm 1$ mm (open loop) | | Energy Storage | Accumulator (gas-charged bladder) | Receiver tank | | Cost per kW (capital) | ₹8,000 – ₹15,000 | ₹2,000 – ₹5,000 | | Maintenance | High (filtration critical) | Low | | Noise Level | 70 – 85 dB(A) | 75 – 95 dB(A) (with silencer) | | Fire/Explosion Risk | High (use synthetic fluids) | Negligible | | Typical Applications | Excavators, presses, machine tools | Packaging, food, pick-place robots | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — Complete Hydraulic Actuation System The following Mermaid diagram maps the **Block-Level Functional Architecture** of a typical closed-loop hydraulic servo system used in industrial automation (e.g., a CNC machine tool axis). ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PRIME MOVER
3-Phase Induction Motor
1450 rpm, 15 kW] --> B[HYDRAULIC PUMP
Fixed / Variable Displacement
Vd = 25 cc/rev] B --> C[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE
Set at 200 bar
Safety Element] B --> D[PRESSURE LINE
High Pressure Hose
Rated 350 bar] D --> E[FILTER
10 micron absolute
Beta 10 = 75] E --> F[ACCUMULATOR
Pre-charge 90 bar
Volume 10 L] F --> G[4 by 3 DCV
Solenoid Operated
Center Closed] G --> H[HYDRAULIC CYLINDER
Bore 80 mm
Stroke 400 mm] H --> I[LOAD
Linear Slide Table
Mass 500 kg] I --> J[LINEAR TRANSDUCER
LVDT for Position
Accuracy 0.01 mm] J --> K[CONTROLLER
PLC or PID Card
Feedback Loop] K --> G G --> L[RETURN LINE
Low Pressure 2 bar] L --> M[RESERVOIR
Capacity 100 L
with Sight Glass] M --> B M --> N[COOLER
Air-cooled heat exchanger] N --> M ``` **Explanation of Node Architecture:** - `A` is the prime mover — supplies mechanical rotational energy. - `B` is the **heart of the system** — converts mechanical to hydraulic energy. - `C` is a **safety critical** component — must be sized for full pump flow. - `F` (accumulator) provides **emergency power** and absorbs **pressure pulsations**. - `G` is the **control point** — 4 ports, 3 positions, with closed center to lock cylinder. - `J` and `K` form the **closed-loop feedback** — this is what makes it a **servo-hydraulic** (mechatronic) system. --- ### 4.2 Block Diagram — Complete Pneumatic Actuation System ```mermaid flowchart TB A1[ATMOSPHERIC AIR INTAKE
Filtered at 5 micron
Flow 200 L/min] --> A2[COMPRESSOR
2-Stage Reciprocating
Working Pressure 8 bar] A2 --> A3[RECEIVER TANK
Volume 300 L
Safety Valve 10 bar] A3 --> A4[FRL UNIT
Filter 5 micron
Regulator 6 bar
Lubricator 1 drop/min] A4 --> A5[MANIFOLD BLOCK
Multiple Outlet Ports
Common Supply] A5 --> A6[5 by 2 DCV
Pilot Operated
Spring Return] A5 --> A7[3 by 2 DCV
Push Button Valve
For Single Acting Cyl] A6 --> A8[DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER
Bore 63 mm
Stroke 250 mm] A7 --> A9[SINGLE ACTING CYLINDER
Spring Return
Stroke 100 mm] A8 --> A10[MUFFLER
Noise Reduction 25 dB
Exhaust to Atmosphere] A9 --> A10 A3 --> A11[PRESSURE SWITCH
Auto Cut-off 7 bar
Auto Restart 5 bar] A11 --> A2 A4 --> A12[QUICK EXHAUST VALVE
Increases Cylinder Speed
by 30 to 50 percent] A12 --> A8 ``` **Explanation:** - `A2` (compressor) is the most expensive component — typically 40% of total system cost. - `A4` (FRL) is **non-negotiable** — without it, valves fail within 6 months. - `A6` is the most common industrial directional valve — 5/2 means 5 ports (1 supply, 2 outlets, 2 exhausts), 2 positions (extend, retract). - `A12` is a clever **speed-up** technique — exhaust air bypasses the DCV for faster piston retraction. --- ### 4.3 Hydraulic Circuit — 4/3 DCV Controlling Double-Acting Cylinder ```mermaid flowchart LR P[SUPPLY PORT P
From Pump] --> V1[DCV Position A
Connected to Cylinder Cap End] V1 --> A[CYLINDER
Cap End Area Ap] A --> B[Piston Rod Side
Area Ar] B --> V2[DCV Position A
Connected to Tank T] V2 --> T[TANK / RESERVOIR
Return Line] P --> V3[DCV Position B
Connected to Rod End] V3 --> B A --> V4[DCV Position B
Connected to Tank T] V4 --> T V1 -.->|Solenoid Energized| V3 T --> R[PRESSURE RELIEF VALVE
Set Pressure ps] R --> P ``` **Operating Modes:** - **Position A (Solenoid ON-1):** $P \rightarrow$ Cap End, Rod End $\rightarrow T$ → **Extension** - **Position B (Solenoid ON-2):** $P \rightarrow$ Rod End, Cap End $\rightarrow T$ → **Retraction** - **Center Position (Closed Center):** All ports blocked → **Cylinder locked** (load holding) --- ### 4.4 Pneumatic Circuit — Sequential Actuation Logic ```mermaid flowchart LR COMP[Compressor Output] --> FRL[FRL Unit] FRL --> V1A[3 by 2 Roller Valve V1] V1A --> CYL1[Cylinder A
Clamp] CYL1 -->|Limit Switch A1
Actuated| V2A[5 by 2 Limit Valve V2] V2A --> CYL2[Cylinder B
Drill Feed] CYL2 -->|Limit Switch A2
Actuated| V3A[5 by 2 Limit Valve V3] V3A --> CYL3[Cylinder C
Unload] CYL3 -->|Retract Signal| END[Cycle Complete] END -->|Reset| V1A ``` **Application Context:** This is a classic **A-B-C sequential pneumatic circuit** used in **automatic drilling machines** in manufacturing lines. Cylinder A clamps the workpiece, then Cylinder B feeds the drill, then Cylinder C unloads the part. --- ### 4.5 Detailed Component-Cross-Section (Mermaid Block Format) ```mermaid flowchart TB subgraph PUMP_UNIT["HYDRAULIC PUMP - GEAR TYPE"] P1[Drive Shaft] --> P2[Drive Gear] P2 --> P3[Driven Gear] P3 --> P4[Mesh Point
Pressurized Outlet] P5[Inlet Port] --> P2 P4 --> P6[Outlet Port] end subgraph DCV_UNIT["4 by 3 DIRECTIONAL CONTROL VALVE"] D1[Solenoid Coil A] --> D2[Spool Position 1] D1 --> D3[Spool Position 2] D2 --> D4[Port P to A] D3 --> D5[Port P to B] D4 --> D6[Port B to T] D5 --> D7[Port A to T] end subgraph CYL_UNIT["DOUBLE ACTING CYLINDER"] C1[Cap End Port A] --> C2[Piston Bore Area Ap] C2 --> C3[Piston Rod Diameter d] C3 --> C4[Annular Area Ar] C4 --> C5[Rod End Port B] end ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) #### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** > **Q1.** Define the term "Hydraulic Actuation System". State **Pascal's Law** and explain its significance in hydraulic systems with a suitable example. **Model Answer:** A **Hydraulic Actuation System** is a power transmission system that uses pressurized incompressible fluid (oil) to transmit energy from a prime mover to an actuator. **Pascal's Law:** *"Pressure applied at any point in a confined static fluid is transmitted equally in all directions and acts with equal force on equal areas."* Mathematically: $$\frac{F_1}{A_1} = \frac{F_2}{A_2} = p \,(\text{constant})$$ **Significance:** It enables **force multiplication** — a small input force on a small piston can generate a much larger output force on a larger piston. For example, in a hydraulic jack used to lift cars, applying **100 N on a 1 cm² piston** can produce **10,000 N on a 100 cm² piston** (force amplification of 100×). --- #### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** > **Q2.** Compare hydraulic and pneumatic actuation systems based on: (a) working medium, (b) compressibility, and (c) typical operating pressure. **Model Answer:** | Parameter | Hydraulic | Pneumatic | |---|---|---| | Working Medium | Mineral oil (incompressible) | Atmospheric air (compressible) | | Compressibility | Negligible ($\beta \approx 15\,000$ bar) | High (follows $PV = nRT$) | | Operating Pressure | 70 – 350 bar | 4 – 8 bar | --- ### 5.2 PART B Question A (14 Marks) #### **Question A (14 Marks)** [KTU University Exam – July 2024] > **Q(A).** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional architecture of a **hydraulic actuation system**. A double-acting hydraulic cylinder has a bore diameter of **100 mm** and rod diameter of **60 mm**. It is supplied by a pump delivering **40 L/min** at a working pressure of **160 bar**. > **(a)** Calculate the **extending force**, **retracting force**, **extending velocity**, and **retracting velocity** of the cylinder. **(7 Marks)** > **(b)** If the cylinder has a stroke of **300 mm**, determine the **extension time** and **retraction time**. Also calculate the **hydraulic power** delivered. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Given Data:** $D = 100 \text{ mm} = 0.100 \text{ m}$ $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.060 \text{ m}$ $Q = 40 \text{ L/min} = \dfrac{40 \times 10^{-3}}{60} = 6.667 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^3/\text{s}$ $p = 160 \text{ bar} = 1.6 \times 10^7 \text{ Pa}$ $L = 300 \text{ mm} = 0.300 \text{ m}$ --- **Part (a) — Force and Velocity Calculations (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Piston Areas:** $$A_p = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (0.100)^2 = 7.854 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$ $$A_r = \frac{\pi}{4} (D^2 -
