```mermaid
    IN7["CPU PROGRAM EXECUTION"]
    IN8["OUTPUT IMAGE TABLE RAM"]
    IN9["OUTPUT DRIVER RELAY TRANSISTOR"]
    IN10["FIELD ACTUATOR CONTACTOR SOLENOID"]

    IN1 --> IN2
    IN2 --> IN3
    IN3 --> IN4
    IN4 --> IN5
    IN5 --> IN6
    IN6 --> IN7
    IN7 --> IN8
    IN8 --> IN9
    IN9 --> IN10
```

---

## 4.5 Ladder Logic — Motor Start-Stop with Overload

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph RUNG1["RUNG 1 MOTOR SEAL IN LATCH"]
        L1A["PB1 START NO"]
        L1B["PB2 STOP NC"]
        L1C["OL OVERLOAD NC"]
        L1D["M1 CONTACTOR COIL"]
    end

    L1A --> L1B
    L1B --> L1C
    L1C --> L1D
    L1D -.->|"SEAL IN PARALLEL WITH PB1"| L1A
```

The **seal-in contact** (a normally-open contact of M1 in parallel with PB1) is what gives this circuit its "memory" — once M1 energizes, it keeps itself on even after PB1 is released.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 🟢 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> - Students **frequently lose 2–3 marks** by forgetting to mention the **Input Image Table (IIT)** and **Output Image Table (OIT)** while explaining the scan cycle. Always state that I/O status is latched into image tables.
> - In scan time problems, students often **omit the housekeeping time** $T_{oh}$ — full marks require all four phases.
> - When drawing ladder diagrams, students forget the **seal-in / latching contact** for start-stop circuits. This is a classic 3-mark deduction.
> - In counter problems, always state the **preset value (PV)** and the **accumulator (ACC)** explicitly.
> - For maximum response time, the correct formula is **$2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in}$**, not $2 \cdot T_{scan}$. Many students write the wrong formula and lose 1 mark.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. Define a Programmable Logic Controller (PLC). List any four characteristics.**  *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

A **Programmable Logic Controller (PLC)** is a solid-state, microprocessor-based industrial computer that uses a programmable memory to store user-defined instructions for implementing specific control functions such as logic, sequencing, timing, counting, and arithmetic operations. It controls industrial machines and processes through digital and analog input/output modules (per **NEMA** definition).

**Four characteristics of a PLC:**
1. **Rugged and reliable** — designed to withstand harsh industrial environments (dust, vibration, temperature extremes, electrical noise).
2. **Easily programmable and reprogrammable** — logic can be modified without rewiring the hardware.
3. **Cyclic scan operation** — executes the user program in a repetitive, deterministic loop (input scan → program scan → output scan → housekeeping).
4. **Modular I/O architecture** — plug-in input/output cards allow flexible system expansion.
5. **Built-in diagnostics** — LED indicators, fault indicators, and online monitoring simplify troubleshooting.

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

---

### **Q2. List the four phases of the PLC scan cycle with a one-line description of each.**  *(3 Marks)*

**Model Answer:**

1. **Input Scan (Read Inputs):** The CPU reads the status of all physical input devices and stores them in the **Input Image Table (IIT)**.
2. **Program Scan (Execute Logic):** The CPU executes the user-written control program sequentially, using IIT values as inputs and updating the **Output Image Table (OIT)**.
3. **Output Scan (Write Outputs):** The CPU transfers the OIT contents to the physical output modules, energizing or de-energizing the connected actuators.
4. **Housekeeping (Overhead):** The CPU performs internal diagnostics, communication servicing, memory checks, and watchdog timer reset.

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Rule (Module Internal Choice):** Answer **ONE** full question. Each question has sub-parts (a) and (b), each carrying **7 marks**.

---

### **Question A: PLC Scan Cycle and Timing**  *(14 Marks)*

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** With a neat block diagram, explain the internal architecture of a PLC. Describe the function of each block.

**Model Solution:**

A PLC consists of **five major functional blocks** working in coordination. The block diagram is given below (refer to Section 4.1 for the full diagram).

**1. Central Processing Unit (CPU):**
The CPU is the brain of the PLC. It contains a **microprocessor** (typically 32-bit), a **system clock** (8 MHz to 1 GHz), and a **watchdog timer**. It fetches, decodes, and executes program instructions; performs all arithmetic, logic, and data manipulation; and coordinates all internal activities. The watchdog timer resets the CPU if the scan cycle hangs, ensuring fail-safe operation.

**2. Memory Subsystem:**
Memory is divided into:
- **System ROM/Flash** — stores firmware, operating system, and interpreter.
- **User Program Memory (Flash/EEPROM)** — stores the user-written Ladder/Structured Text program; retains data even without power.
- **Data RAM (Battery-Backed)** — stores the Input Image Table (IIT), Output Image Table (OIT), timer/counter values, and internal flags. Battery backup (typical **3–5 years**) ensures retentive data survives power loss.

**3. Input/Output (I/O) Modules:**
These provide the interface between the CPU and field devices.
- **Digital I/O** — handles on/off signals (pushbuttons, limit switches, contactors, solenoids). Typical voltages: 24 V DC, 120 V AC.
- **Analog I/O** — handles continuous signals (temperature, pressure, flow). Typical ranges: **4–20 mA**, **0–10 V**, **0–5 V**, with 12–16 bit resolution.
- **Specialty I/O** — high-speed counters, PID controllers, motion control, RTD/thermocouple inputs.
- Each module provides **electrical isolation** (typically 1500 V RMS optocoupler isolation) to protect the CPU from field-side transients.

**4. Power Supply Unit (PSU):**
Converts mains AC (**230 V, 50 Hz**) to regulated DC voltages:
- **5 V DC** for CPU and logic.
- **24 V DC** for sensor power and discrete I/O.
- **±15 V DC** for analog I/O.
A UPS or battery backup is often added for critical processes.

**5. Communication / Programming Interface:**
Allows program upload/download, online monitoring, and networking.
- **Serial ports:** RS-232, RS-485 (legacy).
- **USB** for local programming.
- **Ethernet (TCP/IP)** for SCADA/HMI integration.
- **Industrial fieldbuses:** Modbus, Profibus, Profinet, EtherCAT, DeviceNet for distributed I/O and inter-PLC communication.

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - May 2024]

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** A PLC has 64 digital inputs, 48 digital outputs, and a user program with 750 instructions. Given that each input scan takes 2 μs per point, each output scan takes 3 μs per point, the average instruction execution time is 4 μs, and housekeeping requires 1.2 ms. Calculate (i) the total scan time, (ii) the scan frequency, and (iii) the maximum input-to-output response time.

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $N_{in} = 64$, $t_{in} = 2 \, \mu s = 0.002 \, ms$
- $N_{out} = 48$, $t_{out} = 3 \, \mu s = 0.003 \, ms$
- $N_{inst} = 750$, $t_{inst} = 4 \, \mu s = 0.004 \, ms$
- $T_{oh} = 1.2 \, ms$

**Step 1 — Input scan time:**

$$
T_{in} = N_{in} \cdot t_{in} = 64 \times 0.002 = 0.128 \, ms
$$

**Step 2 — Output scan time:**

$$
T_{out} = N_{out} \cdot t_{out} = 48 \times 0.003 = 0.144 \, ms
$$

**Step 3 — Program scan time:**

$$
T_{prog} = N_{inst} \cdot t_{inst} = 750 \times 0.004 = 3.0 \, ms
$$

**Step 4 — Total scan time:**

$$
T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{oh}
$$

$$
T_{scan} = 0.128 + 3.0 + 0.144 + 1.2 = 4.472 \, ms
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{scan} = 4.472 \, ms}
$$

**Step 5 — Scan frequency:**

$$
f_{scan} = \frac{1}{T_{scan}} = \frac{1}{4.472 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 223.6 \, Hz
$$

$$
\boxed{f_{scan} \approx 223.6 \, Hz}
$$

**Step 6 — Maximum response time:**

$$
T_{resp,max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in} = 2 \times 4.472 - 0.128 = 8.816 \, ms
$$

$$
\boxed{T_{resp,max} = 8.816 \, ms}
$$

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]

---

### **Question B: Ladder Logic Design and PLC Applications**  *(14 Marks)*

#### **Part (a) [7 Marks]:** Explain the five standard PLC programming languages as per IEC 61131-3. List the advantages and limitations of Ladder Diagram (LD).

**Model Solution:**

**IEC 61131-3** is the international standard for PLC programming languages. It defines **five languages**, classified as **graphical** and **textual**.

**1. Ladder Diagram (LD) — Graphical:**
- Resembles electrical relay logic schematics.
- Uses **rungs** with **normally open (NO)** and **normally closed (NC) contacts** and **output coils**.
- Most widely used in industry due to its familiarity to electricians.
- Best for: discrete on/off logic, interlock sequences, and motor control.

**2. Function Block Diagram (FBD) — Graphical:**
- Uses graphical **function blocks** (AND, OR, PID, timers, counters) connected by signal lines.
- Excellent for **continuous process control** and signal processing.
- Best for: analog control, closed-loop PID, complex math operations.

**3. Structured Text (ST) — Textual:**
- High-level language similar to **Pascal or C**.
- Supports loops (`FOR`, `WHILE`), conditionals (`IF…THEN…ELSE`), and functions.
- Best for: complex math, data manipulation, recipe management.

**4. Instruction List (IL) — Textual:**
- Low-level, **assembly-like** mnemonic language.
- Now deprecated in favor of ST in modern PLCs.
- Best for: compact, performance-critical routines (legacy systems).

**5. Sequential Function Chart (SFC) — Graphical:**
- Uses **steps, transitions, and actions** to describe a sequential process.
- Excellent for **batch processes** and state-based machines.
- Best for: pharmaceutical, food processing, and chemical batch control.

**Advantages of Ladder Diagram (LD):**
- Easy to learn for electricians and maintenance technicians.
- Direct translation of relay logic schematics reduces training time.
- Visual representation makes troubleshooting intuitive.
- Online monitoring allows real-time observation of rung status.
- Widely supported across all PLC manufacturers.

**Limitations of Ladder Diagram (LD):**
- Becomes cumbersome and unwieldy for large, complex programs.
- Not suitable for complex mathematical or data processing operations.
- No native support for loops or advanced control structures.
- Sequential execution model (top-to-bottom) limits parallelism.
- Documenting large ladder programs is difficult compared to ST.

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - May 2023]

#### **Part (b) [7 Marks]:** Design a ladder logic program for the following process: A mixing tank has two level sensors (L1 = low level, L2 = high level) and one temperature sensor (T1). A pump (P1) fills the tank. A heater (H1) turns ON when the tank is full and stays ON until the temperature reaches 70 °C. A stirrer (S1) runs only when the heater is ON. An emergency stop (E-Stop, NC) shuts down everything immediately.

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Identify I/O:**

| Address | Device | Type |
|---------|--------|------|
| `I:0/0` | L1 (low level sensor, NO) | DI |
| `I:0/1` | L2 (high level sensor, NO) | DI |
| `I:0/2` | T1 (temperature sensor, analog ≥ 70 °C) | AI |
| `I:0/3` | E-Stop (NC pushbutton) | DI |
| `O:2/0` | P1 (fill pump) | DO |
| `O:2/1` | H1 (heater) | DO |
| `O:2/2` | S1 (stirrer) | DO |

**Step 2 — Boolean Logic:**

The system must operate **only when E-Stop is healthy** (i.e., E-Stop is **NOT** pressed). Since E-Stop is NC, the contact is **closed (TRUE)** when healthy. If pressed, it opens (FALSE), and we use it as a normally-closed contact in **series** with every rung.

**Pump logic:** Fill the tank until high level is reached. Seal in until L2 is reached.

$$
P1 = \overline{E\text{-}Stop} \cdot L1 \cdot \overline{L2} \quad \text{(with seal-in contact of P1)}
$$

**Heater logic:** Turn ON only when tank is full (L2 TRUE) and temperature is below 70 °C ($\overline{T1}$).

$$
H1 = \overline{E\text{-}Stop} \cdot L2 \cdot \overline{T1} \quad \text{(with seal-in contact of H1)}
$$

**Stirrer logic:** Run only when heater is ON.

$$
S1 = \overline{E\text{-}Stop} \cdot H1
$$

**Step 3 — Ladder Diagram:**

```
Rung 0 (Master Control): |  E-Stop (NC)  |------( GRT - Global Reset )--|     ← or use in series

Rung 1 (Pump): |--| E-Stop |--| L1 |--|/|L2 |--+--( P1 )--|              Pump
                                 |                 |
                                 +--| P1 |--------+  (Seal-in)

Rung 2 (Heater): |--| E-Stop |--| L2 |--|/|T1 |--+--( H1 )--|            Heater
                                   |                |
                                   +--| H1 |--------+  (Seal-in)

Rung 3 (Stirrer): |--| E-Stop |--| H1 |------------( S1 )--|              Stirrer
```

**Step 4 — Operation Verification:**

1. **Empty tank** (L1 = 0, L2 = 0): Pump is OFF (no L1). Heater OFF. Stirrer OFF.
2. **Low level reached** (L1 = 1, L2 = 0): Pump starts, seal-in keeps it running. Fills the tank.
3. **High level reached** (L1 = 1, L2 = 1): Pump stops ($\overline{L2}$ opens seal-in path). Heater starts (L2 closes, T1 assumed < 70 °C). Stirrer starts (H1 closes).
4. **Temperature reaches 70 °C** (T1 = 1): Heater stops ($\overline{T1}$ opens). Stirrer stops (H1 opens).
5. **E-Stop pressed:** All rungs de-energize immediately. All outputs OFF. ✓

**Step 5 — Industrial Enhancement:** A real implementation would also include a **high-high level cutoff** (overflow protection) and a **temperature high-limit cutoff** (overheat protection) to ensure fail-safe operation.

> **Source Tag:** [KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU Exam Day:**

- [ ] **PLC = solid-state, microprocessor-based, industrial digital computer** (NEMA definition).
- [ ] **Five internal blocks:** CPU, Memory, I/O Modules, Power Supply, Communication Interface.
- [ ] **Memory types:** ROM (firmware), Flash (user program), RAM (IIT/OIT/timers — battery-backed).
- [ ] **Four phases of scan cycle:** Input Scan → Program Scan → Output Scan → Housekeeping.
- [ ] **IIT and OIT are critical** — always mention them when explaining the scan cycle.
- [ ] **Total scan time:** $T_{scan} = T_{in} + T_{prog} + T_{out} + T_{oh}$.
- [ ] **Maximum response time:** $T_{resp,max} = 2 \cdot T_{scan} - T_{in}$.
- [ ] **Scan frequency:** $f_{scan} = 1 / T_{scan}$ [Hz].
- [ ] **IEC 61131-3 languages:** LD, FBD, ST, IL, SFC — know one-line description of each.
- [ ] **Ladder Logic elements:** NO contact `| |`, NC contact `|/|`, output coil `( )`, timers, counters.
- [ ] **Seal-in / latching contact:** parallel NO contact of the output coil — gives "memory" to a momentary start pushbutton.
- [ ] **TON timer:** counts up when input is TRUE; sets DN bit when ACC ≥ PRE.
- [ ] **CTU counter:** increments on rising-edge input pulses; sets DN when ACC ≥ PRE.
- [ ] **I/O addressing convention:** `I:slot/bit` for inputs, `O:slot/bit` for outputs (Allen-Bradley); `IX.X` (Siemens).
- [ ] **Typical scan time:** 1–50 ms for most industrial PLCs.
- [ ] **Optocoupler isolation:** 1500 V RMS — protects CPU from field transients.
- [ ] **Discrete I/O voltages:** 24 V DC, 120 V AC, 230 V AC; Analog: 4–20 mA, 0–10 V.
- [ ] **Industrial fieldbuses:** Modbus, Profibus, Profinet, EtherCAT, DeviceNet.
- [ ] **Watchdog timer:** failsafe — resets CPU if scan hangs.
- [ ] **PLC advantages over relays:** reprogrammable, smaller footprint, easier diagnostics, modular, networkable.
- [ ] **Common KTU question pattern:** (i) Define PLC and list characteristics (3 marks); (ii) Draw block diagram and explain scan cycle (7 marks); (iii) Solve a scan time numerical (4 marks) — or — (i) IEC 61131-3 languages (7 marks); (ii) Design ladder logic for a process (7 marks).
- [ ] **Always write the Boolean expression** before drawing the ladder — examiners award partial marks for the logic.
- [ ] **Memorize the scan cycle formula and response time formula** — they are guaranteed questions in KTU exams.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Input/Output and Communication Systems
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Input/Output and Communication Systems # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition In the context of **Mechatronic Systems**, the **Input/Output (I/O) and Communication Systems** form the critical bridge between the physical (mechanical) domain and the computational (electronic/control) domain. According to the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEEET863)** syllabus framework: > **Input/Output Systems** are the hardware and software interfaces through which a mechatronic system perceives physical phenomena (via **sensors/transducers**) and executes physical actions (via **actuators**), while **Communication Systems** are the structured protocols, buses, and standards that govern the reliable, real-time, and noise-immune transfer of data between subsystems. A mechatronic system without well-defined I/O and communication is essentially a "brain in a jar" — it has intelligence but cannot sense or act upon the world, nor can it coordinate with other intelligent modules. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Think of a mechatronic system exactly like a **human being driving a car**: | Human Body Part | Mechatronic Equivalent | Function | |-----------------|------------------------|----------| | Eyes, Ears, Skin | **Sensors** (LIDAR, Encoders, Thermistors) | **Input** — sense the world | | Spinal Cord & Nerves | **Communication Bus** (CAN, I²C, SPI) | **Data transfer** — carry signals | | Brain (Cortex) | **Microcontroller / PLC** | **Processing** — decision making | | Hands, Feet, Voice | **Actuators** (Motors, Solenoids, Speakers) | **Output** — act on the world | | Nervous system tuning | **Signal Conditioning** (Filters, Amplifiers) | **Refinement** — clean signals | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 module stresses that a mechatronics engineer must master *both* the **physical interfacing** (analog/digital signal levels, impedance matching) *and* the **logical interfacing** (protocols, handshaking, error checking). ## 1.3 Categorization Overview ### Input Systems (Sensors & Transducers) - **Active sensors** — generate their own signal (e.g., piezoelectric, thermocouple). - **Passive sensors** — require external excitation (e.g., RTD, strain gauge, LVDT). - **Digital sensors** — output discrete bits (e.g., encoders, Hall-effect switches). - **Analog sensors** — output continuous voltage/current (e.g., thermistor, LDR). ### Output Systems (Actuators) - **Electrical actuators** — DC/Stepper/Servo motors, solenoids, relays. - **Hydraulic actuators** — cylinders, motors using incompressible fluid. - **Pneumatic actuators** — cylinders, motors using compressible air. - **Thermal/Optical actuators** — heaters, LEDs, LCDs, displays. ### Communication Systems - **Parallel communication** — multiple bits transferred simultaneously (e.g., PCI, parallel ATA, GPIB/IEEE-488). - **Serial communication** — bits sent sequentially (e.g., RS-232, USB, CAN, SPI, I²C, Ethernet). - **Synchronous** — clock-shared (SPI, I²C). - **Asynchronous** — no shared clock, uses start/stop bits (RS-232, UART). > [!NOTE] > **Key Definition Box — Transducer vs. Sensor vs. Actuator** > - A **transducer** is the umbrella term for *any* device that converts one form of energy to another. > - A **sensor** is a transducer that converts a *physical measurand* into a usable electrical signal (**input**). > - An **actuator** is a transducer that converts an *electrical control signal* into a *physical action* (**output**). # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Signal Conditioning — The Hidden Hero Raw sensor outputs are almost never directly usable by a microcontroller. They are: 1. **Too small** (e.g., a thermocouple outputs ~41 µV/°C — for 100 °C, that's just **4.1 mV**). 2. **Noisy** (mixed with 50 Hz mains hum, EMI from motors). 3. **Non-linear** (e.g., thermistor resistance vs. temperature is exponential). 4. **High-impedance** (cannot drive ADC input directly). ### 2.1.1 The Four Canonical Conditioning Stages 1. **Amplification** — Boost the signal to ADC range (typically 0–5 V or 0–3.3 V). - Most common: **Instrumentation Amplifier (INA)**. - Gain: $G = 1 + \frac{2R_1}{R_{gain}}$ (for classic 3-op-amp topology). 2. **Filtering** — Remove unwanted frequency content. - **Low-pass filter** cutoff: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. - **High-pass filter** cutoff: $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$. 3. **Linearization** — Use look-up tables, piece-wise linear approximation, or curve fitting. - For NTC thermistor (Steinhart-Hart equation): $$ \frac{1}{T} = A + B \ln(R) + C [\ln(R)]^3 $$ where $T$ is in **Kelvin (K)** and $R$ in **ohms (Ω)**, with $A$, $B$, $C$ as fitted constants. 4. **Isolation & Level Shifting** — Galvanic isolation via **opto-couplers** or **isolation transformers** to protect low-voltage electronics from high-voltage transients. > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU examiners frequently test the relationship between the **Nyquist Sampling Theorem** and anti-aliasing filter selection. The sampling frequency must be at least twice the highest frequency component: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. ## 2.2 Data Acquisition System (DAS) Architecture A standard **Data Acquisition System (DAS)** block diagram consists of: $$ \text{Physical Variable} \rightarrow \text{Sensor} \rightarrow \text{Signal Conditioner} \rightarrow \text{Multiplexer (MUX)} \rightarrow \text{ADC} \rightarrow \text{Microcontroller} $$ The reverse path (D/A and actuators) is called the **Distributed Control System (DCS)** output chain. ## 2.3 Actuator Selection — Engineering Trade-offs | Actuator Type | Bandwidth | Precision | Force/Torque Density | Cost | Typical Use | |---------------|-----------|-----------|----------------------|------|-------------| | **DC Motor (brushed)** | Medium | Medium | Medium | Low | Toys, small robotics | | **Brushless DC (BLDC)** | High | High | High | Medium | Drones, EV drives | | **Stepper Motor** | Low-Medium | Very High (open loop) | Medium | Low | 3D printers, CNC | | **Servo Motor (closed-loop)** | High | Very High | High | High | Industrial robots, CNC | | **Hydraulic Cylinder** | Medium | High | Very High | High | Heavy machinery | | **Pneumatic Cylinder** | High | Low | Medium | Low | Pick-and-place | | **Piezoelectric Stack** | Very High | Nano-meter | Low (force) | Very High | Micro-positioning, AFM | ## 2.4 Communication Protocols — KTU High-Yield Summary | Protocol | Type | Speed | Topology | Nodes | Distance | Use Case | |----------|------|-------|----------|-------|----------|----------| | **RS-232** | Serial, Async | ≤ **20 kbps** | Point-to-point | 1:1 | ≤ 15 m | Legacy PC peripherals | | **RS-485** | Serial, Differential | ≤ **10 Mbps** | Multi-drop (bus) | ≤ 32 (or 256 with repeaters) | ≤ 1200 m | Industrial, Modbus | | **I²C** | Serial, Sync | ≤ **5 Mbps** (Fast-mode plus) | Master-Slave (bus) | ≤ 127 (theoretical) | ≤ ~1 m (on PCB) | Sensor ICs, EEPROMs | | **SPI** | Serial, Sync | ≤ **50+ Mbps** | Master-Slave (star) | Few (no formal limit) | ≤ ~1 m (on PCB) | SD cards, displays, ADCs | | **CAN** | Serial, Differential | ≤ **1 Mbps** (Classical) / **5 Mbps** (CAN-FD) | Multi-master (bus) | Many | ≤ 40 m @ 1 Mbps / 1 km @ 50 kbps | Automotive, industrial | | **USB** | Serial, Differential | ≤ **480 Mbps** (USB 2.0) / 20 Gbps (USB 4) | Host-Peripheral (star/hub) | Up to 127 | ≤ 5 m | PC peripherals | | **Ethernet** | Serial, Differential | ≤ **100 Gbps** | Star (switched) | Many | ≤ 100 m (copper) | Industrial IoT, vision systems | | **Modbus** | Application layer over RS-485 | Protocol-dependent | Master-Slave | ≤ 247 (Modbus RTU) | — | PLC networks, SCADA | | **Profibus / Profinet** | Fieldbus | 12 Mbps (Profibus DP) | Multi-drop | Many | ≤ 1.2 km | Siemens industrial | | **Bluetooth / BLE** | Wireless | ≤ 3 Mbps | Star (piconet) | ≤ 7 active | ≤ 100 m | Wearables, mobile | | **Wi-Fi (IEEE 802.11)** | Wireless | Up to 9.6 Gbps (Wi-Fi 6) | Star (AP) | Many | ≤ 100 m indoor | Wireless IoT | | **Zigbee (IEEE 802.15.4)** | Wireless, mesh | ≤ 250 kbps | Mesh | Thousands | ≤ 100 m | Wireless sensor networks | ## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet | # | Concept | Formula | Variables / Units | |---|---------|---------|-------------------| | 1 | Thermocouple sensitivity (Type K) | $S \approx 41 \frac{\mu V}{^{\circ}C}$ | $S$ = Seebeck coefficient | | 2 | RTD (PT100) resistance | $R_T = R_0 [1 + \alpha T]$ | $R_0 = 100\,\Omega$, $\alpha \approx 3.85 \times 10^{-3}\,/^{\circ}C$ | | 3 | Strain gauge factor | $GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon}$ | $GF \approx 2$ for metal; $\epsilon$ = strain | | 4 | Inverting op-amp gain | $A_v = -\frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | | 5 | Non-inverting op-amp gain | $A_v = 1 + \frac{R_f}{R_{in}}$ | Dimensionless | | 6 | Wheatstone bridge balance | $\frac{R_1}{R_2} = \frac{R_3}{R_4}$ | At null, $V_{out} = 0$ | | 7 | Bridge output voltage (quasi-balanced) | $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$ | $V_{ex}$ = excitation voltage | | 8 | RC low-pass cutoff | $f_c = \frac{1}{2\pi RC}$ | $f_c$ in Hz | | 9 | ADC resolution | $Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n}$ | $Q$ = LSB voltage | | 10 | Nyquist sampling rate | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sample frequency | | 11 | Signal-to-Noise Ratio (dB) | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10} \left( \frac{V_{signal}}{V_{noise}} \right)$ | — | | 12 | RS-232 voltage levels | Logic 1 = −3 to −25 V; Logic 0 = +3 to +25 V | Mark/Space inverted from TTL | | 13 | CAN bus dominant bit voltage differential | $V_{diff} \geq 1.5\,V$ | Differential signaling across CAN-H, CAN-L | | 14 | Asynchronous serial frame size | $N_{bits} = 1 \text{ start} + n_{\text{data}} + P + 1\text{ or }2 \text{ stop}$ | $P$ = parity bit (optional) | | 15 | Hydraulic cylinder force | $F = P \cdot A$ | $P$ = pressure (Pa), $A$ = piston area (m²) | | 16 | Torque of DC motor | $\tau = K_t \cdot I_a$ | $K_t$ = torque constant (Nm/A) | | 17 | Back-EMF of DC motor | $E_b = K_e \cdot \omega$ | $K_e$ in V·s/rad | > [!NOTE] > The KTU examiner will **almost certainly** test one formula from the above list combined with a numerical problem on ADC resolution, sampling theorem, or strain gauge bridge. ## 2.6 Real-World Utility - **Automotive** — Modern cars use **CAN bus** to coordinate ~70 ECUs (engine, ABS, airbags, infotainment). - **Factory Automation** — **Profibus/Profinet** links PLCs, drives, and HMIs. - **Medical Robotics** — Da Vinci surgical robot uses redundant **EtherCAT** for hard real-time control. - **Smartphones** — Combination of **I²C** (touchscreen, sensors), **SPI** (display), **USB** (charging), and **Bluetooth/Wi-Fi** (wireless). - **Aerospace** — **ARINC 429 / 664** and **MIL-STD-1553** buses for avionics. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — ADC Resolution and Quantization Error **Problem Context:** A mechatronic system uses a 12-bit ADC with a reference voltage $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V to digitize a temperature sensor signal that ranges from 0 to 100 °C, producing 0 to 5 V. Determine: (a) The size of one Least Significant Bit (LSB). (b) The temperature resolution per bit. (c) The maximum theoretical quantization error. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Identify the number of quantization levels.** An $n$-bit ADC produces $2^n$ distinct output codes: $$ N_{levels} = 2^n $$ For $n = 12$: $$ N_{levels} = 2^{12} = 4096 $$ **Step 2 — Compute the LSB voltage (the step size $Q$).** The LSB is the smallest distinguishable voltage change: $$ Q = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^n} $$ Substituting $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V and $n = 12$: $$ Q = \frac{5.0}{4096} $$ $$ Q = 0.001220703125 \text{ V} $$ $$ Q \approx 1.22 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 3 — Convert LSB to temperature resolution.** Since 5.0 V corresponds to 100 °C: $$ \Delta T = \frac{T_{range}}{2^n} = \frac{100}{4096} \approx 0.0244 \,^{\circ}C $$ **Step 4 — Maximum Quantization Error.** The maximum theoretical quantization error is half of one LSB (because the analog value can be up to ±½ LSB from the digital representation): $$ E_{q,\max} = \pm \frac{Q}{2} = \pm \frac{0.00122}{2} $$ $$ E_{q,\max} \approx \pm 0.61 \text{ mV} $$ In temperature units: $$ E_{q,\max} \approx \pm 0.0122 \,^{\circ}C $$ ### Final Result Summary | Quantity | Value | |----------|-------| | LSB voltage ($Q$) | **1.22 mV** | | Temperature resolution | **0.0244 °C / bit** | | Max quantization error (voltage) | **± 0.61 mV** | | Max quantization error (temperature) | **± 0.0122 °C** | > [!IMPORTANT] > KTU frequently frames this as: *"Justify why a 16-bit ADC is preferred over a 10-bit ADC for a precision mechatronic sensor."* — the answer lies in the 256× finer resolution afforded by 6 extra bits. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Strain Gauge Bridge Output **Problem Context:** A quarter-bridge strain gauge is mounted on a steel beam. Given: - Gauge factor: $GF = 2.05$ - Strain: $\epsilon = 1200 \, \mu\epsilon$ (micro-strain) - Excitation: $V_{ex} = 5.0$ V - Nominal resistance: $R = 350\,\Omega$ Compute the bridge output voltage $V_{out}$. ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Write the gauge factor definition and solve for $\Delta R$.** By definition: $$ GF = \frac{\Delta R / R}{\epsilon} \implies \Delta R = GF \cdot R \cdot \epsilon $$ **Step 2 — Insert the values.** Convert strain to dimensionless: $\epsilon = 1200 \times 10^{-6} = 1.2 \times 10^{-3}$. $$ \Delta R = 2.05 \times 350 \times 1.2 \times 10^{-3} $$ $$ \Delta R = 0.861 \, \Omega $$ **Step 3 — Apply the quarter-bridge output equation.** For a single active gauge in a Wheatstone bridge: $$ V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R} $$ **Step 4 — Substitute.** $$ V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{0.861}{4 \times 350} $$ $$ V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot \frac{0.861}{1400} $$ $$ V_{out} = 5.0 \cdot 6.15 \times 10^{-4} $$ $$ V_{out} \approx 3.075 \times 10^{-3} \text{ V} = 3.075 \text{ mV} $$ ### Interpretation The output is in millivolts — this is **why amplification is mandatory** before ADC sampling. A typical INA with gain ~160 would scale this to ~0.5 V, comfortably within the 0–5 V ADC range. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Nyquist Criterion for Anti-Aliasing **Problem Context:** A vibration sensor on a CNC spindle produces signals with harmonics up to 2.5 kHz. The ADC samples at $f_s = 6$ kHz. Will aliasing occur? What is the minimum $f_s$ to avoid it? ### Step-by-Step Derivation **Step 1 — Recall Nyquist Theorem.** $$ f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} $$ **Step 2 — Compute Nyquist frequency.** $$ f_{Nyquist} = \frac{f_s}{2} = \frac{6000}{2} = 3000 \text{ Hz} = 3.0 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 3 — Compare to the signal bandwidth.** $$ f_{max} = 2.5 \text{ kHz} < 3.0 \text{ kHz} $$ Strictly speaking, sampling above $2 f_{max}$ is the *theoretical minimum*. However, in practice an **anti-aliasing analog low-pass filter** with cutoff just below 3 kHz is required, because the filter has a finite roll-off (non-ideal). **Step 4 — Compute the minimum sampling rate (theoretical).** $$ f_{s,min} = 2 \times 2.5 \text{ kHz} = 5.0 \text{ kHz} $$ **Step 5 — Engineering recommendation.** In practice, oversample by a factor of **5× to 10×** to ease analog filter design: $$ f_{s,recommended} \geq 10 \times f_{max} = 25 \text{ kHz} $$ > [!NOTE] > KTU often adds a *twist*: "If $f_s$ was accidentally set to **4 kHz**, what alias frequency would appear for a 2.5 kHz tone?" — Answer: $f_{alias} = \vert f_s - f_{signal} \vert = 4 - 2.5 = 1.5$ kHz, masquerading as a real signal. # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Generic Mechatronic I/O System ```mermaid flowchart LR A[PHYSICAL WORLD
Temperature, Pressure,
Displacement, Force] --> B[SENSORS
Input Transducers] B --> C[SIGNAL CONDITIONING
Amplify, Filter,
Linearize, Isolate] C --> D[ADC
Analog to Digital
Converter] D --> E[CONTROLLER
Microcontroller / PLC /
Embedded PC] E --> F[COMMUNICATION BUS
CAN / RS-485 /
Ethernet / I2C / SPI] F --> G[OTHER SUBSYSTEMS
HMI, SCADA,
Other MCUs] E --> H[DAC / PWM
Digital to Analog
Converter] H --> I[POWER AMPLIFIER
Driver Stage] I --> J[ACTUATORS
Output Transducers] J --> K[PHYSICAL ACTION
Motion, Heat,
Flow, Light] A -. feedback .-> E style A fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 style B fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style D fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style E fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style F fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style G fill:#fce4ec,stroke:#880e4f,color:#000 style H fill:#f3e5f5,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style I fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style J fill:#fff3e0,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style K fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#1565c0,color:#000 ``` ## 4.2 Block Diagram — Industrial Communication Hierarchy (Purdue / ISA-95 Layers) ```mermaid flowchart TB L0[LEVEL 0
Physical Process
Sensors and Actuators in the field] L1[LEVEL 1
Basic Control
PLCs, RTUs, DCS Controllers] L2[LEVEL 2
Supervisory Control
SCADA HMIs and Alarms] L3[LEVEL 3
Operations Management
Plant Historians and Reports] L4[LEVEL 4
Enterprise Systems
ERP, MES, Business Planning] L0 -->|Analog and Digital IO
4-20 mA, 0-10 V| L1 L1 -->|Fieldbus Protocols
CAN, Profibus, Modbus| L2 L2 -->|Ethernet and OPC UA| L3 L3 -->|ERP Integration and
SQL Databases| L4 style L0 fill:#ffebee,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style L1 fill:#fff8e1,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style L2 fill:#e8f5e9,stroke:#2e7d32,color:#000 style L3 fill:#e3f2fd,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style L4 fill:#ede7f6,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 ``` ## 4.3 Block Diagram — Sensor Signal Conditioning Chain ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Raw Sensor Output
microvolt to millivolt range] --> B[INSTRUMENTATION
AMPLIFIER INA] B --> C[ANTI-ALIASING
LOW PASS FILTER] C --> D[LEVEL SHIFTER
and OFFSET ADJUST] D --> E[12 to 16 BIT ADC] E --> F[MICROCONTROLLER
DSP or FPGA] F --> G[CALIBRATION TABLE
and LINEARIZATION] G --> H[ENGINEERING UNITS
Displayed and Acted Upon] style A fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style B fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style C fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style D fill:#dcedc8,stroke:#33691e,color:#000 style E fill:#b2dfdb,stroke:#00695c,color:#000 style F fill:#b3e5fc,stroke:#01579b,color:#000 style G fill:#d1c4e9,stroke:#311b92,color:#000 style H fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — CAN Bus Multi-Node Network ```mermaid flowchart LR M1[MCU 1
Engine ECU] -->|CAN H and CAN L| BUS[(CAN BUS
Twisted Pair Differential)] M2[MCU 2
Brake ECU] -->|CAN H and CAN L| BUS M3[MCU 3
Airbag ECU] -->|CAN H and CAN L| BUS M4[MCU 4
Body Control] -->|CAN H and CAN L| BUS M5[MCU 5
Infotainment] -->|CAN H and CAN L| BUS BUS -->|120 ohm Termination| T1[Terminator] BUS -->|120 ohm Termination| T2[Terminator] style BUS fill:#fff59d,stroke:#f57f17,color:#000 style M1 fill:#bbdefb,stroke:#0d47a1,color:#000 style M2 fill:#c8e6c9,stroke:#1b5e20,color:#000 style M3 fill:#ffcdd2,stroke:#b71c1c,color:#000 style M4 fill:#e1bee7,stroke:#4a148c,color:#000 style M5 fill:#ffe0b2,stroke:#e65100,color:#000 style T1 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000 style T2 fill:#cfd8dc,stroke:#263238,color:#000 ``` > [!NOTE] > The **two 120 Ω terminators** at the physical ends of the CAN bus are **mandatory** — they prevent signal reflection. This is a common viva question in KTU practical exams. # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]** **Differentiate between an active sensor and a passive sensor. Give one engineering example of each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | Aspect | Active Sensor | Passive Sensor | |--------|---------------|----------------| | **Energy source** | Self-generating (no external excitation required) | Requires external excitation (voltage or current) | | **Output** | Produces its own EMF/signal proportional to measurand | Modulates the excitation signal | | **Example** | **Thermocouple** (Seebeck effect) — generates mV from temperature gradient | **Strain gauge** — resistance changes when stretched; needs excitation | | **Other examples** | Piezoelectric accelerometer, photovoltaic cell | RTD, LVDT, thermistor, Hall-effect sensor | > **[Marking Key — 3 marks]:** 1 mark for definition of each, 1 mark for examples. Partial credit is given for any valid example. --- ### Question 2: **[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]** **List any three differences between serial communication (RS-232) and parallel communication. Mention one typical use case for each.** #### Model Answer (3 Marks): | # | Aspect | RS-232 (Serial) | Parallel | |---|--------|-----------------|----------| | 1 | **Lines used** | 1 data line (plus ground) | 8 (or more) data lines | | 2 | **Speed at short distance** | Slower per byte | Faster per byte | | 3 | **Distance** | Up to **15 m** reliably | Limited to a few meters (skew issues) | | 4 | **Cost / Wiring** | Cheap, 2–3 wires | Expensive, multi-conductor cable | | 5 | **Use case** | PC-to-modem, GPS modules, PLC programming ports | Internal PC buses (PCI, older printer ports, GPIB instruments) | > **[Marking Key — 3 marks]:** 1 mark for each valid difference. Use case carries no extra mark if differences are correct, but earns partial credit if a difference is missed. --- ## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model]** **(a)** With the help of a neat block diagram, describe the **four major functional blocks of a Data Acquisition System (DAS)** in a mechatronic application. Explain the role of an **anti-aliasing filter** with a suitable mathematical justification based on the **Nyquist-Shannon sampling theorem**. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A temperature-measuring mechatronic system uses a **Type-K thermocouple** with sensitivity $S = 41 \, \mu V/^{\circ}C$ and a 10-bit ADC with $V_{ref} = 5.0$ V. The signal is first amplified by an instrumentation amplifier of gain $G = 100$. Determine: **(7 Marks)** 1. The voltage produced by the thermocouple at $T = 200 \,^{\circ}C$. 2. The amplified voltage reaching the ADC. 3. The LSB (resolution) of the ADC. 4. The temperature represented by **1 LSB** in °C. 5. Whether the 10-bit ADC is sufficient, or if upgrading to a 12-bit ADC is justified. Justify with numbers. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — DAS Block Diagram & Anti-Aliasing Filter (7 Marks):** The four functional blocks of a DAS are: 1. **Sensor / Transducer** — converts physical quantity (temperature, pressure, displacement) into an electrical signal. 2. **Signal Conditioner** — amplifies weak signals, filters noise, linearizes, and provides isolation. The anti-aliasing filter is housed here. 3. **Multiplexer (MUX) + Sample-and-Hold (S/H)** — allows multiple channels to share a single ADC by time-division; S/H freezes the input during conversion. 4. **ADC + Microcontroller** — converts the conditioned analog signal to digital code; the µC processes, stores, or transmits the data. **Nyquist-Shannon Sampling Theorem:** $$ f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} $$ If this condition is **violated**, frequency components above $f_s/2$ will **fold back** (alias) into the lower frequency band, producing false readings. For example, a 1.8 kHz vibration signal sampled at $f_s = 2$ kHz appears as a 200 Hz signal — completely wrong. **Why an Anti-Aliasing Filter is Essential:** A real analog filter has a finite roll-off (e.g., a 2nd-order Butterworth attenuates at 40 dB/decade, not infinitely). To keep aliasing error below the ADC's quantization noise, the filter cutoff $f_c$ is typically placed at: $$ f_c \leq 0.45 \cdot f_s $$ This way, by the time the signal reaches $f_s/2$, the filter has attenuated it well below the noise floor. Without this filter, **no amount of digital post-processing** can recover the original signal. > **[Marking Key — 7 marks]:** Block diagram (3 marks) — must include all 4 blocks labelled. Nyquist equation (2 marks). Anti-aliasing justification (2 marks). **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Thermocouple voltage at 200 °C:** $$ V_{TC} = S \times T = 41 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V/°C} \times 200 \text{ °C} $$ $$ V_{TC} = 8200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ V} = 8.2 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 2 — Amplified voltage:** $$ V_{amp} = G \times V_{TC} = 100 \times 8.2 \text{ mV} = 820 \text{ mV} = 0.82 \text{ V} $$ **Step 3 — LSB of 10-bit ADC:** $$ Q_{10} = \frac{V_{ref}}{2^{10}} = \frac{5.0}{1024} \approx 4.883 \text{ mV} $$ **Step 4 — Temperature per LSB:** The ADC's 5 V range corresponds to 200 °C (post-amplification). Therefore: $$ \Delta T_{10} = \frac{200}{1024} \approx 0.195 \,^{\circ}C \text{ per LSB} $$ **Step 5 — Compare with 12-bit ADC:** $$ Q_{12} = \frac{5.0}{4096} \approx 1.221 \text{ mV} $$ $$ \Delta T_{12} = \frac{200}{4096} \approx 0.0488 \,^{\circ}C \text{ per LSB} $$ **Justification:** The 12-bit ADC provides **4× finer resolution** (0.0488 °C vs 0.195 °C). If the application demands better than **0.1 °C accuracy** (e.g., pharmaceutical or scientific use), the 10-bit ADC is **insufficient**, and upgrading to 12-bit (or 16-bit) is justified. For general HVAC use, 10-bit may suffice. > **[Marking Key — 7 marks]:** Step 1 (1.5), Step 2 (1), Step 3 (1), Step 4 (1), Step 5 (1.5 — must include both numerical and engineering justification). --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]** **(a)** Compare the **CAN bus**, **RS-485**, and **I²C** communication protocols under the following heads: physical layer (differential/single-ended), topology, maximum nodes, typical maximum data rate, and primary application domain. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An industrial PLC system uses **Modbus RTU over RS-485** to read a pressure sensor. The data frame contains: 1 start bit, 8 data bits, no parity, and 1 stop bit. The baud rate is **9600 bps**. Calculate: **(7 Marks)** 1. The number of bits in one full character frame. 2. The time taken to transmit one character. 3. The **maximum number of characters per second** the link can carry. 4. If a master polls 10 slaves in a round-robin fashion, each requiring 2 characters of response plus 1 character of request, and an inter-message gap of 3.5 character times (silence interval), compute the **total cycle time** in milliseconds. 5. Is this bus suitable for **real-time control** at a 100 Hz loop rate? Justify. #### Model Solution: **Part (a) — Protocol Comparison (7 Marks):** | Head | CAN | RS-485 | I²C | |------|-----|--------|-----| | **Physical layer** | Differential (CAN-H, CAN-L) | Differential (A, B twisted pair) | Single-ended (SDA, SCL) with open-drain | | **Topology** | Multi-master linear bus | Multi-drop linear bus (half-duplex) | Master-slave bus (multi-master capable) | | **Max nodes** | Practically 30–50 per bus segment | 32 unit loads (256 with repeaters) | 127 (7-bit addressing) | | **Max data rate** | 1 Mbps (Classical) / 5 Mbps (CAN-FD) | 10 Mbps (short), 100 kbps at 1200 m | 100 kbps (Standard), 400 kbps (Fast), 5 Mbps (Ultra-Fast) | | **Distance vs speed trade-off** | 40 m @ 1 Mbps; 1 km @ 50 kbps | 1200 m @ 100 kbps; 10 m @ 10 Mbps | < 1 m typical (PCB-level) | | **Application** | Automotive ECUs, industrial machinery, medical | Industrial SCADA, Modbus networks, motor drives | On-board sensor ICs, EEPROMs, low-speed peripherals | > **[Marking Key — 7 marks]:** 1 mark per row; full credit for any 5 well-explained rows; partial marks for partial table. **Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks):** **Step 1 — Bits per character frame:** $$ N_{bits} = 1_{\text{start}} + 8_{\text{data}} + 0_{\text{parity}} + 1_{\text{stop}} = 10 \text{ bits} $$ **Step 2 — Time per character:** $$ t_c = \frac{N_{bits}}{Baud} = \frac{10}{9600} = 1.0417 \times 10^{-3} \text{ s} = 1.0417 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 3 — Maximum characters per second:** $$ C_{max} = \frac{Baud}{N_{bits}} = \frac{9600}{10} = 960 \text{ characters/second} $$ **Step 4 — Total cycle time for 10 slaves:** Per slave, traffic = 1 request + 2 response = 3 characters. Plus inter-message silence of 3.5 character times. Number of inter-message gaps per cycle = 10 (between each slave). $$ t_{silence} = 10 \times 3.5 \times t_c = 10 \times 3.5 \times 1.0417 \text{ ms} $$ $$ t_{silence} = 36.46 \text{ ms} $$ $$ t_{data} = 10 \times 3 \times t_c = 30 \times 1.0417 \text{ ms} = 31.25 \text{ ms} $$ $$ T_{cycle} = t_{data} + t_{silence} = 31.25 + 36.46 = 67.71 \text{ ms} $$ **Step 5 — Suitability for 100 Hz control:** 100 Hz control loop requires an update period of: $$ T_{ctrl} = \frac{1}{100} = 10 \text{ ms} $$ Since $T_{cycle} = 67.71 \text{ ms} \gg 10 \text{ ms}$, the bus latency is **6.7× higher** than the control period. **Not suitable** for 100 Hz closed-loop control. For such high-speed loops, use **EtherCAT, Profinet IRT, or CANopen with synchronous PDOs** (cycle times ~1 ms). > **[Marking Key — 7 marks]:** Step 1 (1), Step 2 (1), Step 3 (1), Step 4 (2), Step 5 (2 — must compute the 10 ms target and compare explicitly). --- ## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in I/O & Communication Questions:** > > 1. **Forgetting units** — Always write °C, mV, kbps, m, etc. An answer with bare numbers loses 0.5–1 mark per sub-question. > 2. **Confusing baud rate with bit rate** — Baud = symbols/second; for binary NRZ, baud = bps, but the student must mention this assumption. > 3. **Missing anti-aliasing filter** — A common 2-mark loss in DAS questions. Always mention it explicitly. > 4. **Stating "12-bit ADC is better" without calculation** — Examiners want the *quantitative* resolution gain (4× finer LSB). > 5. **Confusing RS-232 with RS-485** — RS-232 is single-ended point-to-point; RS-485 is differential multi-drop. Mixing these = 2-mark loss. > 6. **Forgetting the 120 Ω termination** on CAN bus diagrams. > 7. **Hand-waving "real-time"** — always compare cycle time to control loop period explicitly. > 8. **No shielding/grounding** mentioned in noisy industrial environments — instrumentation questions often expect this. --- ## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember ### ⚡ Rapid-Revision Checklist - **Sensors vs. Actuators**: Sensors = input (energy in → electrical out); Actuators = output (electrical in → physical action out). - **Active vs. Passive Sensors**: Active generates own signal (thermocouple, piezoelectric); Passive needs excitation (RTD, strain gauge). - **Signal Conditioning Chain**: Amplify → Filter → Linearize → Isolate → Level-shift → ADC. - **Wheatstone Bridge** is the canonical interface for resistive sensors (strain gauge, RTD, LVDT). Output for small $\Delta R$ is $V_{out} \approx V_{ex} \cdot \frac{\Delta R}{4R}$. - **ADC Resolution**: $Q = V_{ref}/2^n$. Quantization error $= \pm Q/2$. Each extra bit = 2× finer resolution. - **Nyquist Theorem**: $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$. **Anti-aliasing filter cutoff ≤ 0.45·$f_s$** is engineering practice. - **RS-232**: Single-ended, point-to-point, ≤ 15 m, ±12 V logic, legacy. - **RS-485**: Differential, multi-drop, ≤ 32 nodes, ≤ 1200 m, foundation for Modbus RTU. - **I²C**: 2-wire (SDA, SCL), 7-bit addressing, ≤ 127 nodes, ≤ ~1 m (PCB), master-slave. - **SPI**: 4-wire (MOSI, MISO, SCK, CS), full-duplex, fastest of the common serial protocols. - **CAN**: Differential, multi-master, message-ID arbitration, 1 Mbps classical / 5 Mbps FD, mandatory in automotive. **120 Ω terminators** at both ends. - **Industrial Hierarchy** (ISA-95): Level 0 (field) → Level 1 (PLC/RTU) → Level 2 (SCADA/HMI) → Level 3 (Historian) → Level 4 (ERP). - **Modbus RTU frame**: 1 start + 8 data + (parity) + 1-2 stop bits. Use 3.5-character silence gap to mark frame boundaries. - **Actuator Selection Trade-offs**: Stepper = precise open-loop but slow; Servo = precise closed-loop high speed; Hydraulic = high force; Pneumatic = fast but low precision. - **DC Motor Torque**: $\tau = K_t I_a$. **Back-EMF**: $E_b = K_e \omega$. At steady state, $V_a = E_b + I_a R_a$. - **Hydraulic Force**: $F = P \cdot A$. **Pneumatic** is similar but uses compressible air → softer, less precise. - **Industrial Buses**: Profibus (legacy), Profinet (modern Ethernet-based), EtherCAT (real-time, 100 µs cycle), Modbus (simple, ubiquitous). - **Wireless Protocols**: Wi-Fi (high speed, high power), Bluetooth/BLE (low power, short range), Zigbee (mesh, very low power, IoT). - **Communication speed vs. distance** is *always* a trade-off: faster signaling → shorter reliable distance. - **Shielded twisted pair (STP)** + **single-point grounding** = the default noise-mitigation strategy for industrial cables. - **Open systems interconnection (OSI) 7-layer model** is rarely asked but is useful to map protocols: CAN = Layer 1–2; Modbus = Layer 7; Ethernet = Layer 1–2; Profinet = Layer 1–7. > **Final Exam Tip:** Always frame your answer with **(i) the physical principle, (ii) the mathematical model, (iii) the engineering trade-off, and (iv) a real-world example**. KTU 2024 scheme rewards answers that demonstrate *applied* understanding, not just rote definitions.

topics.title: Fault Findings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Fault Findings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

**Fault Finding** in a mechatronic system refers to the systematic process of **identifying, isolating, localizing, and characterizing** a malfunction, deviation, or degradation within one or more of the integrated subsystems (mechanical, electrical/electronic, control software, sensor, and actuator) such that the root cause of the failure can be determined and corrective action can be initiated.

According to the **ISO 13373 (Condition monitoring and diagnostics of machines)** and **IEC 61508 (Functional safety of electrical/electronic/programmable electronic safety-related systems)** standards adopted in KTU 2024 scheme references, a **fault** is defined as:

> An abnormal condition that may cause a reduction in, or loss of, the capability of a functional unit to perform a required function. A fault is the *cause* of an **error** (deviation from correct state), which in turn is the *cause* of a **failure** (termination of the ability of an element to perform a required function).

In the mechatronics context, faults are not just mechanical breakdowns — they span across the **M**echanical, **E**lectrical, **C**ontrol, **C**omputer, and **S**ensor/Actuator (MECCS) domains simultaneously because the system is *tightly integrated*.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Human Body & a Mechatronic System**
>
> Think of a car (a mechatronic system) as a human body.
> - The **engine, gearbox, brakes** = muscles and skeleton (mechanical subsystem).
> - The **wiring, battery, ECU** = nervous system (electrical/electronic subsystem).
> - The **sensors (oxygen sensor, ABS sensors)** = senses (sensory organs).
> - The **software/firmware** = brain.
> - **Fault finding** = going to a doctor. The mechanic listens to symptoms (vibration, noise, warning lights), runs diagnostic tests (OBD-II scan, multimeter, oscilloscope), and pinpoints whether the problem is in the heart (engine), nerves (wiring), or brain (ECU software).

**Plain-English Takeaway:** A mechatronic system is a *team* of parts working together. When one teammate underperforms, the whole team loses. Fault finding is the detective work that figures out *which* teammate is slacking, *why*, and *how badly*.

## 1.3 Hierarchy of Fault Terminology (KTU 2024 Critical Vocabulary)

| Term | Precise Meaning | Example in a CNC Machine |
|---|---|---|
| **Defect** | Imperfection in a component (latent) | Micro-crack in ball screw |
| **Fault** | Cause of an error; abnormal condition | Broken wire to limit switch |
| **Error** | Deviation of computed/observed value from true value | Encoder reading 1.02 mm when true = 1.00 mm |
| **Failure** | Termination of the system's required function | Spindle stops responding to speed command |
| **Malfunction** | Intermittent or partial failure | Random servo overshoot under load |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Students must distinguish between **fault**, **failure**, and **error**. The examiner frequently tests this 3-mark distinction in Part A questions. A **fault** is the *cause*; an **error** is the *manifestation in data*; a **failure** is the *loss of system function*.

## 1.4 Categories of Faults in Mechatronic Systems

```text
FAULTS
├── 1. HARDWARE FAULTS
│   ├── Mechanical (wear, fracture, misalignment, lubrication loss)
│   ├── Electrical (open circuit, short circuit, earth fault)
│   ├── Electronic (component drift, semiconductor failure)
│   ├── Sensor (bias, scale-factor error, complete dropout, noise)
│   └── Actuator (stiction, dead-band, hysteresis, saturation)
├── 2. SOFTWARE / LOGIC FAULTS
│   ├── Coding errors (infinite loop, buffer overflow)
│   ├── Algorithm errors (wrong PID tuning)
│   └── Timing/synchronization errors
├── 3. EXTERNAL / ENVIRONMENTAL FAULTS
│   ├── EMI / RFI interference
│   ├── Temperature extremes
│   ├── Dust, moisture, vibration
│   └── Power supply disturbances (sag, surge, harmonics)
└── 4. INTERACTION / OPERATIONAL FAULTS
    ├── Human error
    ├── Out-of-specification operation
    └── Lack of maintenance
```

> [!TIP]
> A **single observable failure** in a mechatronic product (e.g., a robotic arm drops its payload) may be caused by faults from *any* of the above categories. The diagnostic procedure must therefore evaluate the system **holistically**, not just the most obvious subsystem.

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Fault-Finding Pipeline (Universal Procedure)

A mechatronic fault-finding exercise always follows the same logical pipeline. Understanding this pipeline is essential because KTU questions (especially 14-mark Part B) often demand an *ordered methodology*.

### Step 1 — Symptom Detection (Observation)
The user/operator notices an abnormal behavior:
- Warning lamps, error codes on HMI
- Unusual noise, vibration, smell, heat
- Reduced performance (slower, less accurate, less powerful)
- Complete non-function

### Step 2 — Data Acquisition (Measurement)
Use instruments to gather quantitative evidence:
- **Multimeter** (voltage, current, resistance, continuity)
- **Oscilloscope / Logic Analyzer** (waveform integrity, timing)
- **Thermal camera** (hot spots in PCB or motor)
- **Vibration analyzer / FFT** (mechanical resonances, bearing faults)
- **Tachometer / Encoder readout** (rotational speed accuracy)

### Step 3 — Hypothesis Generation
Form candidate causes ranked by probability (often guided by **Pareto / 80-20 rule** — 80% of failures come from 20% of causes).

### Step 4 — Isolation & Localization
Apply a *binary-search* style isolation: cut the system in half; determine which half is faulty; cut that half again; iterate.

### Step 5 — Root-Cause Confirmation
Verify the hypothesis by replacing/repairing the suspect component and confirming that the symptom disappears.

### Step 6 — Documentation & Feedback
Log the fault, root cause, fix, and time-to-repair. This data drives **predictive maintenance** and **reliability growth**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why "Why-Why" Analysis?** Every fault should be subjected to a 5-Whys analysis to identify the *systemic root cause* rather than the *proximate symptom*. Example: Robot arm stalled → Why? Motor overcurrent → Why? Bearing seized → Why? Lubrication absent → Why? Maintenance schedule lapsed → Why? No automated maintenance logger. The *real* fault is procedural, not mechanical.

## 2.2 Reliability & Maintainability Metrics (Core Quantitative Foundation)

These metrics are the **mathematical language** of fault finding. KTU 2024 scheme questions in Module 4 frequently test these formulas.

### 2.2.1 Mean Time Between Failures (MTBF)

**MTBF** is the average elapsed time between two consecutive failures of a repairable system. It is an *availability* and *reliability* metric.

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{\sum_{i=1}^{N} t_{i}}{N}
$$

where:
- $t_i$ = operating time between the $(i-1)^{th}$ and $i^{th}$ failure
- $N$ = total number of failures observed in the test period

**Unit:** hours (typically)
**Higher MTBF = more reliable system**

### 2.2.2 Mean Time To Repair (MTTR)

**MTTR** is the average time required to perform corrective maintenance (diagnose, repair, verify, restore).

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{\sum_{j=1}^{M} T_{j}}{M}
$$

where:
- $T_j$ = actual repair time for the $j^{th}$ failure
- $M$ = total number of repairs

**Unit:** hours
**Lower MTTR = better maintainability**

### 2.2.3 Failure Rate (λ)

For non-repairable components following an **exponential distribution** (which is appropriate when components are in their *useful life* period, between infant-mortality and wear-out), the failure rate is constant:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant} = \frac{1}{\text{MTBF}}
$$

The reliability function (probability of surviving without failure up to time $t$) is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t} = e^{-t / \text{MTBF}}
$$

### 2.2.4 Inherent Availability ($A_i$)

**Availability** is the fraction of time the system is *up* and *ready to perform*:

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note that $A_i$ uses MTBF *and* MTTR — this is why fault-finding skills (which reduce MTTR) directly translate to **higher availability** of the mechatronic asset.

### 2.2.5 Probability of Failure on Demand (PFD) — Safety Instrumented Systems

For SIL (Safety Integrity Level) calculations per IEC 61508:

$$
\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \frac{1}{2} \, \lambda_D \, T_{proof}
$$

where:
- $\lambda_D$ = dangerous failure rate (per hour)
- $T_{proof}$ = proof-test interval (hours)

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Quantity | Unit | When to Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N}$ | Mean Time Between Failures | hours | Reliability reporting |
| 2 | $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_j}{M}$ | Mean Time To Repair | hours | Maintainability analysis |
| 3 | $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$ | Constant failure rate | failures/hr | Useful-life modeling |
| 4 | $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ | Reliability function | dimensionless | Survival probability |
| 5 | $F(t) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t}$ | Cumulative failure prob. | dimensionless | Probability of failure by time $t$ |
| 6 | $A_i = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$ | Inherent availability | dimensionless | Uptime fraction |
| 7 | $\text{PFD}_{\text{avg}} = \tfrac{1}{2} \lambda_D T_{proof}$ | Probability of Failure on Demand | dimensionless | SIL assessment |
| 8 | $\text{RPN} = S \times O \times D$ | Risk Priority Number | score | FMEA scoring |
| 9 | $\text{Weibull shape } \beta$ | Failure mode indicator | dimensionless | $\beta < 1$ infant mortality, $\beta = 1$ random, $\beta > 1$ wear-out |
| 10 | $\text{SNR} = 10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{signal}}{P_{noise}}\right)$ | Signal-to-Noise Ratio | dB | Sensor fault detection |

> [!WARNING]
> **Table Syntax Note:** All absolute-value / dividing bars in the table above use the LaTeX `\dfrac` (display-style fraction) and `\tfrac` (text-style fraction) commands, which render as a horizontal bar — *not* a vertical pipe. This avoids breaking the markdown table parser.

## 2.4 Real-World Utility of Fault Finding Theory

- **Industrial Robotics:** Cell downtime in an automotive welding line costs ≈ \$20,000/minute. A 1-hour MTTR reduction yields six-figure annual savings.
- **Aerospace:** Fault-tolerant flight control systems (e.g., Airbus A320 FBW) use *redundancy* + *voting* — three or more identical sensors, output is the median, dissenting sensor is flagged as faulty.
- **Automotive:** OBD-II (On-Board Diagnostics, Generation 2) is a standardized **fault-finding protocol** mandated globally. Diagnostic Trouble Codes (DTCs) such as P0301 (Cylinder 1 Misfire) follow a strict format.
- **Medical Devices:** Pacemakers and infusion pumps use IEC 62304 + IEC 61508 fault-detection software — a missed fault is *life-critical*.
- **Smart Manufacturing / Industry 4.0:** Vibration spectrum, motor current signature analysis (MCSA), and thermal imaging feed ML models for **predictive** fault finding — *before* a failure occurs.

## 2.5 Diagnostic Strategies in Mechatronic Systems

Three principal philosophies are taught in KTU 2024 Module 4:

### A. Signal-Based Diagnostics (Data-Driven)
- Compare real-time sensor signals (vibration, current, acoustic emission, temperature) against **baseline** or **threshold** values.
- Techniques: Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), wavelet analysis, envelope analysis, statistical process control (SPC) charts.
- **Pros:** Non-invasive, real-time, no system model needed.
- **Cons:** Requires good historical baseline; sensitive to operating-condition drift.

### B. Model-Based Diagnostics (Analytical Redundancy)
- Build a mathematical model of the *healthy* system (e.g., state-space equations).
- Generate **residuals**: $r(t) = y_{measured}(t) - y_{model}(t)$.
- A non-zero residual indicates a fault. The residual's *pattern* identifies *which* fault.
- **Pros:** No hardware redundancy needed; sensitive to incipient faults.
- **Cons:** Model must be accurate; sensitive to disturbances and parameter drift.

### C. Knowledge-Based / AI Diagnostics
- Uses expert rules, fuzzy logic, neural networks, or Bayesian networks trained on historical fault data.
- **Pros:** Handles non-linearity, multiple simultaneous faults, noisy data.
- **Cons:** "Black-box"; needs large labeled datasets; requires retraining.

> [!TIP]
> A complete mechatronic diagnostic system typically **fuses** all three strategies — sensor-level (A), model-level (B), and decision-level (C).

# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — The Reliability Function from the Failure-Rate Definition

We start from the fundamental definition of the **failure rate** (also called the *hazard function*):

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t \mid T > t)}{\Delta t}
$$

Read this carefully: it is the *conditional* probability of failing in the next instant, *given* the component has already survived to time $t$.

**Step 1** — Rewrite the conditional probability using the definition of conditional probability:

$$
\lambda(t) = \lim_{\Delta t \to 0} \frac{P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t)}{P(T > t) \cdot \Delta t}
$$

**Step 2** — Define the **cumulative distribution function** of failure time as $F(t) = P(T \leq t)$, so $P(t < T \leq t + \Delta t) \approx f(t) \, \Delta t$, where $f(t)$ is the probability density function. Also, $P(T > t) = 1 - F(t) = R(t)$, the reliability function.

Substituting:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{f(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 3** — Recognize that the PDF is the derivative of the CDF: $f(t) = \dfrac{dF(t)}{dt} = -\dfrac{dR(t)}{dt}$.

Therefore:

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

**Step 4** — Rearrange into a separable first-order ODE:

$$
\lambda(t) \, dt = -\frac{dR(t)}{R(t)}
$$

**Step 5** — Integrate both sides from $0$ to $t$, with the boundary condition $R(0) = 1$ (a brand-new component is certainly functional at $t = 0$):

$$
\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau = -\int_{1}^{R(t)} \frac{dR}{R} = -\ln R(t)
$$

**Step 6** — Exponentiate to obtain the **general reliability function**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\int_{0}^{t} \lambda(\tau) \, d\tau}
$$

**Step 7** — Specialize to the **constant failure rate** case ($\lambda(t) = \lambda = \text{constant}$), which corresponds to the *useful life* region of the **bathtub curve**:

$$
R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}
$$

This is the exponential reliability model that KTU questions test most often.

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:** The exponential distribution is *memoryless* — $P(T > s + t \mid T > s) = P(T > t)$. A mechatronic system in its useful life behaves as if "today is day 1" regardless of how long it has already run. This counter-intuitive property is what makes the exponential model so useful in random-failure regimes.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Weibull Distribution (Failure-Mode Identification)

The exponential distribution assumes constant $\lambda$, which is often too restrictive. The **Weibull distribution** lets $\lambda$ vary with time and is therefore used in mechatronic fault finding to *classify* failure modes.

The Weibull CDF is:

$$
F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

and the corresponding reliability function is:

$$
R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

where:
- $\beta$ = **shape parameter** (dimensionless) — encodes failure *type*
- $\eta$ = **scale parameter** (same unit as $t$) — represents the *characteristic life* (time at which 63.2% of the population has failed)

**Derivation of the Weibull failure rate** by differentiating the general reliability function derived above:

$$
\lambda(t) = -\frac{1}{R(t)} \cdot \frac{dR(t)}{dt}
$$

Step (a): Differentiate $R(t)$:

$$
\frac{dR}{dt} = -\beta \cdot \frac{t^{\beta - 1}}{\eta^{\beta}} \cdot e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}
$$

Step (b): Divide by $-R(t)$:

$$
\lambda(t) = \frac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\frac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}
$$

Step (c): Interpret $\beta$:

- If $\beta < 1$: $\lambda(t)$ is *decreasing* with time → **infant mortality** (manufacturing defects, poor installation)
- If $\beta = 1$: $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta$ = constant → **random failures** (exponential distribution as a special case)
- If $\beta > 1$: $\lambda(t)$ is *increasing* with time → **wear-out failures** (fatigue, aging, corrosion)

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-style):**

A mechatronic conveyor's motor bearing has been monitored for 5000 hours, with 8 failures observed. Cumulative operating times between failures: 720, 650, 810, 590, 700, 680, 540, 310 hours. Calculate MTBF, MTTR (assume average repair time per failure = 4.2 hours), and inherent availability.

**Step 1 — MTBF:**

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{720 + 650 + 810 + 590 + 700 + 680 + 540 + 310}{8} = \frac{5000}{8} = 625 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR:**

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{8 \times 4.2}{8} = 4.2 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Availability:**

$$
A_i = \frac{625}{625 + 4.2} = \frac{625}{629.2} \approx 0.9933 \text{ or } 99.33\%
$$

**Step 4 — Probability of surviving 1000 hours without failure** (assuming exponential model):

$$
R(1000) = e^{-1000 / 625} = e^{-1.6} \approx 0.2019
$$

i.e., ≈ 20% probability of survival to 1000 hours.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Students often forget to add the *test duration* to the MTTR numerator calculation when failures happen "during" a test, vs. "at the end". Read the question wording carefully: *operating time between failures* vs. *total downtime including repair*.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Fault Signatures by Subsystem

| Subsystem | Typical Fault | Observable Signature | Diagnostic Instrument |
|---|---|---|---|
| DC Motor (brushes) | Brush wear | Sparking, current spikes, brush chatter | Oscilloscope on armature current |
| Bearing | Race defect, lubrication loss | Vibration at BPFO/BPFI frequencies; temperature rise | Accelerometer + FFT, thermal probe |
| Ball screw | Backlash, wear | Positional error, repeatability loss | Laser interferometer, dial indicator |
| Hydraulic actuator | Seal leakage, air ingress | Drift, slow response, foamy oil | Pressure gauge, flow meter |
| Pneumatic system | Filter clog, regulator drift | Slow cycle, pressure sag | Pressure transducer |
| Encoder | Disc contamination, LED fade | Missed counts, jitter | Logic analyzer, scope on A/B channels |
| Power supply | Capacitor aging | Ripple, sagging under load | Scope on output, ESR meter |
| PLC / Controller | I/O card failure, memory error | Specific input stuck, watchdog trips | PLC diagnostic software |
| Sensor (analog) | Bias, drift, noise | Offset from expected, low SNR | Calibrated reference + multimeter |
| Sensor (digital) | Stale data, communication loss | CRC errors, frozen readings | Protocol analyzer (Modbus, CAN) |
| Software | Infinite loop, race condition | Watchdog timeout, frozen HMI | Source debugger, log inspection |
| Communication bus | EMI, termination mismatch | CRC errors, retries, latency | Bus analyzer, oscilloscope |

## 3.4 Derivation 3 — Vibration-Based Fault Frequency for a Rolling-Element Bearing

A bearing fault (inner race, outer race, ball/roller defect) produces vibration at a *characteristic frequency* determined purely by the bearing geometry. The four fundamental **fault frequencies** are:

$$
f_{BPFO} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_r \left(1 - \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha\right)
$$

$$
f_{BPFI} = \frac{N}{2} \cdot f_r \left(1 + \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha\right)
$$

$$
f_{BSF} = \frac{D}{2d} \cdot f_r \left[1 - \left(\frac{d}{D}\right)^2 \cos^2\alpha\right]
$$

$$
f_{FTF} = \frac{1}{2} \cdot f_r \left(1 - \frac{d}{D} \cos\alpha\right)
$$

where:
- $f_r$ = shaft rotational frequency (Hz)
- $N$ = number of rolling elements
- $d$ = rolling-element diameter
- $D$ = pitch diameter
- $\alpha$ = contact angle

**Worked Example:**

A motor bearing has $N = 9$ balls, $d = 12$ mm, $D = 60$ mm, $\alpha = 0^{\circ}$, and runs at 1500 rpm.

**Step 1** — Compute $f_r$:

$$
f_r = \frac{1500}{60} = 25 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 2** — Compute BPFO (Ball Pass Frequency Outer race):

$$
f_{BPFO} = \frac{9}{2} \times 25 \times \left(1 - \frac{12}{60}\cos 0^{\circ}\right)
$$

$$
f_{BPFO} = 4.5 \times 25 \times (1 - 0.2) = 112.5 \times 0.8 = 90 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 3** — Compute BPFI (Ball Pass Frequency Inner race):

$$
f_{BPFI} = 4.5 \times 25 \times (1 + 0.2) = 112.5 \times 1.2 = 135 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 4** — Compute BSF (Ball Spin Frequency):

$$
f_{BSF} = \frac{60}{2 \times 12} \times 25 \times \left[1 - \left(\frac{12}{60}\right)^2 \right]
$$

$$
f_{BSF} = 2.5 \times 25 \times (1 - 0.04) = 62.5 \times 0.96 = 60 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 5** — Compute FTF (Fundamental Train Frequency — cage frequency):

$$
f_{FTF} = \frac{1}{2} \times 25 \times (1 - 0.2) = 0.5 \times 25 \times 0.8 = 10 \text{ Hz}
$$

**Step 6** — FFT interpretation:** A peak at 90 Hz in the vibration spectrum ⇒ outer-race defect. A peak at 135 Hz ⇒ inner-race defect. Sidebands at harmonics of $f_r$ around these peaks confirm a developing fault.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Trick for KTU exam:** "**O**uter = **O**uter = $(1 - d\cos\alpha/D)$" — both BPFO and FTF use the *minus* sign. "**I**nner = **I**nside = $(1 + d\cos\alpha/D)$". "**B**all = $D/(2d)$".

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Residual Generation in Model-Based Fault Detection

Consider a linear time-invariant mechatronic system described by a state-space model:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}(t)
$$

where $\mathbf{x} \in \mathbb{R}^n$, $\mathbf{u} \in \mathbb{R}^m$, $\mathbf{y} \in \mathbb{R}^p$. This is the *healthy* model running in parallel with the real plant.

**Step 1** — The real plant has a fault vector $\mathbf{f}(t)$ added to the dynamics and measurements:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}_p(t) = \mathbf{A}\mathbf{x}_p(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t) + \mathbf{E}_f \mathbf{f}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}_p(t) = \mathbf{C}\mathbf{x}_p(t) + \mathbf{F}_f \mathbf{f}(t)
$$

**Step 2** — Run an **observer** (e.g., Luenberger observer or Kalman filter) that estimates $\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t)$:

$$
\dot{\hat{\mathbf{x}}}(t) = \mathbf{A}\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t) + \mathbf{B}\mathbf{u}(t) + \mathbf{L}\left(\mathbf{y}_p(t) - \hat{\mathbf{y}}(t)\right)
$$

**Step 3** — Compute the **output residual** (the diagnostic signal):

$$
\mathbf{r}(t) = \mathbf{y}_p(t) - \hat{\mathbf{y}}(t) = \mathbf{y}_p(t) - \mathbf{C}\hat{\mathbf{x}}(t)
$$

**Step 4** — In the no-fault case, $\mathbf{f}(t) = 0$ and the observer converges: $\mathbf{r}(t) \to 0$ (with appropriate choice of observer gain $\mathbf{L}$ placing eigenvalues of $(\mathbf{A} - \mathbf{L}\mathbf{C})$ in the left half-plane).

**Step 5** — When a fault occurs, $\mathbf{r}(t) \neq 0$. The **decision function** is:

$$
J(\mathbf{r}) = \sqrt{\mathbf{r}^{\top}(t) \mathbf{r}(t)} \;\; \overset{?}{\underset{H_0}{\gtrless}} \;\; T_h
$$

where $T_h$ is a threshold chosen to balance false alarms against missed detections (typically via Neyman–Pearson criterion for a given false-alarm rate).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:** The residual is essentially the *error between what the healthy model predicts and what the real plant produces*. When a sensor biases, an actuator sticks, or a parameter drifts, the residual grows. The *direction* of the residual vector (when $\mathbf{r} \in \mathbb{R}^p$, $p > 1$) indicates *which* fault occurred — this is called **fault isolation**.
