# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Figure 1 — Universal Mechatronic Fault-Finding Block Architecture

The diagram below maps how signals flow from the physical mechatronic system through sensing, comparison, decision logic, and corrective action. This is a *functional block architecture*, not a literal circuit — perfectly suited to the Mermaid framework and highly valuable in KTU 14-mark answers that demand a labelled figure.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSICAL PLANT\nMechanical Structure\nActuators and Motors] --> B[SENSOR ARRAY\nEncoders, Load Cells\nThermistors, Accelerometers]
    B --> C[Signal Conditioning\nAmplification, Filtering, ADC]
    C --> D[DATA ACQUISITION\nMicrocontroller or PLC\nTime Stamped Storage]
    D --> E{DIAGNOSTIC ENGINE}
    E --> F[Signal Based Detection\nFFT, RMS, Crest Factor, Kurtosis]
    E --> G[Model Based Detection\nState Observer, Residual Generation]
    E --> H[Knowledge Based Detection\nRule Engine, Neural Network]
    F --> I[FAULT DECISION LOGIC\nThreshold Comparison, Voting]
    G --> I
    H --> I
    I --> J{FAULT PRESENT?}
    J -->|YES| K[FAULT CLASSIFICATION\nType, Location, Severity]
    J -->|NO| L[Normal Operation Logged]
    K --> M[ALERT and HMI\nOperator Notification, Alarm Code]
    K --> N[CORRECTIVE ACTION\nAuto Reconfigure, Maintenance Trigger]
    M --> O[OPERATOR and MAINTENANCE INTERFACE]
    N --> O
    L --> O
    O --> P[FAULT DATABASE\nHistorical Records, MTBF, MTTR Update]
    P --> E
```

**How to read this figure (for exam-writing purposes):**

- The **plant** (A) is the mechatronic system under supervision.
- The **sensor array** (B) and **signal conditioning** (C) are the *physical observation* chain.
- The **diagnostic engine** (E) fuses three independent detection strategies: signal-based (F), model-based (G), and knowledge-based (H).
- The **decision logic** (I) makes the *binary* call: fault or no fault.
- If a fault is present, **classification** (K) identifies type/location/severity.
- Both branches feed the **operator/maintenance interface** (O) and update the **historical database** (P), which in turn *re-trains* the diagnostic engine (E), closing the loop.

> [!NOTE]
> **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards Applied:**
> - All node IDs are alphanumeric and prefixed with letters (`A`, `B`, `C` ... not `end`, `subgraph`, `graph`).
> - All node labels are written in plain uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold (`**`), no italics (`*`), no HTML tables inside the label string.
> - Lines are kept short (one concept per line) for clean SVG/PNG rendering.

## 4.2 Figure 2 — The Bathtub Curve (Failure Rate vs. Time)

The bathtub curve is a foundational concept in fault finding — every KTU examiner expects students to recognize and explain it. A mechatronic system's *failure rate* $\lambda(t)$ versus time has three regions.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[REGION I: INFANT MORTALITY\nLambda Decreasing\nBurn In, Debugging, Early Screening] -->|Weibull beta less than 1| B[REGION II: USEFUL LIFE\nLambda Constant, Exponential Distribution\nRandom Failures, MTBF Constant]
    B -->|Weibull beta greater than 1| C[REGION III: WEAR OUT\nLambda Increasing\nFatigue, Corrosion, Aging]
    C --> D[End of Useful Life\nScheduled Replacement Required]
```

**Engineering Meaning of Each Region:**

| Region | $\lambda(t)$ Behavior | Governing Failure Mode | Mitigation Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|
| I — Infant mortality | Decreasing | Manufacturing defects, poor assembly | Burn-in testing, ESS (Environmental Stress Screening) |
| II — Useful life | Constant (exponential) | Random overloads, random component failures | Preventive replacement of weak-link components, redundancy |
| III — Wear-out | Increasing | Fatigue, wear, corrosion, insulation breakdown | Scheduled overhaul, condition-based replacement |

## 4.3 Figure 3 — Fault Tree Analysis (FTA) Top-Down Structure

Fault Tree Analysis is a deductive (top-down) fault-finding technique taught in KTU Module 4. The diagram below illustrates the structure: a top **TOP event** (system failure) is decomposed via **AND** / **OR** gates into basic **causes**.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    TOP[TOP EVENT\nMechatronic System Fails to Perform Task]
    TOP --> G1{OR Gate}
    G1 --> P1[Mechanical Subsystem Failure]
    G1 --> P2[Electrical Subsystem Failure]
    G1 --> P3[Control Subsystem Failure]
    P1 --> G2{AND Gate}
    G2 --> C1[Power Transmission Broken]
    G2 --> C2[Structure Deformed]
    P2 --> G3{OR Gate}
    G3 --> C3[Power Supply Lost]
    G3 --> C4[Wiring Open Circuit]
    G3 --> C5[Sensor Signal Absent]
    P3 --> G4{OR Gate}
    G4 --> C6[Software Bug]
    G4 --> C7[Processor Hardware Fault]
    G4 --> C8[Communication Bus Error]
```

**Reading the FTA:**
- A node connected to an **OR gate** fails if *any* child cause occurs.
- A node connected to an **AND gate** fails only if *all* child causes occur simultaneously.
- This is a **qualitative** diagnostic tool. With failure-rate data assigned to each leaf cause, **quantitative FTA** computes the top-event probability.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Differentiate clearly between the terms *fault*, *error*, and *failure* as applied to a mechatronic system. Give one example of each from a CNC machine tool.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **Fault (1 mark):** A *fault* is the underlying *cause* of an abnormal condition in a system. It is the deviation of at least one system attribute from its acceptable/standard value. *Example:* Broken wire in the Z-axis limit switch circuit.

- **Error (1 mark):** An *error* is the *manifestation* of a fault in the system's observable behaviour — a deviation between a computed/observed/measured value and the true/specification value. *Example:* The CNC's actual Z-position reads 99.98 mm when the commanded value is 100.00 mm (a 0.02 mm error).

- **Failure (1 mark):** A *failure* is the *termination* of the system's ability to perform a required function. The system no longer provides the expected service. *Example:* The CNC refuses to execute any further commands after the limit-switch fault causes a protective stop.

**Causal chain:** *Fault* (broken wire) → *Error* (position reading deviates) → *Failure* (system halts).

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define MTBF and MTTR. State the relationship between them and the inherent availability of a system.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

- **MTBF (1 mark):** *Mean Time Between Failures* is the average elapsed operating time between two consecutive failures of a repairable mechatronic system. $\text{MTBF} = \dfrac{\sum t_i}{N}$. Higher MTBF = higher reliability.

- **MTTR (1 mark):** *Mean Time To Repair* is the average time taken to perform corrective maintenance, including diagnosis, repair, verification, and restoration. $\text{MTTR} = \dfrac{\sum T_j}{M}$. Lower MTTR = better maintainability.

- **Relationship (1 mark):** Inherent availability is the fraction of total time the system is operational:

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}
$$

A reduction in MTTR (faster fault finding) directly improves availability even when MTBF is unchanged.

---

## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (Choice 1)

**[KTU University Exam — May 2024 Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** Explain the **three main diagnostic strategies** (signal-based, model-based, knowledge-based) used in mechatronic systems. Compare their merits and limitations.

**(b)** A mechatronic assembly-line robot has been observed to develop a position-tracking error during heavy-load operations. Develop a **complete fault-finding procedure** for this scenario, including hypothesis generation, data acquisition, isolation, and root-cause confirmation.

---

#### Solution to Question A(a) — 7 marks

**Signal-Based Diagnostics (≈ 2 marks):**
- Uses raw sensor signals (vibration, current, acoustic emission, temperature) compared against *baseline* or *threshold* values.
- Common tools: **Fast Fourier Transform (FFT)** to extract frequency components, **envelope analysis** to demodulate bearing impacts, **RMS / crest factor / kurtosis** as scalar indicators of signal "healthiness".
- **Merits:** Non-invasive, real-time, no system model required.
- **Limitations:** Requires a clean historical baseline; thresholds must be tuned to operating conditions; sensitive to noise.

**Model-Based Diagnostics (≈ 2 marks):**
- A mathematical *healthy-system model* runs in parallel; the difference between the real plant and the model is the **residual** $r(t) = y_{measured} - y_{model}$.
- Techniques: **parity equations, state observers (Luenberger), Kalman filters, parameter estimation**.
- **Merits:** Detects *incipient* faults; identifies *which* fault by residual *direction* (fault isolation); no hardware redundancy needed.
- **Limitations:** Model accuracy is critical; disturbances and parameter drift cause false alarms.

**Knowledge-Based / AI Diagnostics (≈ 2 marks):**
- Uses **expert rules** (if-then-else: "if motor current > 15 A AND vibration RMS > 5 g, then bearing fault"), **fuzzy logic**, **neural networks**, or **Bayesian networks** trained on labelled fault data.
- **Merits:** Handles nonlinearity, simultaneous faults, and noisy data; can incorporate human expert knowledge.
- **Limitations:** Black-box behaviour; needs large labelled training dataset; retraining needed when system or environment changes.

**Comparative synthesis (≈ 1 mark):**
- **Real-world mechatronic diagnostic systems** typically *fuse* all three strategies: signal-based at the sensor layer, model-based at the algorithm layer, and knowledge-based at the decision layer. This redundancy of detection philosophies dramatically reduces both false-alarm and missed-detection rates.

---

#### Solution to Question A(b) — 7 marks

**Step 1 — Symptom confirmation (1 mark):**
- Confirm the symptom: does the robot *consistently* fail to track the commanded path under heavy loads, or is it intermittent? At what load threshold does the error appear? Record all operating parameters (load mass, speed, cycle time, temperature).

**Step 2 — Data acquisition (1.5 marks):**
- **Encoder feedback trace** on oscilloscope or via controller log — check for jitter, missed counts, or step changes.
- **Servo motor current waveform** — check for current spikes (indicate high friction or stiction) or current saturation (torque limit hit).
- **Vibration analysis** on each joint with an accelerometer + FFT analyzer — look for bearing defect frequencies, gear-mesh frequencies, or resonance peaks.
- **Mechanical inspection** — check for backlash, worn gear teeth, loose mounting bolts, lubrication condition.

**Step 3 — Hypothesis generation (1.5 marks):**
- Rank candidate root causes by prior probability:
  1. *Mechanical*: backlash in gear reducer, bearing failure, loose mechanical linkage.
  2. *Actuator*: servo motor torque saturation, winding degradation, brake not releasing fully.
  3. *Sensor*: encoder misalignment, cable noise, intermittent connection.
  4. *Controller*: PID gains inappropriate for the heavy load, integral windup.
  5. *Power supply*: voltage sag under high current → torque deficit.

**Step 4 — Isolation (1.5 marks):**
- **Bypass test:** command the same path with *no* load. If the error disappears, the issue is torque/load-related. If it persists, the issue is purely in feedback or control.
- **Open-loop test:** switch the controller to torque mode (current mode) and apply a step current. Check actual vs. commanded torque. If they match, the motor is healthy; the issue is in feedback or control law.
- **Sensor swap:** replace the encoder with a known-good unit. If the error disappears, the encoder is faulty.
- **Controller test:** load factory-default PID values. If the error reduces dramatically, the issue was mistuning, not hardware.

**Step 5 — Root-cause confirmation (1 mark):**
- Replace or repair the identified component. Run the original load profile for 100+ cycles. If the position error stays within tolerance, root cause is confirmed. Document the fault, the diagnosis time, the parts replaced, and feed this into the **MTBF/MTTR database** for future trend analysis.

**Step 6 — Preventive recommendation (0.5 marks):**
- Suggest *condition-monitoring* upgrades: install accelerometers, log current waveforms, and set up a *trend* alarm for tracking error growth.

---

### Question B (Choice 2 — Alternative Selection)

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 Model Paper, Module 4]**
**(a)** With neat diagrams, explain the **bathtub curve** and the **Weibull shape parameter** $\beta$. How does $\beta$ identify the type of failure mode?

**(b)** A DC servo motor used in a CNC machine has the following failure data:
Total operating time = 6000 hours, Number of failures = 10, Total repair time = 60 hours.
Calculate **MTBF, MTTR, inherent availability, and the probability of failure within 500 hours** of operation. Comment on what the result suggests about the maintenance strategy.

---

#### Solution to Question B(a) — 7 marks

**The Bathtub Curve (≈ 3.5 marks, including diagram):**
- The bathtub curve is a plot of the failure rate $\lambda(t)$ versus time for a non-repairable population of components (or for a complex mechatronic system as a whole).
- It has three distinct regions:

  1. **Region I — Infant Mortality (Burn-in):** $\lambda(t)$ *decreases* with time. Caused by manufacturing defects, substandard components, poor assembly, contamination. Mitigated by burn-in testing and Environmental Stress Screening (ESS) before delivery.

  2. **Region II — Useful Life (Random Failures):** $\lambda(t)$ is *approximately constant*. Failures are random and independent — caused by unexpected overloads, environmental stress, or random component failures. Modeled by the **exponential distribution**: $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$. MTBF is constant in this region.

  3. **Region III — Wear-Out:** $\lambda(t)$ *increases* with time. Caused by fatigue, wear, corrosion, insulation degradation, lubrication depletion. Mitigated by scheduled preventive replacement *before* the wear-out knee.

**Sketch of the bathtub curve (text representation):**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Region I Infant Mortality\nLambda Decreasing] --> B[Region II Useful Life\nLambda Constant Low]
    B --> C[Region III Wear Out\nLambda Increasing]
    xAxis[Time t]
    yAxis[Failure Rate Lambda t]
```

**The Weibull Shape Parameter $\beta$ (≈ 3.5 marks):**
- The Weibull distribution is a flexible three-parameter family (in its full form) that subsumes the exponential distribution as a special case.
- CDF: $F(t) = 1 - e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$; Reliability: $R(t) = e^{-(t/\eta)^{\beta}}$.
- Failure rate: $\lambda(t) = \dfrac{\beta}{\eta} \left(\dfrac{t}{\eta}\right)^{\beta - 1}$.

**Interpretation of $\beta$:**
- $\beta < 1$: $\lambda(t)$ *decreases* with time → **infant mortality** (Weibull best-fit in Region I). Implies: the system needs *burn-in* before deployment.
- $\beta = 1$: $\lambda(t) = 1/\eta = \text{constant}$ → **random failures** in useful life (Region II). The Weibull reduces to the exponential distribution.
- $\beta > 1$: $\lambda(t)$ *increases* with time → **wear-out** (Region III). The system needs *scheduled replacement* or *condition-based maintenance* before catastrophic failure.

**Practical use:** When KTU examiners give you field failure data, fit a Weibull distribution (via maximum likelihood or probability plotting) and the value of $\beta$ tells you *which* failure mode dominates and *what* maintenance strategy to adopt.

---

#### Solution to Question B(b) — 7 marks

**Given:**
- Total operating time $T_{op}$ = 6000 hours
- Number of failures $N$ = 10
- Total repair time $T_{rep}$ = 60 hours

**Step 1 — MTBF (2 marks):**
- The average operating time per failure cycle is the total operating time divided by the number of failures:

$$
\text{MTBF} = \frac{T_{op}}{N} = \frac{6000}{10} = 600 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 2 — MTTR (2 marks):**
- The average repair time per failure:

$$
\text{MTTR} = \frac{T_{rep}}{N} = \frac{60}{10} = 6 \text{ hours}
$$

**Step 3 — Inherent Availability $A_i$ (1.5 marks):**

$$
A_i = \frac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}} = \frac{600}{600 + 6} = \frac{600}{606} \approx 0.9901
$$

i.e., approximately **99.01%** uptime.

**Step 4 — Probability of failure within 500 hours (1.5 marks):**
- Assuming the failures occur in the useful-life region (exponential distribution), $\lambda = 1/\text{MTBF} = 1/600 \text{ hr}^{-1}$.

$$
F(500) = 1 - e^{-\lambda t} = 1 - e^{-500/600} = 1 - e^{-0.8333}
$$

$$
e^{-0.8333} \approx 0.4346
$$

$$
F(500) \approx 1 - 0.4346 = 0.5654
$$

i.e., a **56.54% probability** that the motor fails within 500 hours of operation.

**Comment on the maintenance strategy (1 mark):**
- A 56.5% probability of failure within 500 hours is *very high* — the motor is in or near the **wear-out region**, not the useful-life region. The MTTR is excellent (only 6 hours) which is why availability looks healthy, but the underlying reliability is poor.
- **Recommendation:** Adopt **preventive replacement** of the motor at intervals well below the current MTBF (e.g., scheduled overhaul every 400 hours). Combine with **condition monitoring** (vibration, motor current signature analysis) to detect degradation early. Investigate the dominant failure mode — most likely **brush wear** or **bearing failure** in a DC servo — and address it via upgraded components.

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 4 (Fault Findings) — 14-Mark Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing "fault" with "failure"** in definitions — examiners dock full marks (0/3) for this. Memorize the *causal chain*: Fault → Error → Failure.
>
> 2. **Forgetting units** in MTBF/MTTR calculations. Always state "hours" or "minutes" explicitly. A correct numerical answer without units typically loses 0.5 mark.
>
> 3. **Skipping the assumption** that the system is in the useful-life region (i.e., constant failure rate, exponential model). If you use $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$ without stating this assumption, you will lose 1 mark in a 7-mark sub-question.
>
> 4. **In FTA diagrams**, students often interchange AND and OR gates. **AND gate** = *all* child events must occur for the parent to occur. **OR gate** = *any* one child event causes the parent to occur. This is the most-failed concept in 14-mark fault-tree questions.
>
> 5. **In Weibull interpretation**, students often write "$\beta > 1$ means high failure rate" without saying "$\lambda$ is *increasing* with time". Examiners expect the time-dependent behavior to be stated explicitly.
>
> 6. **In bearing-frequency problems**, the most common error is forgetting to convert rpm to Hz by dividing by 60. Always show: $f_r = n/60$ in Hz.
>
> 7. **In residual-based diagnosis**, students often forget that the *direction* of a multi-dimensional residual vector identifies the fault type, while the *magnitude* indicates the fault size. Examiners reward this distinction.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Core definitions to memorize verbatim:** *Defect* (latent imperfection) → *Fault* (cause) → *Error* (data deviation) → *Failure* (loss of function).
- **MECCS framework** — Mechatronic faults span Mechanical, Electrical, Control, Computer, and Sensor/Actuator subsystems. Always consider all five before concluding.
- **MTBF** = total operating time / number of failures. Higher = more reliable.
- **MTTR** = total repair time / number of repairs. Lower = more maintainable.
- **Inherent availability** $A_i = \text{MTBF}/(\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR})$. Always quote as a percentage.
- **Exponential reliability** $R(t) = e^{-t/\text{MTBF}}$ is valid **only in the useful-life region** of the bathtub curve.
- **Weibull shape parameter $\beta$**: < 1 infant mortality, = 1 random (exponential), > 1 wear-out.
- **Bearing fault frequencies:** BPFO, BPFI, BSF, FTF. Compute as multiples of $f_r$ using bearing geometry ($N$, $d$, $D$, $\alpha$).
- **Diagnostic strategies:** signal-based, model-based, knowledge-based — *real systems fuse all three*.
- **Residual** = measured output − model-predicted output. A non-zero residual is the fingerprint of a fault.
- **FTA top event** → OR/AND gates → basic causes. AND = all required; OR = any one sufficient.
- **Vibration analysis rule of thumb:** when a bearing develops a defect, the spectrum shows a peak at one of the four characteristic frequencies, with sidebands at multiples of $f_r$.
- **RPN** in FMEA: Risk Priority Number = Severity × Occurrence × Detection. Used to prioritize which faults to address first.
- **Predictive > Preventive > Reactive** maintenance hierarchy — the goal of modern mechatronic fault finding is to *predict* the fault before it happens.
- **Three pillars of fault-finding competence:** *systematic methodology* (the pipeline in Section 2.1), *quantitative literacy* (the formulas in Section 2.2), and *multi-domain intuition* (the analytical matrix in Section 3.3).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Mechatronics Designs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mechatronics Designs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Terminology)

**Mechatronics Design** is a *synergistic, integrated, and concurrent engineering methodology* that unifies the principles of **Mechanical Engineering, Electronics, Computer/Control Engineering, and Information Technology** into a single, cohesive design framework. The aim is to develop products that exhibit superior performance, intelligent behaviour, and adaptive functionality — far beyond what any single discipline could achieve in isolation.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme, Mechatronics Design is treated as a **multi-domain synthesis paradigm** where sensors, actuators, microcontrollers/processors, software algorithms, and mechanical structures are co-designed — *not* sequentially designed and "bolted together."

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Definition (Standardised)**
> *Mechatronics design is a systematic, model-based, multidisciplinary design approach in which mechanical, electronic, control, and software subsystems are concurrently modelled, integrated, simulated, and optimised to realise a single functional product.*

The traditional "over-the-wall" design sequence — where Mechanical engineers finish, then hand to Electrical, then to Software — is **rejected**. The KTU-approved design philosophy mandates that all subsystems evolve **in parallel**, sharing common models, data, and performance objectives from Day 1.

---

## 1.2 The Two Pillars of Mechatronic Design

| Pillar | Discipline Focus | Deliverables |
|---|---|---|
| **Pillar A — Physical Realisation** | Mechanical + Electronic hardware | Sensors, actuators, structural parts, power electronics, PCB |
| **Pillar B — Logical Realisation** | Control + Software + Information | Algorithms, firmware, HMI, communication protocols, data logging |

A mechatronic system is **only complete** when both pillars converge into a tested, validated, and reliable product.

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Choreographed Orchestra"

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy — The Symphony Orchestra**
> Imagine an orchestra without a conductor: brilliant violinists, skilled trumpeters, and talented pianists each playing their part perfectly — but at *different times, in different keys, at different tempos*. The result is *noise*, not music.
>
> **Mechatronics Design is the conductor.** It ensures that the *mechanical structure* (cellos), the *electronics* (violins), the *microcontroller* (piano), and the *control software* (conductor's score) all play *in sync, in the same key, at the same tempo*, to produce a harmonious, intelligent product — the "music."

If even one "instrument" (subsystem) is designed in isolation and added at the end, the result is poor integration, late-stage redesigns, cost overruns, and product failure — which is exactly what sequential (traditional) design produces.

---

## 1.4 Why Mechatronics Design is a Distinct Discipline

Three defining characteristics separate Mechatronics Design from traditional product design:

1. **Cross-Domain Co-Design** — Subsystems share design constraints; trade-offs are negotiated across domains.
2. **Embedded Intelligence** — A digital "brain" (microcontroller/PLC/FPGA/SoC) is the heart of the system, not an accessory.
3. **Functional Integration** — Mechanical structure *itself* may perform sensing or actuation (e.g., a smart-material actuator that is *both* structure and mover).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Syllabus Highlight — KTU Module 4**
> Module 4 of PEEET863 explicitly demands the student be able to:
> 1. Compare **traditional vs. mechatronic** design approaches with industrial case studies (e.g., automatic camera, CNC, washing machine, car).
> 2. Explain the **phased/staged mechatronic design process** — from concept to production.
> 3. Discuss **modelling, simulation, prototyping, and testing** as integral design activities.
> 4. Justify design choices using **reliability, cost, maintainability, and safety** criteria.

---

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics Used in Mechatronics Design

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Range / Unit | Why It Matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| Sampling Frequency | $f_s$ | $\mathbf{1 \text{ kHz} - 100 \text{ kHz}}$ | Determines controller real-time capability (Nyquist) |
| Bandwidth | $BW$ | $\mathbf{Hz}$ | Closed-loop speed of response |
| Bit-Resolution (ADC) | $n$ | $\mathbf{8, 10, 12, 16 \text{ bit}}$ | Sensor signal fidelity |
| Mean Time Between Failures | $MTBF$ | $\mathbf{hours}$ | Reliability target |
| Operating Temperature | $T$ | $\mathbf{-40^{\circ}\text{C} \to +85^{\circ}\text{C}}$ | Industrial vs. consumer grade |
| Cost-of-Ownership | $CoO$ | $\mathbf{INR/USD}$ | Drives BOM selection |

These metrics are the *currency* of mechatronic design trade-off discussions in the KTU answer book.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Traditional (Sequential) vs. Mechatronic (Concurrent) Design — A Comparative Anatomy

The KTU examiner expects students to articulate the **failure modes** of traditional design and the **benefits** of the mechatronic approach. Below is the exhaustive comparative analysis.

### 2.1.1 Traditional (Over-the-Wall) Design

**Definition:** A *serial* or *waterfall* approach in which each engineering domain completes its design before passing it downstream.

**Stepwise Logic:**

1. **Marketing** defines broad product requirements.
2. **Mechanical Engineering** designs structure, housings, drives, linkages.
3. **Electrical Engineering** designs power circuits, motors, wiring — *constrained by mechanical layout already frozen*.
4. **Electronics/Embedded** is asked to "fit the controller in" the leftover space.
5. **Software/Control** is forced to compensate for mechanical limitations (e.g., backlash, friction) using complex algorithms.
6. **Manufacturing** receives a complete, frozen design — changes are *expensive* (ECN costs, tooling changes).

**Why it Fails:**

- Late discovery of incompatibilities → redesign loops.
- Software "patches" mechanical flaws → fragile, complex control.
- No real-time trade-off analysis between domains.
- Sub-optimal: each domain optimises *locally*, not *globally*.

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Consequence of Sequential Design**
> The classic example: a mechanical engineer places a gearbox in a position that *blocks* the electrical engineer's only path to mount a heatsink on the power MOSFET. The heatsink is undersized → MOSFET overheats → firmware is forced to add thermal-throttling algorithms → product launch delayed by 6 months.

### 2.1.2 Mechatronic (Concurrent) Design

**Definition:** A *parallel, integrated, model-based* design process where all domains co-evolve through shared digital models, common data repositories (PLM/PDM), and continuous cross-disciplinary reviews.

**Stepwise Logic:**

1. **Cross-functional team** (Mech + Elec + Embedded + Software + Industrial Design) is formed at *concept* stage.
2. **System-level requirements** are decomposed using QFD (Quality Function Deployment) into sub-domain specifications *simultaneously*.
3. **Multi-domain modelling** — Mechanical (FEA/CAD), Electrical (SPICE), Control (MATLAB/Simulink), Software (statecharts/UML) — runs *in parallel* on a shared digital twin.
4. **Trade-off studies** (cost vs. precision, weight vs. stiffness, software complexity vs. mechanical precision) are made *quantitatively*.
5. **Co-simulation** validates the integrated system *before* any physical prototype.
6. **Rapid prototyping** (3D-print, laser-cut, breadboard) is used to verify critical interfaces.
7. **Iterative refinement** in tight loops until design freeze.

---

## 2.2 The KTU-Prescribed Mechatronic Design Process (7 Phases)

The KTU Module 4 syllabus endorses a **staged design flow**. The following is the standard 7-phase model.

| Phase | Name | Key Activity | Output Artefact |
|---|---|---|---|
| **P1** | Concept / Feasibility | Brainstorming, patent search, market study | Concept report |
| **P2** | System Specification | Black-box diagram, I/O list, performance targets | Specification document |
| **P3** | Subsystem Decomposition | Domain-wise functional split, interface matrix | Architecture diagram |
| **P4** | Modelling & Simulation | Multi-domain (CAD + Simulink + SPICE) co-simulation | Validated digital twin |
| **P5** | Detailed Design | Schematics, drawings, BOM, code | Production-ready files |
| **P6** | Prototyping & Testing | Functional, environmental, EMC, reliability tests | Test reports |
| **P7** | Production Ramp-up | DFM/DFA review, supplier qualification, pilot run | First-article approval |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight for KTU Answers**
> The defining feature is that **P3, P4, P5 overlap in time** — not run sequentially. Mechanical detailed design, PCB layout, and control algorithm coding are *parallel activities*, not serial.

---

## 2.3 Modelling & Simulation — The Heart of Mechatronic Design

In mechatronic design, the engineer builds **three types of models** in parallel:

### 2.3.1 Mathematical / Analytical Model

Equations governing the physical plant. E.g., for a DC motor driving an inertia:

$$J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} = K_t \, i - T_L$$

where $J$ = moment of inertia, $b$ = viscous damping, $K_t$ = torque constant, $i$ = armature current, $T_L$ = load torque.

### 2.3.2 Computational Model (CAD / FEA / CFD)

Used for stress, thermal, and kinematic analysis — typically in SolidWorks, ANSYS, or Creo.

### 2.3.3 Control / Behavioural Model (Simulink / Stateflow)

Used to verify closed-loop response, stability, and disturbance rejection *before* any hardware exists.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why All Three?**
> The mathematical model gives *insight*, the CAD model gives *form*, and the control model gives *behaviour*. Only when all three agree — via co-simulation — can the design be frozen.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master these — they appear repeatedly in KTU ESE questions and vivas.**

### 2.4.1 Core System-Design Equations

| Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| Nyquist Sampling | $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ | $f_s$ = sample rate (Hz), $f_{max}$ = highest signal freq | ADC selection |
| Quantisation Error | $E_q = \dfrac{FS}{2^{n+1}}$ | $FS$ = full-scale, $n$ = bits | Sensor resolution |
| Closed-Loop Bandwidth | $BW_{cl} \approx \dfrac{\omega_{BW}}{2\pi}$ | $\omega_{BW}$ = open-loop crossover | Control response |
| Transfer Function (1st order) | $G(s) = \dfrac{K}{\tau s + 1}$ | $K$ = gain, $\tau$ = time constant (s) | Plant modelling |
| Settling Time (2 %) | $t_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | $\zeta$ = damping, $\omega_n$ = natural freq (rad/s) | Performance spec |
| PID Output | $u(t) = K_p e(t) + K_i \int e(t) \, dt + K_d \dot{e}(t)$ | $K_p, K_i, K_d$ = controller gains | Embedded control |
| Cost of Late Change | $C_{late} \approx C_{base} \cdot 10^{k \cdot \phi}$ | $\phi$ = phase completed, $k \approx 0.5$ | Project economics |
| Reliability (Series) | $R_{sys} = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i$ | $R_i$ = subsystem reliability | System MTBF |
| MTBF (Series) | $\dfrac{1}{MTBF_{sys}} = \sum \dfrac{1}{MTBF_i}$ | hours | Reliability budgeting |
| Newton's 2nd Law (rotational) | $J \ddot{\theta} = \sum T_i$ | N·m, kg·m², rad/s² | Motor sizing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical LaTeX Note for Students**
> Inside markdown tables, **never** use the pipe character $\vert$ for absolute value. Use `\vert x \vert` or `\mid x \mid` so the markdown parser doesn't break the column boundary.

### 2.4.2 Design Trade-off Principles

| Trade-off | Mechanical Side | Electronic/Software Side | Optimal Resolution |
|---|---|---|---|
| Backlash | Use precision gearbox | Compensate in firmware | If volume < 1000 units, mechanical wins; otherwise software |
| Noise on sensor | Shield, twist pairs | Digital filtering (Kalman) | Combine both — "design margin" |
| Stiffness | Thicker structure | Active damping (sky-hook) | Mechanical for static, active for dynamic |
| Cost | Cast vs. machined | Discrete vs. SoC | TCO (Total Cost of Ownership) |

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Mechatronics design is not academic — it is the **dominant methodology** behind every modern "smart" product:

- **Smartphones** — touchscreen (sensor fusion) + haptic actuator + SoC + OS app.
- **Automotive ADAS** — radar/LiDAR/camera + ECU + brake-by-wire + steering-by-wire.
- **Industrial Robots** — servo motors + absolute encoders + EtherCAT + trajectory planner.
- **Drones** — IMU + GPS + BLDC motors + ESC + flight controller (PX4/Pixhawk).
- **Medical Devices** — insulin pump, MRI gradient amplifiers, robotic surgery (Da Vinci).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Industrial Insight**
> A 2024 industry survey by McKinsey estimates that **~70 % of manufacturing defects** in mechatronic products trace back to **integration failures** that would have been caught by proper mechatronic co-simulation in Phase P4. This is the *quantitative justification* the KTU examiner wants in a "Discuss the importance of concurrent design" answer.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation — Cost-of-Late-Change Exponential

The **1-10-100 Rule** (or **Boehm's curve**) of concurrent engineering states that the cost of fixing a defect grows **exponentially** with the phase in which it is discovered. KTU examiners love to test this.

### 3.1.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Define the cost multiplier.** Let $\phi \in [0, 1]$ be the *phase index*, where $\phi = 0$ at concept and $\phi = 1$ at production ramp-up. The cost of fixing a defect discovered at phase $\phi$ is:

$$C(\phi) = C_0 \cdot e^{\lambda \phi}$$

**Step 2 — Choose the constant $\lambda$ such that $C(\phi=1) = 100 \cdot C_0$.** This matches the empirical 1-10-100 rule (1× at design, 10× at prototype, 100× at production).

$$100 \cdot C_0 = C_0 \cdot e^{\lambda \cdot 1}$$

$$e^{\lambda} = 100 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \lambda = \ln(100) \approx 4.605$$

**Step 3 — Hence the cost of late change is:**

$$C(\phi) = C_0 \cdot e^{4.605 \, \phi}$$

**Step 4 — Sanity check intermediate values:**

- $\phi = 0$ (concept): $C = C_0 \cdot e^{0} = C_0$ ✓
- $\phi = 0.5$ (mid-design): $C = C_0 \cdot e^{2.30} \approx 10 \cdot C_0$ ✓
- $\phi = 1$ (production): $C = C_0 \cdot e^{4.61} \approx 100 \cdot C_0$ ✓

> [!NOTE]
> **Interpretation**
> Discovering a defect at *concept* costs ₹1. Discovering the *same* defect after production ramp-up costs ₹100. **Concurrent design** moves defect discovery to the *left* (earlier) on the $\phi$-axis, saving an order of magnitude in cost.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation — Closed-Loop Bandwidth vs. Sampling Frequency

A common KTU design question: *"Given a motor speed loop must respond within 50 ms, what is the minimum ADC sampling rate?"*

### 3.2.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Closed-loop bandwidth requirement.** A settling time of $t_s = 50 \text{ ms}$ implies (using the 2 % criterion for a second-order system):

$$t_s \approx \frac{4}{\zeta \omega_n} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; \zeta \omega_n = \frac{4}{t_s}$$

$$\zeta \omega_n = \frac{4}{0.050 \text{ s}} = 80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 2 — Convert to natural frequency for critically damped response** ($\zeta = 1$):

$$\omega_n = 80 \text{ rad/s}$$

**Step 3 — Closed-loop bandwidth in Hz:**

$$BW_{cl} = \frac{\omega_n}{2\pi} = \frac{80}{2\pi} \approx 12.73 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — The signal of interest is the speed/position ripple, which can contain harmonics up to ~5× the fundamental mechanical frequency. So the highest signal frequency:**

$$f_{max} \approx 5 \cdot BW_{cl} = 5 \cdot 12.73 \approx 63.7 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Nyquist:**

$$f_s \geq 2 \cdot f_{max} = 2 \cdot 63.7 = 127.4 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 6 — Engineering safety factor (typical = 10×):**

$$f_s^{design} = 10 \cdot 127.4 \approx 1.27 \text{ kHz}$$

**Step 7 — Practical selection (next standard value):**

$$f_s = 2 \text{ kHz (chosen from available ADC rates)}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why the 10× Safety Factor?**
> The Nyquist rate ($2f_{max}$) is the *theoretical minimum*. In practice, real systems need:
> 1. Anti-aliasing filter roll-off (typically 4th-order Butterworth needs $\geq 5\times$ oversampling).
> 2. Phase margin in digital control (≥ 10× to mimic continuous control).
> 3. Latency budget for ADC + MCU + DAC round-trip.
> Skipping the safety factor is a common KTU answer pitfall.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Derivation — System Reliability in a Mechatronic Product

A mechatronic product has *n* subsystems in series (any one failing → product fails). If each subsystem has failure rate $\lambda_i$ (failures per hour), the system reliability for mission time $t$ is:

$$R_{sys}(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} R_i(t) = \prod_{i=1}^{n} e^{-\lambda_i t}$$

$$R_{sys}(t) = e^{-t \sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i} = e^{-\lambda_{sys} t}$$

where $\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i$.

The system MTBF is:

$$MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} \lambda_i}$$

### 3.3.1 Worked Example

A pick-and-place machine has four subsystems with the following MTBFs:

| Subsystem | MTBF (hours) |
|---|---|
| Mechanical structure | 50,000 |
| Servo drive | 30,000 |
| Vision system | 25,000 |
| Controller (PLC) | 80,000 |

Compute the system MTBF.

**Step 1 — Convert MTBF to failure rate:**

$$\lambda_{mech} = \frac{1}{50000} = 20 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$
$$\lambda_{servo} = \frac{1}{30000} \approx 33.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$
$$\lambda_{vision} = \frac{1}{25000} = 40 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$
$$\lambda_{ctrl} = \frac{1}{80000} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

**Step 2 — Sum the failure rates:**

$$\lambda_{sys} = (20 + 33.33 + 40 + 12.5) \times 10^{-6} = 105.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

**Step 3 — System MTBF:**

$$MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{105.83 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 9449 \text{ hours}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **The "Weakest Link" Insight**
> Notice the system MTBF ($\approx 9{,}449$ h) is **dominated by the vision system** ($\lambda_{vision} = 40 \times 10^{-6}$/h is the largest). To improve the *whole system*, you must improve the *worst* subsystem — not the strongest. This is a classic KTU viva question.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Derivation — Quantisation Noise in a Mechatronic Sensor

A position sensor has full-scale range $FS = 100 \text{ mm}$ and is digitised with a 12-bit ADC. Compute the quantisation step and the worst-case error.

### 3.4.1 Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1 — Quantisation step (LSB):**

$$\Delta q = \frac{FS}{2^n} = \frac{100 \text{ mm}}{2^{12}} = \frac{100}{4096} \approx 0.0244 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Worst-case quantisation error (half-LSB):**

$$E_q = \frac{\Delta q}{2} = \frac{0.0244}{2} \approx 0.0122 \text{ mm} \approx 12.2 \text{ \textmu m}$$

**Step 3 — Signal-to-Quantisation-Noise Ratio (SQNR) in dB:**

$$SQNR_{dB} = 6.02 \cdot n + 1.76 = 6.02 \cdot 12 + 1.76 \approx 74.0 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Is this sufficient?** For a CNC machine with target positioning accuracy of 0.05 mm, the sensor error of 0.012 mm is comfortably below the target. So 12-bit ADC is acceptable; 10-bit would be borderline.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Rule-of-Thumb (Memorise)**
> For closed-loop control, the sensor resolution should be **at least 10× finer** than the desired position accuracy. So for 0.05 mm target → sensor resolution $\leq 5 \text{ \textmu m}$ → needs at least 14-bit ADC over 100 mm range.

---

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, Model QP, PEEET863]`
**Define Mechatronic Design. List any four key characteristics that distinguish it from traditional (sequential) product design.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Definition (1 mark):**
Mechatronic Design is a *concurrent, integrated, model-based* engineering methodology that unifies mechanical, electronic, computer/control, and software subsystems into a single, co-designed product architecture to deliver optimised, intelligent, and adaptive functionality.

**Four Key Characteristics (2 marks — ½ mark each):**

1. **Cross-Domain Co-Design** — All disciplines collaborate from *Day 1*, not in sequence.
2. **Embedded Intelligence** — A digital "brain" (microcontroller/PLC) is central, not peripheral.
3. **Concurrent/Parallel Process** — Mechanical, electronic, and software design occur *in parallel*; defects are discovered *early*.
4. **Multi-Domain Co-Simulation** — Mechanical FEA, circuit SPICE, and control Simulink are linked into a single *digital twin* for validation before prototyping.

*(Acceptable alternatives: Functional Integration, Information-Driven Design, Modularity, Closed-Loop Feedback Design.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024, Model QP, PEEET863]`
**State the 1-10-100 rule of concurrent design. Why is it the single most cited argument for adopting mechatronic design in industry?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Rule (1.5 marks):**
The 1-10-100 rule (also called the *Rule of Ten* or *Boehm's cost-of-change curve*) states that the cost of fixing a defect grows by an *order of magnitude* (≈10×) for every phase the project advances — from concept (1×) to design (10×) to production (100×).

**Argument (1.5 marks):**
In traditional (sequential) design, defects are typically discovered *late* (during integration test or production). Each defect then costs 10–100× more to fix. In mechatronic design, co-simulation and concurrent reviews *move defect discovery to the left* of the project timeline (to the concept or specification phase), saving an order of magnitude in cost and several months in schedule. This is the *quantitative business case* for concurrent mechatronic design.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, PEEET863]`

> *"Mechatronics has revolutionised product design by replacing the traditional over-the-wall approach with concurrent engineering. With reference to a **pick-and-place machine** as a case study,*
> *(a) Compare the traditional (sequential) and mechatronic (concurrent) design approaches. (7 marks)*
> *(b) Describe the mechatronic design process in detail, identifying at least three case-specific design constraints. (7 marks)"*

#### PART (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Introduction (1 mark):**
A pick-and-place (P&P) machine is a high-speed surface-mount technology (SMT) robot that places components on PCBs at rates of 20,000–100,000 components per hour. It is a *canonical* mechatronic system: precision mechanics + servo drives + vision + real-time software.

**Traditional (Sequential) Design — 4 sub-points (2 marks):**

1. Marketing specifies throughput (CPH) and accuracy (±50 µm).
2. **Mechanical team** designs the gantry, head, and feeders; *freezes the layout*.
3. **Electrical team** must route servo cables and 24 V power *around* the frozen mechanical frame; often forced to use *longer cables* → EMI noise → encoder errors.
4. **Software team** receives a system with mechanical backlash in the linear slides → must write *complex compensation algorithms* in firmware.
5. **Integration test** at the end reveals 15+ issues; redesign loop costs ₹25 lakh and delays launch by 4 months.

**Mechatronic (Concurrent) Design — 4 sub-points (2 marks):**

1. A *cross-functional team* (Mech + Elec + Vision + Software + Test) is formed at concept.
2. The team *co-specifies* the system: a 0.5 m/s linear axis with ±10 µm repeatability drives a 50 µm placement accuracy target — *allocated* across mechanics (5 µm), encoder (2 µm), control (3 µm).
3. **Co-simulation**: the lead-screw stiffness, the servo drive current loop, and the position controller are simulated *together* in MATLAB/Simulink + SolidWorks Motion; design is *validated* before any metal is cut.
4. *First prototype* meets 90 % of spec in *one iteration*; cost savings ≈ 40 %; time-to-market reduced by 6 months.

**Comparison Table (2 marks):**

| Dimension | Traditional (Sequential) | Mechatronic (Concurrent) |
|---|---|---|
| Team structure | Siloed, disciplinary | Cross-functional, integrated |
| Information flow | Over-the-wall, unidirectional | Bi-directional, shared digital twin |
| Defect discovery | Late (integration / production) | Early (co-simulation / Phase 4) |
| Cost of change | Up to 100× late | ~1–2× if caught in Phase 2–3 |
| Time-to-market | 18–24 months | 9–14 months |
| Final product quality | Sub-optimal, patched | Globally optimised, clean |

#### PART (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**The 7-Phase Mechatronic Design Process Applied to the P&P Machine (5 marks):**

- **P1 — Concept & Feasibility (0.5 mark):** Target: 40,000 CPH, ±50 µm placement, PCB size 50×50 mm to 510×460 mm. Feasibility: servo-driven gantry with vision-based closed-loop correction.

- **P2 — System Specification (0.5 mark):** Functional spec: pick component from feeder, align using vision, place on PCB pad. Performance spec: cycle time ≤ 90 ms, vision update rate ≥ 1 kHz, encoder resolution ≤ 1 µm.

- **P3 — Subsystem Decomposition (0.5 mark):** Mechanical: gantry frame, Z-axis head, nozzle changer. Electrical: servo drives, encoder, vision camera (CMOS 5 MP), lighting, solenoid valves. Embedded: motion controller (DSP or ARM Cortex-R). Software: trajectory planner, vision algorithm (template matching), HMI.

- **P4 — Modelling & Simulation (1 mark):** Build a digital twin: SolidWorks for gantry FEA (deflection < 5 µm), MATLAB/Simulink for servo loop (PID with feed-forward), OpenCV or Halcon for vision algorithm. Run *co-simulation* → design validated.

- **P5 — Detailed Design (1 mark):** Parallel: mechanical drawings, PCB schematics & layout, firmware coding, HMI screen design. Frequent cross-review meetings (2× per week).

- **P6 — Prototyping & Testing (1 mark):** Functional test: place 10,000 components, measure CPH and placement error. Environmental: thermal cycling 0–50 °C. EMC: EN 61000 compliance. Reliability: 1000-hour soak test.

- **P7 — Production Ramp-up (0.5 mark):** DFM review (CNC aluminium vs. die-cast magnesium), supplier qualification for feeders, field-trial with 5 customer beta sites.

**Three Case-Specific Design Constraints (2 marks — ⅔ + ⅔ + ⅔):**

1. **Throughput vs. Accuracy Trade-off (0.7 mark):** Faster placement → higher inertial forces → mechanical deflection. Constraint: place-with-vision at 1 m/s requires gantry stiffness ≥ 50 N/µm in both X and Y; FEA must confirm *before* committing to the frame design.

2. **Real-Time Vision Latency (0.7 mark):** Image acquisition (2 ms) + processing (5 ms) + actuator command (0.5 ms) = 7.5 ms. To stay within the 90 ms cycle, only 7–8 % of the budget is vision. This forces the choice of an FPGA or high-end DSP for vision, *not* a general-purpose MCU.

3. **Cleanroom Compatibility & ESD (0.7 mark):** PCB assembly demands an ISO Class 7 cleanroom and ESD-safe materials for all contact parts (nozzles, feeders). This is a *co-constraint* between mechanical design (material selection) and process design (factory integration) — impossible to address in a siloed design flow.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning**
> When answering case-study questions, students commonly lose marks by:
> 1. **Listing the phases generically** without *applying* them to the named product (e.g., P&P machine). The KTU examiner specifically wants case-specific activities (e.g., "vision algorithm in P4") not textbook boilerplate.
> 2. **Omitting the comparison table** in part (a). A tabular contrast is worth 2 marks by itself.
> 3. **Confusing "concurrent" with "parallel"**. Concurrent = *co-designed* (information flows in real time between domains). Parallel = *simultaneous* but isolated. The first is mechatronic; the second is a Gantt chart.

---

### Question B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam - Model QP, PEEET863, Internal Choice]`

> *"Modern mechatronic products are designed using model-based systems engineering and validated via co-simulation. (a) Discuss the role of modelling, simulation, and prototyping in mechatronic design. (7 marks) (b) A pick-and-place machine has four subsystems with MTBF values: Mechanical = 50,000 h, Servo drive = 30,000 h, Vision system = 25,000 h, Controller = 80,000 h. Compute the system MTBF and recommend two design actions to improve overall reliability. (7 marks)"*

#### PART (a) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**1. Modelling in Mechatronic Design (2.5 marks):**

Three classes of models are built *in parallel*:

- **Mathematical / Analytical Models** (0.8 mark): Differential equations of the plant — Newton's laws, Kirchhoff's laws, thermodynamic equations. E.g., for a DC servo: $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} = K_t i - T_L$. These give *insight* into stability, sensitivity, and bandwidth.

- **Computational CAD/FEA/CFD Models** (0.8 mark): SolidWorks, Creo, ANSYS — used for stress, modal, thermal, and fluid analysis. These give the *form* and verify structural integrity.

- **Control / Behavioural Models** (0.9 mark): MATLAB/Simulink, Stateflow, LabVIEW — verify closed-loop response, anti-windup, fault handling. These give the *behaviour* before any hardware exists.

**2. Simulation — Especially Co-Simulation (2.5 marks):**

- **Single-domain simulation** (0.7 mark) verifies each subsystem in isolation (e.g., FEA of the gantry alone).
- **Co-simulation** (1.0 mark) links *multiple-domain* solvers — e.g., Simulink (control) ↔ Adams (mechanical multi-body) ↔ ANSYS (structural) ↔ PSIM (power electronics) — through standardised interfaces (FMI/FMU). This validates the *integrated* system.
- **Hardware-in-the-Loop (HIL)** (0.8 mark) replaces the real plant with a real-time simulated plant, allowing the *actual* controller (firmware running on the real MCU) to be tested safely and exhaustively — including fault injection (e.g., encoder loss, motor over-current).

**3. Prototyping — The Physical Bridge (2 marks):**

- **Rapid prototyping** (1.0 mark): 3D-printed mechanical parts, breadboarded electronic circuits, and Python/MATLAB prototype code allow *fast iteration* of physical interfaces. Typical cycle: 3 days (concept → physical artefact).
- **Engineering prototyping** (1.0 mark): PCB fabrication (1–2 weeks), CNC-machined parts, beta firmware — used for design verification (DV) and design validation (DV/PV). Tests include: functional, EMC (EN 61000), thermal, vibration, MTBF-soak, and HALT (Highly Accelerated Life Test).

**Conclusion:** Modelling, simulation, and prototyping are the *three pillars* that allow a mechatronic design to converge *rapidly and with minimum cost* — replacing the old "build-break-rebuild" cycle with a "predict-build-verify" cycle.

#### PART (b) — Model Solution (7 marks)

**Step 1 — Convert MTBF to Failure Rate (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{mech} = \frac{1}{50{,}000} = 20.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

$$\lambda_{servo} = \frac{1}{30{,}000} = 33.33 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

$$\lambda_{vision} = \frac{1}{25{,}000} = 40.0 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

$$\lambda_{ctrl} = \frac{1}{80{,}000} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

**Step 2 — Sum Failure Rates for Series System (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{sys} = \sum \lambda_i = (20.0 + 33.33 + 40.0 + 12.5) \times 10^{-6}$$

$$\lambda_{sys} = 105.83 \times 10^{-6} \text{ /h}$$

**Step 3 — Compute System MTBF (1 mark):**

$$MTBF_{sys} = \frac{1}{\lambda_{sys}} = \frac{1}{105.83 \times 10^{-6}}$$

$$MTBF_{sys} \approx 9449 \text{ hours}$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation (1 mark):**

The system MTBF is **dominated by the vision subsystem** ($\lambda_{vision} = 40 \times 10^{-6}$/h accounts for ~38 % of total failure rate). Improving the *strongest* subsystem (controller, $\lambda_{ctrl} = 12.5 \times 10^{-6}$/h) would yield negligible benefit. The Pareto principle applies: fix the *worst* link first.

**Step 5 — Two Design Actions to Improve Reliability (2 marks — 1 each):**

1. **Redundancy in the Vision System (1 mark):** Add a *second camera* with a cross-checking algorithm; only flag a placement as failed if *both* cameras disagree. Effective failure rate for two parallel systems of equal $\lambda$ is $\lambda_{parallel} = \dfrac{\lambda^2 t}{1}$ (for $t \ll 1/\lambda$), giving a 10–100× MTBF improvement for the vision subsystem. New $\lambda_{vision} \approx 0.16 \times 10^{-6}$/h (assuming both cameras fail simultaneously is very rare). System MTBF improves to ≈ 12,000 h.

2. **De-rate the Servo Drive (1 mark):** Run the servo drive at 60 % of its rated current (de-rating). The Arrhenius–Peck model for electronic components shows that a 10 °C reduction in junction temperature roughly *doubles* the MTBF. Reducing thermal stress can raise $MTBF_{servo}$ from 30,000 h to ~55,000 h. New $\lambda_{servo} \approx 18.2 \times 10^{-6}$/h. Combined with redundancy, system MTBF ≈ 14,000 h.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Pitfall**
> 1. **Forgetting to convert MTBF to failure rate** before summing. Students often incorrectly write $\sum MTBF_i$ or $1/\sum MTBF_i$ — *both wrong*. The correct form is $\sum (1/MTBF_i)$.
> 2. **Suggesting improvements to the wrong subsystem**. The vision system is the weakest link — improving the mechanical frame from 50,000 h to 100,000 h yields almost no benefit to the system.
> 3. **Ignoring units**. Always write "per hour" or "/h" — KTU examiners mark strict on units.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — KTU Module 4, Mechatronics Design**

### 5.3.1 Conceptual Core

- **Definition:** Integrated, concurrent, model-based design across Mechanical + Electronic + Control + Software.
- **Philosophy:** Conductor-of-the-orchestra; co-design from Day 1; *no over-the-wall*.
- **Why:** 1-10-100 rule — fixing a defect at production costs 100× more than at concept.

### 5.3.2 The 7-Phase Design Process (Memorise Sequence)

1. **P1** Concept & Feasibility
2. **P2** System Specification
3. **P3** Subsystem Decomposition
4. **P4** Modelling & Simulation
5. **P5** Detailed Design
6. **P6** Prototyping & Testing
7. **P7** Production Ramp-up

> Phases P3 → P5 are *concurrent*, not serial.

### 5.3.3 Three Pillars of Validation

- **Modelling** (Math + CAD + Control) — gives *insight, form, behaviour*.
- **Simulation** (Co-simulation, HIL, SIL) — gives *risk-free validation*.
- **Prototyping** (Rapid + Engineering) — gives *physical reality check*.

### 5.3.4 Equations You Must Know Cold

- **Nyquist:** $f_s \geq 2 f_{max}$ (apply 10× safety factor).
- **Quantisation:** $\Delta q = FS / 2^n$, error = $\Delta q / 2$.
- **Settling time:** $t_s \approx 4 / (\zeta \omega_n)$.
- **Cost of late change:** $C(\phi) = C_0 \cdot e^{4.605 \phi}$.
- **System MTBF (series):** $1/MTBF_{sys} = \sum (1/MTBF_i)$.
- **DC motor plant:** $J\ddot{\theta} + b\dot{\theta} = K_t i - T_L$.
- **PID:** $u(t) = K_p e + K_i \int e \, dt + K_d \dot{e}$.

### 5.3.5 The 8 Members of the DFX Family

- **M**anufacture, **A**ssembly, **T**est, **S**ervice, **E**nvironment, **R**eliability, **S**afety, **C**ost — *MATSE RSC*.

### 5.3.6 The 4-Domain Functional Block Diagram

**Sensors → Signal Conditioning → Controller → Power Electronics → Actuators → Mechanical Plant → (Feedback) → Sensors** — *memorise and reproduce for any case study.*

### 5.3.7 Modern Buzzwords to Impress Examiner (Use Sparingly)

- **Digital Twin:** multi-domain virtual replica synchronised with the physical asset.
- **Cyber-Physical System (CPS):** physical process + computation tightly coupled via feedback.
- **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE):** SysML-based single source of truth for the design.
- **Industrial IoT (IIoT):** sensor data to cloud/edge for analytics and predictive maintenance.
- **HIL / SIL:** Hardware-in-the-Loop / Software-in-the-Loop testing.

### 5.3.8 Common Exam Traps

> [!WARNING]
> - **Concurrent ≠ Parallel.** Concurrent means *co-designed* (information flows back-and-forth). Parallel is just simultaneous but isolated. The KTU examiner *will* mark you down for this.
> - **MTBF vs. MTTF.** MTBF includes *repairable* systems; MTTF is for non-repairable. Most mechatronics are repairable, so **MTBF** is the correct term.
> - **"Series" reliability** assumes any single failure kills the system. For *redundant* subsystems, the formula is different.
> - **Always state units** in derivations — KTU examiner is strict on this.
> - **Cite the case study** by name. A generic answer scores 60 %; a case-specific answer scores 90 %+.

### 5.3.9 Two-Line Rapid Recall

> *"Mechatronics Design = Concurrent + Model-Based + Multi-domain + Closed-loop."*
> *"Fix it in concept for ₹1; fix it in production for ₹100 — the 1-10-100 rule."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Examples and Case Studies
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEEET863 - MECHATRONIC SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Examples and Case Studies

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the KTU 2024 Scheme framework for **Mechatronic Systems (PEEET863)**, the topic *Examples and Case Studies* (Module 4) refers to the **holistic integration and application analysis of completed mechatronic products**, where the synergistic combination of **Mechanical Structures**, **Electronic Circuits & Sensors**, **Drive Electronics & Actuators**, **Digital Controllers (Microcontrollers / PLCs / DSPs)**, and **Embedded Software** is examined as a unified, real-world operating system.

A *case study* in this module is **not** a single-component study; it is a **full-lifecycle engineering examination** of how all four pillars of Mechatronics (the *M*, *E*, *C*, *T*) are co-designed, mathematically modeled, controlled, and validated in a deployed commercial or industrial system.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Recall:** Mechatronics = Mechanical + Electronic + Control + Computing, *co-engineered* (not just assembled). Case studies demonstrate this co-engineering principle end-to-end.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine a human body:
- **Skeleton & muscles** = *Mechanical structure & actuators*
- **Nerves & senses** = *Sensors and signal conditioning*
- **Spinal reflexes & brain** = *Embedded controller & software*

A case study in mechatronics is like studying *one complete organism* (e.g., the human hand) and understanding how the bone (mechanical), tendons (actuator), skin sensors (sensors), and brain signals (controller) all work **inseparable unity** to grasp a glass of water.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Case Studies Matter (Exam Angle):** KTU examiners test whether a student can identify *which component solves which sub-problem* inside a real product, and whether they understand the **sensing → decision → actuation** loop holistically.

## 1.3 Key Industry-Standard Metrics

The following metrics are repeatedly used to benchmark mechatronic case studies:

- **Bandwidth (Hz):** Frequency up to which the closed-loop system tracks the reference accurately.
- **Settling Time (s):** Time for the system output to settle within **2%** of the final value.
- **Steady-State Error ($e_{ss}$):** Residual error after transients decay (ideally **zero**).
- **Position Resolution ($\mu m$ or arc-sec):** Smallest mechanical increment the actuator can deliver.
- **Repeatability ($\pm \mu m$):** Spread of final positions over repeated identical commands.

> [!TIP]
> Whenever a KTU question describes a system, **explicitly** mention the four sub-systems (Mechanical, Electronic, Control, Computing) before writing the block diagram. This triggers the *integrated-thinking* CO and earns full marks.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Mechatronic Design Methodology (used in all case studies)

Every case study in Module 4 must be approached through this **5-stage recursive loop**:

1. **System Requirement Analysis** – Define functional specs (range, accuracy, speed, payload).
2. **Mathematical Modeling** – Derive equations of motion, electrical KVL/KCL, thermal PDEs.
3. **Component Selection (S-S-A-C)** – Select **Sensors**, **Signal Conditioning**, **Actuators**, **Controller**.
4. **Simulation & Controller Design** – Use MATLAB/Simulink to design **PID / Lead-Lag / State-Feedback**.
5. **Implementation, Testing & Iteration** – Deploy, measure, retune.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education maps this to **CO4 (Analyze)** and **CO5 (Design integrated mechatronic systems)**.

## 2.2 Generic Signal-Flow of a Mechatronic System

The fundamental closed-loop architecture of *every* mechatronic case study is:

**Reference → Comparator → Controller → Power Amplifier → Actuator → Mechanical Plant → Output**  
with **Sensor → Signal Conditioner → Feedback to Comparator**.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> All formulas below are **directly applicable** to derivation/numerical questions on CNC machines, pick-and-place robots, AGVs, and washing machines.

| # | Formula / Equation | Variable Definitions | Units | Application |
|---|--------------------|----------------------|-------|-------------|
| 1 | $T_m(s) = \dfrac{K_m \cdot I_a(s)}{1 + \tau_e s}$ | $K_m$ = torque const, $\tau_e$ = electrical time const $= L_a/R_a$ | Nm, A, s | DC motor torque build-up |
| 2 | $V_b(s) = K_b \cdot \omega_m(s)$ | $K_b$ = back-EMF constant | V·s/rad | Back-EMF feedback |
| 3 | $J\dot{\omega} + B\omega = T_m - T_L$ | $J$ = inertia, $B$ = viscous damping, $T_L$ = load torque | kg·m², N·m·s | Newton-Euler mechanical eq. |
| 4 | $\dfrac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \dfrac{K_m}{s[(L_a s + R_a)(J s + B) + K_b K_m]}$ | Full DC motor TF (armature controlled) | rad/V | Open-loop motor TF |
| 5 | $G_c(s) = K_p + \dfrac{K_i}{s} + K_d s$ | PID controller TF | — | Continuous-time PID |
| 6 | $e_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot E(s) = \dfrac{1}{1 + K_p}$ (Type-0, step input) | Steady-state error for unit step | — | Position control accuracy |
| 7 | $\omega_n = \sqrt{\dfrac{K}{J}}, \quad \zeta = \dfrac{B}{2\sqrt{KJ}}$ | Natural freq. & damping ratio | rad/s, — | 2nd-order response shaping |
| 8 | $M_p = e^{-\pi\zeta/\sqrt{1-\zeta^2}} \times 100\%$ | Maximum overshoot | % | Transient specification |
| 9 | $T_s \approx \dfrac{4}{\zeta \omega_n}$ | 2% settling time | s | Speed of response |
| 10 | $\text{Gear ratio } N = \dfrac{\theta_m}{\theta_L} = \dfrac{T_L}{T_m}$ | $N$ = gear reduction ratio | — | Mechanical amplification |

> [!WARNING]
> In tables, the absolute value / magnitude separator is written as $\vert$ — never use the literal pipe $\vert$ inside markdown table cells; otherwise the table breaks.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **CNC Machines:** Math Model 1-4 + PID (5,6) → sub-$\mu m$ positioning.
- **Pick-and-Place Robots:** Math Model 7-9 → joint trajectory tracking.
- **Washing Machines:** Timer-driven state machine (computing) + motor speed profile (control).
- **AGVs / Drones:** Sensor fusion (electronic) + state feedback (control) + mechanical chassis.
- **Hard Disk Drive (HDD):** A classic KTU example combining voice-coil actuator, servo, and air-bearing mechanics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Master Case Study: **DC Servo-Driven Pick-and-Place Robot Joint** (Detailed Mathematical Model)

This is the **highest-frequency derivation** in KTU past papers (seen in Dec 2021, May 2022, Dec 2023 university exams). The joint is driven by an armature-controlled DC motor through a gear train, with a position encoder providing feedback.

### 3.1.1 Electrical Subsystem Derivation

Apply **Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL)** around the armature circuit:

$$V_a(t) = R_a i_a(t) + L_a \frac{di_a(t)}{dt} + e_b(t)$$

The back-EMF induced by the rotating armature is proportional to angular velocity:

$$e_b(t) = K_b \cdot \omega_m(t)$$

Taking the **Laplace Transform** (zero initial conditions):

$$V_a(s) = (R_a + L_a s) I_a(s) + K_b \omega_m(s)$$

Solving for armature current:

$$I_a(s) = \frac{V_a(s) - K_b \omega_m(s)}{R_a + L_a s}$$

### 3.1.2 Mechanical Subsystem Derivation

The motor produces torque proportional to armature current. After gear reduction with ratio $N = \theta_m / \theta_L$:

$$T_m(t) = K_m \cdot i_a(t)$$

Newton's 2nd law for rotation, reflecting all inertia and damping to the **motor shaft** side:

$$J_m \frac{d\omega_m}{dt} + B_m \omega_m(t) = T_m(t) - \frac{T_L(t)}{N}$$

In Laplace domain:

$$(J_m s + B_m) \omega_m(s) = K_m I_a(s) - \frac{T_L(s)}{N}$$

### 3.1.3 Eliminating $I_a(s)$ — Substituting

Substitute the expression for $I_a(s)$ into the mechanical equation:

$$(J_m s + B_m) \omega_m(s) = K_m \cdot \frac{V_a(s) - K_b \omega_m(s)}{R_a + L_a s} - \frac{T_L(s)}{N}$$

Multiply through by $(R_a + L_a s)$:

$$(J_m s + B_m)(R_a + L_a s) \omega_m(s) = K_m V_a(s) - K_m K_b \omega_m(s) - \frac{(R_a + L_a s) T_L(s)}{N}$$

Collecting $\omega_m(s)$ terms to the LHS:

$$\left[ (J_m s + B_m)(R_a + L_a s) + K_m K_b \right] \omega_m(s) = K_m V_a(s) - \frac{(R_a + L_a s) T_L(s)}{N}$$

### 3.1.4 Open-Loop Transfer Function

For **$T_L(s) = 0$** (no-load analysis, standard in KTU derivations):

$$\frac{\omega_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_m K_b}$$

Since position $\theta_m(s) = \omega_m(s)/s$:

$$\boxed{\;\frac{\theta_m(s)}{V_a(s)} = \frac{K_m}{s\left[(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_m K_b\right]}\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **plant transfer function** $G_p(s)$ to which a PID controller $G_c(s)$ is cascaded in all pick-and-place / CNC / AGV case studies.

### 3.1.5 Closed-Loop Transfer Function with PID

The PID controller has the transfer function:

$$G_c(s) = K_p + \frac{K_i}{s} + K_d s = \frac{K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i}{s}$$

The forward-path transfer function is:

$$G(s) = G_c(s) \cdot G_p(s) = \frac{(K_d s^2 + K_p s + K_i) \cdot K_m}{s^2 \left[(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_m K_b\right]}$$

With unity feedback $H(s) = 1$ (encoder provides direct position):

$$\frac{\theta_m(s)}{\theta_{ref}(s)} = \frac{G(s)}{1 + G(s)}$$

This becomes a **Type-2 system** because of the $s^2$ in the denominator of $G(s)$ — meaning **zero steady-state error** to both **step** and **ramp** inputs (a KTU favourite for 7-mark questions).

### 3.1.6 Numerical Worked Example (KTU 2019-style 7-mark)

**Given:** $R_a = 2\,\Omega$, $L_a = 0.5\,H$, $K_m = 0.05\,Nm/A$, $K_b = 0.05\,V\cdot s/rad$, $J_m = 2\times 10^{-4}\,kg\cdot m^2$, $B_m = 1\times 10^{-4}\,Nm\cdot s/rad$.  
Find the **no-load steady-state speed** for $V_a = 24\,V$.

At steady state, $s = 0$:

$$\omega_{ss} = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \omega_m(s) = \lim_{s \to 0} s \cdot \frac{K_m \cdot (24/s)}{(L_a s + R_a)(J_m s + B_m) + K_m K_b}$$

$$\omega_{ss} = \frac{0.05 \times 24}{2 \times 1\times 10^{-4} + 0.05 \times 0.05} = \frac{1.2}{0.0002 + 0.0025} = \frac{1.2}{0.0027}$$

$$\boxed{\;\omega_{ss} \approx 444.44\,rad/s \approx 4243\,RPM\;}$$

### 3.1.7 Analytical Matrix Mapping

| Subsystem | Governing Principle | Mathematical Form | Case-Study Example |
|-----------|---------------------|-------------------|--------------------|
| Mechanical | Newton's 2nd law (rotational) | $J\ddot{\theta} + B\dot{\theta} = T$ | Robot joint, HDD head |
| Electrical | Kirchhoff's Voltage Law | $V = Ri + L\frac{di}{dt} + e_b$ | DC motor, solenoid |
| Electronic | Op-amp signal conditioning | $V_{out} = \left(1 + \frac{R_f}{R_i}\right)V_{in}$ | Encoder signal amplifier |
| Control | PID / State-Feedback | $u(t) = K_p e + K_i\int e + K_d \dot e$ | Position servo |
| Computing | State machine / FSM | Next-state logic in MCU | Washing machine cycles |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Block Diagram: Generic Mechatronic System

The diagram below is the *single most important* block diagram for Module 4. **Memorize it** — it can be re-drawn for any case study in 1-2 minutes in the exam hall.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Reference Input] --> B[Comparator]
    B --> C[Microcontroller PLC DSP]
    C --> D[Power Amplifier]
    D --> E[Actuator DC Motor Stepper Solenoid]
    E --> F[Mechanical Plant Gear Linkage]
    F --> G[Physical Output]
    F --> H[Sensor Encoder LVDT Thermistor]
    H --> I[Signal Conditioner]
    I --> B
    style A fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000
    style C fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style E fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style H fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style G fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

## 4.2 Case Study 1: Automatic Washing Machine (State-Machine View)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    s1[IDLE State] --> s2[WATER FILL Level Sensor]
    s2 --> s3[HEAT Temperature Sensor]
    s3 --> s4[WASH Motor PWM Drive]
    s4 --> s5[RINSE Tachometer Feedback]
    s5 --> s6[SPIN High Speed Dry]
    s6 --> s7[END Buzzer]
    s7 --> s1
    stepA[Microcontroller Master Clock] -.-> s1
    stepA -.-> s2
    stepA -.-> s3
    stepA -.-> s4
    stepA -.-> s5
    stepA -.-> s6
    stepA -.-> s7
    style stepA fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000
    style s1 fill:#E0E0E0,stroke:#000
    style s4 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style s6 fill:#FF6347,stroke:#000
```

## 4.3 Case Study 2: Pick-and-Place Robot Arm (Closed-Loop Control)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    a1[Target Position Xd Yd Zd] --> a2[Trajectory Planner]
    a2 --> a3[Joint Angle Setpoint theta ref]
    a3 --> a4[PID Controller in MCU]
    a4 --> a5[PWM H Bridge Driver]
    a5 --> a6[DC Servo Motor with Gearbox]
    a6 --> a7[Robot Link]
    a7 --> a8[End Effector Position]
    a8 --> a9[Optical Encoder]
    a9 --> a10[Decoder and Counter]
    a10 --> a4
    style a4 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000
    style a6 fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000
    style a9 fill:#FFB6C1,stroke:#000
    style a8 fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000
```

## 4.4 Case Study 3: CNC Milling Machine (Hierarchical Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    top1[G Code Program NC File] --> top2[CNC Controller Interpreter]
    top2 --> top3[Trajectory Interpolator]
    top3 --> top4[Axis Servo Loop X]
    top3 --> top5[Axis Servo Loop Y]
    top3 --> top6[Axis Servo Loop Z]
    top4 --> top7[Ball Screw Linear Stage]
    top5 --> top7
    top6 --> top7
    top7 --> top8[Tool Workpiece Interaction]
    top8 --> top9[Force Vibration Sensor]
    top9 --> top4
```
